Hello! It sounds like you are mixing up a few details between the two eras and the geography of the continent. Let's get the locations and the timeline straightened out.

First, the capital of Edrad has always been the coastal city of Jia, which sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea. Rathakos is not the capital of Edrad; in the present-day Qamis era, Rathakos is a separate mountainous border nation that only recently gained independence. In fact, during the Qaringil prequel era (set 200 years earlier), Rathakos did not even exist as a nation yet.

Regarding the Keyberos dynasty:

1. In the Qaringil era (~200 years ago), the Keyberos had already been ruling Edrad for two centuries.
2. In the Qamis present, their total history spans four centuries, but they were recently deposed for thirty years during the Ryraxian War—first by a Revolutionary Council and then by the dragon Ryraxos.
3. The "restoration" mentioned in the notes refers to them returning to the throne in Jia now that the war is over and Ryraxos has been exiled to the moon Secundus.

Does that help clarify the distinction between the ancient dynasty in Jia and the new nation of Rathakos?

That clears it up. I must have been looking at the section about the Red Mountain Republic and got turned around. So in the Qaringil era, was Edrad already the big dominant power? Or were they still trying to expand their borders?

In both eras, Edrad is described as the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis, but its borders were different in the past. 

In the Qaringil prequel (~200 years ago), Edrad did not yet control the city of Qeth or the region to the northeast of it. Conquering those lands was a primary goal of Edran foreign policy at that time. 

By the Qamis present day, those territories are part of their historical lines, but the central government in Jia is struggling. The notes mention that while the Keyberos dynasty has signed edicts to put everything back the way it was before the thirty-year war, enforcing those laws is much harder now. Many regions have changed significantly during the occupation and the fighting.
Got it. One last thing—the notes mention the drow in Qama. Is that something that happened in the Qaringil era too, or is that part of the newer history?

The drow presence on the surface is a very recent development specific to the Qamis present. They were recruited as allies by Artkos during the Ryraxian War out of desperation. 

In the Qaringil prequel, the holy city of Qama (in the region of Qismos) was the traditional, undisputed seat of the Church of Bahamut. It is only in the present day that the drow have occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, causing a religious schism with the alternate candidate, Qachu II. If you are looking at the Qaringil era, the drow are still primarily underground and haven't established those surface enclaves yet.

I am so hyped to be playing a member of the Gondolier Guild for our Qaringil campaign! Since the city is basically built on canals and handles the maintenance of the Great Western Bridge, I really want to know: how intense are the tides? With all those moons, is the water level just constantly slamming up and down?

That is an amazing character concept! Being part of one of the Great Guilds in Qaringil means you would be intimately familiar with the waters of the central sea. To answer your question: yes, the tides are significantly more powerful and complex than what we experience on Earth. You have three main moons creating the daily oscillating tidal load: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Combined, they exert a tidal force about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's moon! Tertius is your biggest driver, contributing about 2.04 times Luna's force all by itself, followed by Secundus at 1.47 and Quartus at 0.87. As a Gondolier, you'd be tracking three different overlapping cycles ranging from roughly 10 to 14 hours. It makes for a very dynamic environment for those canal runs!

4.4 times the force? That is wild! My gondolier is going to have some serious muscles. But wait, what about Primus? I saw that one is geostationary, so it just sits there in the sky over the same spot. Does it just pull the water into one giant permanent wave?

You hit the nail on the head! Because Primus is magically anchored and co-rotates perfectly with Qaia, it never moves across the sky and therefore produces no rising or falling tide. Instead, it creates a permanent static bulge in the ocean of about 2 centimeters. It is essentially a fixed geographic feature where the sea level is slightly higher along the Primus-antiprimus axis. While it doesn't add to the daily chaos of the 'slamming' tides, it does mean the baseline water level in Qaringil—if you are in the hemisphere that can see Primus—is permanently offset by that small amount. Your Gondolier Guild charts would definitely have that 2cm baseline baked into every calculation!

Hey there. I'm checking out the Qamis setting notes and I noticed a really specific detail about dragons making excellent artillery officers because they can 'help carry the guns.' Is that a joke, or is there a genuine tactical reason for it in this world's history?

It is a very literal piece of military history in the Qamis era. It specifically refers to Ryraxos, a bronze dragon who rose to prominence during the thirty-year Ryraxian War. In this setting, dragons are formidable but not invincible; Ryraxos himself was relatively young—around sixty—and roughly comparable in power to a well-commanded artillery battery or a high-level adventuring party. The 'carrying the guns' part is a practical logistical advantage: because of their immense strength and ability to fly, dragons can reposition heavy field pieces across difficult terrain much faster than horse teams or manual labor, allowing for high-mobility heavy fire.

That makes sense for moving them, but the note calls him an 'artillery officer.' Was he just a beast of burden, or was he actually commanding the units? And does the shapeshifting come into play at all for a commander like that?

Ryraxos was definitely a commander, not just a pack animal. He actually began his rise to power specifically as an artillery officer. Like all dragons in this setting, Ryraxos can shapeshift, which allows a dragon to integrate into a standard military hierarchy, interact with human or half-elven generals in a command tent, and study maps or treaties. When the battle starts, they can revert to their draconic form to provide aerial reconnaissance, direct fire from an elevated position, or physically haul a cannon to a cliffside that the enemy thought was inaccessible. It was this combination of tactical intellect and physical utility that allowed him to eventually seize control of Edrad and wage war across the continent for twenty-five years.
If a single dragon was that effective, are there other dragon-led batteries or 'draconic artillery' divisions in the other nations, like the Artklan League or Forthyr?

In the current Qamis timeline, Ryraxos was quite unique. Most dragons are no longer present in the main continental politics; indeed, Ryraxos's defeat resulted in his exile to Secundus, Qaia's second moon. In Forthyr, to the northeast, there is a dynasty of 'Dragoncallers' (currently Shanlong VI), but their draconic bloodlines have thinned over time. While Forthyr has a religious sect that wants the 'Dragon Emperors' to return, you don't see dragons acting as commissioned officers in their modern military. Most nations, like those in the Artklan League or the restored Keyberos dynasty in Edrad, are currently focused on rebuilding from the damage Ryraxos caused, rather than recruiting more dragons. There's even a rumor that the remaining dragons might have retreated to the big, unexplored island far to the south, near Freeport.

Hey, I was reading the moon notes for our game and I'm confused about Primus. It says it doesn't have a daily tide like the other moons, just a 'permanent bulge' in the ocean. How does that work? Shouldn't every moon cause tides that go in and out?

That is a great question. On Earth (and for the other moons on Qaia), tides go in and out because the moon is moving across the sky relative to the ground. As the moon passes over an ocean, its gravity pulls the water toward it, creating a wave or 'bulge' that follows the moon's position.

Primus is different because it is geostationary. This means it is magically anchored in an orbit that perfectly matches the rotation of the planet. Because it takes exactly one day to orbit, it stays fixed over the same spot on the equator (the prime meridian) and never rises or sets. 

Since Primus never moves from its spot in the sky, the gravity it exerts on the ocean is always pulling from the exact same direction. Instead of a wave that travels around the world, you get a permanent, stationary hill of water about 2 centimeters high right under the moon. It's basically a fixed geographic feature of the ocean level rather than a daily cycle. The actual 'high' and 'low' tides you'll experience in the campaign are caused by the other moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—because they actually move through the sky.

Hey! I'm joining my first ever D&D game soon and the DM said the world is called Qamis. I was looking at the map and saw a huge area on the left called Westron. What's the deal with that place? Is it like a kingdom or something?

Welcome to your first campaign! Westron is actually a completely different continent located to the west of Qamis. In the campaign notes, it is described as being very large and 'exotic,' but for the most part, it is a 'blank space.' This means the DM hasn't filled in the specific kingdoms or history yet, leaving it open for players to help create those details through their character backstories.
Oh, cool! So I could actually be from there? If I made a character from Westron, would they be like the people in Qis'mos or Edrad? Are there any details at all about the people?

You definitely could be from there! There are a few hints in the lore. In the records from the Qaringil era—which is a prequel set about 200 years before the main game—it mentions that Westron is 'important' but has major religious differences. Specifically, the people there mostly do not worship Bahamut, the dragon god who is a huge deal in the regions of Qis'mos and the holy city of Qama. It is a land where the traditions you see in Qamis might not exist at all.
Wait, so if they don't worship the dragon god, who do they worship? And what does 'exotic' mean here—do they have different magic or weird monsters?

That is one of the biggest 'blank spaces' in the DM's notes. It isn't specified which gods they follow or if their magic works differently. The word 'exotic' is just a placeholder for now. This is a great chance for you to talk to your DM; if you want your character to come from a land of unique alchemy or different legends, Westron is the perfect place to put them because the lore hasn't been written yet.
That's awesome. I was also reading about the moons and how they help with sailing. If Westron is so far away, how do ships get there? Would they use that moon Primus that stays in one spot?

Navigating to Westron would be a major challenge! In Qamis, sailors use Primus—the geostationary moon—to find their longitude because it stays fixed in the sky above the equator. However, Primus is only visible from one side of the planet. If you sail far enough west toward Westron, Primus would eventually sink below the horizon and disappear. At that point, a navigator would have to use much harder methods, like tracking the cycles of the other moons like Secundus, Tertius, or Quartus.

So if my character grew up in Westron, they might have never even seen Primus? That's trippy. Also, does the name 'Westron' follow that rule where Q sounds like a 'Kh'? Like is it pronounced Khestron?

Actually, since Westron starts with a 'W' and doesn't have a 'Q' in it, you just pronounce it normally! The 'Q as Kh' rule comes from Old Draconic, which is why Qamis is pronounced 'Khamis' and Qaringil is 'Kharingil.' But you're right about the sky—if you're from the far side of Westron, Primus is permanently hidden behind the curve of the world, so it would be a total mystery to your people.

So I'd be a total outsider. No dragon god, no 'fixed moon' in the sky, and probably a weird accent. If I showed up in Rathakos where the game starts, would people even know what I'm talking about? Is there a trade route or something?

You would definitely be a curiosity! The notes don't mention any official trade routes or famous explorers from Westron yet. Since the campaign starts in Rathakos—a mountainous border state dealing with its own internal unrest and old dragon cults—a traveler from a continent that doesn't even see the same moons would seem very mysterious. It's a perfect setup for a character who feels like they come from a completely different world.

Hey, I missed the deep-dive on the lunar system the other day. I am really into the worldbuilding aspects of these settings, and I heard Tertius is the reason the calendar has an eight-day week? Can you break down the physics of that for me?

Exactly right. In the Qaia moon system, Tertius is a prograde moon with an orbital period of 8.15 days. Because it is one of the most prominent objects in the sky, people on the surface use that cycle as the basis for the eight-day week. It is much faster than Quartus, which handles the monthly 29.5-day cycle. Physically, Tertius is quite dense—about 1.6 times the density of Earth's moon—meaning it is iron-enriched. Even though it is physically smaller than Quartus, it is much closer (0.45 LD), so it actually appears slightly larger in the sky, with a mean angular diameter of 33.7 arcminutes.

That is fascinating. Does it look brighter than our moon because it is closer? Also, since you said it is prograde, how does its movement across the sky look compared to the retrograde ones like Secundus?

Actually, it is slightly dimmer. While it is larger in the sky than Earth's moon, its albedo—or surface reflectiveness—is only 0.09 compared to our moon's 0.12. This darker, rocky surface compensates for the proximity, resulting in a full-moon brightness of magnitude -12.60, which is just a hair dimmer than a terrestrial full moon. As for the movement, it creates a very busy sky. Since Tertius is prograde, it moves West-to-East across the stars. However, Secundus is retrograde. If you were standing on the surface, you would see Tertius and Quartus moving one way, while Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus actually rise in the West and sweep across the sky toward the East. It makes for a complex celestial dance, especially considering the stationary Primus is just sitting there at a fixed point for half the world.

Hey nanochat. I missed the end of the last session and the guys were rambling about "Full-Moon Eras" and "New-Moon Eras." Honestly, it sounds like some hand-wavy flavor text the DM threw in to sound epic. Is there an actual system for this or is it just vibes?

It is actually based on a specific 12-year cycle in the campaign's orbital mechanics. The three main inner moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—have orbital periods of roughly 4.5, 8.1, and 29.5 days. Because of the way these periods interact, they go through phases where triple-alignments (syzygy) happen frequently.

A 12-year cycle? That sounds way too convenient for a campaign timeline. You're telling me the math actually works out for "eras" where the moons are always full at the same time? It sounds like the DM is just making up an excuse for a Blood Moon plot point.

The math is actually quite rigid in the notes. For example, the decade from 2253 to 2263 is split into distinct blocks. 2253–2254 is a full-moon era where you see near-perfect triple-full alignments—like on February 13, 2253, where all three moons are within 0.2% of full simultaneously. Then the system shifts into a new-moon era from 2255–2258, where triple-full moons don't happen at all, but triple-new moons (where all three are dark) occur regularly. It isn't just a random event; it's a predictable astronomical phenomenon.

Okay, so there's a calendar. But does it actually matter for gameplay, or is it just flavor text I can ignore while I'm hitting goblins? Like, does a 'Full-Moon Era' change anything besides how much light we have at night?

It has significant mechanical implications for the world. First, the tidal force on Qaia is roughly 4.4 times that of Earth's Moon because these three moons are massive and close. During these alignment eras, the high tides are synchronized and extreme. Second, for navigation, the brightness is a factor. Tertius and Quartus are almost as bright as Earth's moon, so during a triple-full alignment, the night is incredibly bright—almost 2.2 times the illumination of a single Earth full moon. This affects stealth and nighttime travel. Finally, for sailors, these eras determine visibility for Primus, the geostationary moon used to calculate longitude. If you're in a new-moon era, celestial navigation becomes much harder.

I have been playing in the Forgotten Realms and Greyhawk for decades, and I have to say, the celestial mechanics of Qaia are absolutely fascinating! A geostationary moon for longitude? That is such a cool mechanical detail for a DM to play with. But I just realized—Primus is only visible from one side of the planet. If I am a captain sailing into the far-side hemisphere where Primus is permanently below the horizon, what do I do? Is there a 'hard mode' for navigation out there?

It is definitely 'Hard Mode' out there! You have hit on the massive navigational asymmetry of this world. While near-side sailors have it easy with the 'fixed point' of Primus (pronounced 'Khrimus' if we are sticking to the Old Draconic Q-sounds), far-side navigators are basically living through Earth's 18th-century longitude crisis every single night. According to the navigation notes, they have to rely on three much more difficult methods: dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, and the 'lunar-distance' method. Without that geostationary anchor, you are back to needing a high-precision chronometer or doing some incredibly complex math involving the other moons against a star catalogue.

That is so cool! The lunar-distance method sounds like a nightmare with so many moons. Since there are five other freely-orbiting moons—Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus—which ones are the navigators actually using? I assume some are better than others for tracking position!

You are exactly right! Not all moons are created equal for navigation. Quartus is the most reliable 'standard' because it is basically a clone of Earth's Moon with a 29.5-day cycle. However, for a navigator trying to find their position quickly, Secundus is the real MVP of the far-side. Because Secundus is retrograde (it rises in the west!) and has a very fast orbital period of only 4.53 days, its motion against the 'fixed' background of stars is much more rapid than the others. This makes it easier to measure its exact position at a specific moment to determine the time. Of course, you still have to deal with the fact that the total tidal force on Qaia is about 4.4 times what we see on Earth, so while you are squinting at Secundus to find your longitude, the tides are probably doing something absolutely wild to your ship!

I am fascinated by the linguistic history of Qaia, specifically how the 'Q' in place names like Qis'mos is pronounced as 'Kh' due to Old Draconic influence. I noticed the notes describe Forthyr as somewhat 'backwards' compared to nations like Edrad. Does this 'backwards' label imply they have preserved more of that Old Draconic 'Q-without-U' terminology in their daily life, or is it strictly about their politics and the Dragoncaller dynasty?

In the context of the Qamis campaign, the 'backwards' label for Forthyr refers primarily to its social and religious structure rather than its linguistic preservation. While Old Draconic (or Old Qis) is the prestige language for scholarly work and treaties across all of Qamis, Forthyr is considered backwards because of its intense cultural fixation on the past. Specifically, a prominent religious sect there calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors, a movement that the ruling Dragoncaller dynasty—currently led by Shanlong VI—tacitly encourages.

Linguistically, the 'Q' pronounced as 'Kh' is a standard feature of the setting's formal and historical geography, derived from the Old Draconic that Forthyr respects. However, while Edrad and the Artklan League have moved toward half-elven nobility and republican experiments, Forthyr remains under the rule of sorcerer-kings who still lean on their thinning dragonborn bloodlines. Their 'backwardness' is a matter of yearning for an ancient draconic imperial order that the rest of the continent has largely transitioned away from following the Ryraxian War.
Hi! I am totally new to D&D and my friend is starting a game soon. He said we are playing in a 'prequel' setting in a city called Qaringil. Who is actually in charge there? Is there a king?

Welcome to the hobby! Qaringil is a great place to start your first adventure. To answer your question: No, there is no king. Qaringil is an island city-state run as a republic. The top official is called an Exarch. The current one is named Giovanni Pesaro, but he has been in power for 19 years and is reportedly very ill. However, the real day-to-day power lies with the 'Great Guilds' and the 'Five Families' that control them. Since the city relies on trade and fishing rather than owning farmland, these families are merchant princes rather than traditional lords.
The Five Families? That sounds like the mafia. Are they all humans? And do they have like, special logos or something?

They aren't exactly the mafia, though they can be very ruthless! They are mostly human, but in this setting, all five families actually have some elf ancestry as well. They do have logos, which are called 'sigils.' Each family generally controls one of the Great Guilds that runs the city's economy. For example, the Peixota family has a Dolphin as their sigil and they lead the Shipping Guild. The Manos family uses a Hand as their sigil and they lead the Judicial Guild, which handles the laws and courts.
A Dolphin and a Hand, got it. Who are the other three? I want to know who might be my boss or my enemy.

The other three are the Volmer, Vitelli, and Qanto families. The Volmer family uses a Crow sigil and they lead the Banking Guild—very useful if you need a loan or a place to keep your gold. The Vitelli family uses a Moth sigil and they lead the Silk Guild. Finally, there is the Qanto family, whose sigil is a Dove. Interestingly, the Qantos recently lost control of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family called the Tiddwars, who are more aligned with the current Exarch. This has made the Qantos a bit of an underdog among the big players right now.
Wait, what's the Silk Guild? Is that just for making clothes, or is it a power thing?

In Qaringil, it's a huge power thing. The Silk and Wool guilds handle both the production and the trade of those materials, which are massive exports for the city. Because the Vitelli family leads the Silk Guild, they have a lot of money and influence over foreign trade. There's actually a lot of drama right now because the Manos family (the Hand sigil) is trying to get into the wool business, and the other guilds are fighting them over it. As a level 5 player, you might find yourself caught in the middle of these commercial wars!

Hey, I missed the last session! The group was talking about some navigation math called 'primsine' and how it is the local version of a haversine? Also, did we get any more clarity on the Bahamut situation? I am still stuck on why anyone in Qis'mos would listen to that drow-appointed priest in Qama when the other guy is right there in the capital.

Welcome back. The party spent some time with a navigator, so the 'primsine' talk was about how they find their way at sea. It is analogous to Earth's haversine because it is a precomputed geometric function of a single variable designed to reduce a complex navigation problem into a simple table lookup. On Earth, the haversine helps find great-circle distances. On Qaia, the 'primsine' uses the geostationary moon, Primus. Because Primus is fixed at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude, measuring its elevation above the horizon gives you a 'primsine' value. You then just divide that by the cosine of your latitude (from the pole star) to get your longitude. No clocks or chronometers needed, which is a huge deal for sailors in this era.

As for the Church of Bahamut, it is a mess. You are right that Qachu II is in the city of Qis, and the Qis language is the closest thing to the 'holy' Old Draconic (pronounced 'Kh-is'). However, the city of Qama is the traditional, ancestral holy seat. The drow priest there, Qe'lth IV, was appointed by the drow enclaves that stayed behind after the Ryraxian War. Even though he is a drow appointee, he holds the actual physical city and all the holy relics of the office. Qachu II is essentially a 'pope in exile' without his regalia, which creates a massive crisis of legitimacy for the faithful.

That is wild. So the 'primsine' is basically a shortcut for the moon's parallax? And regarding the schism, is there any talk of the Dragoncallers in Forthyr getting involved? If they want the Dragon Emperors back, wouldn't they have a stake in who the High Priest of Bahamut is?

Exactly. Because Primus is relatively close (0.11 lunar distances), its parallax is significant. The 'primsine' formula—rho times cosine squared of elevation plus the sine of elevation times a square root—factors in that distance constant (rho = 0.1511). It is a permanent mathematical feature of the planet's relationship with that specific moon.

Regarding Forthyr and the Dragoncallers, the notes indicate that Forthyr is currently considered 'somewhat backwards' and has historically neglected the southwest. However, they are ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, and there is a religious sect there calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. While the Dragoncaller dynasty gives this sect tacit encouragement, we haven't seen them make a formal move on the Qama-Qis schism yet. They are more focused on their own internal sorcerer-king politics, though a unified Church of Bahamut would certainly be a threat—or a powerful tool—for their 'return of the emperors' agenda. For now, the schism remains a primary headache for the Potentate of Qis'mos.

Hi there! I'm totally new to D&D, but my friend is starting a game in Qamis and mentioned that half-elves aren't the usual wandering outcasts here. He said they actually run the main kingdom, Edrad. Coming from fantasy novels where they're usually caught between two worlds, that sounds really cool. How did they end up as the high-and-mighty nobility there, and is there a specific reason they stay in power?

In Edrad, half-elves are far from outcasts; they are the ruling elite. The Keyberos dynasty has sat on the throne in the capital city of Jia for four hundred years in the present Qamis era (or two hundred years if your campaign is the Qaringil prequel). The social structure is very rigid about this: to be considered part of the Edran nobility, a person must legally be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. This unique requirement ensures the aristocracy remains specifically half-elven. Because Edrad is the most politically and economically dominant nation on the continent, their half-elven culture is seen as the height of fashion and prestige across Qamis. While the notes don't explain the exact historical origin of why this bloodline requirement began, it is a strictly enforced law of the land. Just a quick tip as you start: the letter Q in this setting is pronounced like 'Kh', so the continent name Qamis actually sounds like 'Khamis'.

I am thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil era, but I want to understand the endgame of the world first. How exactly did the Ryraxian War end in the 'present' Qamis setting? I see Ryraxos is on a moon now.

The Ryraxian War, which lasted thirty years, concluded when the bronze dragon Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, Qaia's second moon. This followed a joint effort by Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire to depose the Revolutionary Council in Edrad and restore the Keyberos dynasty. While Ryraxos originally delivered Edrad from defeat, he eventually waged aggressive war across Qamis for twenty-five years. His defeat led to the 'Settlements of Ath'r,' which attempted to restore the continent to its pre-war state. The Keyberos family returned to the throne in Jia, and the borders of Edrad were pushed back to their historical lines. However, while the edicts say everything is back to normal, the reality is far more fractured.

That is interesting. In the Qaringil notes, it mentions the Holy Qis Empire rules from Ath'r, but in the Qamis era, it is the Artklan League. How did the resolution of the war specifically change that power structure?

The transition is significant. In the Qaringil prequel era (200 years earlier), the Holy Qis Empire is the dominant force in central Qamis, though the notes describe it as 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' By the Qamis present, that centralized authority has crumbled. During the Ryraxian War, the dragon actually dissolved the old Artklan Empire. The resolution created the 'Artklan League,' which the High Kings of Artkos view as a pale shadow of their former glory. Many territories, like the new nations of Rathakos and Acrokos in the Red Mountains, emerged from the vacuum left by the collapse of the Empire and the subsequent failure of Edran occupation. Essentially, the war ended with a legal 'restoration' that masked the permanent loss of imperial control.

I see a note about drow being in the holy city of Qama as well. Did that happen during the resolution of the war, and how does that affect the Church of Bahamut, which seems quite central in the Qaringil era?

The drow presence in Qama is one of the most lasting 'changes' the post-war treaties couldn't fix. During the late phases of the war, Artkos recruited drow as allies out of desperation. The drow occupied Qama, the traditional holy city of Bahamut in Qis'mos. Although they didn't destroy the city, they appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This created a major religious schism that defines the Qamis present: there is a rival candidate, Qachu II, who claims the title from the city of Qis but lacks the traditional relics of office held in Qama. In your Qaringil prequel one-shot, the Church of Bahamut would still be unified and powerful in Qama, 200 years before this occupation and schism ever occurred.
hey, sorry I missed the first session! The guys were talking about something called 'primsine' in the group chat and I am totally lost. Is that like a magic spell or a math thing?

No worries, welcome to the game! It's definitely more of a math thing, but it's very important for how people get around in this world. Primsine is a mathematical function used for navigation on the planet Qaia. To understand why it's useful, you have to know about the moon Primus.
Wait, how many moons are there? And what makes that one special?

There are actually six main moons orbiting Qaia (plus a tiny particle called Quintus). Primus is the closest one, and it is 'geostationary.' Because it's magically anchored, it never rises or sets. It stays fixed over a single spot on the equator. If you live in the hemisphere that can see it, Primus is just a motionless dot in the sky that's always there, 24/7.
So it's like a permanent North Star, but for the equator?

Precisely! Because it's a fixed point, navigators use it to solve the 'longitude problem'—figuring out how far east or west you are. Usually, that requires a super accurate clock, which most D&D settings don't have. But on Qaia, you just measure how high Primus is in the sky (its elevation). You plug 그 angle into the 'primsine' formula, and it tells you your longitude instantly. It's basically a shortcut for finding your exact position on a map.
That's actually pretty cool. Does every character know how to do the math, or is that just for the smart wizard types?

Most regular people wouldn't know the actual algebra behind it, but any sailor or traveler would carry a 'Nautical Almanac' with pre-printed primsine tables. You just look up your angle in the book and it gives you the number. It's a huge advantage for the nations of Qamis—well, at least for the side of the planet where Primus is visible. If you're on the far side of the world, you're stuck with old-fashioned guesswork!

Hey, I am rolling up a Drow character who decided to stay on the surface in Qamis after the Ryraxian War ended. I was looking at the notes on Qis'mos and saw something about a schism in the Church of Bahamut. Can you explain what is going on there?

Welcome to Qamis! Since you are playing a Drow, this is a very relevant bit of lore for your character. In the late phases of the war, Artkos recruited your people as allies in a desperation move. During the fighting, Drow forces occupied Qama, which is the holy city of Bahumat (remember, Q is pronounced Kh). After the war ended, many Drow stayed. Instead of leaving Qama, they appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This caused a massive split because the traditional clergy did not recognize him, leading to what is now known as the Bahamut schism.
So we have two rival high priests now? Tell me more about Qe'lth IV and this other guy, Qachu II. Why is Qachu stuck in Qis?

That is correct. It is a classic 'two popes' situation. Qe'lth IV (Khe'lth) is the Drow-appointed priest sitting in the traditional seat of power in Qama. Because he holds the city, he has all the physical relics of office, which carries a lot of weight. Qachu II (Khachu) is the candidate supported by the traditionalists and much of the nobility in Qis'mos. He is forced to operate out of the city of Qis because the Drow still physically control Qama. The big problem for Qachu II is that while people might see him as the 'legitimate' choice, he does not have the actual holy relics, which makes his authority feel incomplete to some.
If my character is one of those Drow still on the surface, but maybe not living in Qama, how do people look at me? Am I automatically seen as a supporter of the Drow priest just because of my race?

It is definitely a source of tension. The notes mention the Drow are scattered in about a dozen enclaves now, with Qama being the most prominent. In the rest of Qamis, people will likely assume you are aligned with Qe'lth IV and the 'occupiers' of the holy city. If your character is a devout follower of Bahamut, you are in a tough spot: do you stay loyal to your people and the priest who holds the relics in Qama, or do you follow the tradition of the Church even if it means supporting Qachu II, who might see your kind as usurpers? Since the war just ended and the Keyberos dynasty was just restored in Edrad, the political climate for a surface-dwelling Drow is quite fragile.

Hey, I missed the session last night and I am trying to fix my character sheet. The DM mentioned something about a 'capital dialect' rule for languages. Look, I have been playing since the boxed sets, and usually, if I am a dwarf, I speak Dwarvish. This 'no racial languages' homebrew stuff always seems like a headache. Give me the summary of how this works and what it actually means for my PC's survival.

In the Qamis setting, there are no racial languages like Elvish or Dwarvish. Communication is divided along national lines instead. The main languages are Edran, Artklan, Qis, Forthrian, and Islas. Also, remember that 'Q' is always pronounced 'Kh' in this world.

Because these nations are large and communication is slow, the languages are not standardized. The 'capital dialect' refers to the prestige version of the language spoken by the ruling class and in the capital city (like Jia in Edrad or Ath'r in Artkos). Someone from the capital of Artkos might struggle to understand a commoner from a border state like Rathakos, even though they both technically speak Artklan.

For your character, the rule is straightforward: you start knowing the capital dialect of your home nation and one regional dialect. You also get one bonus language, but you have to provide a backstory reason for how you learned it. Practically speaking, this means you will have no trouble in high society or the capital, but you might face some social friction or 'translation' checks when dealing with isolated mountain communities or distant provinces unless you have the specific regional dialect for that area.

So if I am a dwarf from Rathakos, I do not speak a word of 'Dwarven' because it does not exist? I just speak two flavors of Artklan? That sounds like a recipe for getting stuck in a dungeon with a sign I cannot read.

That is correct—Dwarven does not exist as a language here. If you are a dwarf from Rathakos, you would speak the Rathakos regional dialect of Artklan and the prestige Artklan dialect used in Ath'r. 

Regarding the dungeon signs, scholarly publications and ancient treaties are written in Old Draconic (also called Old Qis). It is the 'q without u' language you see in many place names. If you are worried about reading ancient inscriptions, you might want to use your one bonus language slot on Old Draconic. Otherwise, most modern signs in the Artklan League will be in some form of Artklan, which you would understand. It is the spoken nuances and local slang of the 'regional' versions that cause the most trouble, not the basic written word.
Fine. If I take Edran as my bonus language, do I get the 'capital' version of that too, or do I have to pick a specific city there as well?

The notes state that 'without qualification, the languages will be understood as meaning the prestige dialect of the language spoken in the capital and by the ruling class.' So if you take Edran as your bonus, you gain the prestige dialect of Jia. You would not automatically know the regional dialects of the Edran countryside or its southern reaches unless you spent another way to acquire them. In short: the capital versions are the default 'common' tongue for each nation.
Hi. Just joined a new campaign and I need an executive summary of the setting. My DM mentioned Qamis and Qaringil. What is the high-level overview for a player?

Qamis is a D&D setting based on the continent of the same name on the planet Qaia. There are two distinct eras to keep track of: the 'Qamis' present day and the 'Qaringil' prequel set 200 years earlier. 

In the present day (Qamis), a thirty-year war involving the dragon Ryraxos has just ended. The Keyberos dynasty has been restored in the half-elven nation of Edrad, and the campaign typically starts in the mountainous border state of Rathakos. 

In the prequel era (Qaringil), the map is quite different. The Holy Qis Empire rules from the city of Ath'r, and the campaign focuses on the island city-state of Qaringil, which is a mercantile republic dominated by powerful guilds and families like the Peixota and the Vitelli.

Key rule for both: the letter 'Q' is always pronounced 'Kh' (like 'Khamis').
Thanks. I saw the name 'Westron' mentioned. What is the executive summary for that continent? Is it a major political player?

Regarding Westron, the honest answer is that it is currently a 'blank space' in the lore. The DM has explicitly noted that very little material is prepared for it yet. 

Here is what is known: it is a very large continent located to the west of Qamis. Culturally and religiously, it is distinct because the people there generally do not worship Bahamut, who is the primary deity in the central lands of Qis'mos. In the Qaringil prequel era, it is described as 'important' but vague; in the Qamis present, it is simply referred to as 'exotic.' Because it's a blank space, it's often used as a hook for player backstories that need a distant or mysterious origin.
I like hard systems and clear mechanics. If we wanted to sail there, how does the navigation work? Is it just standard fantasy 'guessing' or is there a system?

The navigation system on Qaia is actually quite precise because of the moon system. Navigators use two primary anchors: the Pole Star for latitude and the moon Primus for longitude.

Primus is a geostationary moon magically anchored directly over the equator at the prime meridian (0, 0). Because it is geostationary, it never rises or sets; it stays at a fixed point in the sky for the entire 'near-side' hemisphere. By measuring the elevation of Primus and knowing your latitude, you can use a 'primsine' table to calculate your exact longitude without needing a chronometer.

However, for a trip to Westron, there is a major technical challenge: visibility. Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. Once you sail roughly 81 degrees west of the prime meridian, Primus sinks below the horizon. On the 'far-side' of the planet, where Westron likely lies, you lose that fixed reference. Sailors there have to rely on much more difficult methods, like dead reckoning or 'lunar-distance' calculations using the other moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—against the stars. It makes Westron significantly harder to reach and map than the local coastlines.

haiii! i am getting ready for the qaringil campaign and i am really interested in the religious side of things. i was reading about the great guilds and i am wondering how the high priest qelth iv and the bahumat schism are affecting the guilds in the city? does qachu ii have any influence over the judicial or banking guilds?

Welcome to the world of Qaia! It looks like you have crossed some wires between the two different eras in the campaign notes. The situation with High Priest Qe'lth IV, Qachu II, and the schism in the Church of Bahamut is part of the Qamis era, which is the 'present day' setting after the Ryraxian War. The Qaringil campaign is a prequel set roughly 200 years earlier. In the Qaringil era, the city-state of Qaringil is a merchant republic, and the religious schism you mentioned hasn't happened yet. In fact, the drow occupation of the holy city of Qama (which led to Qe'lth IV's appointment) is a very recent event in the Qamis timeline, not the Qaringil one. In Qaringil, the Great Guilds are secular, commercial organizations. There are seven of them: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. While the Church of Bahamut is a major presence in the region of Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos), the power in Qaringil specifically lies with the Great Guilds and the Five Families like the Peixota and the Manos.

ohh i see! my bad. i guess i got excited about the drow priests. so if i want to play someone religious in the qaringil prequel, which of the guilds would be the best fit? are any of them particularly holy or tied to the church since qaringil was built by people from qismos?

No problem at all, the history of Qamis can be dense! In the Qaringil prequel era, the city is definitely a commercial thassolocracy, meaning the guilds are focused on trade and industry. However, you are right that the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, so the Qis language and culture are very influential there. While none of the Great Guilds are strictly religious, the Judicial Guild might be your best bet for a faith-leaning character. It is currently led by the Manos family (sigil: the Hand). Because they handle the legal and moral regulation of the city's incomprehensible republic, it is the natural home for those concerned with justice and order—key tenets of Bahamut. If you want a more 'street-level' religious hook, the Gondolier Guild is unique because it hasn't been taken over by the major noble families, or you could look into the small Mage or Alchemist guilds. Just keep in mind that the Church of Bahamut has its traditional seat in Qama, which at this point in time—200 years before the main campaign—is still the stable, holy center of the faith without the drow schism.

YO!!! I am so hyped to start this Qaringil prequel. I was reading the notes and saw a mention of "Dragon Emperors." That sounds absolutely metal. Who were they exactly, and who is trying to bring them back?

Welcome to the prequel! It is a great time to be building a character. In the Qaringil era (roughly 200 years before the present-day Qamis setting), the talk of Dragon Emperors is centered mostly in the northeast nation of Forthyr. The Dragon Emperors were the actual dragons who ruled the ancient Draconic empire. While those dragons departed long ago, their legacy still looms large, especially since the "Q" in names like Qaringil and Qis comes from Old Draconic!
That is so cool. So is there like a cult or something? I'm playing a character in the city of Qaringil, but I want them to have a connection to this.

Exactly! In Forthyr, there is a prominent religious sect that actively calls for the return of these Dragon Emperors. They get a lot of quiet encouragement—and maybe even direct support—from the ruling Dragoncaller dynasty. In your era, that would be Dragoncaller Shanlong I, a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king. Even though Qaringil is a city of merchants and canals far to the west, you could definitely be a believer or even an agent for the sect. The city has 100,000 people and is a huge melting pot, so a Forthrian immigrant or a scholar of the Old Qis language would fit right in among the Great Guilds!

Wait, did you say half-dragonborn sorcerer-king? That's amazing. I definitely want to be a member of the Mage's Guild who secretly supports the Forthrian sect. Quick question though—how do I pronounce 'Qis'? Is it like 'kiss'?

Good catch! Actually, in this setting, the letter Q is always pronounced like "Kh" (think of a breathy 'K' sound). So the nation of Qis'mos is "Khis-mos," the city of Qaringil is "Kharingil," and the old language is Old Khis. Using the proper "Kh" pronunciation will definitely make your Mage's Guild member sound like a proper scholar of the Draconic era!

Hey, I was digging into the orbital mechanics of your world. I love that the 8-day week is tied to the moon Quartus. Is that calendar still standard in the Holy Qis Empire now that the Ryraxian War is over, or did the Dragoncaller change things?

It sounds like you have a few details from the different eras mixed up, but you are looking at the right celestial bodies. First, the moon that defines the 8-day week is actually Tertius, not Quartus. Tertius has an orbital period of 8.15 days, which provides the natural basis for the week. Quartus is the moon most similar to Earth's, with a 29.5-day cycle that tracks the months. Regarding the political situation, the Holy Qis Empire (pronounced 'Kh-is') actually belongs to the Qaringil prequel era, set about 200 years before the present day. In the current Qamis setting, the empire is gone; the central continent is now dominated by the Artklan League and the restored Keyberos dynasty in Edrad. The Dragoncallers rule the nation of Forthyr in the northeast, but they do not dictate the calendar for the entire continent. The 8-day week based on Tertius remains the standard across both time periods because the moons themselves haven't changed.

Ah, thanks for clearing that up. I keep flipping the moon names in my head. So if Tertius is the weekly moon, how big does it actually look in the sky compared to Quartus? And since it's closer, does it mess with the tides more?

Tertius is actually the most prominent object in the sky. Its mean angular diameter is 33.7 arcminutes, making it slightly larger than Quartus, which sits at 29.6 arcminutes. Even though it is larger, it's a bit dimmer because it has a darker surface—an albedo of 0.09 compared to the 0.12 of the more lunar-like Quartus. You are exactly right about the tides. Because Tertius is closer (0.45 LD) and denser (1.6x lunar density), it exerts a much stronger gravitational pull. Its tidal force on Qaia is 2.04 times that of Earth's Moon. When you combine it with Secundus and Quartus, the total oscillating tidal force on Qaia is about 4.4 times what we experience on Earth, making for a very complex and powerful maritime environment.

I've been reading up on the Bahamut schism between the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV in Qama and Qachu II in Qis. It's a mess. But I'm looking at the map and wondering about the northeast. What is the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr all about? Do they take a side in the Church of Bahamut's drama?

In the present day of Qamis, Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. He is a sorcerer-king and the latest in a long dynasty. Historically, this bloodline was dragonborn, but the notes indicate that over the generations the dragon blood has run thin. 

Regarding the Church of Bahamut (pronounced Ba-ha-mut, with the Q in other names like Qama sounding like 'Kh'), Forthyr is described as somewhat 'backwards' compared to the heartlands like Qis'mos. While the Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence in Qis'mos, Forthyr has its own unique religious climate. There is a prominent sect there that calls for the return of the 'Dragon Emperors.' This sect receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty, though it's not explicitly stated if they've officially entered the fray of the Qama-Qis schism.

That 'Dragon Emperors' sect sounds like a potential rival to the Bahamut orthodoxy. Does the Dragoncaller himself claim to be one of these emperors, or is he just waiting for them? And is there any mention of how the Potentate in Qis'mos views this Forthrian sect?

The notes don't specify that Shanlong VI claims the title of Dragon Emperor himself; rather, he is the 'Dragoncaller' and his dynasty supports the religious movement calling for the emperors' return. It's a bit of a 'blank space' whether they view these emperors as divine beings, historical rulers, or perhaps the dragons that some say fled to the big island in the south. 

As for the Potentate in Qis'mos, the records are silent on a direct confrontation or official stance regarding the Forthrian sect. However, since Qis'mos is the region where the old draconic imperial institutions persisted the longest, they might see the Forthrian movement as a simplified or 'backwards' version of their own history. The notes emphasize that while the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad is trying to restore the old status quo after the Ryraxian War, the religious schism between Qama and Qis is the primary focus of Bahamut worshipers right now, leaving Forthyr's fringe beliefs as a secondary concern for the central powers.

Hey, I am working on a character for the Rathakos campaign—a navigator who spent way too much time staring at the sky during the Ryraxian War. I am trying to get my 'nautical almanac' notes right. Which moons exactly are retrograde? I know it messes with how they rise and set.

That is a great character hook. For your almanac, three of the six main moons are retrograde: Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus. This means they rise in the west and set in the east, which is the opposite of the sun and the prograde moons like Tertius and Quartus. Primus, of course, is geostationary, so it just hangs there without rising or setting at all for anyone on the near-side hemisphere.

Right, Primus is the 'fixed' one at 0,0. I was actually just playing with the primsine formula to see how precise my PC could get with a standard cross-staff. But back to the retrograde ones—does Secundus look weird since it is so big? The notes say it is nearly the size of Earth's moon but dark.

It would definitely look distinct. Secundus has an angular diameter of about 28 arcminutes on average, which is about 90 percent the size of our Moon, but because its surface is dark iron (albedo 0.06), it is about a full magnitude dimmer. Seeing a massive dark disc rushing across the sky from west to east every 4.5 days would be a striking sight for a navigator in Rathakos. It also provides the strongest tidal force at 1.47 times Luna's, so your character would be very attuned to its position for harbor timing.

That is cool. Since I am starting in Rathakos in the 'present day' setting, how would my training differ from someone 200 years ago in Qaringil? I assume the math for the primsine lookup is the same, but the political stuff might change which ports I can even visit to calibrate my instruments.

The orbital physics and the primsine(e) function are constant across both eras because the moon system is stable—the DM's notes say it's stable for at least 2,000 years. However, the 'local color' of your training would be very different. In the Qaringil era, you'd likely be part of the Gondolier or Shipping guilds in the city-state, dealing with the Great Western Bridge and the merchant families like the Peixotas. In the present day in Rathakos, you're in a mountainous border state that just came out of a thirty-year war. You might have learned your trade on military ships or artillery batteries—remember that Ryraxos himself was an artillery commander. Navigation in the Red Mountains of Rathakos is also trickier because you're inland, relying more on the pole star for latitude and Primus for longitude to map unexplored peaks rather than just crossing the Central Sea.

Hi there! I am brand new to tabletop games, but I have read a ton of fantasy novels. A friend was telling me about their campaign in a world called Qamis and mentioned a place called Lock that has free cities. What exactly does that mean in this world?

Welcome! It is great to have a fantasy reader diving into the lore of Qaia. To understand Lock, it helps to know which era your friend is playing in, as the world changes quite a bit over time. There are two main settings: the present-day Qamis era and the Qaringil prequel set 200 years earlier.

In both eras, the name is spelled with a Q in many places (though Lock itself is an exception), and you should know that Q is pronounced as a Kh sound, like in Old Draconic.

In the present day (the Qamis campaign), Lock is a kingdom, but the city of Lock itself and several other major hubs are designated as free cities. This means they operate with a level of autonomy from the central crown. The most striking thing about Lock right now is its burgeoning industrial presence. It is one of the few places where you see that kind of early technological development.

If your friend is playing the prequel (Qaringil), the situation is a bit more tense. Back then, the river cities including Lock were technically part of the Holy Qis Empire, but they were free cities locked in a cold conflict with the Emperor over their ancient prerogatives. They were constantly pushing back against imperial control from the capital of Ath'r.

The industrial bit sounds cool! Is it like a steampunk vibe? And I think my friend mentioned something about a Holy Qis Empire, so they must be doing the prequel. What are these ancient prerogatives they were fighting over?

If they mentioned the Holy Qis Empire, they are definitely in the Qaringil era. In that time, the Empire rules from the city of Ath'r, but its hold on the region is often more theoretical than practical.

The campaign notes do not list every specific ancient prerogative, but they are described as the traditional rights and privileges the cities held before the Empire tried to centralize power. It is a classic setup for political intrigue: the Emperor wants more control and taxes, while the free cities of the river want to maintain their own laws and trade rights. This is why it is called a cold conflict—lots of posturing and legal battles, even if they are not in an open shooting war.

As for the industrial side, that is much more prominent in the present day of the Qamis setting. By the time of the main campaign, Lock has become a significant industrial center. The notes leave some blank spaces regarding the specific technology used, so that is something players and the DM often fill in together, but the transition from a group of defiant trade cities to an industrial kingdom is a major part of Lock's history.
Got it. So if I were to make a character from there in the prequel, I'd probably be someone who doesn't like the Emperor much?

That would be a very natural fit! A character from the city of Lock in the Qaringil era would likely be proud of their city's independence and wary of imperial overreach. Since the elites in the island city-states like Qaringil are primarily commercial rather than landowners, you might even have a character with ties to the merchant or banking guilds who sees the Empire as a threat to free trade.

Just remember that while the Empire claims to be holy and Qis, it is famously described as being neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire in the strictest sense. It is a crumbling or at least inefficient institution, which gives a character from a free city plenty of reasons to be skeptical of its authority.
I've seen plenty of 'High Kings' in homebrew campaigns. Usually it is just a generic fantasy trope for a guy in a big chair. Who is this Ezevir Arth guy in your notes?

In the present-day Qamis setting, High King Ezevir Arth is the ruler of Artkos. He isn't a global emperor; rather, he is the leader of the Artklan League, which is described as a pale shadow of the old Artklan Empire. He governs the territory of Artkos directly, including its historical heartland in the northwest and a central enclave containing the capital city, Ath'r. His primary motivation is restoring the Artklan supremacy over the central continent following the end of the thirty-year Ryraxian War.
Wait, I saw a reference to an Ezevir Arth II in some of the historical blurbs. Is this the same guy? How old is this king supposed to be if he's been around that long?

That is a different person. The campaign has two distinct eras. The current High King Ezevir Arth is the ruler in the 'Qamis' present day. The 'Ezevir Arth II' you saw appears in the 'Qaringil' prequel setting, which takes place roughly 200 years earlier. In that prequel era, King Ezevir Arth II was the ruler who successfully doubled the size of Artkos's holdings in the northwest. He was eventually succeeded by his daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I. The current Ezevir is a descendant of that dynasty, ruling a much more fractured landscape after the recent war.
So he's trying to live up to the ancestor who actually did the heavy lifting. What is his actual reach? Most 'High Kings' are just kings of one city in practice once you look at the map.

His direct control is actually quite split. He rules Artkos, which consists of the northwestern 'West Artkos' territory and the central enclave around the capital, Ath'r. He also claims a southwestern enclave centered on the city of Kard, which is a major financial and shipping center. However, his governance in Kard is noted as fragile because much of the population speaks Edran and actually welcomed the Edran invasion twenty-eight years ago. While he is the 'High King' of the Artklan League, his nominal subjects in places like Rathakos and Lock are clinging to a tenuous quasi-independence, so his actual authority varies wildly depending on which border you are standing in.

I have been looking at these Qaringil prequel notes. Usually, when a DM says it is a prequel, it is just the same map with different names. But you are saying Rathakos and Acrokos straight up do not exist yet? Where are they? Just a blank space on the hex crawl?

In the Qaringil era, which is set roughly 200 years before the present day of the Qamis campaign, the political geography of the Red Mountains is completely different. Rathakos and Acrokos are not just renamed; they have not been founded as sovereign states. During this period, the Holy Qis Empire rules from the capital city of Ath'r and claims the central continent and the Red Mountains. However, the notes specify that while the Empire claims that territory, they do not effectively administer it. It is occupied by various non-state peoples and mountain communities rather than a unified government.

So it is just an unorganized frontier? That sounds like a recipe for a messy transition. If the Holy Qis Empire was the big dog back then, how did it fall apart enough for two mountain nations to just pop up out of nowhere?

The transition is tied to the rise of Artkos and the later Ryraxian War. In the Qaringil era, Artkos is still just its historical heartland in the northwest. About 200 years later, by the time of the Qamis campaign, Artkos has conquered the central territories, including the capital Ath'r. The specific creation of Rathakos and Acrokos happened much later, during the 30-year war involving the dragon Ryraxos. When the Artklan Empire was dissolved by the dragon, those mountain provinces were briefly organized into the Red Mountain Republic under Edran occupation. It was only when Edrad withdrew that a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran elements and split the region into the two nations we see now: Rathakos and Acrokos.

Wait, you mentioned Rathakos is currently a Princedom under a Regent named John Dunne in the Qamis era. If it came from a Republic, why are they looking for a Prince? Seems like they are going backwards.

The shift from Republic to Princedom was a political reaction. The Red Mountain Republic was associated with the Edran occupation. When the Red Mountain Assembly took control, they purged the pro-Edran faction and sought to establish a new identity. The Settlements of Ath'r officially designated Rathakos as a Princedom. Currently, Regent John Dunne is governing while a Grand Council deliberates on who the actual monarch should be. It is a period of significant internal unrest because many mountain communities still follow old dragon cults or represent the annexed Southern Reaches that used to be Edran.
You keep mentioning these old dragon cults in the mountains. Were those active in the Qaringil era too, or is that a new problem for the modern campaign?

They were active, though the notes for the Qaringil era describe the Red Mountains as being occupied by 'non-state peoples' without naming the cults specifically. However, the Qis'mos notes clarify that while the dragons departed long ago, the institutions of the old Draconic empire persisted longest in the Qis heartland. The mountain communities in what becomes Rathakos have maintained these 'old dragon cults' for centuries, meaning they would have been present during the prequel era, likely as part of the reason the Holy Qis Empire failed to effectively govern the region.
If I am playing in the prequel, and I travel to the spot where Rathakos is supposed to be, what do I actually see? Is it just the Holy Qis Empire's tax collectors getting lost in the woods?

Exactly. You would find rugged, mountainous terrain claimed on paper by the Holy Qis Empire but ruled in practice by local clans and potentially those dragon-worshipping sects. There are no major capitals like Rath in the northern foothills yet. Instead, you would find the 'blank spaces' the DM mentions, which are intended to be filled in with player backstories or discovered through exploration. You are essentially entering a wilderness that the bureaucrats in Ath'r haven't managed to map or tax properly.

One last thing. The notes say Artklan is spoken in the Holy Qis Empire during the prequel. Is that the same Artklan they speak in Rathakos 200 years later, or am I going to have a 'prestige dialect' problem?

You will definitely have a dialect problem. The notes state that languages in Qamis are not standardized. Even in the present day, a speaker from the capital of Artkos would have trouble understanding the Artklan spoken in Rathakos. In the Qaringil era, the version of Artklan spoken in the central parts of the Holy Qis Empire is described as being 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' If you start in the prequel, you would know the prestige dialect of the capital and perhaps one regional variant, but the mountain dialects would be distinct and likely difficult for a city-dweller to parse.

This moon navigation stuff in the Rathakos campaign is way too much homework. Why do I have to track Primus moving across the sky while I'm trying to sail to Qaringil? It feels like I need a math degree just to play a pirate.

I can see why it looks intimidating, but there are two small mix-ups there that actually make it much simpler! First, Qaringil is a city-state from the prequel setting, about 200 years before the time of Rathakos. In the present-day Qamis setting where Rathakos exists, you're likely sailing the Central Sea or the coastal waters of Edrad instead. Second, the best part about Primus is that it doesn't move across the sky at all. It is geostationary, meaning it stays fixed in one exact spot over the equator. You don't have to track its motion; it's basically a second pole star that sits on the horizon instead of at the pole.

Fine, so it's a fixed dot. But on Earth, you need a high-tech chronometer to find longitude. You're telling me I just look at a moon and suddenly I know if I'm 30 degrees East? That sounds like the DM is just making up fake physics to be 'unique' and confusing.

It is definitely unique, but it's based on very specific geometry from the campaign notes. On Earth, we need clocks because our 'fixed' stars are only fixed relative to each other, not to the ground—the Earth rotates under them. But Primus is 'magically anchored' to rotate at the exact same speed as the planet Qaia. Because it is always hanging over the same point (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude), its elevation in the sky tells you exactly how far you are from that point. Navigators use something called the 'primsine' function. You measure the elevation of Primus with a sextant, look up that angle in a pre-printed table, divide by the cosine of your latitude (which you get from the pole star), and boom—you have your longitude. No clocks, no waiting for high noon. It's actually much easier than real-world historical navigation, provided you're on the half of the world that can see Primus.

See, you said 'divide by the cosine' and 'primsine function.' That is exactly what I mean by too complicated. My character is a fighter, not a wizard. If I'm lost at sea, am I really expected to do trigonometry at the table?

Not at all! Your character would just have a 'Nautical Almanac.' The campaign notes mention that for players who want to skip the math, there are two-variable tables that just say 'If Primus is at 42 degrees and the Pole Star is at 30 degrees, you are at 30 degrees East.' It's just a table lookup. The math is just there for the lore to explain why the prime meridian is where it is—the sub-Primus point is the natural zero-mark for the whole world because everyone can see it. It's a huge advantage for navigators in the Qamis era compared to the poor sailors on the 'far-side' of the planet who can't see Primus and have to guess their position the hard way.

I was reading through the Qamis campaign primer and saw a mention about dragons making excellent artillery officers because they can 'help carry the guns.' That is such a specific and grounded detail for a fantasy setting. Can you explain the history behind that?

In the Qamis setting, that detail is tied specifically to the rise of the dragon Ryraxos. During the thirty-year war that just ended, Ryraxos distinguished himself not just as a beast of war, but as a tactical commander. The campaign notes explain that dragons are prized as artillery officers because their immense strength allows them to physically transport and reposition heavy artillery pieces. While human or elven batteries might struggle with the logistics of moving massive guns through difficult terrain, a dragon can essentially act as the heavy lifting equipment for their own unit. This gave Ryraxos's forces a level of mobility and positioning speed that traditional armies couldn't match.
That makes a lot of sense. So is Ryraxos this ancient, god-tier dragon who took over through raw power?

Actually, no. The setting notes clarify that Ryraxos is not an ancient dragon; he is a bronze dragon and relatively young, perhaps sixty years old. While he is formidable, the notes say he is less impressive than a well-commanded artillery battery or a competent group of adventurers. His rise to power was driven by his military background and his ability to shapeshift—a trait shared by all dragons in this world—which allowed him to integrate into the military structure. He eventually became a 'dragon overlord' and waged war across the continent for twenty-five years, but his power was as much about his command of the guns as his own breath weapon.
What happened to him once the war ended? Is he still around in the 'present day' Qamis campaign?

The war ended with Ryraxos's defeat, and he accepted a very literal exile. He was sent to Secundus, which is the second moon of the planet Qaia. Secundus is a dark, iron-rich moon that orbits in a retrograde direction (rising in the west). Because of this exile, the campaign notes state he will play no role in the current campaign. With him gone, the Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in Edrad, and the borders have largely returned to their historical lines, though the continent is still reeling from his three decades of warfare.

DUDE I just started my first ever campaign and my DM said the big bad dragon Ryraxos is literally stuck on the moon?? Like, the second one? That is the coolest thing I have ever heard in a D&D game! How did they even get him up there and why that specific moon??

It is an incredible way to start a campaign! You are playing in the Qamis setting, right after the thirty-year Ryraxian War ended. Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who actually started out as an artillery commander before taking over as an overlord. After he was finally defeated, he accepted exile to Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. It is a very 'metal' choice because Secundus is actually made of dark iron! It is way less reflective than a normal moon, so even though it is almost as big as Earth's moon in the sky, it looks much darker and more ominous.
Wait he just accepted it?? Why not just stay and fight? And is he like, the only thing up there? Also how do you even say his name, is it R-eye-rax-os?

For the name, remember that in this world, Q is pronounced like 'Kh' from Old Draconic, though Ryraxos starts with an R so you are probably safe with Rih-rax-os! As for why he accepted it, the notes say he was formidable but not invincible—a 'well-commanded artillery battery' or a high-level party could take him. Accepting exile was likely part of the peace treaty that restored the Keyberos dynasty to the throne in Edrad. As for what else is on Secundus, the notes are currently a 'blank space' on that detail, though we know it is a retrograde moon, meaning it rises in the west and sets in the east, which is opposite to most of the other moons like Tertius and Quartus!
A moon that rises in the west... that is so trippy. So he is just chilling on a giant ball of iron watching the world he used to rule? Can we see him from the ground??

Exactly! He has a front-row seat to the continent he ruled for twenty-five years. Secundus is close enough (0.30 LD) that it has a mean angular diameter of 28 arcminutes—that is nearly the size of Earth's moon. Because it is so dark, it is about 1 magnitude dimmer than our moon, but you can definitely see the moon clearly. Seeing Ryraxos himself would be a stretch for the naked eye since he is just a dragon on a moon 471 kilometers in radius, but knowing he is up there definitely changes how people look at the night sky in Rathakos!

Hey, I'm a tech lead getting ready for a weekend D&D game in your Qamis setting. I need an executive summary on the second moon, Secundus. My DM mentioned it's a 'prison moon' or something? I'm workshopping a character from Rathakos and want to know how this moon fits into the vibe.

Secundus is one of the most significant bodies in Qaia's sky, both physically and politically in the current Qamis era. Physically, it is a dense, iron-rich moon with a very dark surface (albedo 0.06). It is slightly smaller than Earth's moon in the sky but appears about one magnitude dimmer because of that dark iron composition. Politically, the big 'executive' detail is that the bronze dragon Ryraxos was exiled there after the thirty-year Ryraxian War ended. In the Qamis present, he is effectively imprisoned on that moon, which has ended his reign over the continent.

Okay, exile-to-the-moon is a cool hook. Give me some local color though. If I'm a person on the ground in Rathakos looking up, what makes Secundus weird compared to the other moons? Is it just a dark rock?

The 'weird' factor is its motion. Secundus is a retrograde moon, meaning it rises in the West and sets in the East, which is opposite to the Sun and the prograde moons like Tertius and Quartus. It moves across the sky very quickly, completing a full orbit in just 4.53 days. Because it's so close (0.30 LD) and iron-dense, it has a massive impact on the tides—about 1.47 times the tidal force of Earth's moon. For someone in the mountains of Rathakos, it’s a dark, fast-moving disk that represents the literal 'overlord' now trapped in the heavens.

A 4.5 day period is fast. I'm thinking about making my PC a navigator or a former sailor who relocated to the Red Mountains. How do people use Secundus for navigation? Does it work like the pole star?

Actually, for navigation, Secundus is 'hard mode.' The 'easy mode' is Primus, the geostationary moon that hangs motionless over the 0-degree meridian, allowing for instant longitude fixes. However, Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If your sailor was on the 'far side' of Qaia where Primus is below the horizon, they would have to use Secundus for 'lunar distance' methods. Because Secundus moves so rapidly against the background stars, measuring its position is a way to determine time and position, but it requires complex tables and a steady hand with a sextant or cross-staff. In the Qaringil prequel era (200 years ago), this was the primary way far-side explorers avoided getting lost.
Interesting. So if I'm in Rathakos, I can see Primus and Secundus? Does Secundus ever eclipse things? I'm trying to get a sense of the 'sky-scape' for my character's nightly watch.

Rathakos is on the near side, so you see it all. You'd have the stationary Primus (about 1/3 the size of Earth's moon) fixed in the sky, while Secundus sweeps 'backwards' past it every few days. Because Secundus has an 8-degree inclination and a tight orbit, it frequently passes near or in front of other celestial bodies. Triple-moon alignments where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all line up are rare 'eras' in the calendar. For a Rathakos native, seeing the dark iron disk of Secundus cross the path of the brighter Quartus would be a striking sight—and a reminder of the dragon-king watching from above.

Hey, sorry I missed the last session. My character is from Rathakos and the DM mentioned something about the 'Red Mountain Republic' and a 'full-moon era' coming up. As a local, should my character know what those are? Can you summarize the context for me?

No problem. Since your character is from Rathakos, those two topics cover your recent national history and the literal sky above you. Here is the summary based on the campaign notes.

The Red Mountain Republic is the predecessor state to your current home. During the Ryraxian War, when the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire, the mountains were occupied by Edrad. The Edrans organized the mountain provinces into the Red Mountain Republic. However, it didn't last. A faction called the Red Mountain Assembly eventually purged the pro-Edran members, and when the Edran military withdrew, the Republic was split to form the two current nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. In Rathakos, there is still lingering unrest from those who preferred the Republic or the old dragon cults.

The 'full-moon era' refers to the behavior of Qaia's three inner moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Because of their specific orbital periods, they enter cycles that last about 12 years. During a 'full-moon era,' the three moons align to reach their full phase at almost the exact same time roughly five times a year. This makes for incredibly bright nights. We are currently in one of these eras; for instance, in 2253, there was a triple-full moon where all three were within 0.2 percent of syzygy. This alternates with 'new-moon eras'—like the one from 2255 to 2258—where triple-dark nights are common instead.
Wait, three moons being full at once? That sounds like it would be blinding. Does it mess with the tides or anything in the mountains?

It is significantly brighter than a single moon, though even at triple-full, the combined light is still slightly less than a full moon on Earth because Secundus and Tertius have darker, iron-rich surfaces compared to Quartus. However, the tidal impact is massive. 

Combined, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus exert a tidal force about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's moon. When they all align during a full-moon or new-moon era, those forces stack. While Rathakos is a mountainous interior state and doesn't deal with ocean tides directly, the 'Triple-moon alignment' is a major event for the continent. For a mountain dweller, these eras are more about the calendar and visibility. In a new-moon era, the nights are pitch black, which is dangerous in the Red Mountains given the 'adventuring and frontiersmanship' tradition there. In a full-moon era, like the one we are entering now, you have much better visibility for nighttime travel or spotting mountain predators.
Hey nanochat! I totally missed the start of the new Qamis game. Where exactly is the party starting out, and what's the vibe there?

No worries! The campaign starts in a place called Rathakos. It is a mountainous border state tucked into the Red Mountains. The capital city is actually just called Rath, and it sits right in the northern foothills. The vibe is very much 'frontier and adventure' because the Red Mountains are still pretty dangerous and haven't been fully explored yet.
Cool. Is Rathakos part of that big empire? I remember something about an Artklan League.

It is technically part of the Artklan League, but it is struggling to stay independent. History-wise, it's a bit of a mess because of the Ryraxian War. It used to be part of the Red Mountain Republic when Edrad was occupying the area. Once Edrad pulled out, a group called the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran folks and split the territory into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. So, there is still some internal unrest, especially in the areas they took from Edrad.
And who is in charge? I want to make sure I don't accidentally insult the king in my first session back.

Actually, you can't insult the king because they don't have one yet! Rathakos was made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but the Grand Council is still trying to find a monarch. In the meantime, the country is being run by Regent John Dunne. It is a bit of a power vacuum, which is probably why there is so much work for adventurers.
Got it. One last thing—how do I pronounce these names? I don't want to sound like a tourist.
