The most important rule is that the letter Q is always pronounced like 'Kh.' It comes from Old Draconic. So the setting is 'Khamis,' the neighboring country is 'Khismos,' and if you run into any local dragon cults, they might call themselves 'Khis' in the old tongue.

Hey. I've been playing in the Forgotten Realms and Greyhawk for about fifteen years, so I've seen plenty of 'special' moon calendars. This whole business with 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras' in Qamis sounds like a lot of extra bookkeeping for the DM without much payoff. Why not just stick to a normal lunar cycle?

That's a fair concern. Many settings add celestial bodies for flavor that never actually impacts the game. In Qamis, the 'eras' aren't just random events; they are a predictable result of the orbital resonance between the three main inner moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. While a single moon like Selune in the Realms has a simple phase, having three moons means they often fall out of sync. A 'Full-Moon Era' is a period of roughly two years where the orbits align such that all three moons reach their full phase at almost the exact same time—sometimes within 0.1% of a perfect syzygy. During these eras, 'Triple-Full' nights happen about five times a year, while 'Triple-New' nights (where all three are dark) effectively don't happen at all. Then the cycle shifts into a New-Moon Era where the opposite is true.

So it's a twelve-year cycle? That still feels like it would just result in the DM forgetting which 'era' it is halfway through the campaign. In Oerth, we just track Luna and Celene's phases on a grid. How is a player even supposed to keep track of this without a degree in astrophysics?

It actually simplifies the tracking for the table because it groups the moons into 'moods' for the campaign. Instead of tracking three independent cycles daily, the DM just knows that for the next few years of game time, the nights are either exceptionally bright or exceptionally dark. For example, the year 2253 is the peak of a Full-Moon Era. On February 13th of that year, all three moons are over 99.8% full. If you are playing a character whose power depends on moonlight—like a druid or someone tied to the Church of Bahamut—you know that 2253 and 2254 are your time to shine. Conversely, the years 2255 to 2258 are a New-Moon Era, where the triple-alignments only happen when the moons are all dark. It creates long-term narrative arcs rather than just day-to-day math.

I guess I see the narrative appeal. If I were playing a rogue or a drow, I'd probably be praying for a New-Moon Era to start. What's the current 'era' in the Qamis campaign? Since the Ryraxian War just ended, is the sky as messy as the politics?

Exactly. The Qamis campaign starts just after the war ends, which puts the current date right at the beginning of a Full-Moon Era. The triple-full alignment on February 13, 2253, is one of the closest of the decade. It's a time of high visibility and light, which might be ironic given the 'messy politics' you mentioned. The drow who stayed behind in enclaves like Qama might find the next two years particularly challenging with those bright nights. If the campaign lasts long enough to reach 2256, you'll hit the peak of the New-Moon Era, where the world stays dark for days at a time during triple-new alignments. It gives the world a rhythmic quality that standard 'one-moon' settings don't have.

Look, I've seen a hundred homebrew worlds. Usually they're just Forgotten Realms with the serial numbers filed off and some bloated science notes that don't actually matter for the table. I see all this talk about a Hill sphere for Qaia. Why should I care about orbital mechanics? Does any of this actually impact a session, or is it just fluff?

It is definitely more technical than your average setting, but the Hill sphere—which is 3.89 LD (Lunar Distances) for Qaia—is the reason the sky looks the way it does in Qamis. It dictates which moons can exist and where. Because the Hill sphere is roughly four times the distance of Earth's moon, Qaia can hold onto seven distinct satellites without them being stripped away by the sun's gravity. For a DM, this translates into a unique calendar and navigation system. For example, Septimus, the outermost moon, sits at 2.47 LD. It stays stable only because it is in a retrograde orbit, rising in the west rather than the east. If it were prograde at that distance, it would likely be unstable. This affects the long-term calendar, with Septimus providing a 106-day cycle used for long-term record-keeping.

Rising in the west? That sounds like a headache to track. And I see a note about Primus being 'magically anchored.' That sounds like a classic homebrew hand-wave to fix a physics error when the math didn't work out. If it's geostationary, why is it magically fixed instead of just being at the right altitude?

The 'magical anchoring' of Primus is a deliberate lore point rather than a patch for a calculation error. While Primus is at the correct geostationary altitude (about 0.11 LD), a moon that close and that small would normally be subject to tidal torque and orbital migration over geological time. The campaign notes specify it is anchored to prevent this migration, ensuring it stays exactly over the same spot on the equator forever. This has a massive impact on the game: it solves the 'longitude problem' that plagued Earth for centuries. Any navigator on the near-side hemisphere can determine their exact longitude just by measuring the elevation of Primus. You don't need a chronometer or high-level magic; you just need a table of 'primsine' values and a clear night. It gives the setting a very different feel for maritime adventures compared to standard fantasy tropes.
So if I'm playing a thief in Rathakos, do I actually have to care about this? Or is it just for the sailors?

Even for a thief in Rathakos, the moons matter because of the illumination and alignment cycles. The notes describe 'Triple-moon alignment eras.' About five times a year, the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) align. We have 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras' that alternate every few years. If you're planning a heist in the year 2262, you'd know that May 24th is a 'triple-new' moon night—absolute darkness, providing a 0.1 percent illumination compared to a full moon. Conversely, during a 'triple-full' alignment like in February 2253, the combined tidal force hits 4.4 times that of Earth's moon, meaning coastal cities deal with massive tide surges. It provides a predictable, mechanical way for the DM to shift the environment without just saying 'it's dark because I said so.'
hi! I am new to D&D, can you help me understand how Qaringil works? My DM said my character is from there but it sounds kind of complicated with all the canals and bridges.

Welcome to your first campaign! Qaringil is the main setting for the prequel era, which takes place about 200 years before the 'present day' Qamis campaign. It is a famous island city-state built on a volcanic island between two massive bridges that cross the Central Sea. The most important thing to know about its name is that the Q is pronounced like a Kh, so it sounds like Kharingil. It is described as an 'incomprehensible republic,' meaning it doesn't have a king or queen like some other nations. Instead, it is run by merchant elites and powerful guilds.
Wait, so what is a republic exactly in this world? Is it like a group of people voting? Who actually makes the rules?

In Qaringil, the republic is less about citizens voting and more about the power of the 'Great Guilds.' Unlike nations like Edrad where the nobility owns land, the elites here are commercial—they care about trade, money, and ships. There are seven Great Guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. These guilds run the city's economy and its laws. For example, the Gondolier guild doesn't just move people through the canals; they also maintain and control the two massive bridges that connect the continents.
My character sheet says something about the Five Families. Are they the same thing as the guilds or are they like the city's mafia?

They aren't quite the mafia, though they are very powerful! The Five Families—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—are the most prominent bloodlines in the city. They are mostly human but all have some elf ancestry. They exert their power by leading the Great Guilds. For instance, the Peixota family (their sigil is a dolphin) leads the Shipping Guild, while the Vitelli family (sigil of a moth) leads the Silk Guild. They basically compete to see who can influence the most guilds and control the city's wealth.
Who is the guy at the top right now? Is he like the president or something?

The title for the head of the city is the Exarch. The current Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro. He has held the position for 19 years, but he's from a minor family rather than one of the 'Big Five.' However, the notes say he is considered to be on death's doorstep right now. Because he's so old and frail, there's a lot of tension because the Five Families are likely preparing to fight over who gets to replace him.
What if I wanted to play a rogue? Do the guilds just hate criminals, or is there a guild for that too?

Actually, there are criminal organizations that call themselves guilds too! While they are underground and illegal, they style themselves after the official ones. There are specific groups for Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. They aren't part of the official government, but in a city of 100,000 people with so many trade goods moving through the canals, they have a lot of influence. Even some of the Great Families might have secret dealings with them.
Can I actually be a member of one of these guilds when we start the game?

Yes! Since your party is starting at 5th level, you are already established enough to be a full guild member. You could be in one of the Great Guilds like the Banking or Mercantile guild, or one of the smaller ones specifically for characters like you, such as the Adventurer, Mage, or Alchemist guilds. For example, the Tiddwar family—which is a prominent gnomish family—currently leads the Mercantile Guild. Your character's guild membership is a great way to explain how you know the other players or why you're involved in the city's politics.
hello
Hello! I can help you with information regarding the planet Qaia and the continent of Qamis. Are you asking about the present-day Qamis setting, or the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years earlier?

I'm playing a member of the Gondolier Guild in Qaringil, so the prequel era. I spend my days on the canals and the Great Western Bridge, but the merchants talk a lot about the Red Mountains. Who actually lives out there? Is it part of the Holy Qis Empire?

As a Qaringil local, you're part of a major city-state of 100,000 people, but the Red Mountains are much more mysterious. In your era, the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from Ath'r) claims the mountains as part of their territory. However, they don't actually manage them. The notes describe the mountains as being occupied by 'non-state peoples.' This means they are outside the Imperial tax system and law. Because the mountains are underexplored and dangerous, the Empire mostly leaves them alone.
That sounds like a lot of empty space. Are there different races up there, or is it just humans who don't like the Emperor? I know the Edran nobility are all half-elves, for instance.

In Qamis, 'race' works a bit differently than other settings. There are no racial languages like Elvish or Dwarvish; everyone speaks national languages like Qis or Artklan (remembering that Q is pronounced 'Kh'). While the Edran nobility are specifically half-elven, the Red Mountains are home to a variety of folk who simply aren't part of the major nations. In your time, they are loosely categorized as 'non-state.' However, lore from the later Qamis era suggests that some of these mountain communities follow old dragon cults. These groups likely trace their traditions back to the time when dragons ruled the continent before departing for the big island to the south.
Dragon cults? That's a bit more intense than just skipping out on taxes. Do we know any specific names of tribes or cities in the mountains, or is it all just 'blank spaces' for now?

In the Qaringil era, it is mostly a 'blank space' on the map. The nations of Rathakos and Acrokos, which define the mountain region in the future, 'fully do not exist' yet. The Holy Qis Empire treats the region as a frontier. If you were to travel there, you'd find independent mountain communities, but the DM has not yet named specific tribes or settlements for this period. It is one of the areas where you could potentially help fill in the map with your own character's backstory or rumors you've heard at the Gondolier Guild.

Hey! I am so bummed I missed the last session, but the group chat is blowing up about the sky. Someone said there are seven moons? Seven?! I need the list and the details because that sounds incredible. Is there really one that just sits there and never moves?

It is definitely one of the most unique parts of the setting! Qaia has a very busy orbital neighborhood. In order from the planet outward, the moons are: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Quintus, Sextus, and Septimus. And you heard right—Primus is the geostationary one. It is magically anchored right over the equator at the prime meridian. If you are in the right hemisphere, it never rises or sets; it is just a fixed point in the sky. Navigators use it to find their longitude, which is a huge deal for sailors in Qamis.
That is so cool. A moon that acts like a North Star but for the equator! What about the rest of them? Do they all look the same or are they different colors and sizes?

They are actually quite varied! Primus and Secundus are very dense, dark iron-rich moons. Secundus is especially interesting because it is retrograde—it rises in the west instead of the east. Tertius is the one that determines the calendar week; it has an 8-day orbital period. Quartus is the closest match to Earth's moon and handles the monthly cycle. Then there is Quintus, which is actually just a tiny trace particle sitting at a stable point in space ahead of the planet. Finally, you have Sextus and Septimus, which are smaller, distant moons that also move backward (retrograde) across the sky. When they all align, it is a massive event!
Wait, you said some go east and some go west? That must look wild at night. How big do they get? Are we talking giant orbs or just bright stars?

It looks spectacular. Tertius and Quartus are the biggest in the sky, appearing about the same size as the moon we know. Secundus is almost that large too, though it is a bit dimmer because of its dark surface. Primus is smaller—about a third of the size of the others—but since it never moves, you always know where it is. Sextus and Septimus look more like very bright, small discs or tiny coins. The tidal forces are also intense because of all that mass; the total tidal pull on Qaia is about 4.4 times what Earth experiences!

I've been looking into the religious landscape of Qamis, and I keep seeing mentions of 'old dragon cults' in the mountains of Rathakos. Honestly, it sounds like a ghost story or a political excuse. Dragons were basically high-end artillery officers in the Ryraxian War, not divine beings. Is there actually any organized worship, or is this just local propaganda from Regent Dunne's administration to explain away unrest?

The skepticism is understandable given how Ryraxos was treated during the war—more like a formidable general than a god. However, according to the records for the current Qamis era, these cults are a genuine sociological reality, though they aren't a single unified 'church.' In Rathakos specifically, the mountain communities in the Red Mountains still follow 'old dragon cults' that predate the Ryraxian War. These groups often view dragons through the lens of the ancient draconic empires rather than the modern military role they played. It isn't just propaganda; it's a deeply rooted cultural holdover that the new Regency is struggling to manage alongside the internal unrest from the Southern Reaches.

If people want to worship dragons, why wouldn't they just look to the Church of Bahamut? I'm already tracking the schism between Qama and Qis—with the drow appointing Qe'lth IV in the holy city while Qachu II claims the title from Qis. That seems like plenty of 'dragon religion' for one continent. Why bother with fringe mountain cults?

The distinction lies in the object of worship and the historical lineage. The Church of Bahamut, centered in Qis'mos, is a formal institution focused on the deity Bahamut. The 'old dragon cults' in the Red Mountains and the sects in Forthyr are often more focused on the restoration of the Dragon Emperors—actual physical rulers from the planet's history. For instance, in Forthyr, there is a prominent religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. This group receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty, even though the dynasty's own dragonborn bloodlines have run thin. While the Bahamut schism is a conflict over institutional legitimacy and relics, the cults are often looking back to a time when dragons were the sovereign political and spiritual heads of the world.

So it's more about restorationism than theology. That makes more sense, especially if Forthyr's 'Dragoncaller' kings are using it to prop up their own authority. But what about Ryraxos? He's a living bronze dragon currently exiled to Secundus. Do these cults see his exile as a martyrdom, or do they ignore him because he didn't live up to the 'Emperor' legend?

The notes don't explicitly state how the Rathakos cults view Ryraxos's exile, but they do clarify that Ryraxos accepted the exile to the second moon after his defeat. It's worth noting that Ryraxos was considered relatively young—around sixty—and wasn't seen as a god-king by the established powers. The cults in the mountains often cling to the 'old' ways, which might find Ryraxos's role as a former artillery commander for Edrad a bit too 'modern' or compromised. However, with him physically present on Secundus—which is visible in the sky—he remains a potent symbol of the very power these sects want to see return to the continent. Whether they view him as a precursor or a disappointment is currently one of those 'blank spaces' in the regional lore.

so i am really digging into the theology of the Bahamut schism in Qis'mos (the whole Qama vs Qis thing is a mess). it made me realize i don't actually know what kind of dragon Ryraxos is. is he like a red dragon or something? does he have a stake in the church?

Ryraxos is actually a bronze dragon, not a red one. He is also relatively young for a dragon overlord—only about sixty years old. He did not really start as a religious figure; he began his rise to power as an artillery commander because he could help carry the heavy guns. As for the Church of Bahamut, Ryraxos does not have a direct stake in the current schism. He accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, after the war ended. The religious tension is more focused on the drow occupation of the holy city of Qama (pronounced Khama) and their appointment of Qe'lth IV as high priest, which forced the rival candidate Qachu II to operate out of the city of Qis without the official relics.

Bronze, okay. That is interesting given the metallic vs chromatic tropes. And yeah, the Qama situation with the drow is wild since they did not even despoil the city. But what about those old dragon cults in the Red Mountains near Rathakos? Do they care that he is a bronze dragon, or do they worship the idea of a dragon regardless of the type?

The notes on the Red Mountains mention that many mountain communities in Rathakos still follow 'old dragon cults,' but they do not specify if those cults specifically venerate Ryraxos or if his being a bronze dragon affects their worship. In Qamis, the metallic/chromatic distinction might not carry the same moral weight it does in other settings—Ryraxos was a formidable conqueror regardless of his scales. The religious drama in Qis'mos is definitely more about political and institutional control. While the Qis (Khis) language is the closest living link to Old Draconic, the Church of Bahamut is the dominant faith, and they are currently split between the drow-backed high priest in Qama and the exile in Qis.

I am trying to finalize some trade logs for the Gondolier Guild here in Qaringil and I keep seeing names like Qama and Qeth, and even Rathakos on some of the charts. Why does everyone skip the 'u' after the 'Q'? Is it some kind of guild shorthand or am I just misreading the charts?

The 'q without a u' is not a typo or shorthand; it is a feature of Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. This was the language of the ancient draconic empire, and it remains the standard for scholarly publications, maps, and formal treaties. In this tongue, the letter 'Q' is pronounced like 'Kh'. Because so many cities and regions were named during that era, the spelling stuck. However, I should point out a detail that might be confusing your records: if you are a member of the Gondolier Guild in Qaringil, you are living in a time roughly 200 years before the nation of Rathakos is founded. In the current era of the Qaringil prequel, those mountains are technically claimed by the Holy Qis Empire, though they aren't effectively administered. Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist yet; they only emerge as independent nations after the Ryraxian War in the distant future.

That explains why the 'Prince of Rathakos' wasn't responding to my guild queries! I must have grabbed a map from the wrong shelf in the archives. So in Qaringil right now, what is the 'official' language I should be using for these logs if I want to look like a proper professional?

For a professional in Qaringil, the primary language is Islas. However, because Qaringil was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, your local version of Islas has a much higher concentration of Qis vocabulary than the dialects spoken on other islands. If you are writing formal reports for the Great Guilds, using the prestige dialect of Islas is your best bet. If you really want to impress the judicial or mercantile elites, you might pepper in some Old Qis terms, as that remains the language of diplomacy and the academy across all of Qamis, even 200 years before the modern era.

Hey, I was digging through my old Qaringil prep and realized I only have the Peixota dolphin written down for the Five Families. What are the sigils for the other houses in the city-state? I want to make sure my notes match the current campaign setup for the prequel.

In the Qaringil prequel era, the Five Families each have a distinct sigil associated with their house and the guilds they typically lead. Here is the list: Peixota uses the Dolphin (Shipping Guild), Manos uses the Hand (Judicial Guild), Qanto uses the Dove (formerly Mercantile Guild), Volmer uses the Crow (Banking Guild), and Vitelli uses the Moth (Silk Guild). As a quick reminder for your notes, the 'Q' in Qanto follows the Old Draconic pronunciation and is spoken as 'Kh'. There is also the gnomish Tiddwar family to keep in mind; while not one of the 'Five', they are very prominent, use the Gears as their sigil, and currently lead the Mercantile Guild.

Hey! I missed the last lore session we had. I was looking through the documents and saw the planet is called Qaia. Is that basically just Earth with a Q? Also, I am thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil era—can you give me a quick recap on what the main differences are compared to the standard Qamis setting?

No problem! To answer your first question: the notes don't explicitly say if the name is a pun, but linguistically, it's quite different. In this setting, the letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh' (derived from Old Draconic). So, Qaia is pronounced like 'Khaia.' Physically, it shares some traits with Earth—Quartus is a near-perfect clone of our Moon—but the rest of the sky is unique. There are six major moons total, including a magically anchored geostationary moon called Primus that stays fixed over one hemisphere, making longitude calculation trivial for navigators. Regarding your Qaringil one-shot, that era is set roughly 200 years before the Qamis present day. The biggest difference is the political map. In the Qaringil prequel, the nations of Rathakos and Acrokos don't exist yet; they only form after the Ryraxian War. Instead, central Qamis is ruled by the Holy Qis Empire from the city of Ath'r. The Qaringil era focuses on the island city-state of Qaringil itself, which is a republic ruled by an Exarch and dominated by five powerful merchant families: Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli. It's a world of canals, nautical robber barons, and intense guild politics rather than the post-war reconstruction and dragon-exile themes of the Qamis era.

Wait, so if my players want to be from Rathakos for the one-shot, I have to tell them no? That's going to throw them for a loop. And tell me more about these merchant families in Qaringil. Are they all human?

Correct, you'd have to steer them elsewhere. In the Qaringil prequel era, the area that eventually becomes Rathakos is just part of the Red Mountains—a region claimed by the Holy Qis Empire but mostly occupied by non-state peoples and not effectively administered. As for the Five Families of Qaringil, they are all primarily human, though they all have some elven ancestry. They each lead a major guild and have a specific sigil. For example, the Peixota family uses a Dolphin and leads the Shipping Guild, while the Vitelli family uses a Moth and leads the Silk Guild. There is also a prominent gnomish family called Tiddwar (sigil: Gears) that currently leads the Mercantile Guild. It's a very urban, cosmopolitan setting compared to the rugged frontiersmanship you see in the present-day Qamis setting.

Hey there. I am a DM looking for some unique celestial mechanics for a homebrew world and I ran across your notes on Primus. It is a geostationary moon used for longitude? That is a brilliant mechanical hook. But I noticed a note saying it is useless for 'far-side' observers. Is that just because it is below the horizon, or is there more to the physics of it?

In the world of Qaia (pronounced 'Khaya'), the limitation is primarily geometric. Because Primus is geostationary, its orbital period exactly matches the planet's rotation. It is magically anchored at an altitude of approximately 35,800 km directly above the equator at 0 degrees longitude. To an observer on the ground, it never rises or sets; it is a motionless point in the sky. However, because it stays fixed over one spot, the curvature of the planet eventually blocks it from view. Specifically, if you are more than about 81 degrees away from the prime meridian, Primus is permanently below your horizon. For anyone in the opposite hemisphere—the 'far side'—Primus simply does not exist in their sky.

That makes sense. It is like a North Star, but on the equator. But what about the other moons? I see you have several others, like Secundus and Quartus. Why can't far-side navigators just use those to figure out where they are?

The difficulty lies in the difference between a fixed point and a moving one. Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all 'standard' moons that sweep across the sky at different rates. To find longitude using a moving moon, a navigator needs to know the exact time at a reference location (like the capital city) to compare it to their local time. This requires a highly precise chronometer, which doesn't exist in Qamis. Without a clock, you have to use the 'lunar distance' method—measuring the angle between a moon and specific stars using massive, complex tables in an almanac. It is incredibly difficult math. Primus is special because its position is constant; you don't need to know what time it is to use it. You just measure its elevation, look up the 'primsine' value in a table, and you have your longitude.

That creates a massive technological and economic gap between the two sides of the world. Does this play into the campaign's history? Are the civilizations on the far side lagging behind in seafaring because they are stuck with dead reckoning?

It definitely shapes the world's power dynamics. Most of the major nations we track—Edrad, Artkos, and Qis'mos—are on the 'near side' and have mastered the Central Sea because they can fix their position instantly. The far side includes the continent of Westron and the mysterious big island to the south. The DM notes label much of Westron as a 'blank space,' but we know they lack the 'Primus Advantage.' This makes long-distance trade and deep-ocean exploration significantly more dangerous for them. While near-side sailors can traverse the open ocean with confidence, far-side mariners are often tethered to coastlines or forced to rely on much riskier stellar transit methods. It effectively makes the Primus-facing hemisphere the global hub for cartography and naval empire-building.
yo, thinking of doing a one-shot set in Qaringil. trying to get the vibe right for the 'big players'. who actually runs Edrad in that era? is it the same folks from the present day setting?

It is! The Keyberos dynasty is the main constant through both timelines. In the Qaringil prequel (roughly 200 years before the main Qamis campaign), the Keyberos family has already been reigning for two centuries. They rule from the capital city of Jia, which is on a peninsula jutting into the Central Sea. One thing to keep in mind is that the Edran nobility are half-elven—specifically, law and tradition require them to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf to maintain their status.
interesting. so they're already established. do they have the same borders? i saw the notes mention 'blank spaces' and map differences for the prequel.

The borders are definitely different. In the Qaringil era, Edrad doesn't yet control Qeth (pronounced Kheth) or the territory to the northeast of it; conquering those regions is actually a primary goal of their foreign policy at that time. They are also much more stable than in the 'present' day. The Revolutionary Council and the dragon Ryraxos haven't happened yet—Ryraxos isn't even born in the Qaringil era. While the modern Qamis setting is about restoring the dynasty after a thirty-year war, in Qaringil, they are simply the dominant, expanding superpower of the west.
Got it. So no dragon overlord to worry about. Who is the King in the prequel then?

The campaign notes don't actually name the specific individual King of Edrad for the Qaringil era. It just notes that the Keyberos dynasty has been in power for two hundred years and is gradually expanding royal control. This is one of those 'blank spaces' where you have some room to invent a specific monarch for your one-shot, as long as they fit the half-elven Keyberos lineage!

Hey, sorry I missed the last session. I was deep in the weeds trying to calculate my longitude by hand from a Primus elevation of 45 degrees. I think I have the primsine(e) part down as 0.837, but I got distracted by the math. Can you catch me up on the current state of things? Also, the party was talking about the dragon Ryraxos before I left. Someone said he was actually pretty young for a dragon overlord. How old is he exactly?

Your calculation is spot on. For an elevation of 45 degrees, the primsine is indeed 0.837. Using that with your latitude and the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude, right under Primus) should get you where you need to go.

Regarding the recap: we are in the Qamis era. The thirty-year Ryraxian War has officially ended, and the dragon Ryraxos has been defeated and exiled to Secundus, our second moon. The Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne in Edrad, and the borders are supposedly being restored to their historical lines. Our campaign is starting in Rathakos, which is a mountainous border state struggling with its new independence.

As for Ryraxos's age, you heard correctly. Despite his reputation as a dragon overlord, he is relatively young for a dragon—only about sixty years old. He's a bronze dragon who originally rose to power as an artillery commander. While he was formidable enough to wage war for decades, he wasn't an ancient wyrm; a competent group of adventurers or a well-placed artillery battery could realistically stand against him.

Sixty? That is surprisingly young. Most of the Edran half-elf nobility must be older than he is! It is wild that he managed to hold the continent for so long. Since he is on Secundus now, does that mean he is just sitting on a giant ball of iron? I saw in the moon notes that Secundus is nearly iron-density and has that dark surface.

That is essentially correct. Secundus has a density of 6,689 kg/m3, which is near-iron and about twice the density of our own moon. It has a very dark, iron-rich surface with an albedo of only 0.06, making it quite dim even though it is fairly large in the sky. It is a retrograde moon, so Ryraxos would see it rising in the west and setting in the east. Given that he can shape-shift, he is likely surviving the harsh conditions, but it is a desolate exile compared to ruling from Edrad.

Hey! A friend of mine is starting a D&D game in this world and I'm trying to wrap my head around it. I read a lot of fantasy, so I'm used to kingdoms and empires, but they mentioned a place called Lock that has 'free cities.' Can you explain how that works? Is it like a separate country?

Lock is an interesting case because its status changes depending on which 'era' of the world your friend's game is set in. In the present-day setting, called Qamis, Lock is actually a kingdom. However, the city of Lock itself and a few other major urban centers are designated as 'free cities.' This usually means they have a high degree of legal and economic autonomy from the crown—they might collect their own taxes or have their own local councils while still being part of the larger kingdom. The notes also mention that Lock has a burgeoning industrial presence nowadays, which sets it apart from some of the more traditional feudal areas.
Industrial? That sounds a bit more advanced than a typical medieval world. You mentioned it was different in another era—what was it like before?

If your friend is playing the prequel era, which is called Qaringil and set about 200 years earlier, the political situation is much more tense. Back then, Lock and several other river cities were 'free cities' locked in a 'cold conflict' with the Holy Qis Empire. The Empire ruled from a central city called Ath'r and claimed authority over them, but the cities were constantly fighting to protect their 'ancient prerogatives' or traditional rights against the Emperor's control. Also, a quick tip for a newcomer: in this setting, the letter Q is pronounced like 'Kh,' so Qis is pronounced 'Khis.'
Khis, got it. That's a cool linguistic touch. So in the present day, are those free cities still fighting with an Emperor, or is that all over?

In the present day, the Holy Qis Empire doesn't really exist in that form anymore. Instead, the dominant power in that central region is the Artklan League. The city of Ath'r is still a major capital, but the geopolitical map shifted significantly after a thirty-year conflict called the Ryraxian War. Lock moved from being a cluster of rebellious river cities to its current state as a kingdom with industrial interests. The DM has left some 'blank spaces' regarding the specific details of their industry, so if you end up playing a character from there, you might even be able to help decide what they're actually manufacturing or building!

Hey, I'm a DM for a Forgotten Realms campaign and I'm looking for some inspiration for a seafaring arc. I heard your setting has some unique moon mechanics. Is it like Selune and the Tears of Selune? How do your players handle navigation without modern tech?

It's quite different from the Forgotten Realms. While Toril has Selune and her trail of asteroids, Qaia has a complex system of six distinct moons: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. The biggest difference for navigation comes from Primus (pronounced 'Kh-rimus' as Q is 'Kh' in this setting).

Primus is a geostationary moon. It was magically anchored to stay fixed over a single point on the equator (longitude 0, latitude 0). This means for anyone in that hemisphere, Primus never rises or sets; it stays at a fixed elevation in the sky. This solves the longitude problem that plagued Earth's history for centuries.

Wait, so it's like a permanent North Star but sitting over the equator? That sounds like it would make it impossible to get lost at sea. In my FR games, finding longitude is basically impossible without a specialized wizard. How does the math actually work for a sailor on your world?

It's exactly like that, but more geometric. Sailors use the 'Elevation Method.' Since Primus is fixed at 0,0, a navigator just needs two measurements: the elevation of the pole star for latitude, and the elevation of Primus for longitude. 

Because Primus is relatively close (0.11 LD), navigators use a formula involving a 'primsine' function. By measuring the angle of Primus above the horizon, they look up a value in a table and divide it by the cosine of their latitude. This gives them their longitude immediately. No chronometer or clock is required, which on Earth wasn't solved until the 18th century. However, there's a catch: Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you sail to the 'far side' of Qaia, you're back to the difficult Earth-style methods like dead reckoning or tracking lunar distances with the other moons.

That's a cool asymmetrical advantage for one half of the world. Does having six moons mess with the tides? In D&D, people usually just hand-wave tides, but with that many moons, it must be a nightmare.

The tides are significant. The combined oscillating tidal force of the three main inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) is about 4.4 times that of Earth's Moon. It's a very dynamic environment for coastal cities like Jia in Edrad or the canals of Qaringil. Interestingly, Primus doesn't contribute to the daily tidal cycle because it doesn't move across the sky; instead, it creates a permanent, static 2-centimeter bulge in the ocean along the prime meridian.

Also, keep in mind that Secundus is a retrograde moon—it rises in the west—while Tertius and Quartus are prograde and rise in the east. Navigators have to track multiple cycles, which is why the '8-day week' on Qaia is based on the 8.15-day orbital period of Tertius.

Six moons, retrograde orbits, and geostationary anchors... that's a lot for a player to track. Is there like a 'Grand Alignment' moment I could steal for a plot hook? Something like the stars being right in Lovecraft?

The campaign notes actually track a 'Triple-moon alignment.' Roughly once a year, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all hit syzygy (becoming full or new) simultaneously. For example, in the year 2262 of the Qamis era, there is a 'Triple-new' moon on May 24th where all three are within 0.1% of alignment. In the setting's lore, these eras alternate between 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras' every 12 years. It's the perfect timing for powerful rituals or, as seen in the history of Edrad, the movement of a 'dragon overlord' like Ryraxos.

hey! I'm starting to look into the religious landscape for my character. I'm really curious about the Bahamut schism in Qis'mos, but I'm also looking at Edrad as a possible home. What's the deal with the half-elves there? Are they basically the only ones in charge, and how do they feel about the whole drow situation in Qama?

Welcome! In the present-day Qamis setting, Edrad is definitely a unique place. The nobility there is strictly half-elven. Specifically, to be part of the noble class, an individual must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. This has been the standard for the Keyberos dynasty's four-century reign, though they're currently picking up the pieces after the thirty-year Ryraxian War. As for the drow in Qama, it's a complicated situation for the Edran elites. Qama is the holy city of Bahamut, and the drow—who stayed on the surface in several enclaves after the war—now control it. They even appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This caused a major schism with the alternate candidate, Qachu II, who is based in Qis but lacks the holy relics. The notes don't explicitly say which side the Edran half-elves take, but given that Edrad is recovering from decades of war and the Keyberos dynasty was just restored to the throne in Jia, they're likely navigating the religious divide with extreme political caution.

That is a very narrow range for nobility! 25 to 75 percent? It sounds like they are obsessed with heritage. If I'm a religious scholar, would I find that the Church of Bahamut is just as big in Edrad as it is in Qis'mos? I want to know if my character would be caught in the middle of that Qachu vs Qe'lth fight while living in Jia.

It's definitely a rigid system! That heritage requirement is what maintains the Keyberos dynasty's grip on power. Regarding the Church, the notes state that the Church of Bahamut has an 'enormous presence' in Qis'mos specifically, while Edrad is described as the most 'politically and economically dominant' and 'culturally influential.' While Bahamut worship likely exists in Edrad, the heart of the theological conflict is centered in Qis'mos and the holy city of Qama. If you're in the capital city of Jia, you'd be in the cultural hub of Qamis, but the actual schism—where the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV and the rival Qachu II are at odds—is primarily a Qis'mos affair. However, because Qis is the language of diplomacy and the academy, and Edran is the language of the prestige class, a scholar in Jia would definitely be reading all the heated treaties and publications coming out of that conflict. Just remember that 'Qama' and 'Qis' are pronounced with a 'Kh' sound, as they come from Old Draconic!

Khama and Khis, got it. It's wild that the drow are just... hanging out in a holy city now. Did the half-elves in Edrad fight alongside the drow during the Ryraxian War, or were they on opposite sides? That might change how a scholar in Jia views Qe'lth IV.

That's a great question for a scholar to ask! The drow were actually recruited as allies by Artkos (the Artklan Empire) late in the war as a 'desperation move.' Edrad, meanwhile, spent those thirty years in a state of chaos—first under a Revolutionary Council that deposed the Keyberos, and then under the 'dragon overlord' Ryraxos, who actually delivered Edrad from defeat before waging his own war. The war ended with the Keyberos dynasty restored and Ryraxos exiled to the moon Secundus. Since the drow were allies of Artkos, and Edrad was at various times the target of Artklan aggression, there's likely no love lost between the half-elven nobility and the drow enclaves. A scholar in Jia would probably view the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV with significant suspicion, seeing the occupation of Qama as a lingering scar of the war.

Missed the session last night. The group is rambling about some place called 'Westron.' I've been playing since the late 70s and usually these homebrew 'exotic' continents are just half-baked fluff. What's the deal with this place? Do I need to worry about it if we're sitting in Rathakos?

To give you the most accurate recap, are you playing in the current Qamis campaign (set after the Ryraxian War) or the Qaringil prequel set 200 years earlier? The details on Westron vary slightly between them, though in both cases, the DM has explicitly marked it as a 'blank space' that hasn't been fully detailed yet.

We're in the present day, post-war. We just started in Rathakos. If it's a 'blank space,' why is the party talking about it like it's the next big thing? Is there a language I should have picked up? I've got Edran and Artklan, but if they speak some 'Westronese' I'm going to be annoyed.

In the present-day Qamis setting, Westron is described simply as a very large continent to the west that is considered 'exotic.' Regarding language, you don't need to worry about a 'Westronese' racial tongue because this setting doesn't use racial languages like Elvish or Dwarvish. The main languages are national: Edran, Artklan, Qis (pronounced 'Khis'), Forthrian, and Islas. If your character had a reason to know a language from the west, you'd likely just take one of those or a regional dialect.

Since the DM notes it as a blank space, there isn't a pre-written history or a list of cities yet. The DM actually mentions that Westron 'maybe should have a better name,' suggesting it hasn't been a focus of the continental politics in Qamis recently. If your group is talking about it, they might be speculating or using it for a character backstory, which the DM encourages for those empty spots on the map.
Fair enough. What about the gods? My cleric is a Bahamut guy. Does the Church of Bahamut have any reach over there, or is it all 'exotic' paganism?

The notes for the Qaringil era specifically mention that Westron is distinct because the people there are 'mostly not being Bahumet worshipers.' While the present-day notes for Qamis don't elaborate further, it's safe to assume the religious landscape remains very different from the heartland of Qis'mos, where the Church of Bahamut is most powerful. In Qamis, the Holy City of Qama is the traditional seat of Bahamut, and that's quite a distance from the western continent. If you're heading that way, your Cleric might find themselves in the minority.

heyyoo, I am starting to put together a character for the Qaringil prequel game. I was thinking of someone from the Gondolier guild. I saw a mention of a place called Forthyr in the notes. Is that anywhere near the city canals, or is it like a totally different world?

In the era of Qaringil, Forthyr is quite far from the city. While Qaringil is a merchant republic located on the bridges and islands of the central sea isthmus, Forthyr is an enormous nation far to the northeast of the continent. It is definitely not a neighbor you would see every day on the canals. It is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty—specifically Dragoncaller Shanlong I at this time. He is a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king. Compared to the cosmopolitan, commercial vibe of Qaringil, Forthyr is often seen as somewhat backwards by the island elites.

A half-dragonborn king? That sounds wild. Since I am in the Gondolier guild, maybe my family handles some of the transport for the heavy wool or silk trade coming from that way. Would people from Forthyr actually travel to Qaringil? I am trying to figure out if my character would have met anyone from there or if they are just legends.

It is possible. Qaringil has a population of about 100,000 and is a major hub because of the Great Western Bridge. As a member of the Gondolier guild, you do more than just row boats; your guild also covers the maintenance and control of the bridges. While Forthyr is distant, their merchants might travel to the central sea to trade. However, they are a bit insular. There is even a religious sect there that openly calls for the return of the ancient Dragon Emperors. If you met a traveler from Forthyr, they might seem a bit superstitious or old-fashioned compared to the banking and shipping families like the Volmers or the Peixotas who run things in your city.

That is a cool contrast. My guy probably thinks they are a bit intense with the dragon stuff. Speaking of names, I noticed a lot of Q names like Qaringil and Qis. How do I actually say those? Is it like a normal Q sound?

In this setting, the Q is pronounced like a Kh sound, derived from Old Draconic (or Old Qis). So the city you live in is pronounced Kharingil. If you were talking about the Holy Qis Empire to the west, you would say the Holy Khis Empire. This linguistic quirk is very common in scholarly works and treaties. Since your character is a local guild member, you would be very used to that Kh sound in your daily life on the bridges.
Got it, Kharingil. I like it. If Forthyr is backwards, do they have guilds like we do? Or is it all just under the Dragoncaller?

The notes do not mention a complex guild system in Forthyr like the one in Qaringil. In your city, the Seven Great Guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool) are the backbone of the republic. Forthyr is much more traditional, governed by the Dragoncaller dynasty. While Qaringil is run by merchant elites and the Five Families (like the Vitelli or the Qanto), Forthyr is a land of sorcerer-kings and religious sects. The DM has left many details about the interior of Forthyr as blank spaces, so if you wanted your character's family to have a specific contact or a weird story about a Forthrian trade deal gone wrong, there is definitely room for that.

I am finally sitting down with my astrolabe and a draft of the Navigation Guide to work through a longitude fix by hand! It is incredible how Primus just stays there at 0,0. Okay, so if I am in the Red Mountains at 35 degrees North, and I measure the elevation of Primus at exactly 25 degrees, I need the primsine. Let's see... rho is 0.1511. I am getting a primsine of about 0.5427. If I divide that by the cosine of my latitude, what is my longitude? And wait, I was reading a history of the Artklan League—is the current High King Ezevir Arth the same one who conquered all that territory in the northwest, or is that a different guy?

That is some impressive field math! Let's verify that longitude fix first. If your primsine(25 degrees) is 0.5427 and your latitude is 35 degrees North (cos 35 is about 0.8192), then the cosine of your longitude (cos lambda) is 0.5427 / 0.8192, which equals roughly 0.6625. Taking the arccos of that gives you a longitude of approximately 48.5 degrees. Depending on whether Primus is east or west of your meridian, you are either 48.5 degrees East or West of the prime meridian. It is a brilliant system, isn't it? As for High King Ezevir Arth, you are actually looking at two different men depending on which era of the campaign notes you are reading. The Ezevir Arth currently ruling Artkos in the Qamis present day is the one trying to restore the Artklan League's dominance after the Ryraxian War. However, if you are looking at the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years ago, that was King Ezevir Arth II. He was the one who famously doubled the size of Artkos's holdings in the northwest. His daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I, was the one looking to expand even further back then. The current High King shares the name and the ambition, but he is a descendant ruling centuries later!

48.5 degrees! That puts me right in the heart of the mountains. I love that we can do this without a chronometer just because the moon is magically anchored. So, the Ezevir Arth ruling now in the 'present' day of Qamis—is he as successful as his ancestor Ezevir II? The notes say he governs Artkos directly, but is the Artklan League actually listening to him, or are they just 'nominal subjects' like it says in the intro?

You've hit on the exact political tension of the modern era! While Ezevir Arth II was a conqueror who effectively expanded the physical borders of the empire 200 years ago, the current High King Ezevir Arth is finding it much harder to keep the pieces together. The notes describe the modern Artklan League as a 'pale shadow' of the old empire. Even though the restoration of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad was supposed to put everything back the way it was, the League members are clinging to a 'tenuous quasi-independence.' For example, in the city of Kard—which is one of Artkos's southern enclaves—the governance is extremely fragile because so many people there actually speak Edran and welcomed the invasion three decades ago. So, while the current Ezevir Arth has the title and the central territory of Ath'r, his 'subjects' are a lot more rebellious than those of his ancestor, Ezevir II. He is ruling in the wake of a thirty-year war that changed everything, even if the treaties say it didn't!

Hi. I'm looking at the Qaringil prequel notes and I'm a bit skeptical about the linguistic setup. The 'Q' pronounced as 'Kh' because of Old Draconic feels like a bit of a reach—is there actually a reason for that, or is it just for flavor? Also, the guild list for the city seems redundant. Why is there a Mercantile Guild if the Silk and Wool guilds are already handling trade?

The 'Q' to 'Kh' shift is a consistent rule derived from Old Draconic, also called Old Qis, which is the language of scholarly treaties and ancient empires. In Qaringil, names like the city itself or the 'Qis' region are pronounced 'Kharingil' and 'Khis.' Regarding the guilds, the distinction is specific: the Silk and Wool guilds are Great Guilds that control both the production and the trade of those specific textiles. The Mercantile Guild, which is currently led by a gnomish family called Tiddwar, regulates the trade of all other commodities that don't have their own dedicated Great Guild.

If the Silk and Wool guilds are that big, wouldn't they just run the whole city? It seems like a Mercantile Guild wouldn't have any room to breathe if the major families are already tied up in textiles and shipping. And if 'Qis' is 'Khis,' does that mean the Holy Qis Empire is just the 'Holy Khis Empire'? That sounds like a lot for people to keep straight if the languages aren't standardized.

It is indeed the Holy 'Khis' Empire. The linguistic diversity is a major part of the setting; for example, a speaker from the capital of Artkos might struggle to understand the Artklan dialect spoken in Rathakos. As for the guilds, Qaringil's republic is built on a delicate balance between the Five Families and the Great Guilds. While the Vitelli family leads the Silk Guild and the Peixota family leads Shipping, the Mercantile Guild acts as a regulator for other essential goods like grain or metals. The conflict you're sensing is real: for instance, the Manos family is currently trying to contest the Wool scene, which creates political friction that the Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, has to manage.

Hey, I'm starting a character for a Qamis campaign. I'm from Rathakos, and my DM mentioned some 'wizard-made' bridges crossing the central sea. Honestly, that sounds like a bunch of bardic exaggeration to hand-wave how trade moves. Who actually built them, and what's the deal with them supposedly being linked to the Red Mountain Republic?

It's understandable to be skeptical, but in this setting, the 'wizard-made' description is literal. There is a narrow isthmus that crosses the northern end of the Central Sea, which was indeed constructed via magic. It is supported by two enormous bridges that connect the landmasses to a volcanic island in the center. In the Qaringil era (the prequel set 200 years ago), the city of Qaringil sat on that island and its Gondolier Guild was responsible for maintaining the bridges. As for the Red Mountain Republic, it's actually a separate piece of history. That was a short-lived state formed from the mountain provinces—including what is now Rathakos—after the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire. It was essentially an administrative unit during the Edran occupation before it purged its pro-Edran members and split into the modern nations of Rathakos and Acrokos.

If you can magically raise an isthmus, why bother with bridges? It still feels like a tall tale. And if my people in Rathakos were part of this 'Republic,' did they ever actually control that crossing? It seems like whoever holds those bridges holds the continent's wallet.

The bridges were necessary to span the gaps between the mainlands and the volcanic island without completely blocking the sea's currents. While the isthmus itself is magical, the bridges allow the merchant fleets of the Central Sea to pass through. Your character from Rathakos would know that their people never controlled those northern bridges. Rathakos is located in the Red Mountains, which are far to the south and west of the isthmus. The Red Mountain Republic was a regional union of those mountain border states, not a continental empire. The bridges are dominated by the island thassolocracies—nautical robber barons and merchant guilds—not the mountain dwellers of Rathakos.

So the bridges are just an island-hopping route for the rich? That explains why a Rathakosi frontiersman would think they're a myth or just a money pit for the Islas merchants. Who's in charge of them now that the Ryraxian War is over?

Your character's view of them as a 'money pit' for foreign merchants is very accurate to the setting's geopolitics. In the present day of Qamis, the notes don't specify one single ruler for the bridges; instead, the islands of the central sea are populated by exiles and pirate-princes who sell their mercenary navies to the highest bidder. In the Qaringil prequel era, the city of Qaringil and its 'Five Families' (like the Peixota and Vitelli) held the most influence there, but after the thirty-year Ryraxian War, the power dynamic has shifted toward these mercenary thassolocracies. While the Keyberos dynasty has been restored in Edrad and Artkos is trying to regain its old glory, the bridges remain under the control of those who command the sea.

Look, why is this moon system so messy? Seven moons? In the Forgotten Realms, you have Selune and the Tears of Selune and it's simple. Now I am reading about this Primus thing that doesn't even move? That sounds like a GPS satellite, not a fantasy world. It is way too complicated.

I understand the frustration; seven moons is a lot more to track than the single moon of Faerun. In the world of Qaia (pronounced Khaya), Primus is indeed essentially a geostationary satellite, but it is a natural (or magically anchored) body. It stays fixed over the same point on the equator because its orbital period perfectly matches the planet's rotation. While it might seem like science fiction, in the setting of Qamis (pronounced Khamis), it serves a very practical fantasy purpose: it solves the longitude problem for sailors. Because it never moves, you can determine exactly how far east or west you are just by measuring its height in the sky.

If it is a satellite, how do players even use it? Do I need a degree in astrophysics to find my way to Rathakos? In Eberron, you just follow the lightning rail. This 'primsine' stuff I am seeing in the notes looks like homework.

You definitely do not need a degree in physics to play. While the campaign notes include the math for the 'primsine' function (which helps calculate geocentric angular distance), characters in the world would just use precomputed tables found in a nautical almanac. To find your way to a place like Rathakos in the current Qamis era, you would simply check the elevation of the pole star for your latitude and the elevation of Primus for your longitude. If the DM asks for a check, it is usually just a Navigation or Survival roll, not a math test. The math is just there for the 'ground truth' of the world's physics.

Wait, so if I am on the other side of the planet, I am just lost? Like, if I sail too far away from the prime meridian, the moon just disappears? This feels like a way to punish players who go off the map.

It is true that Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you are on the far side of Qaia, you have to rely on more difficult methods like stellar transits or measuring the positions of the other moons like Secundus or Quartus. It creates a 'near-side' advantage for navigation. However, most of the current campaign setting, including the nations of Edrad and the Artklan League, is located on the side where Primus is visible. There are many 'blank spaces' on the far side of the map that the DM has not filled in yet, so going there would be a true journey into the unknown, much like exploring the deep jungles south of Freeport.
hey, I'm trying to wrap my head around the lore for this Qamis game. I keep seeing the name High King Ezevir Arth. Is he the big ruler of everything?

Welcome! To understand Ezevir Arth, it helps to know which era you are looking at. In the current Qamis setting, which is the present day, High King Ezevir Arth rules the nation of Artkos. He isn't the ruler of everything, but he certainly wants to be. He governs Artkos directly from the capital, Ath'r, and is trying to restore the dominance his kingdom once held over the rest of the Artklan League. Think of him as an ambitious monarch trying to reclaim lost glory after the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended.
Wait, my friend mentioned something about him doubling the size of the kingdom. But he also said something about a Queen? I am confused if it is the same guy.

That is a great catch. You are likely hearing about two different people from different time periods. The person who doubled the size of Artkos is actually King Ezevir Arth II. He lived about 200 years before the current setting, in the Qaringil era. After he expanded the kingdom, his daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I, took over. The current ruler in the present day is just called High King Ezevir Arth. He is a descendant carrying on the family name and the ambition to make Artkos a superpower again. Also, a quick tip for a new reader: in this setting, the letter Q is pronounced like Kh, so Qis is Khis.
Got it. So the current Ezevir is living in the shadow of his ancestors. What is the deal with this Artklan League then? If he is the High King, does he own all of it or what?

That is exactly the source of the tension. Long ago, the Artklan Empire ruled about forty percent of the continent. But after the Ryraxian War, the empire effectively dissolved. Today, it has been replaced by the Artklan League. While Ezevir Arth is the High King of Artkos, which is the historical heart of the empire, the other member states of the League only treat their subjection as a technicality. They cling to a tenuous independence, and the notes describe the League as a pale shadow of the old empire. Ezevir wants to put things back the way they were, but he is finding that signing an edict is much easier than actually enforcing it across the continent.

I've been looking over the celestial mechanics for this setting. It's way more detailed than the usual 'Selune and the Tears' setup I'm used to in Toril. What's the deal with Quintus? It's listed in the moon reference but called a 'trace particle'—is it even a moon or just some space dust?

It's definitely closer to space dust than a moon like Quartus or even the tiny Primus. In the notes for the planet Qaia (where the Q is pronounced like 'Kh'), Quintus is described as a 'trace particle' located at the Sun-Qaia L4 point. That means it sits about 60 degrees ahead of the planet in its orbit around the sun. It's effectively massless and included primarily for worldbuilding flavor rather than being a major celestial body you'd see as a disc in the sky.

L4 point? That's getting into real orbital mechanics. So it's essentially a Trojan. How big is it compared to something like Primus or the retrograde moons like Secundus? Does it show up to the naked eye at all?

The notes don't give it a radius or mass because it's 'effectively massless,' which suggests it's likely just a cluster of small debris or a single asteroid-sized rock. Compared to Primus, which is 64 km in radius and looks like a fixed 10.4 arcminute disc in the sky, or Secundus, which is a massive 471 km iron-rich moon, Quintus wouldn't be much to look at. It librates—meaning it wobbles back and forth—between 45 and 80 degrees from Qaia over a 2,000-year period. Unless you're a high-level wizard with a very powerful telescope, it's basically invisible to the average person on the surface.

If it's at L4 and has that long libration cycle, does it have any magical significance? Like, Primus is 'magically anchored' to be geostationary, which is wild for a D&D setting. Is Quintus tied into any of the Dragoncaller myths or the Church of Bahamut, or is it just a 'blank space' in the sky that might be a hideout for the dragons who left?

The notes don't specify any direct magical anchoring for Quintus like they do for Primus. While Primus is a major tool for navigation because it's geostationary over the prime meridian, Quintus is mostly a footnote. There are 'blank spaces' in the lore—like the big island to the south of Qamis where people say the dragons went—but the notes haven't tied Quintus to the Dragoncaller dynasty of Forthyr or the Church of Bahamut in Qama yet. It's listed as being 'included for worldbuilding purposes,' so it's a detail the DM might use later, but for now, it's just a stable little speck of dust 60 degrees ahead of the planet's orbit.
hey! im joining the qaringil prequel game and making a character based in the city. i noticed the language list is all nations, no elvish or dwarvish? is that right?

That is exactly right. In the world of Qaia, there are no racial languages at all. Whether you are a human, elf, gnome, or anything else, your native language is determined by where you were raised rather than your biology. In the city of Qaringil, the primary language is Islas. Because the city was originally built by workers and adventurers from Qismos, the version of Islas spoken there has a lot of Qis vocabulary mixed in.
oh cool. so if i play an elf from one of the five families, i just speak islas? that feels weird for a fantasy game but i kind of dig it. what if i want a second language that feels scholarly?

If you are playing one of the Five Families (like the Peixota or the Vitelli), you would definitely speak the prestige dialect of Islas. Since those families have a lot of elf ancestry and often deal with the mainland, you might also know Edran—the language of the half-elven nobility in Edrad. For something truly scholarly, you would want Old Draconic (also called Old Qis). It is the language used for treaties, academic papers, and diplomacy across the continent. Just a heads up: the letter Q in this setting is always pronounced like a Kh sound, which comes from that Old Draconic roots. So Qaringil is Kharingil and Qis is Khis.
kharingil. got it. i was thinking about being part of the tiddwar family, they are gnomes right? would they have their own language or is it still just islands and merchant talk?

The Tiddwars are indeed a prominent gnomish family in Qaringil—they actually lead the Mercantile Guild. Even as gnomes, they do not have a separate 'gnomish' language. They would speak Islas like everyone else in the city, though they might have a regional dialect or specialized guild jargon. If your character traveled for the family business, you could take a bonus language like Artklan (spoken in the Holy Qis Empire to the north) or Forthrian, but it would be because of your backstory and training, not because you are a gnome.

Hey, I'm comparing my old Qaringil notes with the new Qamis setup. Forthyr looks weirdly identical across 200 years. Is the Dragoncaller dynasty still the same group of people, or did I miss a major regime change?

It is the same dynasty, but there has been a significant change in their physical lineage. In the Qaringil prequel era, Forthyr is ruled by the half-dragonborn sorcerer-king Dragoncaller Shanlong I. By the present-day Qamis era, the ruler is Shanlong VI. The notes specify that while the dynasty used to be dragonborn, the bloodlines 'ran thin' over those two centuries. They still maintain a reputation for being somewhat 'backwards' and continue to give tacit support to the religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, but the literal dragon-blood connection is much weaker now.

So they've lost their dragonborn traits mostly? That's a bit of a downgrade. Also, did the Ryraxian War affect them much? I see the present-day notes mention they find the southwest 'impossible to ignore' now.

The notes don't provide a specific visual description for Shanlong VI, only that the bloodline has thinned out since the time of Shanlong I. Regarding the war, Forthyr stayed out of the main conflict for a long time, effectively neglecting the affairs of the southwest for decades. However, the end of the thirty-year Ryraxian War and the resulting shifts in power—like the restoration of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad and the rise of the Artklan League—have made their old policy of isolationism unsustainable. While they are still described as enormous and somewhat isolated in the northeast, they are now forced to pay attention to the rest of Qamis in a way they didn't have to 200 years ago.

Alright, let's see if this Qamis setting has any meat on its bones or if it's just window dressing. I'm looking at the notes for a place called Ath'r. Where exactly is it on the map, and who's the big boss there? Is it another generic fantasy kingdom or something with actual history?

Ath'r is the traditional seat of power in central Qamis, but its political status depends on which era you are looking at. In the present-day Qamis campaign (following the Ryraxian War), Ath'r is the capital of Artkos. It is governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth. Interestingly, it sits as a central exclave of his territory, separate from the historical Artkos heartland in the northwest. If you are looking at the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years earlier, Ath'r is the capital of the Holy Qis Empire. One quick pronunciation note: 'Q' in this setting is pronounced as 'Kh', so it would be pronounced 'Khath-er'.

An exclave? That's a bold choice for a homebrew map. Usually makes for messy borders and a lot of headaches for the players. And you mentioned a Holy Qis Empire in the prequel. Was it actually a theocracy, or was that just a fancy title the DM slapped on it to sound important? Also, clarify where it sits geographically—is it stuck in those Red Mountains I keep reading about?

The notes for the Qaringil era describe the Holy Qis Empire with a bit of irony, stating it was 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' While it claimed authority over central Qamis, Qirkos, and northern Qis'mos, it was more of a political structure where the Emperor was theoretically elected. Geographically, Ath'r is not in the mountains; it is located in the central plains. The Red Mountains are a separate, more dangerous border region to the south and west where states like Rathakos and Acrokos are located. Ath'r has remained the capital for various regimes for centuries because it is the strategic heart of the continent, which explains why High King Ezevir Arth keeps it as his capital today despite the exclave status.
Hi! I'm a brand new D&D player and I'm looking at these notes. Is Qamis the name of the whole world, or is it that Qaringil place? I'm trying to figure out where my character would actually be.

Welcome to D&D! Qamis and Qaringil are actually two different campaign time periods set in the same world. The planet itself is called Qaia, and the main continent is Qamis. The Qamis campaign is the present day setting, taking place right after a thirty-year conflict called the Ryraxian War. The Qaringil campaign is a prequel set about 200 years before those events. The political map looks very different in the prequel because nations like Rathakos did not exist yet, and the Holy Qis Empire ruled from the city of Ath'r. Also, a quick tip for your first session: in this world, the letter Q is always pronounced like Kh, so Qis sounds like Khis. If you are starting the Qamis campaign, you are in the post-war era.
That helps a lot! So if I'm starting in the current Qamis game, what happened to that dragon Ryraxos? Someone mentioned he's on a moon now? That sounds wild.

It is one of the most significant events in recent history. Ryraxos was a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander and eventually ruled as an overlord for decades. When he was finally defeated at the end of the thirty-year war, he accepted exile to Secundus, which is the second of Qaia's six moons. With him gone, the Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in the nation of Edrad. However, your campaign is likely starting in a place called Rathakos. It is a mountainous border state in the Red Mountains that was formed recently. It is currently being governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council tries to find a permanent monarch to rule as Prince.

I have been obsessed with the navigation logs for Qaia. Finding longitude using a geostationary moon like Primus is brilliant, though I bet those 4.4x lunar tides are a nightmare for coastal traders. I was reading about the holy city of Qama in Qis'mos. Is it a coastal city, and how do those massive tidal cycles affect its importance?

Qama is the traditional seat of the Church of Bahumat and is located in the heartland of Qis'mos. While the notes do not explicitly state if it is directly on the coast, the Qis'mos region is described as having some of the most productive land in Qamis, which historically makes it a major prize for continental powers. Regarding the tides, Qama's importance is more religious and political than tidal. However, with the oscillating tidal force of Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus combined, any major city in that region would have to contend with complex daily cycles. Qama is currently a point of major contention because it was occupied by drow during the Ryraxian War, and they have appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, causing a religious schism.

If Qama is the 'holy city,' does it have a special view of the moons? I noticed Primus (the 'Kh' sound is tricky!) is only visible from one hemisphere. Is Qama on the 'near-side' where Primus stays fixed in the sky, or are they navigating by the more difficult lunar-distance methods using Secundus or Quartus?

Qama is indeed on the near-side hemisphere. Since Qis'mos is in the central part of the continent and the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude) is defined by the sub-Primus point, Qama likely has a very clear view of the geostationary moon. For the faithful in Qama, Primus would be a permanent, motionless fixture in the sky, roughly one-third the size of Earth's moon but much dimmer. This visibility makes navigation for Qis'mos merchants much easier compared to those on the far side of the planet. It also adds a layer of celestial permanence to a city that has seen a lot of recent upheaval, including the current drow occupation.

That celestial permanence is a great contrast to the 'triple-moon alignment' eras. I saw there is a 'triple-new' alignment coming up in May 2262. Given the schism between the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV in Qama and Qachu II in Qis, would an alignment where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all go dark simultaneously be seen as a religious crisis? Does the Church of Bahumat have specific lore about these eras?

The notes do not detail specific prophecies or 'omens' associated with the triple-new alignments, but the schism itself is quite grounded in politics. Qachu II is currently operating from the city of Qis without any of the actual relics of office, which remain in Qama under drow control. A triple-new moon event on May 24, 2262, would certainly be a dark night for the continent. Since the Church of Bahamut is so influential in Qis'mos, such a rare astronomical event—where the combined 4.4x tidal force is pulling in alignment with the sun while the moons are invisible—would likely be a period of high tension for both the drow high priest and his rival.

Speaking of those relics in Qama, are they tied to the 'dragon magic' that keeps Primus anchored? The notes mention Primus is 'magically anchored' so it does not migrate or have tidal torque. If Qama is the heart of the old draconic institutions, I wonder if the drow occupied it specifically to control whatever is keeping the moon system stable.

That is an interesting theory, but the campaign notes are currently silent on the exact nature of the relics or whether they relate to the moon's anchoring. What we do know is that Qis'mos is where the institutions of the old Draconic empire persisted the longest after the dragons departed. The drow were originally recruited as allies by Artkos during the war and simply remained in several enclaves, with Qama being the most prominent. Whether they chose Qama for its celestial importance or simply its religious prestige is one of the 'blank spaces' in the current history.

Fair enough. Let's look at the physics then. Secundus is retrograde and spiraling inward. If I were a navigator in Qama, would I see Secundus rising in the West and moving toward Primus? And since Secundus has 1.47x Luna's tidal force, does its retrograde motion create 'counter-tides' that clash with the prograde pull of Tertius?

Precisely. An observer in Qama would see Primus hanging motionless while Secundus rises in the west and sweeps across the sky to the east. Meanwhile, Tertius and Quartus would be moving west-to-east. This results in a very chaotic tidal environment. The daily tidal rhythm is driven by four different drivers—the solar tide and the three inner moons—all with different periods (9.85h, 13.69h, 12.43h, and 12.03h). These 'counter-tides' would indeed clash, leading to complex interference patterns where high tides might be exceptionally high or surprisingly low depending on the relative positions of those three moons.

One last thing—the 'Qaringil' prequel mentioned that Qaringil was originally built by laborers from Qis'mos. 200 years ago, was Qama already the seat of Bahumat? I'm curious if the drow occupation in the 'present' (Qamis era) is a return to an older conflict, or if the Holy Qis Empire of the past had a more stable relationship with the holy city.

In the Qaringil era (200 years earlier), the Holy Qis Empire ruled from Ath'r and actually claimed authority over Qis'mos north of the river. Qama was already the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut back then. However, the drow occupation is a modern development of the Ryraxian War and was not a factor in the prequel era. Back then, the 'Holy Qis Empire' was described as being neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire—it was a struggling elective monarchy. The city of Qama would have been part of the broader Qis'mos cultural region, which was a fragmented collection of territories rather than the unified nation seen in the present Qamis era.

I've been reading through your setting notes and I'm curious about the dragon cults. Is this like the Cult of the Dragon in the Forgotten Realms where they're trying to make dracoliches, or more like the Wyrmspeakers from Rise of Tiamat?

In Qamis, the dragon cults are quite different from the Forgotten Realms. You won't find mentions of dracoliches or Tiamat in these notes. Instead, dragon cults are presented as a lingering cultural and religious force from a time when dragons actually ruled. In the present-day Qamis campaign, they are most prominent in the mountain communities of Rathakos. These groups still follow 'old dragon cults' that likely date back to the height of the Draconic empire. There's also a religious sect in the nation of Forthyr that calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. It's worth noting that 'Q' in this setting, like in Qis or Qamis, is always pronounced as 'Kh,' so these would be the 'Khish' or 'Khamisi' cults.
