Wait, so no Tiamat? In most D&D settings, dragons are basically gods. Here, it sounds like Ryraxos was just an artillery officer who got lucky? That's a huge step down from someone like Klauth or the ancient red dragons in Greyhawk.

The notes specifically say Ryraxos is 'not truly that impressive' as dragons go—he's a bronze dragon, roughly sixty years old, which is relatively young. His rise to power was more about his tactical skill as an artillery commander than divine might. However, dragons aren't totally diminished. While Ryraxos is now exiled to Secundus (the second moon), the Church of Bahumat remains a massive power in Qis'mos. They have a holy city called Qama, which is currently in a state of religious schism. The drow occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, while a rival candidate, Qachu II, operates out of the city of Qis. So while individual dragons like Ryraxos might be more 'mortal' than in the Realms, the institutions built around them are still very influential.

That Forthyr sect sounds interesting. Are the Dragoncaller kings like the sorcerer-kings of Dark Sun, or more like the Targaryens where it's just about the bloodline? The notes mentioned the bloodline is 'running thin.'

In the present day, Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, a sorcerer-king. The dynasty used to be dragonborn, but as you noted, the bloodline has thinned over generations. They seem to be closer to a traditional sorcerous monarchy than the immortal tyrants of Athas. The religious sect there that wants the 'Dragon Emperors' to return gets tacit encouragement from the Shanlong dynasty, which suggests the rulers want to maintain that draconic mystique even if their actual power is more political. If you look at the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years earlier, the ruler was Shanlong I, and they were described as half-dragonborn then, showing how the bloodline has faded by the time of the Qamis campaign.

And what's the deal with the 'blank spaces' on the map? In the Realms, every inch of the Sword Coast is mapped. Is there a cult presence on that big island to the south near Freeport? Some people say that's where the dragons went, right?

The notes do mention rumors that the 'big island to the south' is where the dragons went after they departed, but that region is mostly unexplored and sits in one of the DM's 'blank spaces.' Freeport is the only known settlement there, a city of exiles and pirates right on the shore, and they're too afraid of the jungle to look deeper. The DM explicitly uses these blank spaces as opportunities for players to help build the world through their backstories, so whether there's a secret dragon cult or an actual draconic civilization in that jungle is currently unwritten lore.

Hiiii! This is my first time playing D&D and I am so excited for the Qaringil game. I am making a fighter from Rathakos and I saw something in the notes about a capital dialect? I want to speak the fancy version of Artklan so I can talk to the High King! How do I do that?

Welcome to your first campaign! It is great to have you. I do need to clarify one thing first: you might be looking at the notes for the Qamis era. In the Qaringil prequel (which is set 200 years earlier), the nation of Rathakos does not actually exist yet. At that time, that region is part of the Holy Qis Empire. If you want to be from the central continent in that era, you would likely be a subject of the Emperor in Ath'r rather than the High King in Artkos. Does that change where you want your fighter to be from?

Ohhh okay, my bad! I got the two timelines mixed up. Let's say I'm from Ath'r then since that's the big empire. What exactly does the capital dialect thing mean for my character sheet? Do I just write Artklan?

No worries, the history is a bit dense! For your character sheet, the rule is that languages aren't fully standardized. Instead of just writing Artklan, the DM's rule for starting characters is that you get to speak the prestige dialect of the capital city and one regional dialect. In your case, you would know the version of Artklan spoken by the nobility in Ath'r (the capital dialect) and then you could pick a regional version, like the way people talk in the mountains or the river cities like Lock. It means you can sound fancy and educated when you need to, but you also understand how common people speak in a specific province. Also, remember that in this world, Q is pronounced like Kh, so the Holy Qis Empire sounds like the Holy Khis Empire!
That is so cool. So if I meet a peasant from a different part of the Empire, I might have a hard time understanding them even if we both speak Artklan?

Exactly! The notes mention that a speaker from the capital of Artkos (in the northwest) would have a lot of trouble understanding someone from a place like Rathakos. Since you have the capital dialect, you'll be able to navigate high society and official business easily, but your regional dialect choice will determine which commoners you can communicate with best. You also get one bonus language—just make sure to tell the DM how your fighter learned it!

Hey! I'm digging into the Qamis setting notes. Forthyr caught my eye—the 'Dragoncaller' title gives me some old-school Mulhorand or Thay vibes from Forgotten Realms. What's the deal with them? Are they a major power or more of a reclusive thing?

Forthyr is definitely a major presence, at least geographically—it's an enormous nation in the northeast of the continent. While it might share that 'ancient/powerful' vibe with places like Mulhorand, the notes describe it as being 'somewhat backwards' compared to the more modern or industrial regions like Edrad or Lock. It's ruled by a sorcerer-king called the Dragoncaller. In the present-day Qamis era, the ruler is Shanlong VI. They're a bit isolated and have a prominent religious sect that wants the old Dragon Emperors to return, which the ruling dynasty quietly encourages.

Shanlong VI? That implies a long history. I saw there's a prequel setting called Qaringil set about 200 years earlier. Does Forthyr change much between the two time periods, or is it one of those 'eternal' empires that stays the same for centuries?

It's remarkably stable, though there is a significant physical change in the ruling family. In the Qaringil prequel era (200 years ago), the ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong I, and the dynasty is explicitly half-dragonborn. By the time you get to the Qamis present day, the notes mention that the bloodlines have 'run thin,' and the family is no longer dragonborn. Despite that loss of draconic essence, the political structure is almost identical: the Dragoncaller still rules, the country is still considered 'backwards,' and the same religious focus on the Dragon Emperors persists. It's a rare case where the map doesn't shift much for them, but the people at the top have physically changed.

That's a cool detail—the 'thinning' of the blood. Is there anything about how they interacted with Ryraxos during the thirty-year war? Did the 'Dragoncaller' actually call the dragon, or did they stay out of it?

Surprisingly, the notes don't link the Dragoncallers to Ryraxos directly. Ryraxos was a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander in Edrad before becoming an overlord. While Forthyr's religious sects dream of the 'Dragon Emperors' returning, Ryraxos's war was mostly a continental struggle involving Edrad, Artkos, and Qis'mos. The campaign notes mention that Forthyr spent decades neglecting the affairs of the southwest (where most of the war happened) and now finds them impossible to ignore in the aftermath. So, they were more like a looming giant in the northeast that stayed on the sidelines while the rest of Qamis burned.

Yo, I am rolling up a PC for the Rathakos start in the Qamis era. I am looking for some background tie-ins and I am curious about Ryraxos. How old is this guy exactly? Like, in 5e terms, are we talking an 'Ancient' tier CR 22 threat, or was he just a heavy hitter? I am trying to see if my PC's father could have been his lead gunner or something during the war.

Ryraxos is actually relatively young for a dragon—the records place him at about sixty years old. In mechanical terms, he is not an 'Ancient' dragon. The campaign notes describe him as formidable, certainly, but less powerful than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. His rise to power was due to his military mind rather than raw age. Since the Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and just ended, your PC's father could definitely have served under him. Ryraxos started as an artillery officer because, as a bronze dragon, he could shape-shift and help carry the heavy guns to advantageous positions.

Sixty? That is actually manageable. I love the 'artillery commander' angle—very different from the usual 'hoarding gold in a cave' trope. My PC is going to be from Lock, so I am leaning into that industrial/artillery flavor. What is the local vibe on his exile to Secundus? Is it a magical 'phantom zone' thing, or is he literally just chilling on the moon? Can we see him up there?

It is a literal, physical exile. Secundus is the second moon, a dark, iron-rich world that moves in a retrograde orbit (rising in the west). It is nearly the same size as Earth's moon in the sky, though much dimmer because its dark surface only reflects about 6% of the sun's light. There is no mention of a magical 'phantom zone'—he accepted physical exile to that moon upon his defeat. In Rathakos, where the campaign starts, the locals would see Secundus sweeping across the sky from west to east. Whether you could spot a single dragon with a telescope is not specified, but he is physically up there on that dark iron rock.

That is metal. One last thing for the backstory—since my guy is from Lock, which has a big industrial presence, would people there still be salty about the war, or are they mostly focused on the new 'Artklan League' status? Just trying to figure out how a veteran's kid from a 'free city' would be treated in Rathakos.

Lock is in a bit of a unique spot. While the country is a kingdom, the city of Lock itself is a 'free city' with a burgeoning industrial presence. In the current Qamis era, the Artklan League is seen as a 'pale shadow' of the old Empire, and many places are clinging to a tenuous independence. Rathakos is a brand new nation formed from the Red Mountain Assembly after they purged pro-Edran factions. As a veteran's kid from Lock, you might find some common ground with the folks in Rathakos who follow the old dragon cults, but you would also encounter plenty of 'internal unrest.' Rathakos is currently governed by a Regent while they look for a Prince, so the political atmosphere is very unsettled.

Good evening! I'm looking for some worldbuilding inspiration for a homebrew campaign I'm running. I was reading through the Qaia moon notes and noticed that Primus and Secundus have the exact same density of 6,689 kg per cubic meter. Is that just a coincidence or is there a lore reason?

That is definitely not a coincidence. In the world of Qamis (and the Qaringil prequel era), both Primus and Secundus are significantly denser than the outer moons. They are near-iron in composition, roughly twice the density of Earth's Moon. Just as a quick note for your table, the letter Q in names like Qamis or Qaringil is always pronounced with a 'Kh' sound, coming from Old Draconic.
Thanks for the pronunciation tip! So, why are they specifically near-iron? The outer moons like Quartus and Sextus seem to be standard rocky bodies. Why the split?

The campaign notes suggest a shared origin story for the inner system. Primus and Secundus are likely fragments of a single 'differentiated progenitor' body. In planetary science, a differentiated body is one where the heavy materials like iron sank to the center to form a core while lighter rocks stayed on the surface. At some point in Qaia's history, this body likely shattered, and these two moons are the surviving chunks of that iron-rich core.
Does the third moon, Tertius, fit into that too? I saw its density is 5,351 kg per cubic meter, which is higher than the outer ones but lower than the first two.

Exactly. Tertius is the 'iron-enriched' middle ground. If Primus and Secundus are the core fragments, Tertius is likely a mix of core and mantle material from that same progenitor. It makes the system feel like a debris field from a single massive ancient event. While Primus is now 'magically anchored' to stay geostationary over one spot on Qaia, and Secundus has been used as a prison for the dragon Ryraxos in the modern Qamis era, their physical makeup points back to that shared metallic ancestor.

While I am sitting here calculating the primsine for a 45-degree elevation to get a longitude fix for my next session, I got distracted by the lore for the northeast. What is the deal with that religious sect in Forthyr that wants the Dragon Emperors to come back? Does the Dragoncaller dynasty actually support them?

In both the Qaringil prequel and the present-day Qamis era, Forthyr is described as a somewhat backwards nation ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty. The religious sect you are asking about is a prominent group that actively calls for the return of the ancient Dragon Emperors. While they are not an official arm of the government, the Dragoncaller kings—such as Shanlong VI in the present day or Shanlong I in the prequel era—give the sect tacit encouragement, if not outright support. It is worth noting that while the dynasty bears the Dragoncaller title, their draconic bloodlines have notably thinned over the centuries, which may explain why they allow a movement centered on the return of the old emperors to persist.

Hey! I'm looking at your moon system for some inspiration for my own homebrew world. I noticed you use 'Luna' as a reference point in the stats. Is the actual Earth Moon part of the lore, or is it just for the DM's benefit?

In the Qaia moon system notes, Luna refers to Earth's Moon and serves as the mathematical baseline for all the ratios provided (mass, radius, albedo, etc.). While Luna itself isn't a physical body in the setting, the moon Quartus (pronounced Kh-uartus) is a literal clone of it. Quartus has the same mass, radius, and brightness as our Moon. The other five moons are then described by how they deviate from that 'Earth-standard.' For example, Primus is only 0.0001 times the mass of Luna and has a much darker, iron-rich surface.
That makes sense. So if I'm a player on Qaia, I'd look up and see Quartus and think 'that looks like home.' Does it follow the same 29-day cycle? And what's 'LD' stand for in those tables?

Exactly. Quartus has a period of 29.50 days, making it the natural calendar body for the world. LD stands for 'Lunar Distance,' which is the average distance from Earth to our Moon (about 384,400 km). Using LD as a unit helps visualize where the moons sit: Secundus is quite close at 0.30 LD, while Septimus is way out at 2.47 LD. Even though Quartus looks familiar, the sky is much more chaotic than Earth's. You have the stationary Primus hanging in one spot, retrograde moons like Secundus rising in the west, and the combined tidal force of the inner moons is about 4.4 times stronger than what we experience on Earth.

Yo, I'm checking out the Qaringil prequel setting. I'm looking at the Great Guilds for a potential background. The Gondolier Guild caught my eye, but is it just rowing people around in Kh-aringil? In TTRPG terms, what's their actual utility or 'domain'? Do they have any mechanical hooks for a PC?

The Gondolier Guild in Qaringil (remember, the Q is pronounced like a Kh) is much more than just a taxi service. According to the notes, they are one of the seven Great Guilds. Their actual domain includes the maintenance and control of the city's bridges, specifically the Great Western Bridge which is a massive strategic asset. Because Qaringil is built on canals and sits between two enormous bridges on a wizard-made isthmus, the Gondoliers essentially control the movement of both maritime and terrestrial traffic. Mechanically, they are a 'Great Guild,' meaning full membership likely grants significant social standing. One of the most interesting political hooks is that, unlike many other guilds, the Gondolier Guild has never been led by any of the Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, or Vitelli). This makes them a rare independent power center in the city.

That sounds like a solid setup for a 'city-crawl' campaign. If they control the bridges and canals, do the notes mention if they have a monopoly on structural repairs or maybe secret 'guild-only' waterways? Also, does the DM give them any specific combat or utility perks, like maybe proficiency with vehicles (water) or some kind of 'Navigator' feat equivalent?

The notes specify they handle maintenance and control of the bridges, which implies a monopoly on that infrastructure. However, the details on secret waterways or specific 'guild-only' tunnels are currently a blank space in the campaign documents. The DM has not explicitly listed secret routes, though the city is described as 'criss-crossed with canals,' which provides plenty of room for you to propose something for a backstory. Regarding mechanical perks like proficiency with water vehicles or specific feats, the notes are silent on those granular details. In Qaringil, you can assume you are a full guild member at level five, which would logically include vehicle (water) proficiency based on the guild's nature, but there isn't a pre-baked 'Gondolier' feat or unique combat style listed in the current lore.

Fair enough. What about their relationship with the 'minor' or 'illegal' guilds? Like, if I'm a Gondolier, am I constantly bumping heads with the Thieves' or Cutpurses' guilds under the bridges? Does the lore say how they handle security?

That is another area where the notes leave a blank space. We know there are underground criminal organizations like the Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses who style themselves as guilds. Given the Gondoliers control the bridges and waterways where these groups might operate, there is high potential for conflict or even illicit cooperation, but the specific 'security' protocols of the Gondolier Guild haven't been defined yet. The documentation focuses on their legitimacy as a Great Guild and their independence from the Five Families. If you wanted to play a character who deals with the criminal underbelly, you could certainly fill that gap by suggesting your character is a liaison between the Gondoliers and the canal-side gangs, but you would need to clear that with the DM since it is not explicitly in the text.

Seven moons? That is a bit much for a campaign world, do you not think? Most homebrews just throw stuff at the wall to see what sticks. Tell me about these outer ones, Sextus and Septimus. Are they just window dressing or do they actually follow some kind of internal logic?

The moon system of Qaia (pronounced Khaya) is quite structured. Sextus and Septimus are the outermost of the seven. Unlike the inner moons, they are retrograde, meaning they rise in the west and set in the east. They are not massive enough to dominate the sky like Quartus or Tertius; instead, they appear as small, bright discs. Sextus is about 4.1 arcminutes in diameter—roughly five times the apparent size of Jupiter from Earth—while Septimus is smaller at 2.7 arcminutes. They serve as reliable celestial markers, particularly for those tracking longer periods of time.
Retrograde? Fine. But if they are that far out, how bright do they actually get? Give me the numbers. I have seen too many DMs hand-wave orbital mechanics and brightness just to sound 'magical.'

The physics are specific. Sextus has a full-moon magnitude of -8.02, which is about 4.7 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's Moon, providing only 1/77th of its illumination. Septimus is even fainter at magnitude -7.14. Both have a geometric albedo of 0.09, making them darker than Earth's Moon (0.12) but similar to the inner moon Tertius. Physically, they are identical twins with a radius of 374 km and a density of 3,344 kg/m3—the same density as Earth's Moon. They are likely rocky captures, sitting at 1.64 LD and 2.47 LD (Lunar Distances) respectively.

If they are that small and far away, I assume they do not do much for the tides. And what about the naming? Sextus and Septimus imply a Quintus. Did the DM lose one, or is there a 'Fifth' moon hiding somewhere in the notes?

You are correct on both counts. Their tidal contribution is negligible—Sextus exerts 0.002 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon, and Septimus is even lower at 0.001. Regarding Quintus, it is not a moon in the traditional sense. The notes describe Quintus as a 'trace particle' located at the Sun-Qaia L4 Lagrange point, roughly 60 degrees ahead of the planet in its orbit. It is effectively massless and librates over a 2,000-year period. It is more of a worldbuilding detail for astronomers than a visible fixture in the night sky.

An L4 trojan. That is surprisingly grounded. But with seven bodies, plus retrograde orbits, that system has to be a gravitational nightmare. Is this more 'wizard-did-it' hand-waving or has there been actual thought put into why these things do not just collide or get ejected?

The setting notes mention that a 2,000-year simulation was conducted for the five freely-orbiting moons (Secundus through Septimus), and they remained stable throughout that timeframe. Primus, the innermost moon, is 'magically anchored' in a geostationary orbit, so it does not migrate. However, the notes acknowledge that Secundus is a retrograde moon and is slowly spiraling inward over geological time. For the 200-year span between the Qaringil prequel and the present-day Qamis campaign, the system is functionally stable, though the long-term fate of the system without dragon magic is noted as an open question.

Since they take 57 and 106 days to orbit, do the locals actually use them for anything besides looking at? Or are they just there for the navigators on the far-side who cannot see that stationary 'Primus' moon?

Septimus, with its 106.22-day period, is frequently used as a long-calendar body. For navigation, you are right that they are more relevant on the far-side of the planet. On the near-side, navigators use Primus to find longitude instantly, which is a massive technological advantage. On the far-side, where Primus is permanently below the horizon, navigators must use 'lunar-distance' methods. This involves measuring the angle between a moon like Sextus or Septimus and specific stars in a catalog—a much harder mathematical problem, similar to the longitude problem on Earth before the chronometer.

Wait, if Sextus and Septimus are retrograde, their 'lunar-distance' math would be inverted compared to the others. Has the DM actually mapped out how that affects navigation tables, or is that one of those 'blank spaces' I keep hearing about?

The specific navigation tables for Sextus and Septimus are not fully detailed in the current notes, which makes them one of those blank spaces you could potentially explore. The 'Navigation Guide' primarily focuses on the 'primsine' function for Primus, which allows near-side navigators to skip the complex math entirely. For a far-side navigator using the retrograde moons, they would certainly need specialized almanacs to account for the west-to-east movement. If you wanted to play a navigator from a distant land like Westron or the islands to the south, figuring out those specific orbital tables could be a great piece of character backstory.

I am so hyped to start this campaign! I'm rolling up a character from Rathakos, and I've been obsessing over the history of the Red Mountain Republic. It sounds like such a gritty, short-lived rebel state! But I got sidetracked reading about Qis'mos—how did the drow end up holding Qama of all places? Isn't that the holy city of Bahumat? That is such a wild twist!

It really is one of the most shocking outcomes of the Ryraxian War! You've got the vibe exactly right—it is total chaos. To answer your question, the drow ended up in Qama (remember, that's pronounced Kha-ma) because of a desperation move by Artkos. When the war was going poorly for them, the High Kings of Artkos recruited the drow as allies. During the late stages of the conflict, the drow occupied the holy city. Even though the war is over now, they haven't left! They've actually established a dozen scattered enclaves on the surface, with Qama being the most significant. They even appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV, which has caused a huge schism in the Church of Bahamut because the rival candidate is currently stuck in Qis without the official relics of the office!

That is absolute madness! I can imagine the people in Qis'mos are furious. So, bringing it back to my Rathakos character—if they grew up in the mountains, would they have seen the transition from the Red Mountain Republic first-hand? My guy's family were probably part of those mountain communities. Was the purge of the pro-Edran faction as violent as it sounds? I want to know if my character has a reason to be suspicious of the new government under Regent John Dunne.

Your character would almost certainly have some strong opinions on that! The shift from the Red Mountain Republic to the current state of Rathakos was a major turning point. Essentially, when Edrad was occupying the mountains, they organized the provinces into the Red Mountain Republic. But when the Edrans were forced to withdraw, a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly rose up and 'purged' the pro-Edran members of the Republic. That's how the nations of Rathakos and Acrokos were born. If your character's family were on the 'wrong' side of that purge, or if they live in the Southern Reaches that were annexed from Edrad, they would definitely be wary of the current Regency. Plus, Regent John Dunne is currently struggling with internal unrest and those old dragon cults in the mountains, so your character is entering a world where the dust from the war hasn't even begun to settle!

Hi! I am starting a PC for the Qaringil prequel game. I am trying to figure out my background. Is Qaringil like Waterdeep where there is a guild for literally everything? I want to be an Alchemist, but I am wondering if they are just part of a larger Mage guild or something like the Zhentarim.

Welcome to Qaringil! It is not exactly like Waterdeep. While Qaringil is a republic built on guilds, there is a very specific hierarchy. The city is dominated by seven Great Guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. These are the heavy hitters with the most political and economic clout.

For an Alchemist, you actually have your own specific organization. In this setting, the Alchemist Guild is separate from the Mage Guild. Both are considered 'smaller' guilds, meaning they don't have the same massive political power as the Great Guilds (which are mostly controlled by the five major merchant families), but they are legitimate professional bodies. Since you are starting at fifth level, you would already be a full member of the Alchemist Guild. 

It is definitely not like the Zhentarim. The Alchemist Guild is a legal, professional organization. If you are looking for something more shadowy, you would be looking at the underground criminal organizations that style themselves as guilds, like the Thieves, Bandits, or Cutpurses. Those are definitely illegal and compete with each other.

That makes sense. In a lot of settings like Greyhawk or the Realms, the Thieves Guild is basically the shadow government. Is that the case here? And you mentioned a Gondolier guild—is that just a bunch of guys on boats, or are they like the Harpers, doing secret stuff across the bridges?

In Qaringil, the 'shadow government' concept doesn't quite fit. The real power is held by the Great Guilds and the five major families—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli. The criminal 'guilds' like the Thieves and Cutpurses are underground and competing, not a unified secret government. 

As for the Gondolier Guild, they are actually one of the Seven Great Guilds, which means they are very powerful. They aren't like the Harpers; they don't have a secret ideological agenda. Instead, they have a practical monopoly. They maintain the canals and, more importantly, they control the Great Bridges. Because Qaringil sits on an isthmus with two massive wizard-made bridges, controlling that traffic is a huge source of wealth and power. They are a commercial powerhouse, not a group of secret agents. 

One interesting detail for your backstory: the Gondolier Guild and the Wool Guild are the only two Great Guilds that have never been led by one of the five major noble families. They are more the domain of the common citizens and smaller interests.

Oh, so if I'm an Alchemist, I'm not in the 'ruling class' guilds. If I wanted my Alchemist to have high-society connections, which of the Five Families should I look at? Are any of them like the Lannisters or the Cassalanters?

Correct, the Alchemist Guild is a professional tier below the Great Guilds. If you want connections to the elite, you should look at the five families. They are all mostly human with some elven ancestry. 

If you want the family with the most political/legal control, look at the Manos family (Sigil: Hand). They lead the Judicial Guild. If you want the wealthiest, the Volmer family (Sigil: Crow) leads the Banking Guild. The Vitelli family (Sigil: Moth) leads the Silk Guild, and the Peixotas (Sigil: Dolphin) lead the Shipping Guild. 

The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is from a minor family but is closely aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. The Qanto family (Sigil: Dove) recently lost their leadership of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family called the Tiddwars, so they might be a good choice if you want a family that is feeling a bit slighted or looking to regain its status. 

Remember that Q in this setting is pronounced like 'Kh', so names like Qanto are pronounced 'Khanto'.

Hey, I'm considering running a quick one-shot set in the Qaringil era for my group. I missed the last session where the world map was discussed—how much of the continent of Westron is actually defined? I was thinking of having a character from there.

In the Qaringil prequel notes, Westron is explicitly mentioned as a blank space. The DM notes say that while the continent is important, there is no prepared material for it yet. The only specific detail provided is that there are religious differences there, as the inhabitants are mostly not worshippers of Bahumat.
That is pretty thin. Does the present-day Qamis campaign guide have more info I can borrow? It would be easier if I had a city name or a specific culture to reference.

Even in the present-day Qamis setting, Westron remains largely undefined. The notes describe it as a very large continent to the west that is considered "exotic," but they also state that the name itself might need to be changed and that it's a space that needs to be filled in. Because it's a blank space, you have the freedom to invent names and cultural details for your one-shot's backstory without contradicting existing lore.

Okay, so if I'm building this one-shot in the city of Qaringil, I can basically treat Westron as an unknown frontier for character origins? Is there anything else about the islands or the Qaringil culture I should know to bridge that gap?

Yes, you can treat Westron as an unknown origin. For your Qaringil one-shot, remember that the city-state is a republic dominated by powerful merchant families and guilds like the Silk, Shipping, and Banking guilds. Since Qaringil's population of 100,000 is built primarily by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, your Westron character would be an outsider in a city that speaks Islas (with a heavy Qis vocabulary). Just keep in mind that Q is always pronounced as "Kh."

Hey, I'm working on a half-elf noble from Edrad for our upcoming Qamis game. I'm looking at the Artklan neighbors. Is West Artkos still the seat of the Holy Qis Empire? I read something about King Ezevir Arth II ruling from there, and I'm wondering if my PC's family, the Keyberos, would have had diplomatic ties to that court during the Ryraxian War.

It sounds like you might be mixing up the two different time periods in the campaign notes. In the present-day Qamis setting (post-Ryraxian War), the Holy Qis Empire no longer exists; that was the dominant power 200 years ago during the Qaringil era. Also, King Ezevir Arth II was a ruler from that earlier time. Currently, in the Qamis era, the nation is called Artkos, and it is ruled by High King Ezevir Arth. West Artkos is the northwestern territory of that kingdom and its historical heartland, but the current capital is the city of Ath'r. As an Edran noble of the Keyberos line, your family has likely viewed Artkos as a major rival for centuries, especially since the Artklan League is basically a shadow of the old empire Artkos once led.

That makes sense, sorry for the confusion. So if I'm from Jia, West Artkos is quite a ways off. Why does that specific region matter so much to the current High King if he's sitting in Ath'r? Is it just sentimental, or is there a dynastic reason my half-elven cousins would care about it?

It matters because West Artkos is the 'old money' and ancestral power base of the Arth family. While High King Ezevir Arth rules from Ath'r—which has been the seat of various empires for centuries—the legitimacy of the Artkos crown is rooted in that northwestern territory. For your Keyberos character, West Artkos represents the stubborn, traditional core of the Artklan people that Edrad could never quite assimilate or conquer. During the Ryraxian War, which just ended, the Artklan League was essentially trying to restore the dominance they feel they lost when their old empire fractured. Your character would know that while Edrad and the city of Jia are the cultural and economic centers of the continent, the Arths still consider themselves the rightful High Kings of the central lands, and that claim starts with their hold on West Artkos.

hiyaaa. Honestly, I am looking at these notes and my head is spinning. Why are there so many names that look exactly the same? It feels like this setting is trying too hard to be complex. Like, who even is High King Ezevir Arth? I see that name everywhere.

It can certainly feel dense at first, especially with the 'Q' always being pronounced as 'Kh.' Regarding High King Ezevir Arth, the confusion usually stems from the fact that there are two different rulers with that name appearing in two different time periods of the setting.

In the present-day Qamis campaign (post-Ryraxian War), High King Ezevir Arth rules the nation of Artkos and is trying to restore the dominance of the Artklan League. However, if you are looking at the Qaringil prequel notes set 200 years earlier, you will see King Ezevir Arth II. He was a previous ruler famous for doubling the size of Artkos's territory in the northwest.

See, that is exactly it. Why use the same name? It is confusing. Is the current one 'Ezevir Arth III' or something? And what is the deal with Artkos? It is all over the map in like three different pieces.

The notes do not actually give a numeral for the current High King; he is just called High King Ezevir Arth. It is common for dynasties to reuse names, which adds to that 'historical' feel but can be a headache for players. 

As for Artkos being split up, that is a result of centuries of conquest. Its historic heartland is in the northwest (West Artkos), but it also holds an enclave in the center of the continent which contains the capital city, Ath'r. It has a third enclave in the southwest centered on the city of Kard. This geography makes High King Ezevir's job very difficult, especially since those enclaves are surrounded by other nations like Edrad and Qis'mos.
Wait, so he is the High King but his subjects in Kard do not even like him? The notes say they are Edran-speaking. Is he just a conqueror then?

In the case of Kard, yes, the situation is very tense. Much of the population there speaks Edran and they actually welcomed an invasion twenty-eight years ago during the Ryraxian War. 

In the Qamis present day, High King Ezevir Arth is trying to project power through the 'Artklan League,' but the notes describe this as a 'pale shadow' of the old Artklan Empire. While he claims supremacy, his subjects are mostly clinging to 'tenuous quasi-independence.' He is essentially trying to enforce a restoration of borders that many people on the ground no longer want.
Okay, let us look at the prequel then. Ezevir Arth II. You said he had a daughter. Was she the one who lost the empire or something? Since the current guy is just a 'shadow' of it?

Not quite. In the Qaringil era (200 years ago), King Ezevir Arth II was followed by his daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I. She actually wanted to expand Artkos even further. 

The Artklan Empire did not collapse until much later, during the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended in the present day. The dragon Ryraxos is the one who actually dissolved the Empire. The current Ezevir Arth is the one dealing with the aftermath of that collapse, trying to put the pieces back together after Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon, Secundus.
This sounds like a logistical nightmare. If he is in Ath'r, which the notes say was the seat of 'central Qamisi empires,' does that mean he is actually Qis or is he still Artklan?

He is definitely Artklan. While the city of Ath'r has been the seat of various empires for a very long time, Artkos conquered it about 200 years before the Qaringil prequel. So, by the time of the current Qamis campaign, the Artklan dynasty has held Ath'r for roughly 400 years. 

Even though the city is a central hub, it remains an Artklan enclave. The prestige dialect spoken there by the ruling class is Artklan, though the notes mention that someone from the capital might have trouble understanding the regional Artklan spoken in a place like Rathakos.
So 'High King' is just a fancy title. Does he actually have power over Rathakos, where the campaign starts, or is that just something he tells himself?

It is largely a title without teeth. Rathakos is a mountainous border state that was formed after the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the old empire. 

While Artkos wants to restore its dominance, Rathakos is currently trying to maintain its independence. It does not even have its own monarch yet; it is being governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council looks for a Prince. So, while Ezevir Arth might claim to be the High King of the League that Rathakos is nominally part of, the reality is one of 'internal unrest' and mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults rather than his decrees.
yo nanochat, our DM mentioned something about a triple-new moon thing happening soon in the game. when is that actually supposed to happen?

Hey! That refers to a rare alignment where the three inner moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—all reach their new moon phase (the dark phase) at the same time. According to the celestial records, the absolute closest triple-new alignment of the decade happens on May 24, 2262, at 03:00 PMT. At that exact moment, all three moons are within 0.1% of being perfectly new. It is the closest alignment in the current ten-year cycle.
2262? my character might be retired by then lol. are there any happening sooner, like in the next year or two? and is it just for cool vibes or do the moons actually do something?

Good point! Since Qamis is in the 2250s right now, you don't have to wait until 2262 for the 'vibes.' We are currently in a new-moon era (2255–2258) where these happen more frequently. There is a triple-new on November 29, 2255, and two very strong ones in 2256: April 26 and September 21. As for what they do, it's pretty intense—the combined tidal force of those three moons is about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's moon, so the oceans go crazy. Also, since the dragon Ryraxos was just exiled to Secundus (the second moon) after the war, people are usually pretty nervous when the moons align, thinking it might be an omen or a weakening of his prison.

Hey! I'm playing a drow who decided to stay on the surface in Qama after the Ryraxian War ended. I'm trying to get a better sense of the world we're on. What's the name of the planet, and what's the deal with the geography outside of Qamis?

The planet you are on is called Qaia. You're currently on the continent of Qamis during the 'present day' era, about thirty years after the Ryraxian War began. Since you're in Qama, you're in the holy city of Bahumat, which your people occupied during the late phases of the war under the direction of the High King of Artkos. As for the rest of the world, there's a very large continent to the west called Westron and a large, mostly unexplored island to the south that hosts the city of Freeport.

Okay, so what can you tell me about Westron? My character might have heard stories from sailors in Qama. Is there a specific empire or a drow presence there too? Also, what's further south than that jungle island?

To be honest, those are 'blank spaces' in the current campaign notes. The DM hasn't filled in the details for Westron yet, other than noting it is very large and that the people there mostly do not worship Bahumat. There's no mention of a drow presence or specific nations there. Similarly, for the big island to the south, the notes only mention the pirate city of Freeport on the shore and the dense jungle pressing against it. Anything further south or deeper into that jungle is currently unmapped and unknown.

That's a lot of empty space. What about my own people's history before we came to the surface? The notes say the drow were recruited as allies by Artkos, but where did we actually live in the Underdark? Are there named cities or a map of the world below?

The notes are silent on the specifics of the Underdark. While it's established that drow enclaves are now scattered across the surface of Qamis—with your city of Qama being the most prominent because of the schism involving your high priest, Qe'lth IV (remember, Q is pronounced like 'Kh')—the names and locations of your original subterranean cities haven't been defined. It's a blank space that you might be able to help fill in with your own character's backstory.

Got it. So I can basically invent my hometown. One more thing: I saw a note about moons. If I'm traveling from Qama up to Rathakos, how many moons am I seeing, and does one of them really just sit still in the sky?

Qaia has a complex system of six moons, plus a trace particle called Quintus. For someone on the surface of Qamis, the most unique one is Primus. It is a small, dark iron moon that is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. Because its 23.5 degree inclination matches Qaia's axial tilt, it stays fixed over the equator at the prime meridian. It never rises or sets; it is a motionless point in the sky. Navigators use it to find their longitude by measuring its elevation. The other moons—Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus—all move, with Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus being retrograde, meaning they rise in the west.

Alright, I am looking at these moon eras in the notes. Sounds like a lot of extra math for something that is usually just flavor. In the Forgotten Realms, we have Selune, it cycles, and we move on. Why do I need to care about a Full-Moon Era versus a New-Moon Era here? Is this just a homebrew version of the Time of Troubles or what?

It is a bit different from the Realms. In the Qamis setting, the sky is dominated by three inner moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Because they have different orbital periods, they occasionally align. A Full-moon era is a multi-year period where triple-full moons happen frequently, about five times a year, while triple-new moons do not happen at all. A New-moon era is the reverse. This cycle repeats roughly every 12 years. It is a predictable astronomical cycle rather than a sudden divine upheaval like the Time of Troubles.

Three moons sounds like Krynn, but at least there the colors matched the wizard robes. If I am playing a cleric of Bahumat in Qis'mos, does a triple-full moon actually do anything for my spells, or is the DM just making me track a calendar? Most homebrews over-complicate the sky and then forget about it by session three.

The current notes do not specify immediate mechanical bonuses for your spells or 'wizard robes' style affiliations. Those are blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in yet. However, the religious significance for a cleric of Bahumat (pronounced Bahumut, with the Q in related names like Qama sounding like Kh) could be huge. The city of Qama, the holy city of Bahumat, is currently occupied by drow who have appointed their own high priest. A triple-full moon alignment, like the one that happened on February 13, 2253, might be seen as a holy omen or a time for the alternate candidate, Qachu II, to make a move. The next decade is actually shifting into a New-moon era, with a near-perfect triple-new moon coming on May 24, 2262.

So the lights are literally going out for a few years. Great. In Greyhawk, at least Celestian makes the moons interesting. What about those other ones, Sextus and Septimus? Do they join the party or are they just sitting out there like flavor text?

Sextus and Septimus are much farther out and move much slower. Sextus takes about 58 days and Septimus takes 106 days to orbit. They do not participate in the triple-moon alignment cycle that creates the eras. They are mostly used for long-term calendars. The main tidal and visual changes come from the inner three. For example, the combined tidal force of the inner three moons is about 4.4 times that of Earth's moon, so a triple-full moon era would mean significantly more dramatic tides and much brighter nights than anything you would see on Toril or Oerth.

Hello! I'm joining the Qaringil campaign soon and it's my first time playing D&D. I was reading the notes and wanted to make a character who is a veteran of the Ryraxian War from a place called Rathakos, but I want them to be originally from the continent of Westron. Does that fit the lore?

Welcome to D&D! It's an exciting time to start, but there are two different eras in this setting that can be easy to mix up. Since you are playing in the Qaringil campaign, that is actually a prequel set about 200 years before the Ryraxian War happened. In the Qaringil era, the dragon Ryraxos hasn't started his war yet, and the nation of Rathakos doesn't even exist—it's still just part of the wild Red Mountains. If you want a character for Qaringil, they would need to be living two centuries before those events.

Oh, I see! Thanks for clearing that up. I'll just be a regular traveler then. Since I want to be from Westron, what can you tell me about the kingdoms there in the Qaringil era? I want to make sure my backstory matches the maps.

That is actually one of the 'blank spaces' in the campaign notes. While Westron is known to be a very large continent to the west, the DM hasn't defined any specific nations, cities, or kings for it yet. The notes only mention that it is considered exotic and that the people there mostly do not worship Bahumat (pronounced Ba-ha-mut). This means you have a lot of freedom to invent a hometown or a small culture with your DM to fill in that blank space for your character's history.
That's helpful. If I'm from Westron, do I speak Qis? I saw that's a main language.

In this setting, everyone usually gets to speak the 'prestige' dialect of their home capital and one regional dialect. Since Westron is so far away, you might want to ask your DM if you speak a unique Westron language. Also, a quick tip for names: the letter Q is always pronounced like 'Kh' in this world. So Qis is pronounced 'Khis' and the city of Qaringil is 'Kharingil.' It comes from an old language called Old Draconic or Old Qis.
Hey, I am joining a Qamis campaign and the DM says we are starting in Rathakos. Can I get an executive summary of why this place is known for adventuring? My character is an explorer type.

Rathakos (pronounced Rath-a-khos) is the prime starting point for explorers because it sits right in the Red Mountains, which are the most dangerous and underexplored regions in the interior of the continent. It is effectively a frontier state. 

Historically, it was part of the Artklan Empire, then the Red Mountain Republic during the Ryraxian War. Now, it is a newly independent Princedom, but it lacks a Prince. It is currently governed by a Regent named John Dunne while they search for a monarch. Because the government is still stabilizing and the terrain is so rugged, there is a massive reliance on independent contractors—adventurers—to handle threats and explore the mountain reaches.
Interesting. If it's surrounded by big powers like Edrad and Artkos, why hasn't it been fully mapped or tamed yet? Is it just the geography?

Geography is a huge factor, but the political history is the real driver. For thirty years, the continent was consumed by the Ryraxian War. During that time, the borders moved constantly. Rathakos was under Edran occupation for a while as part of the Red Mountain Republic. When the Edrans were forced to withdraw due to guerrilla resistance, the region fractured into Rathakos and Acrokos.

Because the administrative control has changed hands so many times, no one has had the long-term stability to properly 'tame' the mountains. Plus, many of the mountain communities still follow old dragon cults and resist outside authority. It is a vacuum of power where the traditional map-making and tax-collecting of nations like Edrad simply fail.
You mentioned dragon cults and that dragon overlord Ryraxos. Are those related? I thought the war ended because he was exiled.

The war did end with Ryraxos's exile, but he is currently on Secundus (the second moon). He was a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander before ruling for decades. However, the dragon cults in the Red Mountains are older than him; they represent a more ancient tradition in the peaks that pre-dates the recent war.

Rathakos is dealing with a dual-threat environment: you have the Southern Reaches, which were annexed from Edrad and are full of civil unrest, and then you have these isolated mountain tribes who might still be loyal to draconic entities from history. For an adventurer, this means plenty of high-stakes work dealing with ancient relics, insurgencies, and the literal 'blank spaces' on the Regent's maps.

Hey. So I am looking at these notes and honestly, this feels like a lot. Between the six moons and the different eras, it is getting a bit complicated for a dnd game. Now I see there is a whole mess of islands in the Central Sea? Who actually lives out there, and do I have to memorize twenty different kings?

It can definitely feel like a lot to take in! You really do not need to memorize the moon math unless you are playing a navigator. For the islands, it is best to split them by the era you are playing in. In the Qamis present day, the islands are mostly home to 'nautical robber barons'—exiles from the main continents who run mercenary navies. They basically sell their fleets to the highest bidder among the continental powers.
Robber barons and mercenaries. Sounds like a headache. What about that city Qaringil? I saw that name somewhere. Is that one of the robber baron places?

Qaringil is actually the focus of the prequel campaign, set about 200 years before the main Qamis timeline. It is a massive city-state built on a volcanic island between two enormous bridges. It is a republic with about 100,000 people, run by an Exarch and several Great Guilds. Most of the people there are humans with some elf ancestry, but there are also gnomes like the Tiddwar family.
Wait, so is it a republic or is it run by 'families'? You mentioned a gnomish family. This is what I mean by complicated—is it a government or a mafia?

In Qaringil, it is a bit of both. It is officially a republic, but five major families hold most of the power because they lead the biggest guilds. For example, the Peixota family—their sigil is a dolphin—leads the Shipping Guild. The Vitelli family has a moth sigil and runs the Silk Guild. They are commercial elites rather than landowners. It is less about 'kings' and more about who controls the trade and the canals.
Okay, so I just need to remember 'dolphins do shipping' and 'moths do silk.' What about that big island to the south? Is that also full of merchant gnomes?

That is a different vibe entirely. That is the site of Freeport, which is a city of pirates and exiles right on the edge of a massive, unexplored jungle. The notes say the residents try not to look too hard at the jungle pressing up against the city. There is a legend that the dragons went there after they left the main continent, but most of that island is still a 'blank space' the DM has not filled in yet.
hi, qamis? I am looking at the Artklan League for a PC background. Who is the current faction head for Artkos? I want to know the political meta-game and what their actual win-cons are right now.

In the present-day Qamis era, Artkos is governed directly by High King Ezevir Arth. His primary objective is to restore Artkos's dominance over the rest of the Artklan League, which the high kings see as a pale shadow of their old empire. Politically, he is dealing with nominal subjects who are clinging to a tenuous quasi-independence. A major friction point is the southwestern enclave of Kard; it is a vital financial center, but the population is largely Edran-speaking and actually welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago, making the High King's governance there very fragile.

Okay, so High King Ezevir Arth. Is he a high-level stat block or more of a narrative presence? And what is the deal with those blank spaces in the League? I am trying to see if there is a mechanical 'Cold War' vibe I can exploit for a rogue agent backstory or if the DM has left a specific power vacuum.

The notes do not provide a specific stat block for High King Ezevir Arth, so his mechanical power level is currently undefined by the DM. However, there are explicitly 'blank spaces' in the Artklan League and on the map. The DM has noted that these can be filled in with your character's backstory. There is definitely a 'Cold War' atmosphere, especially in Rathakos—a mountainous border state currently ruled by Regent John Dunne while they search for a Prince. Rathakos is dealing with internal unrest from pro-Edran factions and mountain communities following old dragon cults, which provides a lot of room for a rogue agent or operative-style background.

If I am from one of those blank spaces, what is the crunch on language? The notes say Artklan isn't standardized. If I take the prestige dialect of the capital, Ath'r (pronounced Ath-kh'r, right?), do I get a mechanical penalty to social checks in a place like Rathakos?

You are close on the pronunciation—Q is 'Kh', so Ath'r is likely closer to 'Ath-er', but names like Qis are 'Khis'. Regarding the mechanics: the notes state that most speakers from the capital of Ath'r would have trouble understanding the Artklan of someone from Rathakos. While the notes don't specify a numerical penalty to Charisma checks, they do say that without qualification, 'Artklan' refers to the prestige dialect of the capital. For your starting build, you get to speak the capital dialect and one regional dialect. If you only have the Ath'r prestige dialect, a DM might interpret the 'trouble understanding' as a higher DC for social interaction or a need for checks where they might otherwise be automatic.

Got it. One last probe: the notes mention a 'Red Mountain Republic' that was purged to form Rathakos and Acrokos. Are there any named survivors or a 'Shadow Cabinet' I should know about, or is that a total blank space?

That is a total blank space. The notes explain that the Red Mountain Republic was organized under Edran occupation and was later purged by the Red Mountain Assembly to establish Rathakos and Acrokos. Aside from Regent John Dunne in Rathakos, no specific members of the old Republic or the purging Assembly are named. This is a prime spot for you to invent NPCs or a secret organization for your backstory, as the DM has not yet filled in those specific historical actors.

hey there. i am rolling up a drow character for a qamis game. my backstory is that i stayed on the surface in qama (khama) after the war ended. i was reading the notes on rathakos and saw mention of old dragon cults in the mountains. is my character going to run into people worshiping ryraxos there?

It is an interesting choice to play a drow remaining in Qama. Since you are in the holy city of Bahumat, you are likely living under the administration of the drow high priest Qe'lth IV, who was appointed during the war. Regarding your question about the dragon cults: the notes for the current Qamis era specify that these cults persist in the mountain communities of Rathakos, particularly in areas that are seeing internal unrest. However, they are described as 'old dragon cults.' While Ryraxos was a 'dragon overlord' who ruled for nearly thirty years, he was a bronze dragon and a former artillery commander who is now exiled to Secundus (the second moon). The cults in the mountains likely predate his rise, as they are part of the 'old' traditions of the Red Mountains rather than a new movement centered on Ryraxos himself.

okay so if they aren't worshiping ryraxos who are they worshiping? is there an actual name for a dragon god or an ancient dragon in the red mountains that i should know about? also the map has a lot of blank spaces in the mountains... are the cults in those areas too?

That is one of those 'blank spaces' the DM has mentioned. The current notes do not provide a specific name for an ancient dragon or a deity for the Rathakos cults beyond the general mention of their existence. It is not clear if they worship a specific physical dragon that once lived in the Red Mountains or a more abstract draconic figure. As for the blank spaces on the map, the notes explicitly say those are areas for the players and DM to fill in together. The cults are definitely confirmed to be in the Southern Reaches of Rathakos (territory annexed from Edrad) and the northern foothills near the capital, Rath. Whether they extend into the unexplored gaps of the Red Mountains or into Acrokos is currently unwritten lore.

what about forthyr? i saw they have a sect wanting the return of the dragon emperors. is that the same thing as the mountain cults? i am trying to figure out if my drow would have any allies among these groups since the drow were allies of artkos during the war.

The situation in Forthyr is slightly different. Forthyr is in the northeast and is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty (currently Shanlong VI). Their religious sect calls for the return of the 'Dragon Emperors,' which seems to be a more formal or nationalistic movement that receive 'tacit encouragement' from the sorcerer-kings. The cults in Rathakos are described more as a source of 'internal unrest' and regional tradition in the southwest. Whether there is a theological link between the Forthyr sect and the Red Mountain cultists is another detail the notes do not cover yet. Regarding your drow being an ally: while the drow were indeed recruited as allies by Artkos during the war, Rathakos is currently a brand-new nation struggling for independence from Artkos. The local cultists might view an Artkos-aligned drow with suspicion, or they might see you as a fellow outlier. The notes are silent on how these cultists feel about drow specifically, so that would be something to explore with the DM as a 'blank space' in the social fabric.
yo, what's this thing about seven moons I keep hearing about? I'm thinking of running a Qaringil one-shot and want to know if the night sky is as complicated as it sounds.

It is definitely a busy sky! There are actually six distinct moons that are visible, plus a tiny trace particle called Quintus. The most interesting part for your players will be the directions they move. Primus, Tertius, and Quartus are prograde, meaning they move west-to-east. However, Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are retrograde—they rise in the west and move 'backward' compared to the others. It makes for some very dynamic nights.

That sounds like a lot for a one-shot! Does the movement change between the main Qamis timeline and the Qaringil prequel? I don't want to give my players the wrong celestial map if they're 200 years in the past.

The orbital physics are essentially the same. Two hundred years is a blink of an eye for these moons, so the prograde and retrograde directions haven't changed. If you run a session in Qaringil, the city-state's navigators would be using the exact same system. The biggest difference is the political map below: instead of the post-war nations like Rathakos, you'd have the Holy Qis Empire ruling from Ath'r. But the sky is a constant.
Wait, what about Primus? I read it's stationary. If it doesn't move, how is it prograde?

Good catch. Primus is 'prograde' in the sense that its orbit follows the direction of the planet's rotation, but because it is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit, it doesn't actually 'rise' or 'set.' It hangs over the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude). For players in the city of Qaringil, it's just a motionless point in the sky that they can use to find their way home. The other moons—like the dark, iron-rich Secundus—will be the ones they see sweeping past it in the opposite direction.

HIII! I'm prepping a session and I'm a bit confused about the political situation after the Ryraxian War. I know the dragon is gone, but who is this Exarch Giovanni Pesaro? Is he the one managing the drow enclaves in Qama now that things are supposedly back to normal?

I can help clarify that! It sounds like you might be mixing up the two different eras of the setting. 

Exarch Giovanni Pesaro isn't involved in the post-Ryraxian War era (the Qamis campaign). He is actually a central figure in the prequel campaign, Qaringil, which is set roughly 200 years earlier. In the present day of Qamis, Giovanni Pesaro is long dead.

In the Qaringil era, he is the ruler of the island city-state of Qaringil. He has been the Exarch for 19 years and is described as being on death's doorstep. He's from a minor family but stays in power by aligning with the more powerful Peixota and Manos families.

As for the drow in the holy city of Qama during the Qamis era, they are currently led by a drow high priest named Qe'lth IV. This is actually a major point of conflict because there is a rival claimant, Qachu II, who operates from the city of Qis without the official relics of office.

Ohhh, okay. My bad. I saw the name and thought he was one of the guys rebuilding Rathakos. So if Rathakos didn't exist in Pesaro's time, what was going on in that part of the Red Mountains during the Qaringil prequel?

That's a great question for a DM looking at the timeline. In the Qaringil era (200 years ago), neither Rathakos nor Acrokos exists. 

The region where Rathakos eventually forms is part of the Holy Qis Empire, which rules from the central city of Ath'r. However, the empire doesn't effectively administer the Red Mountains; they just claim them. The mountains are occupied by various non-state peoples and are considered a wild, dangerous frontier. 

It isn't until the Ryraxian War in the Qamis era that the Red Mountain Republic is formed under Edran occupation. When the Edrans eventually withdraw, that republic splits and is reorganized into the nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. 

If you're looking for inspiration from Giovanni Pesaro's time, Qaringil is much more about maritime trade, powerful merchant guilds (like the Shipping and Silk guilds), and canal-based urban intrigue, whereas the modern Rathakos is more about mountain frontiersmanship and lingering dragon cults.
Hey! I am super new to D&D and I am about to join a game in the Qamis setting. I am a little confused on the geography. Is Qamis the whole world or just a piece of it? What is actually nearby?

Welcome to the hobby! It is totally normal to feel a bit lost at first. Qamis is actually the name of the continent where the story takes place. The entire planet is called Qaia. Since you are playing in the Qamis campaign, the setting is in the present day, just after a massive thirty-year war called the Ryraxian War ended. As for what is nearby, you have a few major neighbors. To the west is a very large continent called Westron, which is quite exotic to people from Qamis and has different religious traditions. To the south, there is a giant, mostly unexplored island with a pirate city called Freeport on the coast. Most people avoid the jungle behind that city because it is dangerous, and some legends say that is where the dragons vanished to.
Oh okay, so Qamis is the continent on the planet Qaia. Got it. What about the islands I saw mentioned? Are they part of the continent or their own thing?

There are several islands in the Central Sea. They are home to what we call thassolocracies—basically nations of nautical robber barons who run powerful mercenary navies. They are often hired by the big continental powers like Edrad or Artkos. There is also a famous wizard-made isthmus in the north of the Central Sea that has two massive bridges. In the prequel era, about 200 years before your game, a city-state called Qaringil sits right there on an island between those bridges, but in your current time, those islands are still key for trade and travel.
Wait, I just realized I might be saying the name wrong. Is it pronounced like Kwamis?

That is a very common mistake! In this setting, the letter Q is pronounced like Kh, which comes from the Old Draconic or Old Qis language. So, Qamis is actually pronounced Khamis. You will see that Q-without-a-U everywhere in place names like Qis, Qama, and Qeth. It is a little quirk of the setting lore.
Khamis. Okay, I can do that. One more thing—is there a big empire ruling everything right now, or is it all split up because of that war?

It is definitely split up. The old Artklan Empire used to rule about forty percent of the continent, but it is gone now. Today, you have the Artklan League, which is a bit of a loose group, and several independent nations like Rathakos, which is a mountainous border state where campaigns often start. Edrad is another big player; it is a kingdom ruled by half-elven nobility that was recently restored to power after the dragon Ryraxos was defeated and exiled to Secundus, one of Qaia's moons.

I'm looking for a quick executive summary of this Qamis setting. My DM mentioned Forthyr is "backwards," which feels like a tired fantasy trope. Give me the high-level view and be honest: is "backwards" just flavor text, or is there a systemic reason for it in the lore?

In the present-day Qamis setting, Forthyr is an enormous nation in the northeast. The label "backwards" isn't just a generic descriptor; it refers to a specific political and religious stagnation. While nations like Lock are experiencing an industrial surge and Edrad is a dominant cultural force, Forthyr is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty—currently Shanlong VI. They are sorcerer-kings whose dragonborn blood has thinned over generations, yet they cling to the past. The systemic reason for this reputation is a prominent religious sect that calls for the return of the ancient Dragon Emperors. The Dragoncaller dynasty doesn't just tolerate this; they give it tacit encouragement. It is a society looking entirely in the rearview mirror while the rest of the continent deals with the fallout of the Ryraxian War.

So it's a legacy system that refuses to patch. That sounds like a recipe for a failed state if their neighbors are modernizing. Is there actually any friction, or does Forthyr just sit in the corner of the map being old?

There is definitely friction. In the present day, Forthyr is finding it impossible to continue ignoring the affairs of the southwest after decades of isolation. To put the timeline in perspective, in the Qaringil prequel era (roughly 200 years earlier), the nation was ruled by Shanlong I. Even then, the notes describe Forthyr as somewhat backwards. By the time of Shanlong VI, the obsession with the return of dragons has only intensified. This creates a cultural divide with places like the Artklan League or Qis'mos, where institutions have evolved significantly since the dragons departed. The DM has left several "blank spaces" regarding Forthyr's specific internal mechanics, but the primary conflict is this tension between their massive size and their desire to restore a prehistoric draconic hierarchy.
Fair enough. One more thing—my DM mentioned something about the letter Q being a 'Kh' sound. Is that related to the Forthyr situation?

That is a global rule for the setting: Q is always pronounced "Kh," like in Qis (Kh-is) or Qaringil (Kh-aringil). This comes from Old Draconic, which is the language of scholarship and ancient treaties. While Forthyr has its own national language, Forthrian, they would be deeply invested in the "q without u" naming conventions found in place names like Ath'r or Qama, as these are markers of the old draconic influence they are so eager to restore. In contrast, a place like Rathakos (the mountainous border state where the present-day campaign starts) is a new nation formed from the recent war, representing a break from those old draconic imperial structures.

Hey! I am joining a Qamis game soon and I am a huge astronomy nerd. Where exactly are we starting? I want to build a character who is obsessed with the math of these seven moons. Does the sky look crazy from the starting town?

The campaign starts in Rathakos, a mountainous border state nestled in the foothills of the Red Mountains. Its capital is a city simply called Rath. For an astronomy enthusiast, Rathakos is a fascinating place to start because of the intense celestial mechanics at play. First, keep in mind that 'Q' is pronounced like 'Kh', so the continent name sounds like 'Khamis'. 

From Rath, the sky is dominated by three main moving moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Together with the sun, they create a combined tidal force about 4.4 times stronger than what we see on Earth. Even in the interior, the rhythm of the tides—driven by three different orbital periods of roughly 4.5, 8.1, and 29.5 days—is a constant topic of study. You also have the two outer moons, Sextus and Septimus, which move in retrograde (rising in the west). If your character is into math, they would know that Tertius, the 8-day moon, is the actual basis for the Qaian week.

Wait, 4.4x tides? That is wild. My character is definitely going to be a navigator or a hobbyist cartographer. I saw something in the notes about a geostationary moon called Primus. Can I see it from Rath? And how does a fixed moon help me find my way through the Red Mountains?

Yes, Primus is visible from Rathakos. It is a geostationary moon magically anchored directly over the equator at the prime meridian. Because it never rises or sets, it sits at a fixed elevation and azimuth in the sky for anyone in the near hemisphere. 

For a navigator in the Red Mountains, Primus is a revolutionary tool. While you use the pole star to find your latitude, you can find your longitude by measuring the elevation of Primus. You would use a specific mathematical lookup called the 'primsine' function. Basically, the angle of Primus tells you how far you are from the 0-degree meridian. It means Qamisi navigators solved the longitude problem centuries ago without needing expensive clocks. Since Rathakos is a frontier with plenty of 'blank spaces' on the map according to the DM, a character who can calculate precise coordinates using the primsine of Primus would be incredibly valuable to an adventuring party exploring the Red Mountains.

That is perfect. I want to time my character's birth or some big life event to a rare alignment. Are there any 'triple-moon' events? If we are starting in the present day, what is the most recent big alignment?

The system goes through 12-year cycles of alignments. We recently transitioned from a 'new-moon era' into a 'full-moon era.' If your campaign is set around the year 2263, the most significant recent event was May 24, 2262. At 03:00 PMT, there was a 'triple-new' alignment where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus were all within 0.1 percent of syzygy simultaneously. It would have been a night of total darkness, save for the stars and the tiny, stationary light of Primus. Before that, there was a very close 'triple-full' moon on February 13, 2253. Depending on your character's age, being born during one of those 1 percent exact alignments would be a very strong piece of local color for an astronomer's backstory.

I'm drafting a half-elven noble from Jia—very much in the Keyberos inner circle—and I was looking at the situation in Rathakos. My DM mentioned someone named John Dunne is the 'Regent' there. Honestly, it sounds like a mess. Is he just some commoner holding a seat for a prince that doesn't exist? It feels like Rathakos is just a failed state waiting to be re-absorbed by Edrad.

From the perspective of an Edran noble in Jia, that skepticism is very well-founded. John Dunne is indeed the Regent of Rathakos, and the state is currently a 'Princedom' without a Prince. He was appointed by the Settlements of Ath'r to govern while a Grand Council deliberates on finding a suitable monarch. The campaign notes don't specify his lineage or background—that is one of the 'blank spaces'—but his authority is definitely precarious. Rathakos only exists because the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction of the former Red Mountain Republic. To someone from the Keyberos dynasty, he might look less like a legitimate ruler and more like the man left standing after a coup.

Exactly! If he's the result of a 'purge' of pro-Edran people, then as a half-elf from the capital, I probably find him pretty distasteful. Is he actually doing anything about the 'internal unrest' the notes mention, or is he just hiding in Rath? It says the Southern Reaches were annexed from Edrad... that's stolen land.

That annexation is a major source of the unrest Dunne is dealing with. The 'Southern Reaches' are populated by Edran-speakers who aren't exactly thrilled with Rathakos's independence. Between those pro-Edran regions and the mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults, Dunne is essentially trying to hold together a country that is pulling itself apart. The notes don't describe him as 'hiding,' but they do emphasize that the state is under extreme stress. Whether he is a capable leader or just a bureaucrat struggling with a 'tenuous independence' isn't explicitly defined, which gives you a lot of room to decide how your PC views him—likely as a squatter on Edran territory.

It's interesting that he's a 'Regent' specifically. If they're looking for a Prince, and the land used to be Edran, could a Jia noble actually have a claim? Or did the Settlements of Ath'r explicitly block that?

The notes don't list the specific criteria the Grand Council is using to find a monarch, but we do know the political climate is hostile to Edran influence. Remember, the Red Mountain Republic was under Edran occupation until guerrilla resistance and the Ryraxian war forced a withdrawal. The current government in Rathakos was born from a faction that explicitly rejected Edran ties. While the campaign notes don't say the Settlements of Ath'r legally 'blocked' Edran claims, the fact that they are currently struggling with unrest in the 'annexed' Edran regions suggests they are looking for someone who represents their independence, not a return to the Keyberos fold. Regent Dunne is the placeholder for that independent identity, however fragile it may be.

Hey! I am rolling up a character for the Qaringil prequel game. I want to be a member of the Gondolier guild, but I noticed the notes mention some 'blank spaces' on the map. Am I allowed to actually fill one of those in with my own backstory stuff?

Yes, absolutely. The campaign notes specifically state that the DM has left blank spaces on the map for exactly that purpose—to be filled in with player backstories. Since you're looking at the Qaringil era (about 200 years before the Qamis present), there are plenty of gaps. For a member of the Gondolier guild in the city of Qaringil, you might look at inventing a specific island in the central sea or adding details to the less-defined parts of the 'Westron' continent. The Gondolier guild is a great choice, by the way; they manage the city's canals and the Great Western Bridge.

That is awesome. I was thinking my character's family originally came from an island that isn't on the map yet. The notes say the islands are mostly 'thassolocracies of nautical robber barons'—does my home island have to fit that vibe, or can I make it something totally different since it's a blank space?

You have a lot of room to play with. While the notes describe the general trend of the islands as being populated by exiles and mercenary navies, it also explicitly says 'many' are thassolocracies, not all. If you want your home island to have a different social structure or a unique culture, that fits perfectly into a blank space. Just keep in mind that the primary language for islanders is 'Islas.' Also, if you name your island using any terms from Old Draconic or the Qis language, remember that 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh.' Other than that, feel free to pitch a specific location or culture to your DM!

Look, I missed the session last night. The guys are playing in this homebrew setting 'Qamis' instead of a classic module. All these 'Q' names without a 'U' following them—Qis, Qaringil, Qama—are just making the notes hard to read. Is it just some gimmick? Anyway, I need a recap of what's going on so I can jump back in.

In this setting, the 'Q' without a 'U' is a convention from Old Draconic (or Old Qis) and is actually pronounced like a 'Kh' sound. So 'Qamis' is pronounced 'Khamis.' It's the standard for scholarly treaties and ancient place names. Regarding your recap, I'll assume you're playing in the 'Qamis' era, which is the present day. The thirty-year Ryraxian War has just concluded. The dragon Ryraxos, who once ruled Edrad as an overlord, has been defeated and exiled to Secundus—the second of Qaia's moons. The Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in the capital city of Jia, and the continent is trying to return to its historical borders, though things are still very unstable.
Wait, the dragon is on a moon? And what was this about him being an artillery commander? That sounds like some weird modern tactical nonsense for a D&D game.

According to the campaign notes, Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who wasn't actually an ancient, god-like wyrm. He was relatively young—about sixty—and formidable but beatable. He gained power initially as an artillery officer because dragons are uniquely suited for the job; they can carry the heavy guns themselves.
