Interestingly, the Gondolier Guild and the Wool Guild are unique because they have never been led by any of the 'Five Families' (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli). 

For local color: Qaringil is a city of 100,000 people criss-crossed with canals. The primary language is Islas, but because the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, the local dialect has a lot of Qis vocabulary. Remember that Q is always pronounced like 'Kh'. Unlike the Holy Qis Empire inland, where the elites are often landholders, the elites in Qaringil are almost entirely commercial and mercantile. It is a republic with an 'incomprehensible' political system currently led by Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, who is quite old and reportedly near death. It is a place of guilds, trade, and rising merchant families rather than traditional feudal lords.

Hey, I'm building a character from Forthyr for a Qamis campaign. I want to be part of that religious sect that's calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. I've been reading about this Ryraxos guy who just got exiled—what color dragon is he? And since my group wants the dragons back, did we see him as a savior during the war?

In the Qamis setting, Ryraxos is a bronze dragon. He is relatively young, likely around sixty years old, and rose to power initially as an artillery officer. As for your sect's view of him, the notes don't explicitly state whether the followers of the Dragon Emperor revival supported Ryraxos specifically. They do mention that the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr gives tacit encouragement to your sect. However, since the war ended with Ryraxos being defeated and exiled to Secundus (the second moon), there might be a divide in your religion over whether he was a true Emperor or just a powerful pretender. The campaign notes leave that specific political relationship as a blank space for you and the DM to define.

Interesting. If he's on the second moon, can I actually see him from the ground? Also, my character spent time in the Red Mountains looking for signs of the old Draconic empire. Are there other dragons hiding there, or maybe some hidden vaults with eggs?

You can see the moon he is on, but not Ryraxos himself. Secundus appears in the sky as a dark, iron-rich disc, about 90 percent the size of Earth's moon but much dimmer due to its low albedo. Regarding the Red Mountains, the notes describe them as one of the most dangerous and underexplored regions in Qamis. While it mentions that some mountain communities still follow the 'old dragon cults,' it does not confirm the existence of other living dragons, vaults, or eggs there. That remains a blank space. The only other mention of where dragons might be is a rumor about the large, unexplored island to the south beyond Freeport, where some say the dragons retreated.
What about the continent of Westron? I was thinking my character might have sailed there to find scrolls about the Dragon Emperors. Is there a big dragon population there or any ancient Qis ruins?

Westron is almost entirely a blank space in the current records. The notes only describe it as a very large continent to the west where the people generally do not worship Bahumat. There is no information regarding its dragon population or the presence of ancient Qis ruins. If you want your character to have found scrolls there, you would need to coordinate that with the DM, as the details of that continent have not been filled in yet. Most of the known 'Old Qis' or Old Draconic influence is centered in the heartland of Qis'mos and the city of Ath'r on the main continent of Qamis.

I am looking at the lore for my character from Rathakos, but I am a bit skeptical about this 'Red Mountain Republic' mentioned in the history. It sounds like Edran propaganda to make an occupation look like a legitimate government. Was it even a real thing?

Your skepticism is actually very grounded in the setting's history. In the present-day Qamis era, the Red Mountain Republic was exactly that: a political entity organized under Edran occupation after the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire. It was essentially a transitional state managed by Edran interests. The local mountain communities didn't all accept it, which is why the Red Mountain Assembly eventually purged the pro-Edran elements and split the territory into the independent nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. So, while it was 'real' in a legal sense, many in Rathakos today view it as a period of foreign meddling.

Okay, that makes more sense. I also saw a mention of a gnomish family called Tiddwar in some notes. If they are big in the merchant scene, did they have a hand in that Republic era? Or are they just another local clan in Rathakos?

The Tiddwar family actually belongs to a different time and place. They are prominent in the 'Qaringil' prequel era, which is set about 200 years before the current Qamis campaign. They aren't from the Red Mountains; they are a gnomish family based in the island city-state of Qaringil. In the present day of Rathakos, there are no specific records of the Tiddwars in the notes, though since the DM has left some 'blank spaces' in the regional lore, you could potentially discuss a distant branch with them.

A gnomish family ruling a major guild in an elven-dominated world 200 years ago? That sounds like a bit of a stretch. How did a bunch of gnomes manage to push out the big human and half-elf families like the Qantos?

It was a result of very specific political maneuvering in the city of Qaringil (remember, Q is pronounced 'Kh'). At the time, the city was a complex republic. The Tiddwar family managed to align themselves perfectly with the long-reigning Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro. The Qanto family—one of the legendary 'Five Families' of the city—had traditionally led the Mercantile Guild, but they fell out of favor. The Tiddwars were seen as more loyal to the Exarch's administration, and with his backing, they successfully seized leadership of the guild. It wasn't just about wealth; it was about being the right allies for a ruler who had been in power for nearly twenty years and wanted to consolidate his influence against the old nobility.

Hey. Looking for a high-level executive summary on Forthyr. My DM mentioned some cult there wanting to bring back 'Dragon Emperors,' but it sounds like a standard fantasy trope. Is there any actual political depth here or is it just 'evil cult #42'?

It is less about 'evil for evil's sake' and more about a dynasty managing legacy debt. Forthyr is an enormous nation in the northeast of Qamis. It is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty—currently Shanlong VI in the present day, or Shanlong I if your campaign is in the Qaringil prequel era.

The core issue is that the Dragoncallers are sorcerer-kings who claim legitimacy through their dragonborn blood. However, over the centuries, those bloodlines have run thin. To keep the population in line in a relatively 'backward' country, the dynasty actually gives tacit encouragement to a religious sect calling for the return of the original Dragon Emperors. It is a calculated move: by promoting the myth of the ancient dragons, the current kings reinforce the idea that dragon-blood is the only valid source of authority, even as their own power wanes.

So the 'Dragoncaller' supports the people who want to replace him with a bigger boss? That feels like a buggy strategy. Why would a ruler encourage a group looking for a 'Return of the King' event that might actually happen?

From the dynasty's perspective, it's a security patch for their failing legitimacy. If they suppress the sect, they are essentially admitting that the 'Dragon' era is over, which would invalidate their own right to rule. By supporting the sect, they position themselves as the 'stewards' of that ancient power until the 'true' emperors return—which, for several centuries, they likely assumed would never actually happen.

There is also a regional linguistic note: Q is pronounced 'Kh' in this world, so names like Qamis or Qis'mos follow that rule, coming from Old Draconic. Forthyr itself is somewhat isolated, and the DM's notes suggest this sect is one of the few things keeping the social fabric together. It's essentially state-sponsored nostalgia used to maintain a status quo, even if the 'Return' they pray for would technically put the current Shanlong out of a job.
Got it. So it's a 'controlled opposition' play. Does this relate to the dragon Ryraxos at all? He's the big one everyone talks about, right?

Actually, no. Ryraxos is a very recent figure—a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander during the thirty-year war that just ended in the Qamis era. He was eventually exiled to Secundus, the second moon. 

The 'Dragon Emperors' the Forthrian sect worships are much older, quasi-divine figures from the deep past. Ryraxos was just a formidable, relatively young dragon (around sixty years old) who capitalized on the chaos in the west. The Forthrian cult likely wouldn't even recognize Ryraxos as one of their 'Emperors'—he's more of a modern warlord than a primordial deity. If you are playing in the Qaringil prequel, Ryraxos hasn't even appeared on the scene yet.

Hey, I am a DM and I love the idea of your geostationary moon, Primus. I am thinking of using something similar for my homebrew. What are the actual visibility limits for people on the ground? Like, how far away from the center point can you still see it before it dips below the horizon?

In the Qamis setting, Primus is fixed directly over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0 degrees N, 0 degrees E). Because of its orbital height, the maximum geocentric angle from which it remains visible is approximately 81.3 degrees. Practically, this means if you are at the equator, you can see Primus up to roughly 81 degrees east or west of the prime meridian. However, as you move toward higher latitudes, that visibility window narrows; at 60 degrees latitude, for example, the moon is only visible within about 72 degrees of longitude. Once an observer crosses into the far-side hemisphere, Primus is permanently below the horizon. Navigators near that 81-degree boundary also have to account for atmospheric refraction and low-horizon distortion, which makes the moon less reliable for precise longitude fixes than when it is positioned higher in the sky.

helo nanochat! I am starting a new campaign in Qaringil and I am making a character from the Peixota family. I heard the night sky is crazy there. How many moons are we talking about and what are their names?

Welcome to Qaringil! Since your character is from the Peixota family, you likely grew up around the shipping guilds and the great canals, where the moons are vital for navigation. Qaia has six main moons, plus a trace particle. They are named using the Old Draconic numbering: Primus (the first), Secundus (the second), Tertius (the third), Quartus (the fourth), Sextus (the sixth), and Septimus (the seventh). There is also a trace particle called Quintus (the fifth) which is effectively massless and sits far away at a stable point ahead of the planet's orbit.
That is a lot to keep track of. Are they all the same size? Like, do they all look like our moon or are some just tiny dots? And why is there no proper fifth moon?

They vary significantly. Tertius and Quartus are the 'big' moons in the sky. Tertius is actually slightly larger than Earth's moon and defines the eight-day week in Qaringil. Quartus is almost an exact twin of Earth's moon and sets the monthly calendar. Secundus is nearly as large as the others but is much darker because it is made of iron-rich rock, so it looks dimmer. Primus is small (about one-third the size of our moon) and quite faint. Sextus and Septimus are much farther away; they look like very bright, small discs rather than full moons. As for the fifth moon, the records show that Quintus is just a cluster of trace particles at the L4 point—essentially a 'blank space' in the physical sequence where a moon didn't fully form.
My character works on a shipping vessel for the family. How do we actually use all these moons to get around the Central Sea? Is it just for light or do they help with steering?

For a Peixota sailor, the moons are better than any clock. In Qaringil, you have a massive advantage: Primus. Because Primus is geostationary, it is magically anchored in the sky. It never rises or sets; it stays at one fixed point above the equator. By measuring how high Primus is above the horizon (its elevation) and whether it is east or west of you, you can calculate your longitude exactly without needing a chronometer. You find your latitude using the pole star, then use Primus to figure out exactly how far east or west you are from the prime meridian. This makes navigation around the islands much safer for the Qaringil fleets compared to people on the far side of the world who can't see Primus at all.
Wait, you said some rise in the west? How does that work if they are moons?

It can be disorienting! Most moons are prograde, meaning they move west-to-east like Tertius and Quartus. However, Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are retrograde. If you are standing on the docks in Qaringil, you will see Tertius and Quartus moving one way, while Secundus rises in the west and sweeps across the sky in the opposite direction. Meanwhile, Primus just hangs there, perfectly still. It makes for a very busy sky, especially during triple-moon alignments when the tides get particularly strong—about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's moon.

Alright, look. I've played in enough homebrew worlds to know the cosmology is usually just flavor text that doesn't actually work if you look at the math. I heard this setting has some weird triple-moon alignment thing. Is it just a random table for the DM, or is there an actual cycle?

It is a mathematically consistent system based on the orbital periods of the three inner moons: Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. While they each have their own months, they periodically align in syzygy. These alignments are frequent enough to be a core part of the calendar and the culture, but they don't happen at the same time every year.
Syzygy, sure. But I heard something about 'eras'. Like, it's not just a one-off alignment. Is there some kind of long-term pattern?

Correct. The system experiences alternating alignment eras. Because the orbital periods are incommensurate, the phase relationship between the moons drifts over time. This creates multi-year blocks where triple-alignments occur as 'Triple-Full' moons, followed by blocks where they occur as 'Triple-New' moons. For example, in the current Qamis era, the years 2253 to 2254 are a full-moon era, while 2255 to 2258 are a new-moon era.
So it's not just random. How long does a full cycle of this take? If I'm playing a long campaign, is the sky going to look the same the whole time?

A full cycle—moving from a full-moon era through a new-moon era and back to the start—takes approximately 12 years. Within that cycle, you will see the three moons align within 5 percent of each other about five times a year. Truly exact alignments, where all three are within 1 percent of full or new, happen roughly once per year. The 'dominant' phase for those alignments switches every few years.
Twelve years. That's specific. Why the flip-flop between full and new? Usually you just get a conjunction or you don't.

It comes down to the relative speeds. Secundus is the fastest with a 4.53-day period, Tertius takes 8.15 days, and Quartus takes 29.5 days. Because their periods aren't simple multiples, the point in their orbits where they 'catch up' to each other rotates relative to the sun. During one part of the 12-year cycle, they catch up to each other on the side of Qaia facing away from the sun (Triple-Full). As the years pass, that meeting point drifts until they are catching up on the side facing the sun (Triple-New).
And let me guess, the DM hasn't filled in what happens during a Triple-Full alignment yet?

The notes don't specify any mechanical effects or magical surges associated with the alignments; it is currently documented as a celestial and chronological phenomenon. However, the 12-year cycle and specific dates—like the closest triple-new of the decade on May 24, 2262—are hard-coded into the setting's history.

I'm playing a drow who stayed behind in Qama after the Ryraxian War. Looking at the night sky is still a bit weird for me. In other settings like the Forgotten Realms, you just have Selune and the Tears, but here there are so many moons. Which one is actually the brightest when it's full, and which one is the smallest or dimmest? I need to know what I'm looking at when I'm out on the surface.

It is quite a transition from the Underdark or even from a single-moon world like Toril. Qaia has six major moons and one trace particle (Quintus). 

The brightest moon in the sky is Quartus. It has a magnitude of -12.64 when full, which is almost exactly the same brightness as Earth's Moon (Luna). It's the standard for the calendar and provides about 0.9 times the illumination of a Terran full moon. 

The dimmest of the recognized moons is Septimus. It's much farther out and has a magnitude of only -7.14. While that is still significantly brighter than any planet (like Jupiter or Venus), it provides only about 1/167th of the light of a standard full moon. It looks like a tiny, dim disc, roughly 0.09 times the size of a standard moon.

Since you are in Qama (pronounced Kha-ma), you are also in the hemisphere that can see Primus, the geostationary moon. It is small (10.4 arcminutes) and relatively faint (-9.62), but it never moves from its spot in the sky.

That sounds a bit like the moons of Krynn from Dragonlance, but more complicated. You mentioned Primus is 'geostationary.' Does that mean it just hangs there like a hovering wizard tower? And how does it compare to the others in size? If I'm used to seeing the big ones, is Primus going to be hard to spot?

Exactly. Because Primus is magically anchored and its orbital period matches the planet's rotation, it never rises or sets. If you can see it from your house in Qama, it will always be in that exact same spot, night or day. 

In terms of size, it's a bit of a 'middle child' for the eyes. The 'big three' that dominate the sky are Tertius (the largest at 33.7 arcminutes), Quartus (29.6 arcminutes), and Secundus (28.0 arcminutes). Primus is only 10.4 arcminutes across, which is about one-third the size of the others. It's not hard to spot—it's still a visible disc and quite bright compared to the stars—but it's much smaller and darker because it's made of iron-rich material with a low albedo.

One thing that might confuse a navigator from another world is that while Tertius and Quartus move West-to-East (prograde) like you'd expect, Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are retrograde. They rise in the West and set in the East.

Retrograde moons rising in the West? That's wild. It makes the 'Tears of Selune' look simple. So if I'm trying to find my way back to Qama at night, which moon gives me the most light to work with if they're all out? Is it just Quartus, or does Tertius put out more light because it's physically larger in the sky?

It's a close contest. Even though Tertius has the largest apparent diameter (33.7 arcminutes), it is made of darker rock (albedo 0.09) than Quartus (albedo 0.12). 

Because of that difference in reflectivity, Quartus actually edges out Tertius for the title of 'brightest.' Quartus reflects about 91 percent of the light of a Terran moon, while Tertius reflects about 88 percent. If both are full, they will illuminate the landscape of Qis'mos quite vividly. 

However, the total tidal force is the real difference. Between Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus, the tidal pull on Qaia is about 4.4 times what you'd find on Earth. If you're near the coast or the marshes of Qis'mos, you'll notice the water levels shifting much more dramatically than in other settings.

Hey! My DM mentioned Qama is a huge deal for the setting. What is the lore 101 on it? I am thinking of playing a Cleric of Bahamut—or Bahumat as I think you guys spell it—and I want to know if there are specific factions or mechanical hooks linked to the city.

In the Qamis setting, Qama is the holy city of the Church of Bahumat, located in the nation of Qis'mos. Its importance right now is defined by a major religious schism. During the late phases of the thirty-year Ryraxian War, the drow—who were surface allies of Artkos—occupied the city. They are still there, and they have actually appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This led to a split: there is a rival candidate named Qachu II who operates from the city of Qis, but he does not have access to the holy relics of office still held in Qama. If you are playing a Cleric or Paladin, your primary hook is deciding which side of this schism your character supports, or if you are trying to reconcile them.

That schism sounds like prime roleplay fuel. What is the vibe with the drow there? Usually, they are the big bads in TTRPGs, but here they were allies? Does that mean I could be a Drow Cleric of Bahumat actually in the holy city without getting smote on sight? Also, are there any unique mechanics for the relics?

You definitely could. In Qamis, the drow were recruited by Artkos out of desperation and they remain on the surface in scattered enclaves. In Qama, they haven't despoiled the city; they have just taken over the ecclesiastical hierarchy. Playing a Drow Cleric of Bahumat would be a very standard fit for the Qama faction. Regarding mechanics for the relics, the campaign notes mention their importance for legitimacy, but the specific stats or powers of the relics are currently 'blank spaces' left for the DM to define. One thing to keep in mind is the language: you wouldn't speak 'Elvish' because there are no racial languages. You would likely speak Qis, which is the national tongue of the region and very similar to Old Draconic, the language of the academy.
Good to know about the languages. One last thing—is Qama just as important in the Qaringil prequel, or is it a newer thing?

It is just as important! In the Qaringil era, roughly 200 years before the current setting, Qama was already the traditional seat of the Church of Bahumat. The big difference is that Qis'mos wasn't a unified nation yet; it was a geographic and cultural region, and the drow had not yet arrived on the surface. So in a Qaringil campaign, Qama would be the undisputed center of the faith without the drow influence or the current schism between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II.

Hi! I'm thinking of playing a navigator or an astronomer in the Qamis era. I'm absolutely fascinated by the moon system you've got here—4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's moon is incredible! I was looking at Jia as a potential home base since it's the cultural capital and sits right on the Central Sea. How does the city's culture or architecture handle those massive tides, and is it a good spot to observe Primus?

Jia (pronounced 'Khia' if we follow the Old Qis 'Q' rule, though it's spelled with a J) is definitely the place for a high-society astronomer. It is the seat of the Keyberos dynasty and the most culturally influential city in Qamis. However, to be honest about the campaign notes, while we know it sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea, the DM hasn't specifically detailed how the architecture handles the 4.4x tidal load. You can imagine massive sea walls or water-adaptive docks, but those specific 'blank spaces' are yours to help fill in. Regarding Primus: since it's geostationary over the prime meridian, its visibility depends on Jia's exact longitude. The notes don't give Jia's precise coordinates, but as it's the center of the Edran world, it's likely on the near-side hemisphere where Primus is a permanent, unmoving fixture in the sky.

That makes sense. If the coordinates are a blank space, I'll talk to the DM about placing it somewhere with a high Primus elevation for easy navigation. Does the 'cultural influence' of Jia extend to celestial research? Like, do they have a 'Royal Observatory' that has mapped out Sextus and Septimus, or is that knowledge held elsewhere? Also, since the notes mention the drow are in Qama now, did they bring any subterranean astronomical secrets with them?

The notes don't explicitly mention a 'Royal Observatory' in Jia, though it would fit the city's vibe as a center of prestige. Most scholarly publications and treaties are written in Old Draconic (Old Qis), so the intellectual infrastructure is definitely there. As for the drow in Qama, the notes mention they've appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV, at the holy city of Bahumat, but their specific 'astronomical secrets' haven't been defined yet. It's another one of those blank spaces. We know they were recruited by Artkos as allies, but their cultural impact on surface-level science is currently unmapped.

Fair enough. What about the distant stuff? Does Jia's elite have records of 'Westron' or that big southern island from a navigational standpoint? I'm curious if the triple-moon alignments—like the big triple-full moon back in 2253—are celebrated as holidays in Edrad, or if that's more of a Qis'mos religious thing.

Westron is a major blank space—the notes literally say it's 'exotic or some shit' and 'very large,' but no celestial or cultural details are provided. Similarly, the big island to the south (near Freeport) is 'mostly unexplored.' Regarding the triple-moon alignments, the notes describe the 12-year cycle of 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras' very clearly, but they don't list specific Edran holidays for them. Given the Church of Bahamut's massive presence in Qis'mos, those alignments likely carry much more weight there than in the more secular, half-elven nobility circles of Jia. If you want to tie your character's backstory to a specific alignment—like the upcoming triple-new moon on May 24, 2262—you'd be helping define how the world reacts to those events!

I've been playing since the Greyhawk folio days, so I've seen every 'special' moon gimmick in the book. This 'Primus' moon in your setting—it's geostationary? In a pre-industrial setting, that usually just means the DM wanted a GPS satellite without doing the math. How does a rock that close not just spiral into the planet or fly off?

You're right to be skeptical, but the math for Primus is quite specific. It sits at an orbital radius of 0.11 LD, which is roughly 42,160 km from Qaia's center. Its orbital period is exactly one Qaian sidereal day, and its inclination of 23.5 degrees perfectly cancels out the planet's axial tilt, keeping it locked over the equator. The campaign notes explicitly describe it as 'magically anchored.' This means it experiences zero tidal torque and no orbital migration, which is why it doesn't spiral in like the retrograde moon Secundus eventually will.

Magically anchored? That's the hand-waving I was talking about. If it's that close and has double the density of our moon—near-iron according to your notes—the tidal forces should be a nightmare. You're telling me it just sits there and doesn't cause a daily apocalypse?

It is dense—6,689 kg per cubic meter—but it is also tiny, only 0.0001 times the mass of Earth's Moon. Because it is geostationary and co-rotates with the planet, it doesn't 'sweep' across the sky like a normal moon. Therefore, it doesn't create an oscillating tide. Instead, it generates a permanent static bulge in the oceans of about 2 centimeters. It's a constant geographic feature of the sea level along the Primus-antiprimus axis, rather than a cycle of rising and falling water.

A 2-centimeter permanent bulge? Fine, I'll give you the physics on the water. But 'magically anchored' implies intent. The notes mention the old Draconic empire and this Ryraxos dragon. Did the dragons build this thing to solve the longitude problem, or is it a natural object they just pinned to the sky?

The notes describe it as 'circular by construction,' which strongly implies it isn't a natural capture. While the text doesn't explicitly name a specific wizard or dragon who 'pinned' it, the language of the 'Old Qis'—pronounced 'Kh'—suggests it dates back to the era when Draconic institutions were at their peak. It functions as a perfect longitude reference because its sub-satellite point is fixed at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude. Navigators just measure its elevation. As for the 'anchor' breaking, the notes mention that the system's long-term fate without 'dragon magic' is an open question, suggesting the magic is what maintains the equilibrium against the 4.4x lunar tidal load caused by the other moons.

Okay, honestly, having a seven-moon system feels like a lot of homework for a D&D session. It is objectively too complicated. But I was looking at the list and Sextus and Septimus caught my eye. Are they really identical twins just hanging out in the outer orbit? That is actually kind of a cool detail.

They really are! It is one of the most symmetrical parts of the sky. Both Sextus and Septimus are physically identical in the notes: they have the same mass (0.010 times Earth's Moon), the same radius (374 km), and the same density and dark rocky surface (albedo 0.09). They are basically cosmic clones, just placed at different distances from the planet.

Wait, the notes say they are both retrograde too? So if I am standing in Rathakos at night, I am watching these two identical little dots moving the 'wrong' way across the sky while the big ones go the other way? That is a wild visual.

It is a fantastic visual. While the 'standard' prograde moons like Tertius and Quartus move west-to-east, Sextus and Septimus rise in the west and sweep toward the east. Sextus looks like a small disc in the sky—about five times the apparent diameter of Jupiter from Earth—while Septimus is slightly smaller and dimmer because it is further out (2.47 LD versus 1.64 LD). Even though they are tiny compared to the inner moons, they are unmistakable because of that backwards motion. Septimus also has a very long orbit of about 106 days, making it a great natural marker for long-term calendars!

I'm a member of the Gondolier guild in Qaringil, so I spend my days on the canals listening to the merchants gossip. They're always talking about Edrad. What's the deal with that place? Is it actually as big and fancy as they say?

In the Qaringil era—about 200 years before the 'present' Qamis setting—Edrad is indeed the most dominant political and economic power on the continent. It's famous for its half-elven nobility and its capital, Jia, which sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea. If you're hearing merchants talk, they probably view Jia as the height of culture. The Keyberos dynasty has been on the throne for about two centuries at this point in your campaign's history, and they are steadily expanding their royal control.
A half-elven nobility? That explains the fancy talk. Is it true they rule the whole coast, or are there places they haven't touched yet?

They don't rule everything just yet. In the Qaringil era, a major goal of Edran foreign policy is conquering Qeth and the region to its northeast, which they don't yet control. Their nobility is strictly defined: to be a noble, you must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. While they are powerful, they share the continent with the Holy Qis (pronounced 'Kh-is') Empire, which rules from Ath'r in the central lands. It's a very different map than what people see 200 years later.
Wait, what happens 200 years later? One of the older sailors mentioned a 'Ryraxian War' and a dragon, but I thought he was just drunk on grog.

Your sailor friend is actually describing the 'present day' of the Qamis setting. Between your time in Qaringil and the Qamis era, things get chaotic. The Keyberos dynasty eventually faces a massive disruption: first by a Revolutionary Council that deposes them, and then by a bronze dragon named Ryraxos who rules as an overlord. A thirty-year war followed, involving almost every nation. By the end of it, the dragon Ryraxos was defeated and exiled to Secundus (the second moon), and the Keyberos family was finally restored to the throne in Jia. So in the 'future' from your perspective, Edrad has survived a thirty-year nightmare, but it's back to its historical borders.
Exiled to the moon? That's wild. So if I were living in the Qamis 'present' instead of the Qaringil prequel, would Edrad look the same to a common guild member like me?

Visually and culturally, Jia remains the most influential city, but the political vibe is much more tense in the Qamis present. While the Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne, they find it much harder to enforce their old edicts after thirty years of war and revolution. Also, the map around them has changed. New nations like Rathakos and Acrokos have appeared in the Red Mountains—places that don't even exist yet in your Qaringil era. In your time, you're dealing with the Holy Qis Empire; in the present day, that empire is long gone, replaced by a fractured Artklan League.

Hey, I'm playing a drow who stayed behind on the surface in Qama after the Ryraxian War. I'm trying to wrap my head around how people actually find their way around up here. I saw a note about 'two celestial anchors' for navigation—what does that actually mean in practice?

For a drow used to navigating by the steady heat-signatures and landmarks of the Underdark, the surface sky must be quite the adjustment. The 'two celestial anchors' refer to the Pole Star and the moon Primus. Together, they allow any navigator on the 'near-side' hemisphere to find their exact latitude and longitude just by looking at the sky. The Pole Star stays fixed above the north (or south) pole, so its elevation above your horizon tells you your latitude. Primus is the real trick, though—it's geostationary.
Wait, a moon that doesn't move? That sounds unsettling. Every other moon I see seems to be racing across the sky at different speeds. How is Primus just... hanging there?

It is unique. Primus is 'magically anchored' in an orbit that exactly matches Qaia's rotation. Because its 23.5-degree orbital inclination perfectly cancels out the planet's axial tilt, it sits motionless over a single spot on the equator (the Prime Meridian). To an observer, it looks like a small, dark iron-density disc—about a third of the size of Earth's Moon—that never rises or sets. It is a permanent landmark in the heavens.
So if I'm at sea or in the desert, I just look at how high Primus is to figure out my east-to-west position? I heard there's some kind of math involved, like a 'primsine' function.

Exactly. Because Primus is at a fixed altitude of about 35,800 km, its elevation 'e' above your horizon is a direct geometric indicator of your distance from the Prime Meridian. Navigators use the 'primsine(e)' function, which accounts for the curvature of the planet. The formula is: cos Longitude = primsine(e) / cos Latitude. In practice, sailors carry a nautical almanac with a precomputed primsine table. You look up the value for your elevation, divide by the cosine of your latitude (from the Pole Star), and you have your longitude.
That's incredible, but you mentioned 'near-side' earlier. If my character travels too far away from the center of the continent, do we lose that anchor? What happens on the other side of the world?

That is the great navigational divide of Qaia. Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you travel more than roughly 80 degrees away from the Prime Meridian, Primus dips below the horizon and never returns. For the 'far-side' hemisphere, longitude is a nightmare. They have to rely on dead reckoning or the 'lunar distance' method—measuring the fast-moving moons like Secundus or Quartus against background stars—which is significantly harder and requires a precise chronometer or complex charts.
Speaking of the other moons, if Primus is fixed, it doesn't cause tides, right? I've seen the oceans here—they are much more chaotic than the Sunless Seas. Who is doing all that pulling?

Correct—Primus only creates a permanent, stationary 2 cm bulge in the water. The real tidal chaos comes from Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Together, they exert about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's moon. Because Secundus is retrograde (it rises in the west!) while Tertius and Quartus are prograde, and they all have different periods (4.5 days, 8 days, and 29.5 days respectively), the tides on Qaia are a complex, shifting rhythm that can be very difficult to predict without an almanac.
It's a lot to keep track of. I've also heard the priests in Qama talking about 'Triple-moon alignments' as omens. Are those common, or is it just religious talk?

It is based on very real astronomy. About once a year, the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) align within 1% of each other. The world goes through 12-year cycles of 'Full-Moon eras' and 'New-Moon eras.' We actually have a very rare alignment coming up: on May 24, 2262, there will be a 'triple-new' moon where all three are within 0.1% of syzygy. For the drow in Qama, especially with the current schism between High Priest Qe'lth IV and the claimant Qachu II, an event that brings total darkness across all three moons would certainly be interpreted as a powerful sign.

Hi, I just started looking into Qamis because a friend mentioned it. He was telling me about this dragon Ryraxos who basically ran the continent for thirty years, but then he said the dragon 'wasn't truly that impressive.' As someone who reads a lot of epic fantasy, that sounds like a massive contradiction. How does a mediocre dragon conquer the world?

It's a fair question. In many fantasy settings, dragons are god-like forces of nature, but Qamis treats them a bit more pragmatically. Ryraxos is a bronze dragon and quite young—only about sixty years old. To put that in perspective, the campaign notes mention he is less formidable than a well-commanded artillery battery or a competent party of adventurers. He isn't a mountain-sized beast that's immune to everything; he's a powerful, mortal creature.
If he's just 'an artillery battery' with wings, how did he win a thirty-year war against an empire like Edrad? That still sounds like he'd need to be unstoppable to pull that off.

His success was more about tactical brilliance and timing than raw, magical muscle. Ryraxos actually started his rise to power as an artillery officer. In this setting, dragons are highly valued in the military because they can shape-shift and are physically strong enough to help transport heavy guns to places humanoids can't easily reach. He used those logistical advantages and his mind for strategy to save Edrad from a combined invasion by Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire. He didn't win because he was an invincible monster; he won because he was a genius commander who knew how to use the tools of modern war.

So he was a general who happened to be a dragon. I guess that's different. But if he wasn't a world-ending threat, why did they exile him to the second moon? Sending someone to space feels like the kind of thing you do to a dark god, not a 'young' bronze dragon.

The exile to Secundus was a political solution to end a thirty-year stalemate. After two and a half decades of aggressive expansion, Ryraxos was eventually defeated, and the restoration of the Keyberos dynasty required him to be removed from the board entirely. Accepting exile was a way for him to survive while allowing the continent to return to its 'historical lines.' It's also worth noting that the 'Q' in names like Qamis or Qaringil is pronounced like 'Kh,' so the world feels very tied to that Old Draconic history, making his presence—and his departure—highly symbolic for the people living there.

I am thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil prequel setting. Is the material pretty much limited to the city itself, or can I branch out into the surrounding nations like Westron or the southern island?

If you are looking for the most detail, the city of Qaringil is the focal point of that era. It is an island city-state on a wizard-made isthmus with a population of 100,000, governed by an incomprehensible republic and dominated by seven Great Guilds and five major families like the Peixota and Vitelli. Regarding your branching out, the notes explicitly label Westron and the large southern island as blank spaces. Westron is mentioned as a continent to the west where they mostly do not worship Bahumat, but the DM notes state no material has been prepared for it yet. Similarly, the southern island is described as mostly unexplored, with only the pirate city of Freeport on the shore and mystery in the jungle beyond. You could use these for your own homebrew hooks, but there is no official lore in the notes to guide you there.
That is fair. What about the Holy Qis Empire? The notes say they rule from Ath'r. Is there enough there to run a political session, or is that also a blank spot?

The Holy Qis Empire is more defined than Westron, but still less detailed than the city of Qaringil. In the prequel era, it rules central Qamis, Qirkos, and northern Qis'mos (remember that Q is pronounced Kh). You have a few solid political hooks: the Emperor is theoretically elected, and the empire is in a cold conflict with 'free cities' like Lock over their ancient prerogatives. However, the notes describe the empire as neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire, suggesting its authority might be fragile. If you need specific street maps or a list of NPCs for Ath'r, those are currently missing from the documentation. The Red Mountains are also claimed by the Empire but not effectively administered, being home to various non-state peoples.
Got it. I might stick to the city then. If I want to use the moon system for a navigation challenge at sea near the bridges, how much of that is established for the prequel?

The moon system and navigation rules are identical across both the Qaringil prequel and the Qamis present. You have a very robust system to work with. There are six moons plus a trace particle called Quintus. For navigation, your players can use Primus, the geostationary moon. Because it is magically anchored over the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude) on the equator, measuring its elevation tells a navigator their longitude without needing a chronometer. You can also find latitude by the pole star. The other moons like Secundus (retrograde), Tertius (prograde and the basis for the 8-day week), and Quartus (the calendar moon) provide a complex tidal system—roughly 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's moon. This would make the waters around the Qaringil bridges quite dynamic for a one-shot.

yo. I am digging through the campaign docs for the new Qamis game and trying to reconcile them with the Qaringil stuff. I am getting a bit twisted on the 'Q' terminology. Can you break down the difference between Qis, Qis'mos, and Old Qis? Also, is the Holy Qis Empire still a thing in the present day?

The 'Q' terminology (pronounced 'Kh') can be tricky because it covers languages, regions, and historical empires across both eras. In the present-day Qamis setting, 'Old Qis' (or Old Draconic) is the dead language of scholars and treaties; it is why many place names have a 'q' without a 'u'. 'Qis' is the modern national language, which is the closest living relative to Old Qis. 'Qismos' is the nation itself—the rich heartland governed by a Potentate. Regarding your second question: no, the Holy Qis Empire no longer exists. It is exclusive to the Qaringil prequel era (200 years ago), where it ruled from the city of Ath'r. In the present day, that central power has been replaced by Artkos and the Artklan League.

Got it. So Qismos in the present is a unified nation, but in Qaringil it was just a 'geographic region.' Does that mean there's no central government there at all in the prequel? And while we are at it, what's the deal with the '???' blank spaces near the Red Mountains? Are those supposed to be Q-affiliated too?

In the Qaringil era, Qismos is indeed described as a cultural region rather than a single state. The Holy Qis Empire rules the northern part of it, but the notes do not specify exactly who governs the southern portions during that time, beyond the fact that independent Potentates eventually emerge from the old draconic institutions. As for those '???' blank spaces near the Red Mountains, the campaign notes explicitly state these are areas the DM has not filled in yet. They aren't necessarily Q-affiliated; they are basically open slots for player backstories or future development. The notes are silent on their names, populations, or political alignments for now.

That makes sense. One more thing—in the present day, the notes mention Qama is the holy city of Bahumat but it's occupied by drow under 'Qe'lth IV'. Is Qe'lth an Old Qis name? And do we know anything about the drow enclaves outside of Qama, or is that another blank space?

The name Qe'lth certainly follows the Old Qis naming convention with the 'q' and the apostrophe, though the notes don't explicitly confirm its linguistic root. As for the drow, the notes state they are scattered in a 'dozen scattered enclaves' across the surface after being recruited as allies by Artkos during the Ryraxian War. However, beyond Qama being the most prominent one, the locations and names of the other eleven enclaves are currently blank spaces. The documentation hasn't provided details on where those other settlements are or how they are governed.

Good evening. I was reading through the orbital data for the Qaia system—the triple-moon alignments and that 4.4x lunar tidal force are fascinating. If I'm starting a game in the Qamis era, where exactly on the map does the story begin, and how much of this celestial math actually impacts the local geography there?

The Qamis campaign is expected to start in Rathakos. It is a mountainous border state located along the Red Mountains, situated between Edrad, Qismos, and Artkos. 

Regarding the celestial math, Rathakos is largely landlocked and high-altitude, so the massive 4.4x oscillating tidal load produced by Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus won't affect your immediate surroundings as much as it would a coastal city like Jia or Qaringil. However, the 8-day orbital period of Tertius provides the literal basis for the Qaian week, which governs the local calendar. Also, since Primus is geostationary and fixed over the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude), your view of it in Rathakos will be a permanent, motionless fixture in the sky, assuming you are on the correct hemisphere. The notes don't explicitly state the exact longitude of Rath, so we'd have to check with the DM to see if Primus hangs directly overhead or sits lower on the horizon for the start of the campaign.

Wait, if the Red Mountains are largely 'underexplored' as the notes say, is there any data on how those massive tidal forces interact with the tectonic stability of the range? With three inner moons and a retrograde one spiraling in, you'd expect some serious tidal heating or seismic activity. Is that why the mountains are so dangerous?

That is an insightful connection, but the campaign notes are currently a 'blank space' regarding tidal heating or specific seismic mechanics. The notes attribute the danger of the Red Mountains to them being underexplored, harboring mountain communities that follow old dragon cults, and containing various 'non-state peoples.' 

While we know Secundus is spiraling inward due to its retrograde orbit and the total tidal force is much higher than Earth's, the DM hasn't specified if this results in active volcanism or frequent earthquakes in the Red Mountains. If you wanted to incorporate tidal stress as a reason for the rugged terrain or the 'dangerous' nature of the region, that would be something to coordinate with the DM as part of the worldbuilding.
Fair enough. What about the 'blank spaces' on the map near Rathakos? Are there any specific moon-observatories or navigational landmarks mentioned for that region, or is that all unwritten too?

In the immediate vicinity of Rathakos, there are no specific observatories or celestial landmarks mentioned in the current documentation. The notes focus on the political transition—Rathakos was part of the Red Mountain Republic before the Edran withdrawal and is now governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a Prince. 

The 'blank spaces' mentioned in the notes are quite literal; the DM has invited players to help fill in the map with their backstories. This includes the Red Mountains themselves and areas beyond the known borders of Rathakos, Acrokos, and Lock. If you wanted your character to be from a specific peak or a hidden observatory used to calculate the 'primsine' function for longitude, the setting currently has the room for you to invent that.

yooo so my DM said for the Qaringil game we are starting at level 5. in the stuff i have seen like Baldurs Gate or the movie that seems like you are already basically a hero. do we get to be in cool groups like the Harpers or the Emerald Enclave or something? also how do i say the name of the city?

In the Qaringil campaign, being level 5 is quite significant. The notes mention that at this level, you are all eligible to be full guild members in any organization relevant to your class. However, this is a unique setting called Qaia, so you won't find the Harpers or the Emerald Enclave here. Those are from the Forgotten Realms. 

Instead, Qaringil is a city-state run by Great Guilds like the Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool guilds. If you are a spellcaster, you might be a full member of the Mage or Alchemist guilds. As for the name, it is pronounced Kah-ring-gil. The letter Q in this world (from Old Draconic) always makes a Kh sound.

Karingil, got it. so if i am a full member of the Shipping Guild am i like the boss? can i tell people what to do like a Noble in Waterdeep? and what about those Five Families i saw in the notes, are they like the Lannisters or something?

At level 5, you are a respected professional member, but you aren't the head of the guild. The leadership of the Great Guilds is usually dominated by the Five Families: Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli. For example, the Peixota family (sigil: Dolphin) leads the Shipping Guild. 

These families are the elite of Qaringil, though they are mostly merchant-focused rather than traditional land-owning royalty. They have human and elven ancestry, and they carry a lot of political weight. The city itself is currently led by Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, who has been in power for 19 years but is reportedly near death, which might create some openings for ambitious guild members.

that sounds cool. i am playing a rogue so i dont really want to be in a Silk guild lol. is there an evil one like the Zhentarim? and does this take place in the same time as that other campaign Rathakos?

There isn't a Zhentarim, but Qaringil does have underground criminal organizations that call themselves guilds, specifically the Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. They are illegal but very active. 

Regarding the timeline, this is a prequel set roughly 200 years before the Qamis/Rathakos campaign. In this era, Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist yet. The political map is very different; the Holy Qis Empire still rules from the city of Ath'r, and Edrad is much smaller, still trying to conquer the region around Qeth. Since you are in Qaringil, you are on a volcanic island between two massive bridges on the Central Sea, which is a very different vibe from the mountain border-states of the later era.
Hey, I am working on a character from Rathakos. I keep seeing mentions of a Red Mountain Republic in the history notes. Was that the old name for the country?

Not exactly an old name, but a very recent and specific phase of history. The Red Mountain Republic was formed during the Ryraxian War when the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the old Artklan Empire. While the region was under Edran occupation, the former mountain provinces were organized into that Republic. However, it did not last. When Edrad eventually withdrew, the Red Mountain Assembly had a bit of a power struggle; one faction purged the pro-Edran members and split the territory into the two nations you see now: Rathakos and Acrokos. So, the Republic was the short-lived predecessor to your character's home.
That makes sense. My character comes from a family that prides itself on guild history, though. Were the guilds in this region always tied to the high nobility, or were some more independent?

In the current era of Rathakos, the political situation is a bit messy because they are still looking for a Prince and are governed by Regent John Dunne. However, if you look back about 200 years to the Qaringil era, we have a very clear picture of how guilds and major families interacted. In the city-state of Qaringil, which was the trade hub of that age, there were seven Great Guilds and five dominant families: Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli. Most of these families held tight control over specific guilds. For example, the Peixota family leads the Shipping Guild, and the Vitelli family leads the Silk Guild.
Were there any guilds that the big families never managed to take over? I want my character's background to be from one of the more 'self-made' or independent groups if possible.

Based on the records from the Qaringil era, there are two Great Guilds that have never been led by one of the five major families: the Gondolier Guild and the Wool Guild. The Gondolier Guild is particularly powerful because they are responsible for the maintenance and control of the bridges, including the Great Western Bridge that dominates the region's trade. The Wool Guild handles both the production and the trade of wool. While the major families like the Manos have tried to enter the wool scene recently, the leadership of these two specific guilds has historically remained outside the 'Five Families' circle. In Qaringil, these were the pathways for families like the Tiddwars (a prominent gnomish family) to rise to power without being part of the old elven-descended aristocracy.

Hey, I missed the last session when we were digging into Qaringil's political layer. My PC is trying to figure out which of the 'Five Families' actually have the Exarch's back and if that's going to affect our Social Encounters or Renown. Who is in Giovanni Pesaro's pocket right now?

No problem. To recap the Qaringil prequel setting, the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is actually from a minor family himself, but he has been in power for 19 years. According to the records, he is most closely aligned with two of the Five Families: the Peixota and the Manos. 

The Peixota family (sigil: Dolphin) leads the Shipping Guild, and the Manos family (sigil: Hand) leads the Judicial Guild. Additionally, the Tiddwar family—a prominent gnomish family that leads the Mercantile Guild—is also noted as being aligned with the Exarch. From a mechanical standpoint, being on good terms with the Exarch likely gives you a lower DC or advantage on checks involving the Shipping, Judicial, or Mercantile guilds.

That makes sense. So if we're looking for favorable outcomes in the legal or shipping sectors, Peixota and Manos are the play. What about the other big ones, like Vitelli or Volmer? Are they hostile or just neutral? Also, did the Qanto family take a hit because of this alliance?

The Qanto family (sigil: Dove) definitely took a hit. They used to lead the Mercantile Guild but recently lost that leadership to the Tiddwar family, who are aligned with Exarch Pesaro. This puts the Qanto family in a bit of an opposition role, especially since they still have interests in the Judicial and Banking sectors. 

As for the Vitelli (sigil: Moth, Silk Guild) and Volmer (sigil: Crow, Banking Guild) families, the notes don't explicitly list them as 'aligned' or 'hostile.' They seem to be more neutral or focused on their specific guild monopolies for now. 

One big narrative hook to keep in mind for your PC: Giovanni Pesaro is described as being on 'death's doorstep.' Any political capital you build with his allies might be volatile, as the city-state's 'incomprehensible republic' will likely be thrown into a power struggle once he passes.

I have been looking over the new Qamis notes and comparing them to the old Qaringil stuff we did. I noticed Freeport is still there on the southern island in both eras. Is the setup basically the same as it was 200 years ago, or has the Ryraxian War changed the status of the city?

In both the Qaringil prequel and the Qamis present, Freeport is described in very similar terms: a city of exiles and pirates sitting on the shore of a big, mostly unexplored island to the south. During the thirty-year Ryraxian War in the present-day Qamis era, while the continent was focused on the Keyberos restoration and the Artklan League, Freeport remained largely detached. The notes suggest it functions as a haven for those who want to avoid continental politics entirely. The core dynamic hasn't changed much in two centuries; it remains a frontier of the known world where people try not to look too hard at the jungle pressing up against the city.

That detail about the jungle always stood out to me. Is there a specific reason the residents are so superstitious about the treeline, or is that one of the blank spaces the DM is leaving for us to mess with? I remember someone in our last game mentioning it might be related to where the dragons went.

It is officially categorized as a blank space. The DM notes specifically state that the big island is mostly unexplored and that residents of Freeport 'try to not look too hard' at the jungle, but the text doesn't define a specific mechanical threat or a named monster. You are right about the dragons, though; the notes mention a rumor that this southern island is where the dragons went after they departed the mainland. Since Ryraxos is currently exiled to Secundus (the second moon), any dragons in that jungle would likely be from the older, more mysterious lineages, rather than the dragon-officers of the recent war.

If the 'old' dragons are down there, it makes sense why people are scared. Does that tie into the Dragon Emperors that the Forthyr religious sects are always talking about? I noticed the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr used to be dragonborn before their blood thinned out.

The notes don't explicitly confirm a link between the southern island and the Dragon Emperors of Forthyr, but the thematic connection is there. In Forthyr, the Dragoncaller Shanlong VI (in the Qamis era) or Shanlong I (in the Qaringil era) rules a nation where a prominent sect calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. Since the rest of the continent—like Edrad and Qis'mos—has moved on to half-elven nobility or elected Potentates, the southern island and Forthyr represent the two main 'blank spaces' where the old draconic influence might still linger. Navigating to that island would also be a challenge; since it's so far south, Primus might be low on the horizon or invisible, making longitude fixes much harder for northern sailors who rely on the geostationary moon for navigation.

Hey, my buddy told me about this Qamis game, but honestly, seven moons sounds like a lot of homework. In books like Forgotten Realms, you just have Selune in the sky and that is that. Why do I need to know the physics of seven different moons just to play a character?

You do not need to be an astronomer to play, but the moons of Qaia are more than just background decoration. Unlike Selune in the Forgotten Realms, which is largely a source of light and a home for a goddess, the moons here dictate daily life and professional skills. For example, Tertius has an 8-day orbital period, which is the basis for the Qaian week. If your character is a sailor or traveler, the moon Primus is vital because it is geostationary. It hangs motionless over one spot on the equator, allowing navigators to find their longitude without needing a clock. It makes the sailors of the Central Sea much more precise than those on Earth or in other fantasy settings. Also, just as a quick tip for the setting: the 'Q' in names like Qamis or Qaringil is pronounced 'Kh' like in Old Draconic.

So it is like a hardcore navigation simulator? I usually just play a rogue who hits things, I do not want to do trig. And what is the deal with the second moon? My friend said something about a dragon being stuck there. Is he like a god like Bahamut?

You definitely do not have to do the math yourself; the notes include the formulas so the DM can describe where things are. As for the dragon, that is Ryraxos. In the Qamis campaign era, the thirty-year Ryraxian War just ended. Ryraxos was a bronze dragon who rose to power as an artillery commander and eventually became a 'dragon overlord' of Edrad. He is not a god like Bahamut—who is worshipped in the holy city of Qama—but he was formidable enough that his defeat resulted in him being exiled to Secundus, the second moon. Secundus is a dark, iron-rich moon that moves in a retrograde orbit, meaning it rises in the west and sets in the east, opposite to the others. Seeing it in the sky is a constant reminder of the war that just ended.
Okay, that is cool. Exile to the moon is very dramatic. But wait, you mentioned Qaringil earlier. Is that just another continent, like how Faerun and Kara-Tur are on the same world?

Actually, Qaringil and Qamis are the same world, but different times. Qamis is the name of the continent and the present-day campaign set after the Ryraxian War. Qaringil is a prequel campaign set about 200 years earlier. The map looks very different then. In the Qaringil era, nations like Rathakos and Acrokos do not even exist yet, and the Holy Qis Empire still rules from the city of Ath'r. The Qaringil campaign is centered on a specific island city-state with canals and merchant guilds. If your friend is starting a game in Rathakos, you are playing in the Qamis era where the Keyberos dynasty has just been restored to the throne in Edrad.
Got it. So if I am playing a rogue in Rathakos, do I need to care about those triple-moon alignments I saw in the notes? Does it make my sneak attack better or something?

The notes do not specify any direct mechanical buff to sneak attacks during alignments, but triple-moon alignments are major events. About once a year, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all reach a 'full' or 'new' phase at the same time. In the Qamis era, specifically around the year 2253, there is a very close triple-full moon alignment. While it might not change your dice rolls, a rogue might find the extra light from three full moons (or the total darkness of a triple-new moon) very useful for certain jobs. If you want to know about specific magical effects, that is something the DM might fill in, as there are 'blank spaces' in the lore regarding how moon cycles affect individual class abilities.

Hi! I'm really interested in the theology of Qamis, especially the Church of Bahamut. I was looking at the moon charts and noticed Quartus is almost exactly like Earth's moon in mass and appearance. Is there a religious reason for that? Does the Church see Quartus as a 'holy' or 'pure' celestial body compared to the darker iron moons like Primus or Secundus?

In the physics of the setting, Quartus is indeed a direct clone of Earth's Moon, serving as the primary natural calendar body for the 29.5-day monthly cycle. The notes don't explicitly state that the Church of Bahamut views Quartus as 'holier' than the others, but it is the most stable and familiar light in the sky. Unlike Primus, which is magically anchored and stationary, or Secundus, which is a dark iron-rich retrograde moon and the current site of Ryraxos's exile, Quartus provides a predictable, lunar-standard illumination. In Qis'mos (where Q is pronounced 'Kh'), the institutions of the old draconic empire and the Church are very focused on order, so the 'natural' regularity of Quartus likely aligns with their sensibilities, though the DM has not detailed a specific hierarchy of lunar sanctity.

That makes sense. Regarding the schism between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II—since Qe'lth is drow-appointed and in Qama, but Qachu is in Qis without the relics, do they use those triple-moon alignments for legitimacy? I saw the calendar mentions 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras.' Does a triple-full moon help Qachu's claim since it's so much light?

The triple-moon alignment calendar is definitely a significant celestial factor. The years 2253-2254 and 2259-2261 are 'full-moon eras' where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus frequently align in their full phases. A triple-full moon, like the nearly perfect syzygy on February 13, 2253, would be an incredibly powerful symbol for the Church. While the notes don't explicitly say Qachu II uses these for his claim, the contrast is sharp: the drow, who now occupy the holy city of Qama and appointed Qe'lth IV, are surface-dwelling but traditionally associated with the Underdark. They might find the 'new-moon era' of 2255-2258 more favorable to their rule. The fact that Qachu II lacks the physical relics of office in Qis means he might rely more heavily on such 'signs from the heavens' to bolster his authority against the drow-backed High Priest.
Hey! I'm joining the Qamis game soon. Quick question - how do you actually say the name of the world? Is it 'Kway-miss'?

Welcome to the game! It's actually pronounced 'Kham-iss.' In this setting, the letter Q is always pronounced like a 'Kh' sound. It’s a bit different from English where Q usually needs a U and makes a 'Kw' sound.
Oh, interesting. Why the 'Kh' sound? Is it a magic thing or just how people talk?

It comes from Old Draconic, which is also called Old Qis. That’s the ancient language of the setting. While most people today speak national languages like Edran or Artklan, Old Draconic is still used for scholarly publications, high-level treaties, and diplomacy. You'll see it everywhere in place names—whenever you see a 'Q' without a 'U' following it, that's a legacy of Old Draconic.
Cool. So if I'm making a character, should I pick 'Draconic' as one of my languages? I was thinking about playing a fighter from Rathakos.

In Qamis, we don't use standard racial languages like Elvish or Dwarvish. Instead, languages are national. For a character from Rathakos, you'd likely speak the Artklan language. Specifically, you’d know the prestige dialect used in the capital and one regional dialect from Rathakos. You do get one bonus language, though! You could pick Old Draconic if your fighter has a background in history or law, but most people treat it like Latin—it's for the academy and the elite rather than daily conversation.

Hey! I missed the first session of the Qaringil game, and I'm trying to wrap my head around the lore for my character's background. I was reading something about the drow occupying the holy city of Qama and appointing their own high priest. How did that happen? Is that going to affect my PC if I'm from the islands?

Welcome to the campaign! It sounds like you might have been looking at the notes for the Qamis campaign (the present-day setting) rather than the Qaringil prequel, which is set roughly 200 years earlier. In the era we are playing in, the drow have not occupied Qama. That event happens much later, during the Ryraxian War, which won't occur for another two centuries. In our current timeline—the Qaringil era—Qama is the undisputed holy city of Bahamut, and its religious authority is still centralized and intact. The drow occupation and the resulting schism between High Priest Qe'lth IV and Qachu II are far in the future.

Oh, that explains it. I was looking at the wrong file. So, in the Qaringil era, what is the situation in Qama? Since I'm playing a PC in the island city-state, do we have much to do with the holy city, or is it mostly a continental thing?

In the Qaringil era, Qama is the heart of the Church of Bahamut and sits within the region of Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos). Unlike the future where Qis'mos is a unified nation, right now it is a collection of independent regions. Many of these areas, including the northern parts of Qis'mos, are technically under the rule of the Holy Qis Empire, which governs from the city of Ath'r. As for your connection in the islands, the city-state of Qaringil actually has very strong ties to that region. Qaringil was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos. Because of this, the local language in Qaringil—a version of Islas—has a lot of Qis vocabulary mixed in. While the island elites are focused on commerce and the Great Guilds rather than continental religion, the influence of the Church of Bahamut is still significant due to the city's history.
Got it. So if I'm building a backstory around one of the Five Families in Qaringil, like the Peixota or the Vitelli, would they care about what's happening in the Holy Qis Empire?

Absolutely. The Five Families are primarily commercial, so they care about anything that affects trade and the security of the Central Sea. The Peixota family leads the Shipping Guild and the Vitelli family leads the Silk Guild. Since the Holy Qis Empire claims to rule central Qamis and the river cities like Lock, any instability there—like the current 'cold conflict' between the Emperor and the free cities—impacts the flow of goods to Qaringil. Your family would be watching the political health of the Empire closely, especially since the current Exarch of Qaringil, Giovanni Pesaro, is rumored to be near death. Any change in leadership in the islands might shift how the city interacts with the continental powers like the Empire or the growing kingdom of Artkos to the northwest.

I was looking through the moon records for your setting and saw these specific dates: May 24, 2262 and February 13, 2253. They look like they are part of some kind of cycle. What is the significance of those dates in the Qamis timeline?

Those dates represent the extremes of the triple-moon alignment cycle on Qaia. Because the three inner moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—have different orbital periods, they only rarely align. February 13, 2253 at 18h PMT is the 'closest triple-full' of the decade, meaning all three moons are at least 99.8 percent illuminated simultaneously. Conversely, May 24, 2262 at 03h PMT is the 'closest triple-new' of the decade, where all three are within 0.1 percent of being completely dark. These events anchor what the setting calls 'eras.' The system oscillates through a full-moon era (like 2253–2254) where triple-full alignments are frequent, followed by a new-moon era (like 2255–2258) where triple-new alignments dominate. A full cycle takes about 12 years. For a DM, these represent major narrative or magical opportunities, as the combined tidal force of the three moons reaches a peak of 4.4 times Earth's lunar tide during these syzygy events.

I'm looking at the northeast of the map—what's the deal with Forthyr? Coming from settings like the Forgotten Realms or Greyhawk, is this basically the dragonborn nation, or is it more of a traditional human kingdom with a draconic aesthetic?

Forthyr is a massive nation in the northeast of Qamis governed by a dynasty of sorcerer-kings titled the Dragoncallers. Its relationship with dragonborn is historical and biological rather than being a 'racial nation.' In the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years ago, the country was ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong I, who was half-dragonborn. However, by the present-day Qamis era under Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, those draconic bloodlines have run thin. The setting notes describe the nation as somewhat backwards compared to central powers like Edrad. A key feature of Forthyr is a prominent religious sect that advocates for the return of the Dragon Emperors, a movement that the Shanlong dynasty tacitly supports or at least encourages. While it isn't a dragonborn-majority state like Tymanther in the Realms, its ruling class and state religion are entirely focused on draconic legacy. In the modern era following the Ryraxian War, Forthyr is finding it impossible to continue its long-standing policy of ignoring the affairs of the southwestern part of the continent.

Hey, I am rolling up a drow character for the Qamis campaign. I read that my people were brought up to the surface as allies during the war and some stayed behind. What exactly is the current state of the world my character is living in? Like, is it a total mess after the fighting?

The Qamis era begins just after a thirty-year conflict called the Ryraxian War. For three decades, the dragon Ryraxos ruled much of the continent, but he has finally been defeated and exiled to Secundus, the second moon. The old Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in Edrad, and Artkos is attempting to reclaim its former glory. It is a period of restoration on paper, but everything has changed. You will likely be starting in Rathakos, a mountainous border state currently governed by a Regent while they look for a Prince. For a drow, the situation is unique: you were recruited as allies by Artkos in a move of desperation. Now, drow remain on the surface in a dozen scattered enclaves, most notably in the holy city of Qama. By the way, remember that Q is pronounced like Kh, so Qamis is pronounced Khamis.

Qama is the holy city of Bahumat, right? That sounds like a powder keg if drow are running the show there. How do the locals feel about us staying? Also, you mentioned the dragon is on the moon—how does that work? Can we see him?

It is definitely a powder keg. While the drow have not despoiled Qama, they have appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This has caused a major schism in the Church of Bahamut; an alternate candidate named Qachu II is operating out of the city of Qis, though he lacks the traditional relics of office. Public opinion on drow is likely strained, as the war only just ended and the political map is still being redrawn. Regarding Ryraxos, he accepted exile to Secundus as part of his defeat. Secundus is a retrograde moon, meaning it rises in the west. It has a dark iron surface and is about 0.90 times the size of Earth's moon in the sky. While you cannot see a single dragon from the surface of Qaia with the naked eye, its presence there is a literal part of the new world order. He is a shape-shifter and a formidable leader, but he is out of the picture for now.

Yo! Looking at rolling a PC from Artkos for the Qamis campaign. Who is the BFG in charge there now that the war is done, and what is their endgame? Does the political landscape offer any specific hooks for Background features or that bonus language we get?

In the present-day Qamis era, Artkos is ruled by High King Ezevir Arth. His primary objective is restoring Artkos's supremacy over the central continent; he views the current Artklan League as a pale shadow of the old empire and wants to bring his nominal subjects back under firm control. For character hooks, your background might be influenced by which enclave you hail from. If you are from the capital, Ath'r, you are at the heart of the old imperial seat. However, if you are from the southwestern enclave of Kard, the vibe is much more precarious—governance there is fragile because much of the population speaks Edran and actually welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago. Mechanically, you start with the prestige dialect of the capital and one regional dialect. You also get one bonus language of your choice; picking Edran for a Kard-based character or Old Draconic (the language of treaties and scholars) for an Ath'r noble would be very thematic. Just remember that Q is always pronounced 'Kh' in this setting.

I am absolutely obsessed with the religious politics in this setting! I was reading about the schism in the Church of Bahamut between Qe'lth IV in Qama and Qachu II in Qis—that drow-appointed high priest angle is wild. But then I saw a mention of a sect in Forthyr calling for the return of the 'Dragon Emperors.' Is that the same thing as Bahamut worship, or are we talking about a whole different theological movement up there?

It is such a fascinating dynamic! To answer your question: No, the revivalist sect in Forthyr is quite distinct from the mainstream Church of Bahamut you see in Qis'mos. While the Church of Bahamut is the dominant, organized religion with its traditional seat in Qama (currently under that drow-led schism), the movement in Forthyr is more of a nationalist-religious pining for the actual Draconic empire of old.

In Forthyr—which is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty—this sect wants the literal Dragon Emperors to return and rule. While the rest of the continent might view the era of dragon rule as ancient history or a dark time, this sect in Forthyr views it as a golden age. The current ruler, Dragoncaller Shanlong VI, actually gives this sect tacit encouragement. It’s worth noting that the Dragoncaller dynasty used to be dragonborn themselves, though the notes mention their bloodlines have run thin over the generations. So, while Qis'mos is the heart of the scholarly, institutionalized Bahamut faith, Forthyr is home to this more 'backward' and radical movement focused on the physical return of dragon overlords.

That is so cool. So if I'm a religious scholar in-game, would I see the Forthyr sect as heretics? Also, does this 'Dragoncaller' title mean they can actually talk to dragons, or is it just a legacy name? I'm trying to figure out if Ryraxos being exiled to Secundus made these Forthyr zealots happy or furious.

If you are a scholar from the Church of Bahamut in Qis'mos, you would almost certainly view the Forthyr sect with a mix of condescension and concern. To the institutionalized Church, the Forthyr movement likely looks less like proper worship and more like a dangerous, primitive cult of personality for literal monsters. 

Regarding the name 'Dragoncaller,' the notes don't explicitly say they have a magical 'Summon Dragon' button, but the rulers are sorcerer-kings who claim descent from dragons. The title is definitely part of their legitimacy. 

As for Ryraxos, that's a great question! Ryraxos is only a bronze dragon and about sixty years old—not nearly as ancient or terrifying as the 'Dragon Emperors' the sect in Forthyr is dreaming of. Ryraxos was more of a military commander who rose to power through artillery tactics and aggression. To the Forthyr zealots, Ryraxos's exile to Secundus (the second moon) might be seen as a sign of the 'weakness' of the current age, or perhaps just a pale imitation of the true Draconic power they want to see restored. They aren't looking for a bronze artillery officer; they are looking for the return of the absolute Dragon Sovereigns of the Old Qis era.

Got it. So there's this massive gap between the 'prestige' religion in Qis'mos and the 'backward' sorcerer-king vibes in the northeast. One last thing—is 'Qis'mos' and 'Qama' pronounced with a 'K' sound? I keep wanting to say 'Kwiss-moss.'

Actually, you'll want to swap that 'Kw' for a 'Kh' sound! In this setting, the letter 'Q' is derived from Old Draconic (or Old Qis) and is always pronounced like 'Kh.' So it's 'Khis-mos' and 'Khama.' That 'q without a u' spelling is a hallmark of the old imperial language that scholars and diplomats still use today!
