Hey, I'm trying to roll up a character for this Qamis game but honestly the lore is kind of a lot. Six moons? And the 'Q' is a 'Kh' sound? It feels like I need a PhD just to order a drink. Anyway, I'm thinking of a Bard from Jia because the notes say it's 'culturally influential,' but what does that actually mean in practice? Is it just a generic big city?

It can definitely feel like a lot to track at first. To keep it simple for your Bard: Jia is essentially the trendsetter for the entire continent. If a song is popular in Jia, musicians in Kard and Rathakos will be playing it three months later. It sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea, making it the primary hub for trade and art. The 'influential' part comes from the fact that the Edran nobility there has set the standard for high society for four hundred years. Even after thirty years of war and the dragon Ryraxos taking over, the first thing the restored Keyberos dynasty did was try to bring back the old Jia fashions and courtly manners.

So it's an elitist half-elf club? The notes say the nobles have to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. That sounds like a nightmare for a traveling performer who isn't 'elf enough.' Does my Bard have to speak a special way to get a gig in the capital?

Pretty much. The Edran language has a 'prestige dialect' spoken in Jia. If you want to play the high-end salons or get a patron from the Keyberos line, you'd need to master that specific accent. Most people in Qamis actually speak a regional version of their national tongue—like an Artklan speaker from Rathakos might struggle to understand someone from the capital of Artkos—but the Jia dialect of Edran is what people use for diplomacy and high-end art. As a Bard, you'd likely know the capital dialect plus one regional one.

That's another thing—the war. It lasted thirty years. Ryraxos was an 'artillery commander' dragon? That's weird. If Jia was occupied by a dragon and a 'Revolutionary Council' before that, why is it still the 'it' city? Wouldn't it be a burnt-out shell by now?

It's definitely scarred, which is a great angle for local color. The notes mention that while the Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne, enforcing their old edicts is harder than signing them. The city itself wasn't leveled—Ryraxos was more of an administrator than a destroyer—but there’s a tension between the 'old guard' half-elves trying to act like the last thirty years didn't happen and the reality of a post-war world. For your Bard, the 'vibe' of Jia right now is a mix of desperate nostalgia and the gritty reality of a city that's trying to reclaim its spot as the center of the world after a long occupation.

Okay, so if I'm from Jia, I'm basically a snob or a survivor. What about the moons? If I'm performing at night in the Central Sea, what am I actually seeing? Please tell me I don't need to track all six for a back-alley performance.

You don't have to track them all, but three are the main ones for daily life. Tertius is the big one that sets the eight-day week. Quartus is the one that looks like our Moon and sets the month. Then there's Primus—it's geostationary, so if you're in Jia, it just hangs in the same spot in the sky 24/7. It never rises or sets. For a Bard, maybe you use Primus as a metaphor for the 'eternal' Keyberos dynasty—fixed and unmoving, even if it's a bit dimmer than the others.

So I'm playing a drow who stayed behind after the Ryraxian War. My character is basically living in one of the enclaves, but everyone keeps talking about Qama like it's some grand, mystical center of the world for the Bahumat worshipers. To be honest, it just feels like a city we're occupying to keep a roof over our heads. Is there actually anything special about it, or is this just religious fluff?

From your character's perspective as a drow soldier or survivor, it probably does feel like just another strategic urban center. But for the people of Qis'mos and the wider Church of Bahumat, Qama is their most sacred site. It is the traditional seat of the church and houses the ancient relics of office that validate their leadership. In the setting's history, Qis'mos is where the institutions of the old Draconic empire lasted the longest after the dragons left, and Qama was the heart of that spiritual continuity. The fact that drow forces occupied it during the late phases of the war—and are still there—is a massive blow to the faithful.

Right, but we didn't even burn it down. The notes say we didn't 'despoil' it. If it was so holy, wouldn't there be some divine retribution? Instead, we just put Qe'lth IV in charge and life went on. It feels like the holiness is just wherever the guy with the biggest sword says it is.

That is exactly the point of the current schism. By appointing Qe'lth IV as a high priest within the city, the drow created a massive political and religious crisis. Because your people hold the city, you also hold the physical symbols of Bahumat's authority. There is a rival candidate for the position, Qachu II, who is trying to lead the church from the city of Qis, but he doesn't have the holy relics. To a Bahumat worshiper, Qachu II is the 'legitimate' choice but lacks the tools of office, while Qe'lth IV has the tools but was appointed by 'invaders.' It has effectively split the religion in two. While you might not see lightning bolts from the sky, the tension is high because the drow have essentially 'kidnapped' the administrative heart of their faith.

Okay, so it's a bureaucratic hostage situation. That makes more sense than 'magic dirt.' Does this mean my character is constantly looking over their shoulder for some 'holy crusade' to take it back? Or are the Qis too busy 'impeaching' their own leaders to actually fight us?

It's a bit of both. Qis'mos is governed by a Potentate who can be impeached by the nobility and clergy, so they do have internal drama to deal with. However, Forthyr to the northeast also has a very active religious sect that wants the Dragon Emperors to return, and they watch these events closely. While a full-scale crusade hasn't cleared the drow out yet, the presence of your enclaves is one of the biggest 'changed' things in the post-war world that the high kings of Artkos and the nobility of Edrad find impossible to ignore. You are living in a powder keg, even if the city itself stays peaceful for now.

Hi! I'm totally new to D&D, but I'm joining a Qamis game soon. My DM mentioned Edrad. Is that basically like the Sword Coast or Waterdeep from the Forgotten Realms? I want to make sure I get the vibe right.

Welcome to the game! While Edrad is the most politically and economically dominant nation in Qamis, it has a distinct flavor compared to the Forgotten Realms. First, a quick tip: the name Qamis is pronounced "Khamis" because Q makes a "Kh" sound in this world. Edrad's capital is a city called Jia, which sits on a peninsula in the Central Sea. It's the most culturally influential city on the continent, much like Waterdeep is to the Sword Coast, but the social structure is quite specific. The nobility in Edrad is strictly half-elven—by law, they must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. Also, unlike the Forgotten Realms, there are no racial languages here; you won't find anyone speaking "Elvish" or "Dwarvish." Instead, people speak national languages like Edran.
Wait, no Elvish? That's crazy. So if I play an elf, I just speak the human language? Also, I heard there was a big dragon war. Was it like those epic battles with Tiamat where the world almost ends?

Exactly—you'd likely speak Edran or a regional dialect. As for the war, it's called the Ryraxian War. It wasn't against a dragon god like Tiamat, but against a bronze dragon named Ryraxos. He actually started out as a formidable artillery commander—dragons are great for that because they can carry the guns! He ruled for about twenty-five years after a revolution overthrew the local council. The war lasted thirty years in total and just ended recently. Ryraxos wasn't killed, though; he was defeated and accepted exile to Secundus, which is the planet's second moon. The old Keyberos dynasty, which has ruled Edrad for four hundred years, is finally back on the throne.
Exiled to the moon? That is so cool. So since the war is over and the kings are back, is Edrad a safe, peaceful place now? Kind of like the Shire or a starting town in a video game?

It’s actually a bit more complicated. The campaign notes say that while the war is over and things were officially "put back the way they were," everything has actually changed. The Keyberos dynasty is finding it very hard to enforce their edicts after thirty years of chaos. If your campaign starts in a place called Rathakos, that’s a mountainous border state next to Edrad. It’s pretty dangerous and filled with internal unrest, especially in the areas they recently took from Edrad. There are even mountain communities that still follow old dragon cults. The DM also left several "blank spaces" on the map, so while the capital Jia is very refined, the frontiers like Rathakos are perfect for adventuring because they're still being explored and settled.
I'm digging into the lore of the Qamis setting. Can you tell me what the capital of Edrad is and where it's located on the map?

The capital of Edrad is a city called Jia. Geographically, it sits on a peninsula that juts out into the Central Sea. It is described as being by far the most culturally influential city in the continent of Qamis, serving as the political and economic heart of the nation.
A peninsula city sounds like a classic trade hub. Who actually runs the place? I saw something about a Keyberos dynasty.

You are correct. The Keyberos dynasty has reigned over Edrad for four centuries, though their rule hasn't been continuous. In the present-day Qamis era, they have just been restored to the throne following a thirty-year war. Before this restoration, they were displaced first by a Revolutionary Council and then by the dragon Ryraxos, who ruled as an overlord. The ruling class in Jia is a half-elven nobility. The setting notes include a very specific detail for worldbuilders: to be considered part of the Edran nobility, an individual must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf.
That bloodline requirement is a really unique detail. Does the city's status change much if I'm looking at the Qaringil prequel era instead of the present day?

In the Qaringil prequel era (set about 200 years earlier), Jia is still the capital and the Keyberos dynasty is already on the throne, though they have only been reigning for two centuries at that point instead of four. The biggest difference is the extent of their reach. In the prequel, the dynasty is still actively working to expand royal control. For example, in the Qaringil era, Edrad does not yet control the city of Qeth (pronounced Kheth) or the territory to its northeast. Conquering those regions is a primary goal of Edran foreign policy during that time, whereas in the present-day Qamis era, those borders have long been established as their historical lines.

I am absolutely obsessed with the moon system for Qaia! I just saw the notes on the orbital directions and I have to know—is it really a mix of prograde and retrograde moons? That's such a wild detail for a sky-map!

It really is! It makes the night sky in Qamis incredibly dynamic. There are six moons in total (well, seven if you count the tiny particle Quintus), and they are split right down the middle: three prograde and three retrograde. Primus, Tertius, and Quartus all orbit prograde (west-to-east), while Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are all retrograde. Seeing those retrograde moons rise in the west must be a total trip for any traveler from Earth!

Rising in the west!! That is so cool. And wait, Secundus is one of the retrograde ones? That's the one where Ryraxos is currently exiled in the present-day campaign, right? Does that mean the 'Dragon Moon' is literally moving against the flow of the others?

You nailed it. In the Qamis era, Ryraxos is stuck on Secundus, and yes, it is moving 'backward' compared to the planet's rotation. If you were standing in Rathakos looking up, you'd see the big moons like Tertius and Quartus moving west-to-east, while Secundus would be sweeping past them going the opposite direction. It’s also quite dark since it's an iron-rich moon with a low albedo of 0.06, so it's this dim, heavy-looking sphere moving against the grain of the sky. It's super atmospheric.
I love that. And then there's Primus just sitting there totally still because it's geostationary. Is that one prograde too?

Yes, Primus is prograde! To be geostationary, it has to orbit in the same direction the planet rotates. Even though it's magically anchored so it doesn't drift, its orbital period matches Qaia's day exactly. So while the retrograde moons are rushing westward and the other prograde moons are drifting eastward, Primus is just a fixed point in the sky for whoever is in the right hemisphere. It creates this amazing contrast between absolute stillness and chaotic, multi-directional motion.

I am trying to wrap my head around the orbital mechanics of Qaia. In settings like the Forgotten Realms, you just have Selune and the Tears, but this system has seven moons? That sounds like a tidal nightmare. Is Ryraxos some kind of ancient Great Wyrm who has seen a thousand cycles of these triple-moon alignments, or is he younger?

Actually, compared to the ancient dragons you might find in other settings, Ryraxos is quite young. The campaign notes describe him as a bronze dragon who is roughly sixty years old. While he is a formidable former artillery commander and shape-shifter, he is not an ancient Great Wyrm. In fact, his entire rise to power and the subsequent Ryraxian War only spanned about thirty years. Regarding those triple-moon alignments, they happen in 12-year cycles, so he has likely seen a few, but he is certainly not a creature of geologic ages.

Only sixty? That is barely an adult by most dragon standards! I assumed he'd be hundreds of years old to pull off a continental conquest. If he is only sixty, he definitely wasn't around for that Qaringil prequel, right? Also, the notes say he was exiled to Secundus. Isn't that the dark iron moon with the 1.47x lunar tidal force? That sounds like a harsh place to live.

You are correct on both counts. Since the Qaringil campaign is set roughly 200 years before the present-day Qamis setting, Ryraxos was not yet born during that era. As for his exile, Secundus is indeed a challenging environment. It is a retrograde moon, meaning it rises in the west, and it has a dark iron-rich surface with an albedo of only 0.06. While it provides a significant tidal tug on Qaia, Ryraxos is stuck there now as part of the terms of his defeat. It is a bit of a leap from the coastal influence of Jia or the Red Mountains where he used to operate.

A retrograde moon rising in the west is fascinating—it's like Triton but much closer. If the total oscillating tidal force on Qaia is 4.4 times what we have on Earth, how do navigators even keep track of the water? Is there a stationary point they use, or is it all just chaos compared to the simpler Selune-driven tides?

Navigators on Qaia actually have a unique advantage that Earth-based or Toril-based sailors lack: Primus. Primus is a geostationary moon magically anchored directly over the equator at the prime meridian. Because its orbital period matches Qaia's sidereal day exactly, it never rises or sets; it stays fixed in the sky for the near-side hemisphere. This allows navigators to find their longitude just by measuring the elevation of Primus, provided they know their latitude from the pole star. The tides are complex because they are driven by Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus simultaneously, but at least the navigation has a literal fixed anchor in the sky.

So what's the deal with these Tiddwars in the Qaringil notes? Gnomes running a major guild in a city of merchant princes? Usually, homebrew settings just throw stuff like that in for 'flavor' without any backing. How'd they actually get to the top?

In the Qaringil era—the prequel setting about 200 years before the main Qamis campaign—the Tiddwars are indeed a notable exception to the human-dominated 'Five Families.' They are a gnomish family with the sigil of Gears, and they currently lead the Mercantile Guild, which is one of the seven Great Guilds of the city.

Their rise is tied directly to the current political climate of the republic. For the last 19 years, the city has been led by Exarch Giovanni Pesaro. He’s from a minor family himself and has stayed in power by playing the major families against each other. The Tiddwars are closely aligned with him. Specifically, they took over the leadership of the Mercantile Guild from the Qantos—one of the legendary Five Families—because they had the Exarch's backing.

A political puppet for the Exarch, then? The Qantos can't be happy about that. In most games, a 'minor gnomish family' taking a major guild from the old nobility usually ends with them at the bottom of a canal. How are they staying alive?

It's definitely a precarious spot. Qaringil is a city of canals and commerce where the elites are merchants, not landowners, so losing a guild is a massive blow to your wallet. The Qantos (whose sigil is a Dove) are still very active in the Judicial and Banking guilds, so they haven't been wiped out, but they are certainly the 'losers' in this current administration.

The Tiddwars stay relevant because the Mercantile Guild regulates the trade of almost all commodities that aren't silk, wool, or shipping. Since Qaringil is an island that relies on imports for food, that's a lot of leverage. However, the notes mention that Exarch Giovanni is currently on death's doorstep. Without his protection, the Tiddwars' position at the top of the Mercantile Guild is likely going to be one of the first things the Five Families challenge.

Got it. So they're the 'new money' gnomes holding the keys while the old man dies. Just to be sure, this is only in the prequel, right? Do they show up in the modern Qamis setting after that 30-year war with the dragon?

The Tiddwars are only explicitly mentioned in the Qaringil prequel documents. In the present-day Qamis setting—set 200 years later—the political map has changed significantly. Qaringil is still there, but the campaign notes for the modern era focus more on the aftermath of the Ryraxian War and the restoration of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad. There's no mention of the Tiddwars or the state of the Qaringil guilds in the modern notes yet; that's one of those 'blank spaces' the DM hasn't filled in.

Hey there. I am building a character from Forthyr who is a die-hard believer in the return of the Dragon Emperors. I noticed the notes mention 'blank spaces' on the map. Can I invent a specific monastery or a hidden valley in the northeast to be my character's home base?

You certainly can. The DM has explicitly stated that those blank spaces are there for you to help fill in with your own details. Forthyr is a vast nation, and while it is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty--Shanlong VI in the present day--it is considered somewhat backwards and has a lot of area that is not fully mapped out. Your idea for a monastery fits perfectly, especially since the religious sect seeking the return of the Dragon Emperors already receives tacit encouragement from the sorcerer-kings themselves. Just a reminder that in this setting, the letter Q is pronounced like 'Kh', so if you name any local landmarks using Old Draconic roots, keep that sound in mind!
hi nanochat. im playing a character in the qaringil campaign. what level are we starting at and how does that affect my status in the guilds? im thinking of being in the gondolier guild.

In the Qaringil prequel campaign, the party starts at 5th level. At this level, you are considered a full guild member of any organization relevant to your class or background. For a Gondolier, this means you are an established professional rather than an apprentice, which fits well with the city's status as a commercial hub where guilds hold significant weight.
that sounds cool. if im a full member of the gondolier guild at level 5, what exactly do we do besides row boats? do we have any political power compared to the big families like the peixotas?

The Gondolier guild is more powerful than it might first appear. Beyond transport in the canals, the guild is responsible for the maintenance and control of the two enormous bridges that connect the city to the mainland. Politically, the Gondoliers are unique because, along with the Wool guild, they have never been led by one of the five major families like the Peixota or Vitelli. As a 5th-level member, you represent the skilled backbone of a guild that controls the city's vital infrastructure, giving you a degree of independence from the noble families who usually run the other Great Guilds.

I've been playing since the white box days, and usually moons are just moons. This Qamis setting has a 'Primus' that sounds like a GPS satellite. Why is the dragon Ryraxos using a geostationary rock as a base in the Qaringil prequel anyway? Seems like too much 'hard sci-fi' for a fantasy game.

I can help clear up those details. First, a few corrections regarding the lore: Ryraxos is actually exiled to Secundus, the second moon, not Primus. Also, Ryraxos does not appear in the Qaringil prequel era (set 200 years ago); his rise to power and subsequent exile happened during the thirty-year Ryraxian War, which just ended in the present-day Qamis era. 

Regarding Primus, while it functions similarly to a geostationary satellite, it is described as being 'magically anchored' in place. Physically, it is much larger than any man-made satellite, with a radius of 64 km. It sits at an altitude of approximately 35,800 km, which does mirror Earth's geostationary belt, but in the sky it appears as a small, faint disc about one-third the size of Earth's moon. It doesn't provide GPS signals; instead, it serves as a fixed navigational point for sailors to find their longitude without needing a chronometer.
Right, Secundus. Too many moons to keep track of. So if I'm playing a navigator in Rathakos during that Qaringil prequel, how am I supposed to use a 'stationary' moon? Isn't it just a dot?

Actually, there is another era-related detail to note: Rathakos did not exist 200 years ago during the Qaringil campaign. At that time, the region was part of the Holy Qis Empire. 

As for navigation, Primus is more than a dot. It has an apparent diameter of 10.4 arcminutes (about 0.34x the size of Earth's Moon) and a magnitude of -9.62. It is a permanent, motionless fixture in the sky for the hemisphere that can see it. Navigators use the 'elevation method'—by measuring how high Primus is above the horizon and knowing their latitude (from the pole star), they can determine their longitude. The setting notes even mention a specific 'primsine' formula used by sailors for this exact purpose. It allows for highly accurate position fixes on any clear night, a major advantage for the merchant fleets of the era.

Hey! Super excited to jump into the Qaringil prequel. My DM mentioned we are starting at level 5. As a tech lead who loves a good org chart, what does being level 5 actually 'unlock' for us in terms of the city's guild structure? Give me the executive summary on what we can do!

It is great to have you on board for the Qaringil campaign! Starting at level 5 is a significant milestone in this setting. In the city-state of Qaringil (remember, that Q is pronounced 'Kh'), level 5 is the threshold where you are considered a 'full guild member' rather than just an apprentice or associate. This is your 'executive access' pass to the city's power structure. Since Qaringil is a republic of 100,000 people where the elites are commercial rather than landowners, being a full member of a guild means you have real professional standing and political weight. You can be a full member of any guild relevant to your class—whether that is one of the 'Great Guilds' like the Judicial or Banking guilds, or the specialized ones like the Adventurer, Alchemist, or Mage guilds.

That is exactly what I wanted to hear! So if I am a full member of, say, the Shipping Guild, am I automatically reporting into one of those 'Five Families' I keep hearing about? I saw the Peixota family has the Dolphin sigil and leads Shipping—is that basically the 'C-suite' for my character?

Spot on! The 'Five Families' are essentially the board of directors for the city's economy. If you are in the Shipping Guild, you are definitely operating within the Peixota family's sphere of influence, as they lead that specific guild. However, the guilds themselves are the formal professional bodies. The families—Peixota (Dolphin), Manos (Hand), Qanto (Dove), Volmer (Crow), and Vitelli (Moth)—exert control by holding leadership positions in the 'Great Guilds.' As a level 5 full member, you are influential enough to be noticed by these families, perhaps even used as a high-level 'individual contributor' for their interests. It is a high-stakes time to be at that level, too, because the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is on death's doorstep after 19 years in office, and the families are definitely looking for capable guild members to secure their positions!

Hey, I missed the last session. The DM mentioned we might be heading toward Lock or dealing with their 'free cities' soon. Since my drow character stayed on the surface after the Ryraxian War ended, what should I know about that place? Is it a good spot for a drow to lay low?

In the present-day Qamis era, Lock is a kingdom known for its burgeoning industrial presence. While it is technically a kingdom, the city of Lock itself and several other major urban centers are designated as 'free cities,' meaning they maintain a high degree of autonomy. Geographically, it borders Rathakos, where the campaign began. 

As for your drow character, the situation is complex. During the war, the drow were recruited as allies by Artkos in a move of desperation. Now that the war is over, drow remain on the surface in about a dozen scattered enclaves. The most prominent is Qama, the holy city of Bahumat, which is actually in neighboring Qis'mos (pronounced 'Khiz-mos'). While the free cities of Lock are busy with industry and trade, the political climate for drow is often tied to these enclaves. You aren't an 'enemy' per se, but you are part of a visible and politically sensitive group left over from the conflict.
You mentioned the free cities have 'autonomy.' Does that mean the King of Lock doesn't really run things there? Also, what's the deal with that enclave in Qama you mentioned?

Exactly. While Lock as a whole is a kingdom, the 'free city' status gives places like the city of Lock special prerogatives and self-governance that often put them at odds with the central monarchy. It is a tug-of-war for power that has existed for centuries—even 200 years ago during the Holy Qis Empire era, these river cities were in a 'cold conflict' with the Emperor over their rights.

Regarding Qama, it's a major flashpoint right now. It is the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut, but it was occupied by drow during the late phases of the Ryraxian War. They didn't destroy the city, but they did appoint their own high priest, Qe'lth IV (pronounced 'Khelth'). This caused a religious schism, as a rival candidate named Qachu II is operating from the city of Qis without the official relics of the office. As a drow on the surface, people might associate you with this religious and political takeover, even if you're just a mercenary or traveler.
Got it. So if we go to Lock, I should probably watch my back. Is there anything in the 'blank spaces' of the map near there I should know about for my backstory?

The DM has specifically noted several blank spaces on the map around the Artklan League and the surrounding islands. If you want your drow to have come from a specific enclave or a hidden community near the Red Mountains—which are famously underexplored and dangerous—that's something the notes allow you to fill in. There are also mentions of 'old dragon cults' in the mountain communities of Rathakos that might serve as a bridge for your backstory if you spent time in the southern reaches before moving toward Lock.

Hey, I'm rolling up a half-elf noble from Edrad for a Qamis campaign. I'm looking at the lunar notes because I want to figure out how my family would track their history. Which moon is the 'main' one for the calendar?

For a noble of the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad, the most important moon for the calendar is Quartus. It acts as the natural calendar body for Qaia because its orbital period is 29.50 days, which defines the monthly cycle. While there are six other moons, Quartus provides the consistent rhythm that historians in Jia would use to record the four-hundred-year timeline of your character's house.
The notes call it a 'clone of Earth's Moon.' Is it literally identical? That seems a bit strange given how weird some of the other moons like Primus are.

In terms of physical properties, yes, it is essentially identical to Earth's Moon. It has the same mass, radius, and density. It even has the same albedo, or surface brightness, of 0.12. While moons like Primus and Secundus are dark, iron-rich fragments with double the lunar density, Quartus is a standard rocky body. It's the familiar anchor in a sky that otherwise behaves quite differently, featuring retrograde moons that rise in the west and a geostationary moon that never moves.
So as a noble in Jia, is Quartus the only thing I'd care about, or do the other moons mess with our schedule?

You'd definitely have to track others. While Quartus sets the month, Tertius sets the week. Tertius has an 8.15-day period, which is the basis for the Qaian 8-day week. Your social calendar in the capital would be a mix of the Quartus month and the Tertian week. Additionally, as an elite, you'd be aware of 'triple-moon alignment eras.' About five times a year, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all reach a full or new phase simultaneously. These alignments follow a 12-year cycle of 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras,' which often carry significant weight in courtly omens or religious observations in the Church of Bahamut.
That sounds like a headache for a clerk. Does the Keyberos family use Quartus for navigation too, or is that what that stationary moon is for?

Edran navigators actually use both, but for different things. Quartus is used for 'lunar-distance' methods—comparing its position against stars—which is the harder way to find longitude. However, your family likely owes much of its maritime dominance to Primus. Because Primus is geostationary over the prime meridian, an Edran navigator can find their longitude just by measuring its elevation in the sky. It's a massive advantage for the Jia fleets; as long as they are in the near hemisphere where Primus is visible, they never need a chronometer to know exactly where they are.

Hello! I am joining the Qaringil game and trying to build a character who is a sailor or a navigator from the city-state itself. I saw a note about the 'two celestial anchors' used for navigation. Can you explain how that works for someone living in Qaringil?

Welcome to the campaign! The 'two celestial anchors' refer to the Pole Star and Primus, the first moon. On Qaia, these two objects allow navigators to find their exact position at sea without needing a chronometer. The Pole Star stays fixed above the north (or south) pole; measuring its elevation above the horizon gives you your latitude. Primus is the unique part: it is a geostationary moon, magically anchored so it stays fixed over one spot on the equator (0 latitude, 0 longitude). Because it never moves, its elevation in the sky tells you exactly how far east or west of the prime meridian you are. For a Qaringil sailor, this means as long as the sky is clear, you always know exactly where you are in the Central Sea.
That is really helpful. I love the idea of my character being obsessed with the sky. What does it actually look like from the docks of Qaringil at night? Is it crowded with moons?

It is quite a spectacle. From Qaringil, you would see Primus as a small, motionless disc, about one-third the size of the moon we know on Earth, hanging permanently in the sky. While it stays still, the other moons are constantly moving. You have Secundus rising in the west (it is a retrograde moon) and looking nearly the size of Earth's moon. Tertius and Quartus move west-to-east. Tertius is the largest in the sky and sets the pace for the eight-day week. There are also the smaller outer moons, Sextus and Septimus. Because Qaringil is a city of canals and bridges, you would see all these moons reflected in the water as they pass by the stationary glow of Primus.

I am thinking of having my navigator be a member of one of the Great Guilds. Which family or guild would be the best fit for someone who works the shipping lanes? And who is this Exarch person I keep hearing about?

For a navigator, the Shipping Guild is the best fit. It is currently led by the Peixota family, whose sigil is a dolphin. They are one of the 'Five Families' that dominate Qaringil's politics and commerce. Since the city is a republic of merchants rather than landowners, the Peixotas are very powerful. As for the Exarch, that is Giovanni Pesaro. He is the head of the republic and has been in power for 19 years. However, the local word is that he is on death's doorstep. His passing will likely trigger a massive power struggle between the Five Families—like the Peixotas, the Manos (who lead the Judicial Guild), or the Qanto—to see who can seize control of the city and its vital bridges.

Evening! I am looking over the campaign notes for a possible Qaringil prequel one-shot. I am a bit of a math nerd, so I was looking at the navigation guide. What does primsine(90) equal in this system, and what does it actually tell a navigator?

In the Qaian navigation system, primsine(90) equals exactly 1.000. This value represents the geometric relationship between an observer and the geostationary moon, Primus. When you measure the elevation of Primus at 90 degrees, it means the moon is directly at your zenith, or perfectly overhead. Since Primus is magically anchored directly above the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude), a primsine of 1 tells a navigator they are standing exactly at the sub-Primus point. Mathematically, the formula is primsine(e) = rho * cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e)). When e is 90, the cosine terms become zero and the sine term becomes one, leaving you with the square root of one, which is one.

That makes sense. If I am running this in the Qaringil era, about 200 years before the Ryraxian War, I want to help a player build a character who is a navigator for one of the big families. Which family would be the best fit for a high-stakes maritime navigator? I saw the Peixotas mentioned.

The Peixota family would be the perfect fit for a navigator in the Qaringil era. They lead the Shipping Guild and use a dolphin as their sigil. Since Qaringil is a thassolocracy of merchant princes, the Peixotas are at the top of the food chain regarding maritime trade and naval power. They are currently aligned with Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, who has ruled for 19 years but is reportedly near death, which adds some nice political tension to your character's backstory. Your player should also know that the city is criss-crossed with canals and relies on the Shipping Guild for its food supply. Also, remember that in this setting, the 'Q' in Qaringil and Peixota is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound, derived from Old Draconic.
Great detail. Is there any specific guild or family that might be a rival for a Peixota navigator? I want some local color for a 'wrong side of the docks' contact.

For a legitimate commercial rival, look at the Vitelli family. Their sigil is a moth, they lead the Silk Guild, and they are heavily involved in shipping and mercantile interests, often competing for the same lanes as the Peixotas. If you want something a bit seedier, you could look at the Gondolier Guild. They aren't one of the 'Five Families,' but they control the maintenance and traffic of the two enormous bridges and the city's internal canals. They are one of the few major guilds never led by a Great Family. If your navigator was involved in something illicit, they might have run afoul of the 'underground' guilds like the Thieves or Cutpurses, though the DM notes mention those are currently unmapped 'blank spaces' you could help fill in with your player's backstory.

hey u! so i just joined my first dnd game and we're starting in rathakos. my character is supposed to be a sailor's kid, and i was wondering how they actually find their way at sea without like, google maps or a gps? is it just stars?

Welcome to your first campaign! In the world of Qaia, specifically in the current Qamis era where you are starting in Rathakos, sailors use the sky as their map. For finding latitude—which tells you how far north or south you are—the most common method is the pole star. Because Qaia's axis points toward a specific star near the celestial north pole, that star stays motionless in the sky all night. A navigator uses an instrument like an astrolabe or a cross-staff to measure the elevation of that star above the horizon. That angle in degrees is equal to your geographic latitude.
Wait, so if the star is like 30 degrees up, I'm at 30 degrees latitude? Does that work everywhere? What if I'm in the south part of the world, like near that big jungle island Freeport is on?

Exactly! If the pole star is 30 degrees above the northern horizon, you are at 30 degrees North latitude. As you move toward the equator, the star gets lower and lower. Once you cross the equator and head south toward the big island where Freeport is, the north pole star disappears below the horizon because the planet itself gets in the way. Navigators in the southern hemisphere have to look for a different star near the south celestial pole and measure its height above the southern horizon. The rule is the same: the angle above the horizon equals your latitude.

That makes sense. But I heard there's this moon called Primus that's also stuck in one spot. Is that just like a second pole star? Like, can I just use that to find my latitude too or is it for something else?

Primus is actually quite different and very special to Qaia. While the pole star is fixed over the pole, Primus is 'geostationary,' meaning it hangs motionless directly over the equator at the prime meridian. It never rises or sets, but it is only visible from one side of the planet. Because it's fixed over a point on the equator instead of the pole, its elevation is primarily used to find longitude—how far east or west you are. Navigators use a mathematical table called a 'primsine' table to calculate their position based on Primus's height. If you can see both the pole star and Primus, you can find your exact location on the map without needing a clock or any fancy magic, which is something Earth navigators couldn't do for centuries!

I am looking at running a one-shot in the Qaringil prequel era and I was considering using Ryraxos as a major boss or a looming threat. But the notes say he is 'not truly that impressive of a dragon.' Why is that? And would he even be a factor 200 years before the main campaign starts?

To answer your second question first: No, Ryraxos would not be a factor in the Qaringil prequel. According to the notes, Ryraxos is a relatively young dragon, approximately sixty years old during the present-day Qamis era. Since Qaringil is set roughly 200 years earlier, he wouldn't even be born yet.

As for why he is described as 'not truly that impressive,' the notes highlight a few specific reasons:

1. Age and Type: He is a bronze dragon and relatively young. While formidable, he lacks the raw power of an ancient wyrm.
2. Power Scale: The text explicitly states he is less dangerous than a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery.
3. Source of Power: His rise to 'dragon overlord' status was based more on his tactical utility and military background than solo magical or physical might. He distinguished himself as an artillery officer because dragons can carry heavy guns, and he used this military leverage to wage war for twenty-five years.

If you are looking for a legendary draconic threat for a Qaringil one-shot, you might look toward the 'Dragoncaller' dynasty in Forthyr. In that era, Dragoncaller Shanlong I is the sorcerer-king. You could also lean into the 'old dragon cults' mentioned in the mountainous regions, or the rumors that dragons fled to the big unexplored island to the south near Freeport.

I'm looking for an executive summary of the religious landscape in Westron. I'm trying to figure out if my character's background there makes sense. How does it compare to the local Bahamut worship we see in Qis'mos?

Regarding Westron, the short version is that it is a vast, 'exotic' continent to the west, but the campaign notes are currently very sparse on specifics. The most critical piece of data we have is that the people of Westron are mostly not worshippers of Bahamut. This is a significant point of difference from Qis'mos, where the Church of Bahamut is a massive institutional force. Beyond the fact that they have different religious practices, the actual gods or pantheons they follow are currently one of the 'blank spaces' in the world documentation.

That's pretty light on the documentation. In my day job, I'd call that a 'to be determined' block. Is there a specific list of non-Bahamut gods already established, or is the architecture of the Westron faith completely open for me to pitch ideas to the DM?

It is effectively a 'to be determined' block. The DM has explicitly stated that material for Westron has not been prepared yet, and it is marked as a space that can be filled in with player backstory. If you want to pitch a specific faith or a set of deities for your character, you have a high degree of flexibility because the existing lore doesn't define any Westron gods yet. The only hard constraint is the one I mentioned: it shouldn't be centered on Bahamut, as that contrast is the main defining characteristic we have for the region's theology.

Got it. If I'm coming from Westron into the current 'Qamis' era timeline, how much would I know about the mess going on in Qama? I see there's a schism with a drow high priest. Is that purely a local Qis'mos problem?

The schism in the Church of Bahamut is a major political event in the present-day Qamis era. It centers on the holy city of Qama, which was occupied by the drow during the Ryraxian War. Currently, a drow high priest named Qe'lth IV (pronounced 'Khelth') has been appointed in Qama, while a rival candidate, Qachu II ('Khachu'), claims the title from the city of Qis. This is a continental-scale conflict for Bahamut worshippers. Since Westron doesn't follow Bahamut, your character might see it as an internal dispute of a foreign religion—though as a traveler in Qamis, you'd quickly find that the Church's influence makes this 'local' problem affect everything from politics to guild law.

Hey, I noticed a lot of the names in these notes start with a Q, like Qaringil and Qis'mos. Is there a specific way that is supposed to be pronounced? I'm curious if any of the local underground groups use that 'Q' naming convention too.

You have a good eye for the phonology. In this setting, the letter Q is always pronounced as a 'Kh' sound, similar to a raspy K. This comes from Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis, which is the language used for scholarly treaties and high diplomacy. The 'Q without a U' spelling is a direct marker of that ancient linguistic heritage.

As for the underground, if we look at the city-state of Qaringil during the prequel era (about 200 years before the current Qamis campaign), there are definitely some seedier groups. While the major 'Great Guilds' run the city's legal commerce, there are several underground criminal organizations that mirror that structure. They explicitly style themselves as guilds to mock or mimic the legitimate ones: specifically the Thieves, the Bandits, and the Cutpurses. They aren't officially recognized, of course, but they use the 'Guild' title as a form of branding.

That's fascinating. So these criminal 'guilds' in Qaringil are essentially LARPing as the official merchant or judicial guilds? Do they have their own sigils or specific Old Draconic jargon they use to distance themselves from the prestige dialects of the ruling class?

It is exactly a form of mimicry. The Great Guilds—like the Shipping, Silk, and Banking guilds—hold the real power in Qaringil and are led by the prominent 'Five Families.' By calling themselves the 'Thieves Guild' or 'Cutpurses Guild,' the criminal elements are adopting the city's obsession with corporate structure.

Regarding their jargon, the notes don't explicitly list a secret 'thieves' cant' derived from Old Draconic. Usually, Old Draconic is the language of the academy and the elite. However, since the common language in Qaringil is Islas—which has a heavier than usual amount of Qis vocabulary because the city was built by adventurers from Qis'mos—it's likely their slang is peppered with those 'Kh' sounds. As for sigils, the notes identify the major families' symbols (like the Peixota Dolphin or the Volmer Crow), but the specific heraldry or sigils for the underground guilds remain one of those 'blank spaces' the DM hasn't filled in yet. They stay underground and style themselves as guilds, but their internal symbology isn't public record.

If the 'Q' sound is so tied to the old draconic empire and prestige, would a group like the 'Bandits Guild' be seen as pretentious for using it, or is the 'Kh' sound just so baked into the regional Islas dialect that even a street-level cutpurse uses it naturally?

It's more the latter. By the time of the Qaringil era, the 'Kh' sound is foundational to the region's identity. While Old Draconic remains the prestige language for a treaty, the actual place names—Qaringil, Qama, Qis—all use that sound. A street-level cutpurse in Qaringil would use that phonology naturally because they are speaking the local variety of Islas.

However, there is a socio-linguistic divide. In the present-day Qamis setting, national languages like Edran, Artklan, and Qis aren't standardized. A high-ranking noble in the capital of Ath'r might speak a 'prestige' dialect of Artklan that sounds very different from someone in a border state like Rathakos. The criminal elements probably speak the regional variations, which are full of local slang, whereas the 'Guild' styling is their way of claiming a piece of the institutional authority that the half-elven nobility and the great merchant families usually hoard for themselves.

I've been looking over the celestial data for the planet Qaia and the moon Secundus caught my eye. It seems like a very strange object compared to a typical moon. Can you give me a technical overview of what makes it unique?

Secundus is indeed a unique celestial body in the Qaian system. Physically, it is a near-iron moon with a density of 6,689 kg/m3, which is about twice the density of Earth's Moon. It has a very dark surface with a geometric albedo of 0.06, making it appear about one magnitude dimmer than Luna despite being roughly 90% of its angular size in the sky. Its most striking orbital feature is that it is retrograde, meaning it orbits in the opposite direction of Qaia's rotation. In the present-day Qamis era, it is also notable as the site of exile for the dragon Ryraxos.
A retrograde orbit that close in must create some interesting visual and physical effects. How does it behave in the sky, and what does it do to the tides?

Because it is retrograde, Secundus rises in the west and sets in the east, the opposite of the sun and the prograde moons like Tertius and Quartus. It has a fast orbital period of only 4.53 days. Physically, it exerts a significant tidal force on Qaia—about 1.47 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon. When combined with the other moons, it contributes to a total oscillating tidal load roughly 4.4 times stronger than what we experience on Earth. Its eccentricity is also quite high at 0.19, so its size in the sky varies noticeably, ranging from 23.6 to 34.4 arcminutes.
The notes mention that both Primus and Secundus share that high iron density. Is there a lore-based reason for this, and how does the geostationary Primus compare to Secundus in terms of visibility?

The shared density of 6,689 kg/m3 suggests that Primus and Secundus are likely fragments of the same differentiated progenitor body that broke apart. However, they appear very differently to an observer. While Secundus sweeps across the sky every few days, Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. This means Primus is a motionless fixed point that never rises or sets for the hemisphere it sits over. Secundus is much larger and brighter; Primus has an angular diameter of only 10.4 arcminutes (about 1/3 the size of Secundus) and is significantly dimmer at magnitude -9.62 compared to Secundus at -11.76.
Let's talk about the Ryraxian War. The notes say Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus. Is that moon actually habitable, or is the exile more of a magical imprisonment?

The notes do not describe Secundus as having an atmosphere or being naturally habitable; it is characterized as a dark, iron-rich rocky world. The exile was a diplomatic and physical move: after his defeat, the bronze dragon Ryraxos accepted exile to the second moon to end the thirty-year war. While he is a formidable shapeshifter and was a high-ranking officer, he is described as a relatively young dragon (around sixty years old) and not an invincible god-like figure. Being on Secundus removes him entirely from continental politics in Qamis.
Does the retrograde orbit of Secundus have any long-term stability issues? I know in real physics retrograde moons often have different fates than prograde ones.

You're correct. Because Secundus is in a retrograde orbit, tidal interactions cause it to slowly spiral inward toward Qaia over geological time. This is the opposite of prograde moons like Quartus, which generally migrate outward. While a 2,000-year simulation shows the system is currently stable, the notes mention that the long-term fate of the moons without continued dragon magic remains an open question. Unlike Primus, which is magically anchored to prevent migration, Secundus is subject to these standard orbital decays.
One last thing—how does Secundus factor into the 'Triple-moon alignment' I read about? If it's moving the opposite way, does it make those syzygy events rarer?

Actually, the alignments still happen quite regularly. All three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) are within 5% of a full or new moon phase simultaneously about five times per year. The system goes through 12-year cycles of 'full-moon eras' and 'new-moon eras.' For example, in the year 2256 of the Qamis era, there are two nearly perfect 'triple-new' alignments where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all reach the new moon phase within 0.2% of each other. Even with Secundus moving retrograde, the mathematical resonance of the system allows for these near-exact syzygies.

Hi, I am building a character for a Qamis game. I was reading the history of Edrad and saw a mention of the Revolutionary Council that ruled before the dragon Ryraxos took over. What exactly was that group? My character is a half-elf from Jia, so I am trying to figure out if my family would have been for or against them.

In the present-day Qamis setting, the Revolutionary Council was the short-lived government that deposed the Keyberos dynasty, which had ruled Edrad for four centuries. The notes describe the Council as 'squabbling' and largely ineffective. Their takeover was the catalyst for the thirty-year Ryraxian War. Interestingly, it was an alliance between the Artklan Empire and Qis'mos that formed specifically to depose this Council and restore the Keyberos. As a half-elf from Jia, your family is likely part of the nobility, as Edran nobles are traditionally required to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. Since the Council was the group that ousted the elven-blood nobility, your family was likely their target rather than their supporters.

That makes sense. So my family probably lost their status during the revolution. Give me some local color for Jia. What was the vibe during the Council years versus now that the Keyberos are back on the throne?

Jia is the cultural heart of Qamis, situated on a peninsula in the Central Sea. During the Revolutionary Council years, it was a period of intense political instability that invited foreign intervention. Then came Ryraxos, the bronze dragon who was initially hailed as a deliverer from defeat but ended up ruling as an overlord for twenty-five years. Now that the war is over and Ryraxos has been exiled to the moon Secundus, the Keyberos dynasty is back. However, the notes mention it is much easier to sign an edict reversing thirty years of change than it is to actually enforce it. The current 'color' of Jia is likely a city attempting to reclaim its historical prestige while dealing with the scars of a three-decade war and a population that has lived through three different regimes.

I like the idea of a family trying to 'reclaim' a ruined estate. Did the notes mention any specific leaders of the Revolutionary Council? I want to see if I can name an old rival who might still be around as a 'blank space' in the bureaucracy.

The campaign notes do not list any specific names for the members of the Revolutionary Council; they are currently one of the 'blank spaces' the DM has left open. This gives you a great opportunity to invent a name for a rival as part of your backstory. We know the Council was deposed roughly thirty years ago at the start of the war, so any survivors would be quite old now or perhaps have children who held onto power during the Ryraxian era. Since the Keyberos find their new edicts hard to enforce, a former Council family might still hold a bureaucratic post in Jia, frustrating your family's efforts to get their land back.

I've been looking into the schism between Qama and the city of Qis regarding the High Priest of Bahamut—especially with the drow appointing their own candidate, Qe'lth IV. But I'm curious about the religious landscape in the northeast. Who exactly are the Dragoncallers of Forthyr, and do they have a stake in the southern Bahamut orthodoxies, or is their support of the Dragon Emperor sect something else entirely?

In the present era of Qamis, the Dragoncaller dynasty of Forthyr is led by Shanlong VI, the latest in a long line of sorcerer-kings. Historically, this dynasty was dragonborn, but the notes indicate that their draconic bloodlines have since run thin. Forthyr is a massive nation in the northeast, and while it isn't directly involved in the schism between the drow-controlled Qama (holy city of Bahumat) and the exiled candidate Qachu II in Qis, its internal religious life is quite distinct. Forthyr is described as somewhat backwards compared to the central continent. The prominent religious sect there calls for the return of the ancient Dragon Emperors, a movement that receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty. While this sect shares a draconic focus, it is separate from the institutional Church of Bahamut in Qis'mos. Following the end of the Ryraxian War, the Dragoncallers are finding it increasingly difficult to ignore the affairs of the southwest after decades of isolation, though their primary loyalty remains to their own sorcerous lineage and the potential return of the old emperors.

Hey! I'm looking over the Qamis campaign notes for our weekend session. I saw the entry on Ryraxos and I'm obsessed with the fact that he was an artillery commander. That is such a high-IQ move for a flying unit. Can I get an executive summary on this dragon? Is he still the big bad we need to worry about?

It is a fantastic tactical detail! Most dragons rely on Frightful Presence, but Ryraxos understood logistics. Here is the executive summary on Ryraxos:

*   **Profile**: A bronze dragon, relatively young (around 60 years old).
*   **Rise to Power**: He didn't start as a king; he started as an artillery officer. He used his flight and strength to carry heavy guns to positions no one else could reach, which turned the tide for Edrad when they were facing a total loss against Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire.
*   **Reign**: After saving Edrad, he stayed in power for about 25 years as a 'dragon overlord' before finally being defeated.
*   **Current Status**: He is officially out of the game. He accepted exile to Secundus, our second moon, after his defeat. The notes explicitly say he will play no role in the current campaign.

While he was formidable, he wasn't an ancient, world-ending titan—the DM notes he was less dangerous than a well-coordinated artillery battery or a high-level adventuring party. He just had the vision to use his kit effectively.

Wait, he accepted exile to the moon? That is metal as hell. Is Secundus even habitable? And since the war lasted thirty years and just ended, I'm guessing the power vacuum he left is why we're starting in a place like Rathakos?

You nailed the geopolitical impact. The 'Thirty Year War' just wrapped up, and even though the Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne in Edrad, the continent is a mess of 'legacy code' that doesn't work anymore. 

As for Secundus, it is a retrograde moon with a dark iron surface, about 0.3 LD away from the planet. Whether it's 'habitable' in the traditional sense is one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't filled in, but since Ryraxos is a shapeshifting dragon, he might have ways to survive that humans don't. 

You're spot on about the power vacuum. Rathakos is a brand new nation formed from the 'Red Mountain Republic' after the Edran occupation collapsed following Ryraxos's defeat. It is currently governed by a Regent (John Dunne) because they can't even find a Prince to take the job. You'll likely be dealing with the fallout of his reign—specifically 'old dragon cults' in the mountains—rather than the dragon himself.

hi! I'm new to D&D and my DM said I should look into the Church of Bahamut for my character's background, but it sounds kind of... boring? Is it just a generic 'be good and help people' thing or is there actually something interesting happening with them in Qamis?

Welcome to your first campaign! It's understandable to be skeptical about the 'holy church' trope, but in the Qamis setting, the Church of Bahamut is actually in the middle of a massive, messy political crisis. It's less about sitting in pews and more about a major religious civil war. To start, the name is pronounced 'Ba-ha-mut,' and the 'Q' in names like Qis'mos is pronounced like 'Kh.' Most of the church's power is centered in a region called Qis'mos, which has some of the richest land on the continent.
Okay, so they're rich and they're in Qis-mos (Khiz-mos, got it). But what's the 'messy crisis' part? Is it just like, two guys arguing over a book?

It's a bit more dramatic than that. In the current era of Qamis, the holy city of the church—called Qama—is actually occupied by the drow. During the Ryraxian War, the nation of Artkos recruited drow as allies, and now they live in surface enclaves. The drow in Qama didn't destroy the city, but they did something arguably worse for the church: they appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This caused a huge schism. There is a rival High Priest named Qachu II operating out of the city of Qis, but he doesn't have any of the official holy relics because the drow have them in Qama. So, depending on who you talk to, the 'true' leader of the faith is either a drow appointee or a guy in exile without his symbols of office.

Wait, the drow are just... hanging out in the holy city and appointing priests? That's actually kind of cool. Does my character have to pick a side? And what do they actually do besides fight over who's the boss?

Picking a side is definitely a great hook for a new character! You could be a loyalist to Qachu II trying to recover the relics, or someone who thinks the drow-led church in Qama is actually more stable. Beyond the schism, the church is deeply tied to the history of the old Draconic empire. Qis'mos is one of the few places where the institutions of the dragons actually survived after they left. The church helps govern the region along with nobility and guildmasters. If you play a character from Rathakos—where the campaign usually starts—you'll find that people are very divided on the issue, especially since Rathakos is right on the border and has its own problems with old dragon cults in the mountains.
That sounds way more interesting than I thought. Is there like a specific list of rules I have to follow, or can I just wing it as a 'complicated' believer?

You can definitely wing it as a complicated believer. The campaign notes don't actually list a strict set of 'commandments' or 'sins' yet—that's one of the 'blank spaces' the DM has left open. This means you can work with your DM to decide what your character's specific version of the faith looks like. Whether you're a devout knight trying to end the schism or a cynical adventurer who thinks both High Priests are just power-hungry, there's plenty of room for you to make the character your own without it being 'generic' or 'boring.'

Hey, I missed the last session of our Qaringil game and I'm a bit lost on the big picture. My character is a member of the Gondolier guild in the city, so I should probably know the basics of how the world and the city-state work. Can you give me a recap of the setting's current state?

Of course. Since we are in the Qaringil era, it is roughly 200 years before the events of the modern Qamis timeline. The political map looks very different right now. Central Qamis is dominated by the Holy Qis Empire, which rules from the city of Ath'r. To the west, the nation of Edrad is a rising power ruled by the Keyberos dynasty from their capital, Jia, and they are currently looking to expand their borders. To the northwest, Artkos is also growing under Queen Ezvia Arth I. Unlike the later era, nations like Rathakos and Acrokos do not exist yet.

Your home, Qaringil, is a unique island city-state built on a wizard-made isthmus between two enormous bridges. It is a republic of merchants and sailors, famous for its canals. As a member of the Gondolier guild, you are part of one of the Seven Great Guilds. Your guild is particularly important because, along with the Wool guild, you have historically remained independent from the influence of the 'Five Families' that dominate the city's politics.
That helps. Who are these 'Five Families' I keep hearing about, and who is actually running the show right now? My notes say something about the Exarch being ill.

The current Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro. He has held the position for 19 years, but he is currently on his deathbed, which is creating a lot of political tension. He is generally aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. 

The Five Families are the real power brokers in Qaringil:
1. Peixota (Dolphin sigil): They lead the Shipping Guild.
2. Manos (Hand sigil): They lead the Judicial Guild.
3. Qanto (Dove sigil): They recently lost control of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family called the Tiddwars.
4. Volmer (Crow sigil): They lead the Banking Guild.
5. Vitelli (Moth sigil): They lead the Silk Guild.

As a Gondolier, your guild handles bridge maintenance and canal transport. You are in a powerful position because the city relies on you for movement, and since the Gondolier guild isn't led by one of these five families, you have a bit more autonomy than, say, a worker in the Shipping or Silk guilds.
One more thing. Our navigator mentioned something about a moon that never moves to find our way back to the isthmus. How does that work? I thought moons always rose and set.

That is Primus, the first moon of Qaia. It is geostationary, which means it is magically anchored over a single spot on the equator (longitude 0). For anyone in your hemisphere, Primus is a fixed point in the sky that never rises or sets. 

Because it is stationary, navigators use it to solve the 'longitude problem' that people on Earth struggled with for centuries. By measuring the elevation of the pole star, you find your latitude. Then, by measuring the elevation of Primus, you can determine your longitude. If Primus is directly overhead, you are at the prime meridian. If it is low on the western horizon, you are far to the east. As a Gondolier working the Central Sea, you likely use Primus every night to verify your position without needing a complex clock or chronometer.

Hey, I'm building a character from Rathakos. My family was part of the old Red Mountain Republic before the split with Acrokos. I was looking at the map and saw a place called Freeport on that big southern island. What's the story there? The notes say people try not to look at the jungle—is it haunted or something?

In the present day of the Qamis campaign, the Red Mountain Republic is a relatively recent memory, having been the transitional government organized during the Edran occupation before the factional split that created the nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. Regarding Freeport, it is a city of exiles and pirates situated on the shore of the large, mostly unexplored island to the south. It's a haven for those who want to avoid the reach of the continental powers. The reason residents avoid looking at the jungle is that it presses right up against the city and remains a total mystery. There are persistent rumors that this island is where the dragons went after they departed the mainland. Currently, the DM has marked the interior of that island as a blank space, so the specific nature of what lies within the jungle is not yet defined.

Hey nanochat, I had to bail on last night's session right as we were getting into the weeds with the local clergy in Qis'mos. Can you give me a recap on the Church of Bahamut's deal in this setting? I'm trying to figure out if my Paladin needs to worry about faction standing or if there are mechanical implications for which 'High Priest' we back.

In the current Qamis era, the Church of Bahamut is in a state of major religious and political schism centered in Qis'mos. The core issue is the control of Qama, the holy city of Bahamut. During the late phases of the Ryraxian War, drow forces (who were recruited as allies by Artkos) occupied Qama. While they haven't destroyed the city, they have installed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This has created a split: you have the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV in the holy city, and a rival candidate, Qachu II, operating out of the city of Qis. Qachu II claims legitimacy but lacks the actual relics of office held in Qama.

Wait, so there's a drow High Priest of Bahamut? That sounds like a massive lore curveball. Does the party actually gain anything from the 'relics of office' if we side with the Qama faction, or is it just fluff? Also, how does this affect spellcasting or divine favor in the region?

The campaign notes don't specify direct mechanical buffs like stat bonuses or extra spell slots for holding the relics, but they are described as essential for the legitimacy of the High Priest's office. If your party is looking for political leverage in Qis'mos, those relics are the primary objective. Regarding the drow, remember that in Qamis, Q is pronounced like 'Kh'. The drow presence in Qama is a result of their alliance with Artkos during the war; they've maintained several surface enclaves, with the holy city being the most prominent. As for divine favor, the notes are silent on whether Bahamut (the deity) favors one side over the other, but the institutional Church is effectively broken in two. Siding with Qachu II in Qis might gain you favor with the traditionalists, but Qe'lth IV holds the actual seat of power.

Got it. One last thing—if we head toward the Red Mountains or Rathakos, does the Church still have that kind of pull? I'm trying to see if I can play these two priests against each other to get an edge in the borderlands.

The Church's 'enormous presence' is specifically tied to Qis'mos. Once you move into Rathakos or the Red Mountains, the dynamic shifts. Rathakos is a new nation currently governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a Prince; it's dealing with its own internal unrest and old dragon cults in the mountains. While the Bahamut schism might matter to individual pilgrims or diplomats there, the Church doesn't have the same institutional grip as it does in the Qis'mos heartland. If you want to leverage the schism for an advantage, Qis'mos is your best bet, as the Potentate there is elected by the clergy and guildmasters, making the identity of the 'true' High Priest a massive domestic political issue.

Yo! I've been geeking out over the NAVIGATION.md doc. This geostationary moon, Primus, is such a cool mechanical hook. What exactly is the 'sub-Primus point' and how does it actually change the game for a PC with the Navigator's Tools proficiency?

It is a fantastic worldbuilding detail! The sub-Primus point is the geographic location on Qaia's surface directly beneath Primus. Because Primus is geostationary and magically anchored, this point is fixed at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude—the literal center of the world's coordinate system. For a PC with Navigator's Tools, this is a game-changer because Primus serves as a permanent, motionless 'celestial lighthouse' for half the planet.

That is sick. So, if I'm running a nautical campaign, my players basically have a built-in GPS? Does that mean they can skip the typical Survival checks for getting lost at sea as long as they can see the sky?

Exactly! If they are in the 'near-side' hemisphere where Primus is visible, the 'longitude problem'—which was a massive hurdle for real-world historical sailors—is completely bypassed. Instead of needing a high-precision chronometer (which doesn't exist in Qamis), they just need to measure the elevation of Primus above the horizon. Mechanical-wise, finding your exact position becomes a simple geometric calculation rather than a series of high-DC checks to track time and speed. The only way they get truly 'lost' is if they cross into the far-side hemisphere where Primus is permanently below the horizon, or if heavy cloud cover obscures the moon.
I noticed the notes mention a 'primsine' function. Is that something I should actually give them as a handout? Like a table for their character's almanac?

That would be a great immersive touch! The notes describe the primsine(e) function as a permanent table that never needs updating because the orbital radius of Primus is fixed. You could give them a simplified table where they cross-reference the moon's elevation with their latitude (found via the Pole Star) to get their longitude. It turns a standard 'roll to not get lost' into an actual procedural part of the game. Just remember that if they're in the Rathakos region in the Qamis era, they're in a prime spot to use it, whereas if they're exploring the 'blank spaces' on the far side of the globe, they'll have to rely on much harder methods like lunar-distance tracking with Secundus or Quartus!

My drow character is staying in Qama after the war ended. I am trying to track the lunar cycles for some personal reasons. When is the next great triple-new alignment going to happen? I mean the one where the sky gets as dark as it possibly can.

In the current decade of the Qamis era (2253-2263), the absolute closest triple-new alignment occurs on May 24, 2262, at 03h PMT. During this event, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus will all be within 0.1% of a perfect new moon phase simultaneously. This is the deepest darkness of the decade. Since you are living in the holy city of Qama, you are on the hemisphere that sees Primus, the geostationary moon. Even during a triple-new alignment, Primus will still be a motionless fixture in your sky at magnitude -9.62 (roughly 1/18th the brightness of Earth's full moon), but the oscillating tides and the light from the other three inner moons will be at their absolute minimum. If your character was already on the surface a few years earlier, you would have seen similar near-perfect triple-new alignments on April 26 and September 21 in the year 2256.

I am looking for some inspiration for a regional language barrier in my own homebrew game, so I was looking at your Qaringil prequel notes. I want to have a scene where a merchant from the Holy Qis Empire capital of Ath'r tries to negotiate with some 'hillbillies' in Rathakos. Why would they have such a hard time understanding each other if they both speak Artklan?

One quick correction before we dive into the linguistics: in the Qaringil prequel era (set roughly 200 years before the main Qamis campaign), Rathakos does not exist yet. It is a new nation formed much later, following the Ryraxian War. In the Qaringil era, that mountainous region is technically part of the Holy Qis Empire's claims, but it is largely unadministered. 

However, the language problem you are looking for is a constant in this world. Whether in the prequel or the present day, national languages like Artklan, Edran, and Qis are not standardized. There is no central authority enforcing a single 'correct' way to speak. Instead, each nation has a 'prestige dialect'—the version spoken in the capital and by the ruling class—and dozens of regional variations. 

A merchant from the capital of Ath'r would speak the prestige Artklan dialect. A speaker from the Red Mountains (where Rathakos eventually forms) would speak a version of Artklan that has drifted significantly due to isolation. Without the 'standardizing' force of modern mass media or universal education, these dialects diverge so much that they can become mutually unintelligible, even though they technically share the same name.
That is exactly the kind of friction I want. Does the scholarship or the church not try to fix this? I saw that Old Draconic is used for treaties.

Old Draconic (also called Old Qis) is indeed the language of diplomacy, the academy, and high-level treaties. You can tell a word comes from Old Draconic if it has a 'q' without a 'u' in it—and remember, that 'q' is always pronounced like a 'kh'. 

While scholars use Old Draconic to ensure precision in legal documents, they don't necessarily try to 'fix' how commoners speak. The Church of Bahamut, centered in the holy city of Qama, is very influential in Qis'mos, but their presence doesn't seem to involve linguistic standardization. In fact, in the Qis'mos heartland, the local Qis language is the closest living relative to Old Draconic, which might even make them feel their specific regional way of speaking is superior to the 'debased' dialects found elsewhere. 

In Edrad, the half-elven nobility in the capital of Jia has its own prestige dialect of Edran, but even there, someone from the coastal cities like Etops or Qeth would sound quite different to a noble from the capital. The languages are essentially a patchwork of local identities rather than a unified national tool.
So if my players start in the modern Qamis era in Rathakos, they would speak a regional dialect and then struggle if they go to the capital of Artkos?

Exactly. In the Qamis present-day campaign, players generally start knowing the prestige dialect of their capital and one regional dialect. If a character from Rathakos travels to Ath'r to speak with High King Ezevir Arth, they might find the 'high' Artklan of the court incredibly stiff or confusing, while the king's guards might view the Rathakos character's speech as uneducated or 'backwards.' It is a great way to bake social tension and regional identity directly into the roleplay.

heyyoo, sorry i missed the last lore session! i am trying to wrap my head around the world for my character in Qaringil. i heard someone mention dragon cults in passing... are those a thing in the city, or should i look elsewhere for that kind of backstory?

No problem at all. Since we are playing in the Qaringil prequel era (roughly 200 years before the modern Qamis setting), the landscape for dragon cults is quite specific. In the city of Qaringil itself, you will not find an official dragon cult. The city is a merchant republic focused on trade, shipping, and its canals. However, because Qaringil is a city of exiles and adventurers, it is entirely possible for someone to have fled to the city from a cult elsewhere. If you want to root your backstory in dragon worship, you should look toward Forthyr or the Red Mountains. In Forthyr, there is a prominent religious sect that actively calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. They even get quiet encouragement from the ruling Dragoncaller dynasty, currently led by Shanlong I. You might also find them in the Red Mountains, which are technically claimed by the Holy Qis Empire but are mostly inhabited by non-state peoples who still follow the old ways.
That makes sense. If I am from Forthyr, how would people in Qaringil look at me? Also, what exactly do they believe? Is it like a secret society thing?

In Forthyr, it is not really a secret society; it is more of a public religious movement. They believe the dragons are the rightful rulers of the world and wait for their return to restore order. Forthyr is seen as a bit backward by the merchant elites of Qaringil, so a character from there might be viewed with a mix of curiosity and skepticism. Most people in Qaringil (where the Q is pronounced like a Kh) are more concerned with the Great Guilds like the Shipping or Banking guilds than with ancient draconic prophecy. If you were a member of such a sect, you might find Qaringil's focus on gold and commerce a bit hollow. Keep in mind that Qis'mos is another place where draconic institutions survived the longest, so there is a deep cultural memory of dragons there too, though it is more institutional than cult-like. Qaringil itself was actually built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, so the local dialect of the Islas language has a lot of Qis vocabulary.
Okay, cool. I think I'll go with a refugee from the Red Mountains who escaped a cult that was being pressured by the Holy Qis Empire. Does the city have an adventurer's guild I could join?

Absolutely. Qaringil has several 'minor' guilds that are very relevant to players, and the Adventurer Guild is one of them. There are also Mage and Alchemist guilds. Since the party is starting at 5th level, the DM's notes say you can already be a full member of any guild relevant to your class. Coming from the Red Mountains as a refugee works well—the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from the city of Ath'r) claims those mountains but does not effectively manage them, leading to a lot of friction with the local mountain communities and their 'old dragon cults.' You would have likely traveled across the Great Western Bridge to reach the city.
