Just be ready for the politics; the city is run by the Five Families—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—and they usually care more about their sigils and shipping lanes than the religious troubles of mountain folk.
HIII! I am totally new to D&D and I'm joining a Qamis game. My DM told me the sky is really busy in this world. Are there actually seven moons? That sounds like a lot to keep track of!

Welcome to the hobby! It can definitely feel like a lot at first. In the world of Qaia (pronounced Khaia, as Q makes a Kh sound), there are six moons that are clearly visible, plus a seventh one called Quintus that is essentially just a tiny speck of dust you wouldn't normally notice. The names are basically Latin numbers: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. Most people in the campaign setting of Qamis pay attention to the first four because they are bigger and closer, but Sextus and Septimus are the outer sentinels of the system.
Sextus and Septimus sound so far away. If they are the outer ones, do they just look like really bright stars, or can you actually see they are moons?

You can definitely see they are moons. They look like small, distinct discs in the sky. To give you an idea of scale, Sextus is about five times the size that Jupiter appears from Earth. It has an apparent diameter of about 4.1 arcminutes. Septimus is physically the same size as Sextus, but since it is further away, it looks a bit smaller, around 2.7 arcminutes. They are both dramatically brighter than any planet or star, but they aren't bright enough to light up the ground like the big inner moons do. They look like small, cold circles of light drifting through the stars.
My DM also mentioned something about them rising 'backwards' or moving the wrong way. Is that what 'retrograde' means? Does the sun do that too?

The sun still rises in the East and sets in the West, just like you would expect! However, Sextus and Septimus are 'retrograde' moons, which means their orbit is the opposite of the planet's rotation. While the sun and the larger moons like Tertius and Quartus rise in the East, Sextus and Septimus actually rise in the West and move across the sky toward the East. Because they are so far out, they move slowly—Sextus takes about 58 days to go through its phases, and Septimus takes about 106 days. People in Qamis often use Septimus as a long-term calendar body because its cycle is so steady and distinct compared to the chaotic rising and setting of the closer moons.

hi, i'm a drow who stayed on the surface in qama after the fighting stopped. i keep hearing people talk about how powerful artkos used to be. how did they get so big compared to a couple hundred years ago?

Welcome to the surface. Since you are staying in Qama, you are living in the traditional seat of the Church of Bahumut (pronounced Ba-hu-maht), which puts you right in the center of Qamisi history. About 200 years ago, during the era of the city-state Qaringil, Artkos was much smaller than it is today. Back then, it was mostly confined to its historical heartland in the northwest of the continent. It wasn't the dominant force; the central part of the continent was actually ruled by the Holy Qis Empire from the city of Ath'r.
So if the Holy Qis Empire was the big power back then, what happened to it? Did Artkos just wait for them to fail or did they take it over?

They took it over by force and diplomacy. In the Qaringil era, King Ezevir Arth II doubled the size of the Artkos holdings, and his daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I, pushed even further. Eventually, Artkos conquered Ath'r itself. Because Ath'r has been the capital of various central empires for centuries, holding it allowed Artkos to transition from a regional kingdom into the Artklan Empire. At its peak, the Empire ruled four-tenths of the continent and exerted massive influence over your current home, Qis'mos.

That is a lot of land. My people were recruited by Artkos as allies when they got desperate during the Ryraxian War, but now that the dragon is exiled to Secundus and the war is over, is Artkos still an empire? It sounds like things changed again.

It has changed significantly. The Artklan Empire technically no longer exists; it has been replaced by the Artklan League, which the High Kings of Artkos see as a pale shadow of their former glory. While High King Ezevir Arth still rules from Ath'r, his territory is split into disconnected enclaves. He has the northwest (West Artkos), the center (Ath'r), and a southwestern enclave around Kard. It is a fragile peace—many of the people in the Kard enclave speak Edran and actually welcomed the invasion twenty-eight years ago. The other members of the League are now only nominal subjects, clinging to a tenuous independence while Artkos tries to find a way to restore its old supremacy.

Hey, sorry I missed the last session. The group was talking about the northern borders and Forthyr came up. Since I'm playing a half-elven noble from the Keyberos court in Edrad, I figure I should probably know the history of our neighbors to the northeast. How does Forthyr look now compared to the Qaringil prequel era? Are the same people still in charge?

In both eras, Forthyr is ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty, but there are some significant changes in their status. In the Qaringil prequel era, roughly 200 years ago, the nation was ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong I. At that time, the dynasty was explicitly half-dragonborn. In the present Qamis era, the ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. The notes mention that the dragonborn bloodlines have run thin over the centuries, so the current dynasty no longer has that same physical dragonborn presence, though they are still sorcerer-kings.
Interesting. My character would definitely look down on a 'thinning' bloodline. Is the country still as isolated? I remember reading they were a bit backwards compared to the coastal culture of Jia.

The notes still describe Forthyr as somewhat backwards in the present day. One thing that hasn't changed is the presence of a religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. This sect receives tacit encouragement from the Shanlong dynasty in both the prequel and the present. However, their relationship with the rest of the continent has shifted. After the Ryraxian War ended, Forthyr found that they can no longer ignore the affairs of the southwest after decades of neglecting them. While Edrad was dealing with the Revolutionary Council and then Ryraxos, Forthyr stayed out of it, but now they are forced to pay attention to the new political map.

So as a Keyberos noble, I'm looking at a dynasty that's lasted 400 years in Edrad, while the Shanlongs have been up there even longer, just getting less 'draconic' over time. Is there any mention of how they handled the Ryraxian War? Did they help Edrad or just watch us struggle?

The campaign notes indicate Forthyr largely neglected the affairs of the southwest during the thirty years of the Ryraxian War. While your Edran ancestors were fighting Ryraxos or dealing with the Artklan League, the Dragoncallers remained in the northeast. It is only now, in the aftermath of the war and the restoration of the Keyberos line to the throne in Jia, that Forthyr is finding the situation impossible to ignore. They aren't specifically listed as combatants on either side, unlike Rathakos which fought for both.

So I am diving deep into the NAVIGATION.md file for the campaign. I am obsessed with this Primus moon. If it is geostationary and fixed at the sub-Primus point of 0 degrees North, 0 degrees East, that makes it the ultimate longitude reference, right? I am trying to run the primsine formula by hand for a hypothetical navigator. Can you walk me through why that sub-Primus point is so critical for the math?

You have it exactly right. The sub-Primus point is the geographic location on Qaia directly beneath the moon Primus (pronounced Kh-amis). Because Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit, it never moves relative to the surface. It stays fixed at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude.

This matters for the math because it simplifies the spherical law of cosines. In a standard coordinate system, finding the distance between two points involves the latitudes and longitudes of both. But since the sub-Primus point is on the equator (latitude 0), the formula collapses into: primsine(e) = cos(latitude) times cos(longitude).

Essentially, the sub-Primus point serves as a 'celestial pole' that happens to sit on the equator instead of the North Pole. If you know your latitude from the pole star, measuring the elevation of Primus gives you everything you need to solve for your longitude without needing a clock. It is a massive advantage for sailors on the near-side hemisphere.

Right, the rho value is 0.1511 because of the orbital radius of 42,160 km. I am trying to map out a route from the city of Qaringil to one of those 'blank spaces' on the map in the Central Sea. If I am at a latitude of 40 degrees North and Primus is at an elevation of 25 degrees in the sky, what is my longitude? Also, what are the exact coordinates for the islands the notes mention as being 'blank spaces'? I want to see if the math holds up for those locations.

Let's run the numbers for your first scenario. 

With an elevation (e) of 25 degrees:
1. cos(25) = 0.9063, so cos squared (e) = 0.8214.
2. sin(25) = 0.4226.
3. primsine(25) = (0.1511 * 0.8214) + (0.4226 * sqrt(1 - (0.1511^2 * 0.8214)))
4. primsine(25) approx 0.1241 + (0.4226 * 0.9953) = 0.5447.

Now, for your longitude (lambda) at latitude 40 degrees North:
cos(lambda) = primsine(25) / cos(40) 
cos(lambda) = 0.5447 / 0.7660 = 0.7111
lambda = arccos(0.7111) approx 44.7 degrees.

As for the second part of your question, the notes explicitly state that those island areas and many parts of the map are 'blank spaces' that the DM has not yet filled in. There are no coordinate tables for those locations in the current records. In fact, for the Qaringil era, the notes mention the DM might add more islands around the continent, but their exact positions are not defined. You would have to work with your DM to place your 'blank space' destination on the grid before you could finalize the navigation charts for it.

That makes sense. It is a literal blank space for me to fill in with my backstory. What about the far side of the planet though? The notes say Primus is invisible there. If I am a navigator in the 'Westron' continent or that big southern island beyond Freeport, am I just totally out of luck for longitude?

For the far-side hemisphere, you are exactly like an Earth navigator before the invention of the marine chronometer. Since Primus is permanently below the horizon, you cannot use the primsine method. You would have to rely on dead reckoning, stellar transits, or 'lunar distance' methods using the other moons like Secundus or Quartus, which is significantly more difficult.

Regarding Westron and the far side, the notes describe them as 'exotic' or 'mostly unexplored.' Westron is specifically called out as a place where the DM hasn't prepared material yet, and the big island to the south of Freeport is also largely unknown. If those lands are on the far side of Qaia, their civilizations would have developed entirely different navigation traditions—likely much more focused on complex star catalogues since they lack the 'fixed' guide of Primus.
Hello! I'm rolling up a character for the Qaringil game—a half-elven noble from the Keyberos line in Edrad. My DM mentioned someone named Exarch Giovanni Pesaro. Who is he exactly?

Welcome to the setting! It is important to note first that Giovanni Pesaro belongs to the Qaringil prequel era, set about 200 years before the Qamis present day. In this era, the Keyberos dynasty has only been on the Edran throne for about two centuries, and they are still actively expanding royal control.

Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch—the elected head of state—of the city-state of Qaringil. He has held the post for 19 years, which is a significant tenure for their republic, but he is currently described as being on death's doorstep. Unlike your character's high-born Edran lineage, Pesaro comes from a relatively minor family in Qaringil, though he is politically savvy.
If he's from a minor family, how does he keep control of a city of 'nautical robber barons'? My character would probably look down on a commoner ruler, even if we need their bridges.

Your character might indeed find the Qaringil system 'incomprehensible,' as the notes describe it. While Pesaro's own family lacks the ancient prestige of the Keyberos line, he maintains power through alliances with the 'Five Families'—the true heavyweights of the city. 

Specifically, the Exarch is closely aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. The Peixotas lead the Shipping Guild (their sigil is the Dolphin), and the Manos family leads the Judicial Guild (sigil of the Hand). By keeping these powerful merchant interests happy, a minor-family Exarch like Pesaro can govern effectively. It is a world of commercial wealth rather than landed titles or elven bloodlines. In Qaringil, the elites are defined by trade in silk, wool, and banking rather than ancestral land.
That makes sense. If he's dying, my family in Jia would be worried about who takes over next. Are there any clear frontrunners among those Five Families, or is it going to be a mess?

The situation is definitely primed for a struggle. Beyond the Peixota and Manos families who support the current Exarch, you have several other major players:

1. The Qanto family (sigil: Dove) recently lost leadership of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family called Tiddwar. They are likely looking for a way back into power.
2. The Volmer family (sigil: Crow) leads the Banking Guild and is involved in shipping.
3. The Vitelli family (sigil: Moth) leads the Silk Guild.

There is also the Tiddwar family itself, a prominent gnomish house that currently leads the Mercantile Guild and is aligned with Pesaro. 

The notes don't name a specific successor yet—that is a 'blank space' your DM might fill or let your party influence. From an Edran perspective, any instability in Qaringil is a threat to the Great Western Bridge and the flow of trade into the Central Sea, so your noble family would likely be watching the Vitelli and Volmer families very closely to see if they try to pivot the city's foreign policy away from Edran interests.

I am absolutely obsessed with the vibe of a 'republic of canals' for my own homebrew world! I saw a mention of Exarch Giovanni Pesaro in the Qaringil notes and I have to know more. Is he basically like a Doge? What's his deal?

He is very much in that mold! Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch of the city-state of Qaringil during the prequel era—which is set about 200 years before the 'modern' Qamis campaign. Qaringil is a massive island city of 100,000 people, criss-crossed with canals and dominating a giant wizard-made bridge. It's described as an 'incomprehensible republic' where the elites are merchant princes rather than landowners. Pesaro has been the Exarch for 19 years, but the current 'hook' for him is that he is considered to be on death's doorstep. It creates this incredible tension because everyone is waiting to see which of the great merchant families will make a move for his seat!
A dying leader in a city of merchant guilds? That is such a perfect campaign setup! Does he have a massive family of his own to protect his legacy, or is he a lone wolf?

Actually, he’s a bit of a political tightrope walker. He comes from a minor family, not one of the 'Five Families' that truly run the city. He’s kept his position for nearly two decades by being a master of alliances. Specifically, he is closely aligned with the Peixota family (who run the Shipping Guild and have a dolphin sigil) and the Manos family (who run the Judicial Guild and use a hand sigil). He even helped a gnomish family called the Tiddwars snatch control of the Mercantile Guild away from the powerful Qanto family. Just a heads up if you're using the names: Qaringil and Qanto are pronounced with a 'Kh' sound, as 'Q' in this setting comes from Old Draconic!

The Tiddwars! That's a great name. So Pesaro is basically the glue holding these rival guilds together while he's fading away? I love the idea of the 'Five Families' circling like sharks. Who are the other big players he has to worry about?

It is a total shark tank! Beyond the Peixota, Manos, and Qanto (who are probably fuming about losing that guild leadership), you have the Volmer family, who lead the Banking Guild and have a crow sigil—always a dangerous group to owe money to. Then there are the Vitelli, who lead the Silk Guild and have a moth sigil. These families aren't just rich; they have elven ancestry and control the most vital parts of the economy, like shipping and banking. Since Pesaro is from a minor house, his death is going to leave a massive power vacuum that these five 'apex' families are definitely going to try to fill. It’s the ultimate setting for a political intrigue or 'cloak and dagger' style game!

Hey there. I am a DM working on some moon-based lore for my own table and I am looking for inspiration. I heard your Qamis setting has a dragon named Ryraxos who was exiled to a moon? Which moon is it, and what is the story there?

In the Qamis setting (pronounced Khamis), Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who was a central figure in the thirty-year Ryraxian War that recently ended. He was originally an artillery officer who rose to power and eventually ruled as a dragon overlord for over two decades. Following his defeat, he accepted exile to Secundus, which is the planet's second moon. He is roughly sixty years old and, like all dragons in this world, can shape-shift. Since his exile, he no longer plays an active role in the current campaign era, effectively removing him from the political board of the continent.
Interesting. Why Secundus specifically? There are seven moons in the system, right? Was there a strategic or physical reason they chose the second one instead of the first or something further out?

The notes do not explicitly state the political reasoning for choosing Secundus over the others, but the physical differences between the moons provide a lot of context. Primus (the first moon) is geostationary and magically anchored; it is a critical navigation tool used to calculate longitude. Placing an exiled dragon there might interfere with its navigational utility. Secundus is a much more formidable physical environment—it is a 'near-iron' body with a density of 6,689 kg per cubic meter and a very dark surface (albedo 0.06). While it is nearly as large in the sky as Earth's moon (0.90x Luna), it is quite dim. It also has a retrograde orbit, meaning it rises in the west and is slowly spiraling inward over geological time. The outer moons like Sextus and Septimus are much smaller and farther away, perhaps making them less secure or too difficult to reach for the terms of the exile.

An iron moon? That is a cool aesthetic. Does the fact that it is a 'dark' moon affect how people see him from the ground? And is there any special event involving that moon that might have timed his exile?

Because of its low albedo, Secundus is about one magnitude dimmer than Earth's moon, providing only about 2/5 of the illumination. Even at full moon, it would look like a massive, dark, metallic sphere in the sky. Regarding timing, the campaign notes mention 'Triple-moon alignment eras' where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus all align. For example, in the year 2253 on February 13th, there was a near-perfect triple-full moon alignment. While the notes do not explicitly link Ryraxos's exile date to one of these alignments, the 12-year cycle of full-moon and new-moon eras is a major part of the celestial calendar. Whether his exile involved a specific alignment is a 'blank space' the DM hasn't filled yet, though the physics of the system make such an event a likely candidate for powerful magic or treaties.

Hi! My friend is starting a game in Qamis and it sounded cool, but they mentioned there are 'blank spaces' in the world guide. As a fantasy reader, I usually expect everything to be mapped out already! What's actually missing? They mentioned something about a place called Westron and a big island in the south?

Welcome to Qamis! In this setting, 'blank spaces' are intentional gaps where the DM hasn't established the lore yet, often so players can help fill them in with their backstories. Regarding Westron, it is a massive continent to the west that is described as exotic with different religious traditions—most there do not worship Bahamut—but the specific nations and geography are currently a blank space. The large island to the south is similarly mysterious; while the pirate city of Freeport sits on the shore, the deep jungle pressing against it remains unexplored. Some believe this is where the dragons vanished to, but it's officially unwritten for now. There are also several unmapped islands in the Central Sea and gaps in the mainland interior like the Red Mountains. It's quite different from a finished novel, as it gives you room to invent. Also, just a heads-up for your first session: the 'Q' in names like Qamis or Qis is pronounced like 'Kh'!

Hey there. I've been working the canals for the Gondolier Guild in Qaringil my whole life, but some of the silk merchants from the Vitelli family keep talking about the 'great city of Jia' like it's the center of the world. Where exactly is this place?

Jia is indeed a major hub. It is the coastal capital of Edrad, which is currently the most politically and economically dominant nation on the continent of Qamis. While you're used to the island life in Qaringil, Jia sits on a large peninsula that juts out into the Central Sea. Because of its location and the power of the Keyberos dynasty, it's considered the most culturally influential city in the region.
A peninsula? Must be a lot of docks then. How does it compare to Qaringil? We've got 100,000 people here and the Great Western Bridge to look after.

Jia is significantly larger and serves as the seat of the Edran royalty. While Qaringil is a republic focused on commerce and controlling the isthmus bridges, Jia is the heart of an expanding kingdom. The city's influence is so great that Edran is one of the main languages of the continent. One big difference you'd notice is the social structure; while Qaringil is run by merchant families and guilds like yours, Jia is ruled by a very specific half-elven nobility.
The merchants did mention something about 'half-elves.' Is it true you have to be part-elf to even have a title there?

Precisely. In Edrad, the nobility is strictly half-elven. To hold a noble title, a person must be at least one-quarter elf but no more than three-quarters elf. This elite class has been led by the Keyberos dynasty for about two centuries now, and they've been steadily expanding their royal control across the mainland. For a gondolier from the islands, the sight of Jia's peninsula would be quite a change from the volcanic rock and wizard-made bridges of your home.

Hey, I missed last night's session. The DM mentioned we might be doing some flashback or prequel stuff in Qaringil soon. Since my character is a drow who just decided to stay on the surface after the Ryraxian War ended, I'm kind of wondering why I should care about stuff from 200 years ago. Does it actually link up with the Qamis campaign we're in now?

It definitely links up, especially for a drow character. While the Qaringil prequel is set about 200 years before the current Qamis era, it establishes the roots of the world you're living in now. For one, the Holy Qis (pronounced Khis) Empire was the dominant power back then, ruling from Ath'r. In your current time, that empire is gone, replaced by the Artklan League and the restored Keyberos dynasty in Edrad. Seeing Qaringil's history helps explain how the political map shattered into the nations you know today, like Rathakos and Acrokos, which didn't even exist 200 years ago. For a drow specifically, the prequel deals heavily with the Church of Bahamut and the 'Old Qis' institutions. In the present, your people are occupying Qama—the holy city of Bahamut—which has caused a massive religious schism between the drow-appointed high priest Qe'lth IV and the rival candidate Qachu II. Understanding the 'golden age' of that church in the prequel helps explain why the surface world is so tense about drow being in Qama now.

That makes sense. So Qama was a big deal even back then? I noticed my character background mentions a 'Holy Qis Empire' that isn't on our current map. If I'm playing a drow who was recruited as an ally by Artkos during the war, would my ancestors have even been around Qaringil or Ath'r?

In the Qaringil era, the drow weren't a visible presence on the surface yet. They were only recruited as allies by Artkos much later, during the desperation of the Ryraxian War that just ended. So, your ancestors were likely still in the Underdark during the prequel. However, the Holy Qis Empire is vital context because it occupied the central continent where Artkos is now. In the prequel, the Empire is described as 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire,' but it held the territory your Artkos allies are currently trying to reclaim. Playing the prequel lets you see the 'before' state of the surface world your character has just joined. You'll see the origins of the Great Guilds in Qaringil and the rise of the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr. It gives weight to the 'thirty years of change' the notes mention—you'll see exactly what was lost or corrupted before the dragon Ryraxos ever showed up.

Okay, I'm starting to see the appeal. What about the moons? I know my drow is still getting used to the surface sky. Are the moons any different in the prequel? I heard something about the second moon being important lately.

The moon system itself—Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus—is physically the same in both eras because the physics are constant. However, the cultural meaning of Secundus has changed drastically. In the Qaringil prequel, Secundus is just the second moon: a dark, iron-rich retrograde moon that rises in the west. In your current Qamis campaign, Secundus is now the prison of the dragon Ryraxos. He was exiled there after his defeat. So, while players in the prequel just see a dark moon in the sky, your drow character looks up at Secundus and sees the literal cage of the tyrant who reshaped the continent. Also, Primus is still geostationary over the same spot, which is why your character can find their longitude so easily on the surface without a clock, provided they're in the right hemisphere.

Look, I'm trying to get into this campaign but it feels like way too much. Six moons, five merchant families, different eras? It's a lot. Just tell me about the Vitelli family and why they have a moth for a sigil. Is it actually important or just more fluff?

I completely understand. The world of Qaia has a lot of moving parts, especially when you're looking at the Qaringil prequel era where the Vitelli family is prominent. To keep it simple: the Vitelli family is one of the 'Five Families' that dominate the city-state of Qaringil. Their moth sigil isn't just fluff; it's a direct reference to their primary source of power: they lead the Silk Guild. Since silk comes from the cocoons of silk moths, the sigil represents their control over that entire industry.
Okay, a silk moth. I guess that's logical. But why do I need to care about them specifically? Are they just another group of rich humans?

In the Qaringil setting, they are a major political pillar. While they are 'mostly human,' like the other four families, they also have some elven ancestry. They don't just do silk, either; they have interests in shipping and general mercantile trade. If your character is dealing with the city's Great Guilds or trying to move cargo through the Central Sea, you'll likely run into their influence. They are part of the merchant elite that essentially runs the republic while the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is nearing the end of his life.
Wait, you mentioned shipping too. Don't the Peixota people do that? This is exactly what I mean by complicated. Everyone seems to do everything.

That is a fair critique. There is definitely overlap because these families are all competing for the same gold. While the Peixota family (the Dolphin sigil) leads the Shipping Guild, the Vitelli family is 'involved' in shipping to move their own silk and trade goods. Think of it less like a list of names to memorize and more like a corporate rivalry. The Vitelli family leads Silk, the Peixota family leads Shipping, and they likely butt heads whenever their business interests cross on the docks. It's meant to give you specific people to bargain with—or get into trouble with—depending on what your party is trying to accomplish.

Hey, spent most of my life in the Dalelands and Greyhawk, so I am used to the classic frontier tropes. Looking at this Qamis setting, I heard Rathakos is the main hub for starting a campaign. What makes it such a hotspot for adventurers compared to somewhere like Edrad?

Rathakos is definitely the frontier of the Artklan League. While Edrad is the cultural and political heavyweight with a half-elven nobility that has been around for four centuries, Rathakos is basically a brand new country trying to find its footing. It sits right in the foothills of the Red Mountains, which the campaign notes describe as one of the most dangerous and underexplored parts of the whole continent. Because the mountains are so wild, there is a deep cultural tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship there. Plus, the political situation is a bit of a mess right now—they are technically a Princedom but they do not actually have a Prince. A guy named Regent John Dunne is running things while the Grand Council argues about who to put on the throne. That kind of instability always means there is plenty of work for people with swords and spells.

A kingdom without a king? Sounds like a recipe for side quests. You mentioned the Red Mountains are underexplored—is that just because of the terrain, or is there something nastier up there? I saw a mention of dragon cults.

It is a bit of both. The terrain is brutal, but the cults are a major factor. Some of the isolated mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults, which is a big headache for the new government. You have to remember the history here: Rathakos only exists because of the chaos of the Ryraxian War. It used to be part of the Red Mountain Republic under Edran occupation, but when the Edrans left, the local assembly purged the pro-Edran faction and split the territory into Rathakos and Acrokos. There is still a lot of internal unrest, especially in the Southern Reaches that they took from Edrad. If you go back 200 years to the Qaringil era, Rathakos did not even exist. Back then, it was just unadministered territory claimed by the Holy Qis Empire. So between the unexplored peaks, the dragon cultists, and the border disputes with Edrad, there is no shortage of trouble to get into.
The Qis Empire sounds like it was a big deal. Does the language have anything to do with why all these places start with Q? Like Qama or Qis'mos?

Exactly. The Q is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound, and it comes from Old Draconic, which is also called Old Qis. It is the language of high-level treaties and scholarly work. In the current Qamis era, the nation of Qis'mos is the heartland where those old draconic institutions lasted the longest. If you are into the lore, those place names with the 'q without a u' are a direct link to that ancient history. Even though the dragons are mostly gone—with Ryraxos himself currently exiled to Secundus, our second moon—the linguistic and religious influence, like the Church of Bahamut, is everywhere.

Look, I will be honest, this whole Qamis setting feels way too complicated. You have six moons, math-heavy navigation, and 'Q' is pronounced 'Kh' for some reason. But I was reading the Edrad notes and it says the dragon Ryraxos was an 'artillery officer.' Why would a dragon be doing math for cannons instead of just breathing fire on people? Seems like an over-engineered detail.

I hear you on the complexity—the notes for Qamis definitely lean into the technical side of things like the orbital mechanics and linguistics. Regarding Ryraxos, the notes explain that dragons make excellent artillery officers specifically because of their utility in moving the equipment. Because a dragon can help carry the heavy guns, they provide a level of mobility for heavy ordnance that a standard army lacks. Additionally, since all dragons in this setting can shapeshift, they can function within a standard military hierarchy and command structure rather than just acting as a wild beast on the battlefield.

If they are just carrying the guns, that makes them a pack animal or a mule, not an officer. Why does the text specifically call Ryraxos an artillery officer? Is it just because he can turn into a human and talk to the generals?

It is because of the combination of their physical power and their intelligence. In the Qamis era, Ryraxos's rapid rise to power actually began with his exploits in this specific role. The notes mention that while a dragon is formidable, a young bronze dragon like Ryraxos—who was only about sixty years old during the war—is roughly equivalent in power to a well-commanded artillery battery or a competent adventuring party. By serving as an officer, he wasn't just a 'mule'; he was a tactical commander who could personally ensure his battery was positioned in spots that were normally unreachable, and his shapeshifting allowed him to coordinate those strikes with the rest of the Edran military.

so the dm said there are blank spaces on the map. is he just lazy? i don't want to write a whole cool backstory for my first character and then have the dm just change it because he finally decided what goes there. can i actually fill those in?

It is actually an intentional part of the design for both the Qamis present-day and the Qaringil prequel. The campaign notes explicitly state that these blank spaces are there for you to help fill with your backstory. For example, the notes mention that the continent of Westron is very large but has no material prepared for it yet, and there are many islands around the continent that haven't been named. It is a way for you to have a permanent impact on the world of Qaia from your very first session.

idk it feels risky. like what if i make up a city and it doesn't match the weird pronunciation rules? he kept saying Q is pronounced like Kh or whatever. will it break the game if i don't use the old draconic stuff?

You won't break the game. While Q is pronounced like Kh (derived from Old Draconic or Old Qis), the notes clarify that languages like Edran, Artklan, and Qis are not standardized. There are major regional variations; for instance, someone from the capital of Artkos might not even understand the Artklan spoken in Rathakos. This gives you plenty of room to create a culture or a city with its own unique feel. The Old Draconic 'q without u' naming style is mostly for scholarly treaties and ancient places, not necessarily for every new location you might invent.
okay i guess. so if i said my family was from an island that nobody knows about yet, near that pirate city freeport, is that allowed? the map looked kinda empty down there.

That is a perfect use of a blank space. The notes describe the big island to the south as 'mostly unexplored' beyond the city of Freeport, with a jungle that the residents are afraid to look too closely at. The DM also mentions specifically wanting to add more islands around the continent. Placing your family on a hidden island in that region fits the lore perfectly—it maintains the mystery of the southern reaches while giving you a clear starting point for your character's journey.

Hey, I'm sorry I missed the last session! I'm still working on my character for the Qamis campaign. I'm planning on a half-elven noble from Edrad, specifically from the Keyberos side of things. Since I missed the lore dump, can you summarize the current state of the Edran nobility after the war? Also, the DM mentioned 'blank spaces' on the map—can I actually use one of those to place my family's estate?

No problem! In the present-day Qamis era, the thirty-year Ryraxian War has just ended. The dragon Ryraxos, who ruled Edrad for about twenty-five years after the Revolutionary Council was deposed, has been exiled to Secundus (Qaia's second moon). The Keyberos dynasty is back on the throne in the capital, Jia. For an Edran noble, the big news is the 'Restoration.' The government is trying to undo thirty years of changes with legal edicts, but it's proving very difficult to enforce. Regarding your heritage, the notes specify that Edran nobility must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf to maintain their status. As for the blank spaces, the DM's notes explicitly state that there are many gaps on the map and that players are encouraged to help fill them with their backstories. You can definitely invent a location or a minor house for your estate.

That's perfect. I'm thinking of a house that stayed loyal to the Keyberos line during the war. If the war was thirty years, my character's father would have seen the whole thing. If I put my estate in a blank space near the Red Mountains, how does that fit with the new nation of Rathakos? I saw that mentioned in the notes.

Placing your estate near the Red Mountains puts you right in the middle of the current political tension. Rathakos was recently formed from the former 'Red Mountain Republic' after the Edran military withdrew. It's a mountainous border state currently governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a Prince. If your family estate is in a blank space right on that border, you might be dealing with the 'Southern Reaches'—territory that Rathakos annexed from Edrad. The notes mention there is a lot of internal unrest there, especially from people who still follow the old dragon cults or remain pro-Edran. It's a very high-conflict area for a noble backstory. Just a quick reminder on terminology: 'Q' in this setting is always pronounced like 'Kh,' so names like Qamis or Qeth have that 'Kh' sound from Old Draconic.

Oh, that's a cool detail. So my family might be Edran nobles whose lands are technically being claimed by Rathakos now? That's a great hook. I noticed the notes mention that Ryraxos was an artillery commander—does that mean my family might have fought against his cannons?

Exactly. Ryraxos wasn't just a dragon; he was a master of logistics and artillery. Dragons in this world are used as artillery officers because they can move the heavy guns themselves. Since your family was loyal to the Keyberos dynasty, they likely faced those batteries during the long war. The conflict was brutal and lasted three decades, so your family's estate might still be showing the scars of that bombardment, especially if you were near the strategic passes of the Red Mountains. Since that area is one of those 'blank spaces,' you could work with the DM to decide exactly which mountain peak or valley your family holds and what kind of state the fortifications are in now that the 'Restoration' is trying to reclaim order.

Just finished the arccos for a Primus elevation of 42.2 at 30 degrees North latitude. If I am looking west, that puts me right at 30 East, assuming the primsine(42.2) is roughly 0.7504. It is incredible that the longitude problem is just a table lookup here. If I were to sail all the way to Westron, though, I would lose the 'Fixed Moon' entirely once I pass the horizon limit, right? Does that lack of a celestial anchor affect their religion? Is it the same Bahamut-heavy worship we see in Qis'mos?

Your navigation math is perfectly accurate. At 30 degrees North with Primus at 42.2 degrees elevation, you are indeed at 30 degrees East longitude. You are also correct about the visibility limit: because Primus is geostationary over the prime meridian (0,0), it disappears from view once you move roughly 81.3 degrees away from that point. If Westron is located on the far-side hemisphere, Primus would be permanently below the horizon for them. Regarding religion, the campaign notes for the Qamis era state that Westron is quite different from the central continent. Most importantly, the people of Westron are mostly not Bahamut worshippers, which sets them apart from the heavy religious influence seen in Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos) and the holy city of Qama. However, the specific details of what they do worship are currently marked as a blank space that the DM hasn't filled in yet.

Interesting. So while Qis'mos is dealing with the schism between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II, Westron is just doing its own thing. Since the notes mention Westron is 'exotic' and lacks Bahamut worship, do they have anything like the Dragoncallers in Forthyr? Or maybe a different celestial-based faith since they have to rely on the moving moons like Secundus or Tertius for tracking time?

The notes do not mention a Dragoncaller dynasty or similar sorcerer-kings for Westron; that specific lineage belongs to the nation of Forthyr in the northeast of Qamis. While it is a logical leap to think they might focus on the moving moons—like Tertius, which defines the eight-day week—the records are silent on the specifics of Westron's faith. The only definitive detail provided is that they represent a religious departure from the Bahamut-centric traditions of the Qis heartland. Because the DM has identified Westron as a large, mostly unexplored 'blank space,' its religious practices and whether they are tied to navigation on the far-side hemisphere (where they must use complex stellar transits or lunar-distance methods) remain open for future development.

Hey! I missed the last session and the group chat is blowing up about the moons. I'm a bit confused—I thought there were only three moons? Also, someone mentioned Ryraxos is living on the one that doesn't move in the sky. Was that the same back in the Qaringil prequel era?

Welcome back! There is actually a bit more to the sky than three moons. Qaia has six main moons: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. There is also a trace particle called Quintus, but it is effectively massless. The 'three' you heard about might be Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus, as they are the ones that drive the daily tides.

Regarding Ryraxos, there are two things to clarify. First, Ryraxos is exiled to Secundus, not the stationary moon. Secundus is a retrograde moon that rises in the west and moves across the sky. The moon that stays still is Primus, which is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. Second, this exile only applies to the 'Qamis' present-day campaign. Ryraxos is only about sixty years old, so in the 'Qaringil' prequel era set 200 years ago, he hasn't even been born yet. Back then, the Holy Qis Empire was still ruling from Ath'r and the map looked completely different.
Oh, okay. So Primus is the stationary one. If it doesn't move, how do people use it for navigation? I think our DM mentioned something called a 'primsine' calculation?

That is exactly right. Because Primus is geostationary and fixed directly over the equator at the prime meridian (0 degrees Latitude, 0 degrees Longitude), it serves as a perfect reference point. 

To find your position, you first find your latitude using the pole star. Then, you measure the elevation of Primus above the horizon. Since Primus's position in the sky only changes based on where you are standing on the planet, you can use the 'primsine' function—a geometric calculation based on the moon's fixed altitude—to determine your longitude. It essentially solves the longitude problem without needing a chronometer, though it only works in the hemisphere where Primus is visible.
Wait, you said Ryraxos is on Secundus now. Was he the one who anchored Primus in the first place? I'm trying to figure out if he's part of that 'Old Qis' draconic empire everyone talks about.

Actually, Ryraxos is not connected to the anchoring of Primus or the old empire. Primus is described as being 'circular by construction,' implying it was placed there by powerful magic or ancient creators long ago. 

Ryraxos is a bronze dragon who rose to power much more recently. He started as an artillery commander and eventually took over Edrad during the thirty-year Ryraxian War, which just ended in the current 'Qamis' setting. He is formidable, but he isn't an ancient 'Dragon Emperor' from the old days. The institutions of the old Draconic empire actually persisted longest in Qis'mos, but the dragons themselves departed long before Ryraxos's time. His exile to Secundus was a condition of his recent defeat.

hi, I missed the last session where the DM was talking about the map. I am playing a level 5 member of the Gondolier Guild in Qaringil, and I was looking at the map sketch. There are a lot of empty spots—can I actually fill those in with my character's family history?

Yes, you definitely can. The campaign notes specifically mention that there are many blank spaces on the map intended for players to fill in with their backstories. Since you are playing in the Qaringil prequel era (about 200 years before the main Qamis timeline), you have a lot of flexibility. For a member of the Gondolier Guild, you might want to consider how your family ended up in the city of canals. Qaringil was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qismos, so a family connection to the productive lands of the south or the Holy Qis Empire (which currently rules from Ath'r) could work well.

That sounds good. My guy works the Great Western Bridge and the canals, but I was thinking his ancestors were explorers from one of those unnamed islands in the Central Sea. How would they have even found their way to Qaringil without modern tools?

Navigation on Qaia is actually very advanced because of the moons. Your ancestors would have used the 'pole star' to find their latitude (its elevation equals the geographic latitude) and the moon Primus to find their longitude. Because Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit, it is a motionless fixed point in the sky above the prime meridian. By measuring Primus's elevation and checking a 'primsine' table, a navigator can determine their longitude without needing a chronometer. This works anywhere in the hemisphere where Primus is visible. If your family came from an island in the Central Sea, they would have had a clear view of Primus to guide them straight to the bridges of Qaringil.
Since I am in the Gondolier Guild, do I have to worry about the 'Five Families' like the Peixota or the Manos? I heard they run everything.

The Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli) are the elite of Qaringil, but they don't control everything. Interestingly, the Gondolier Guild is one of the Seven Major Guilds that has never been led by one of those five families. Your guild handles the maintenance and control of the bridges as well as the canals. While the Peixota lead the Shipping Guild and the Vitelli lead the Silk Guild, the Gondolier Guild remains outside their direct hereditary control. This might give your character a bit of independent pride, or perhaps you find yourself caught in the middle when families like the Manos try to exert influence over the city's infrastructure.

Hey there. I'm looking at joining a Qamis game. I'm a big linguistics nerd and I noticed the 'Q' without the 'u' everywhere. Is that like Draconic in the Forgotten Realms where it's all harsh and glottal? Also, what's the general vibe? Is it a high-magic heroic setting like Faerun or more of a political thing?

The 'Q' in this setting is actually a specific phoneme from Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. It is pronounced like 'Kh', so Qis sounds like 'Kh-iss' and Qamis is 'Kh-ah-miss'. Unlike the Forgotten Realms, there are no racial languages here—no Elvish or Dwarvish. Instead, languages are national, like Edran or Artklan, and Old Draconic is used for scholarly treaties and diplomacy. Regarding the tone, Qamis is quite different from the high-magic heroism of Faerun. The setting begins right after a thirty-year continental conflict called the Ryraxian War. While the 'rightful' Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in Edrad, the world is physically and politically scarred. It's a 'return to normalcy' that feels very tenuous and messy.

No racial languages? That's a big shift from something like Greyhawk. So if I play an elf, I just speak Edran? And what about this dragon Ryraxos? If he's a 'dragon overlord' who ran the place for decades, is he a god-tier threat like Tiamat or a Great Wyrm? It sounds like he was the big bad, but now he's just... gone to a moon?

Correct, if you are an Edran elf, you speak Edran. Edrad's nobility is actually specifically half-elven (between one-quarter and three-quarters elf). As for Ryraxos, he isn't a god-like entity like Tiamat. He is a bronze dragon, only about sixty years old. He rose to power as an artillery commander—dragons are prized in that role because they can move the heavy guns—and eventually seized control during the chaos of the Revolutionary Council. He isn't a world-ending force of nature; a competent party of adventurers or a well-placed battery of cannons could challenge him. He accepted exile to Secundus, our second moon, after his defeat. The 'big bad' isn't a single monster now; it's the instability of the post-war world.

Exile to a moon is a wild way to end a war. Does that mean the Church of Bahamut is the main power now? In most D&D worlds, the 'good' dragon god's church is pretty stable, like the Harpers or the Triad. Is Qama basically the Vatican of this world?

Qama is indeed the holy city of Bahamut, but it's currently at the center of a massive religious schism. During the war, the drow were recruited as allies by Artkos and they actually occupied Qama. They are still there in various enclaves across the continent. In Qama, the drow have appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. There is a rival candidate, Qachu II, claiming the title from the city of Qis, but he doesn't have the holy relics of the office. So unlike a stable religious faction in a traditional setting, the Church of Bahamut is fractured. Even the 'good' institutions are struggling to find their footing after thirty years of total war.

hey. i'm playing a drow who stuck around in Qama after Ryraxos got exiled, but i'm looking for a change of scenery. what can you tell me about Lock? i heard they have 'free cities' and a bunch of industry going on.

In the current era of Qamis, Lock is indeed becoming known for its burgeoning industrial presence. The country is organized as a kingdom, but it has a unique political structure where the city of Lock itself and a few other major urban centers are designated as 'free cities.' This means they operate with significant autonomy from the kingdom's central rule. Since you mentioned staying in Qama (pronounced Khama), you're likely used to a very religious atmosphere; Lock's industrial focus would be a sharp contrast to the holy city of Bahumat.
Wait, so is the whole place just a mess of overlapping laws between the king and the cities? and i read somewhere that Lock used to be part of an empire. are those free cities still on the river?

The tension between central and local power is an old tradition there. About 200 years ago, during the Qaringil prequel era, Lock and several other river cities were part of the Holy Qis Empire (which ruled from Ath'r). Even then, they were 'free cities' locked in a cold conflict with the Emperor over their ancient prerogatives. In the present day, that independent spirit remains. These cities are still primarily river-based, which supports their modern industrial growth. The DM hasn't specified the names of the other free cities besides Lock yet—those are currently blank spaces in the notes that could be filled in with your character's backstory if you're interested.

Hey! I'm playing a drow who stayed on the surface in Qama after the Ryraxian War ended. I was checking some old scrolls from the Qaringil era and it mentioned that the Artklan League 'magically anchored' the moon Primus for navigation. Is that why it doesn't move? And what does anchored actually mean in magic terms?

I can certainly help with that, though we should clear up the historical timeline first. In the Qaringil era (the prequel setting roughly 200 years ago), the Artklan League didn't exist yet. At that time, central Qamis was ruled from Ath'r by the Holy Qis Empire. The Artklan League is a much more modern coalition formed after the recent war. Also, the campaign notes don't actually specify that the Empire (or the League) was responsible for anchoring the moon—it is just described as a physical fact of the system. Primus is called 'magically anchored' because it is in a perfect geostationary orbit that defies natural orbital decay. In a normal system, moons experience tidal torque that causes them to migrate closer or further away over time. Primus is magically held in place so it has zero tidal migration and no torque. It stays fixed over a single point on the equator (longitude 0) and never moves from that spot in the sky.

Got it, Holy Qis Empire, not the League. I'll make sure my drow doesn't sound like a total tourist in Qama. Since it's anchored right there, does it still have phases? I'm trying to figure out if it would ever be dark enough for my character to feel comfortable outside without it being a New Moon for all the moons at once.

Primus does have phases, but they follow a very fast cycle of exactly one day. Because it stays in one spot while Qaia rotates, the angle of sunlight hitting it changes throughout the day. However, Primus is not a very bright moon. It is small—only about 10.4 arcminutes across, which is one-third the size of Earth's moon—and it's made of dark iron with a very low albedo. Even at full brightness, it is about 18 times dimmer than Earth's full moon. For a drow, the bigger 'light' concerns would be the inner moons like Tertius and Quartus, which are much larger and brighter. If you want a truly dark night, you'll want to check the triple-moon alignment calendar; for example, on May 24, 2262, there is a triple-new moon where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all dark at once.

I am so hyped to start this Qaringil campaign! I want my character to be a bit of a high-society climber in the city. Tell me about the Volmer family—the 'Crow' sigil caught my eye immediately and it sounds perfect for a shady-but-wealthy vibe.

That is an excellent choice for a high-society climber! The Volmer family is one of the 'Five Families' of Qaringil, meaning they are at the absolute top of the city-state's social and economic ladder. Their sigil is indeed the Crow, and they are the powerhouse behind the Banking Guild. In a republic like Qaringil, where the elites are defined by commercial wealth rather than land ownership, being the masters of the banks makes them incredibly influential.
Master of the banks? Oh, that is perfect. Do they do anything else? I want to make sure I have plenty of excuses to be out on the docks or in the market districts.

You definitely will! While they lead the Banking Guild, the notes mention the Volmers are also heavily involved in the shipping industry. This puts them in a fascinating position—they provide the capital for others to trade, but they also run their own fleets. Since Qaringil's merchant fleets fan out across the entire inland sea, a Volmer agent would have plenty of reasons to be down at the docks, perhaps checking on a shipment of silk from the Vitelli family or keeping an eye on the Peixota shipping lanes. It's a very busy, high-stakes environment for a character looking to make a name for themselves!
That sounds like a lot of drama. What's the current political situation like for them? Are they in charge of the city right now?

Not quite 'in charge,' but they are major players. Qaringil is a republic, and the current leader is Exarch Giovanni Pesaro. He has been in power for 19 years but is considered to be on his deathbed. He's actually from a minor family and is more closely aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. This means there is likely a lot of behind-the-scenes maneuvering from the Volmers as they look toward the future. It's a perfect time for an ambitious character to join their ranks—there is a lot of money and power up for grabs as the city prepares for a potential change in leadership!

Hey, I missed the last session where the DM was geeking out about the moons and navigation. Can I get a quick executive summary? Specifically, how precise is this 'Primus' longitude thing for our ship? Is it GPS-level or just 'don't hit a continent' level?

Think of Primus as a low-fidelity, single-satellite GPS that never moves from its position at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude (the prime meridian). Because it is geostationary, you can find your longitude just by measuring its angle above the horizon (elevation) and knowing your latitude. For an executive summary on precision: it depends entirely on your hardware. A simple astrolabe gives you about 55-70 km accuracy. A high-end cross-staff gets you down to 11-14 km. If the party has a top-tier sextant and a steady hand, you're looking at roughly 6-7 km. It's not street-address precise, but it's easily 'find a specific harbor' level.
Got it. So it's gear-dependent. Does it work everywhere? The DM mentioned something about a 'visibility boundary' affecting the math.

It only works in the hemisphere that can see Primus. If you sail to the far side of Qaia, it's permanently below the horizon and you're back to old-school dead reckoning. Even on the 'near' side, your accuracy drops as you approach the visibility limit, which is about 81.3 degrees away from the prime meridian. Near that edge, Primus is hugging the horizon, meaning atmospheric refraction messes with your readings. The system is most precise when you're near the equator and the prime meridian because the geometric sensitivity is highest there—small changes in your position result in large, easy-to-measure changes in Primus's elevation.
One last thing—do we need a clock? Usually, longitude needs a synchronized chronometer to compare local time to a reference.

That is the 'killer app' of Primus: no chronometer required. Because Primus is magically anchored and geostationary, it doesn't move relative to the ground. You aren't measuring time; you are measuring a static geometric angle. The 'primsine' function the navigators use is just a lookup table that maps that angle to a distance from the prime meridian. As long as you can see the pole star for latitude and Primus for longitude, you have a fixed coordinate fix on any clear night.

Hello! I am working on a backstory for a half-elven noble in the Qaringil prequel. I was thinking of being a member of the Keyberos family from the Rathakos region. I want my character to be a bit of a navigator, but I was reading the notes on Primus and got confused—how does the air or refraction actually limit your precision when the moon is near the horizon?

I should clarify a few things regarding the timeline first! If you are playing in the Qaringil prequel (set about 200 years before the main campaign), Rathakos does not exist yet. That region is currently part of the Red Mountains, which are claimed by the Holy Qis Empire but not effectively administered. The Keyberos dynasty is certainly ruling in Edrad from the capital of Jia, but they have already been on the throne for two centuries by the time of Qaringil—the 'restoration' of the dynasty only happens in the present-day Qamis era after the Ryraxian War.

Regarding your navigation question: atmospheric refraction is the bending of light as it passes through Qaia's atmosphere. When Primus is high in the sky, this effect is negligible. However, as Primus sinks toward the horizon, the light travels through a much thicker layer of air. This makes the moon appear higher than its true geometric position. 

In navigation, you find longitude using the formula cos L = primsine(e) / cos lat. Because the primsine function depends entirely on the measured elevation (e), an error in that angle caused by refraction will lead to an incorrect longitude. Near the visibility limit—which is about 81.3 degrees away from the prime meridian—Primus is so low that even a small error in e translates to a massive error in your calculated position. The precision drops from a few kilometers to dozens or hundreds of kilometers of error.

Right, my mistake. I'll stick to a family branch in Jia for the prequel then. So, if I'm on a ship near the visibility boundary—say, around 80 degrees longitude—and I'm trying to use my cross-staff to get a fix, is there a specific table I'd use to correct for that refraction, or is Primus just considered useless once it's that low?

In the setting notes, navigators generally consider Primus less reliable near the boundary. The notes mention that at the visibility limit (elevation e = 0), the geocentric angle is approx 81.3 degrees. Once Primus is below 5 or 10 degrees of elevation, the 'shallow geometry' makes the measurement very sensitive.

While an Edran noble might have access to sophisticated astronomical tables, the DM's notes don't specify a standard refraction correction table yet. In practice, most navigators would switch to other methods—like stellar transits or measuring the positions of the faster moons like Secundus or Quartus against known stars—once Primus gets too low. The 'longitude problem' on the far-side hemisphere (where Primus is never visible) is a major theme, and being near the horizon on the near-side is essentially the transition into that higher-difficulty navigation.

I am rolling up a half-elven noble from Jia, so obviously I am looking at the Keyberos history. But these navigation notes keep mentioning 'two celestial anchors' like it is some divine gift to the Edran fleet. It sounds a bit like propaganda. How is this actually supposed to work for a navigator? Is a 'stationary moon' even a real thing or just a way to skip the math for longitude?

It is a real physical property of the moon Primus, and the term 'anchors' refers to the two fixed points in the sky that allow for absolute positioning. The first is the pole star, which gives you latitude based on its elevation above the horizon. The second is Primus, which is geostationary. Because its orbital period matches Qaia's sidereal day and its inclination matches the axial tilt, it hangs motionless over a single spot on the equator (the prime meridian). For an Edran navigator, this means you do not need a chronometer to find longitude. You just measure how high Primus is in the sky and compare it to your latitude.

That still sounds too convenient. On Earth, finding longitude was a disaster because you needed to know the exact time at a reference point. If Primus is just sitting there, how does measuring its height tell you where you are horizontally? What if you are at the same latitude but 500 miles east?

The geometry works because Primus is close enough to have significant parallax. Think of it like this: if you are at the sub-Primus point (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude), Primus is directly overhead at 90 degrees. As you move away in any direction, its elevation drops. Navigators use a specific formula called the 'primsine' to turn that elevation into a geocentric distance from the prime meridian. Since you already know your latitude from the pole star, you can solve for your longitude. The only catch is checking if Primus is east or west of your local meridian to know which side of the world you are on.

Right, but what about sailing to Westron or exploring the far side of the world? If Primus is fixed over one hemisphere, then the moment my ship crosses that 'visibility boundary,' we are back to guessing and dead reckoning, right? It seems like this 'anchor' only works if you stay in the empires the DM has already mapped out.

You are exactly right. The notes confirm a massive 'navigational asymmetry' between the two hemispheres. Primus is only visible within about 81 degrees of the prime meridian. Once you head toward Westron or the far-side, you lose your longitude anchor and have to rely on much harder methods like lunar-distance observations using Secundus or Quartus. It makes those regions significantly more dangerous to navigate. As for Westron itself, the notes list it as a 'blank space' the DM hasn't detailed yet, so that mystery is part of the setting's current state.

I am looking at the notes for the Qaringil prequel. My friend said there are a ton of guilds, but I usually like playing rogue types or street-level characters. Are there any criminal guilds in that city, or is it just merchants selling wool and silk?

In the city-state of Qaringil, which is the focus of the prequel era set roughly 200 years before the main Qamis campaign, there are indeed organizations for shadier characters. While the city is officially run by Great Guilds like the Banking, Shipping, and Silk guilds, there are several underground criminal organizations that specifically style themselves as guilds to mimic the city's legitimate power structures. The notes identify three of these competing groups: the Thieves, the Bandits, and the Cutpurses. They operate as illegal versions of the merchant guilds, navigating the city's canals and bridges outside the control of the Five Families and the official guilds. It is worth noting that the Q in Qaringil is pronounced like a 'Kh', a trait of the Old Draconic language used throughout the continent.

Hey! I'm catching up since I missed the last session. We're in Qaringil right now, and I'm trying to understand the power structure. My character is really focused on the Church of Bahamut and the recent talk about the schism in Qama. How do the Great Guilds of the city fit into that? Is there a religious guild I should know about?

To clarify the timeline for your character: the schism you're thinking of (between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II) is actually a central event in the Qamis present-day campaign, which takes place about 200 years after our current Qaringil prequel. In this era, the Church of Bahamut is still very much unified and centered in the holy city of Qama, within the Qis'mos region. Qama hasn't been occupied by the drow yet.

In Qaringil itself, there isn't a 'Religious Guild.' The city is a republic of merchants and artisans. There are seven 'Great Guilds' that hold the real power:

1. Judicial: Currently led by the Manos family.
2. Mercantile: Recently taken over by the Tiddwar family (gnomes) after the Qanto family lost their lead.
3. Shipping: Led by the Peixota family.
4. Silk: Led by the Vitelli family.
5. Banking: Led by the Volmer family.
6. Gondolier: Controls bridge maintenance and canal traffic; notably never led by the 'Five Families.'
7. Wool: Similar to the Gondoliers, it's a major guild but usually stays out of the hands of the top nobility.

While the Church doesn't have a guild, many of the elites in these guilds are Bahamut worshipers, and the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos. That's why the local Islas language has so much Qis (pronounced Khis) vocabulary.

Got it, so the schism is a 'future' problem. That's good to know. But if the city is so tied to Qis'mos, does the Church have any formal say in how the guilds or the Exarch run things? I heard the Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is pretty old.

The Church of Bahamut has an enormous presence in the region, but Qaringil is a commercial republic first. The elites are bankers and traders, not landowners or high priests. That said, the Church acts as a major cultural force. Many of the guilds, like the Judicial or Banking guilds, likely consult with the clergy on matters of ethics or law, even if they don't have a formal vote in the Great Guilds.

Exarch Giovanni Pesaro has held the post for 19 years and is indeed considered to be on death's doorstep. Because the republic's politics are so 'incomprehensible' and competitive, his eventual passing will likely trigger a massive power struggle between the Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli). 

If you're looking for a religious hook, you might look at the 'minor' guilds. There is an Alchemist Guild and a Mage Guild. While not as politically dominant as Shipping or Banking, they often have closer ties to the scholarly and divine traditions of Old Qis.

That makes sense. Since I'm playing a fifth-level character, I think I'm supposed to be a full member of a guild already. If I want to stay close to the religious and scholarly side of things without being a merchant prince, which one fits best?

At fifth level, you are definitely eligible for full membership in the 'Smaller Guilds' most relevant to adventurers. Your best bets would be:

1. The Mage Guild: If your interest in the Church is more about the history of Old Draconic (Old Qis) and the ancient traditions of the dragons.
2. The Alchemist Guild: Good for a character focused on the practical applications of faith and science.
3. The Adventurer Guild: This is the most common path for someone of your level. It allows you to operate within the city's complex laws while maintaining your independence.

Even the Judicial Guild could work if your character views the law as a divine extension of Bahamut's will. Just keep in mind that the Manos family (the ones with the Hand sigil) currently leads that guild, and they are deep in a 'hotly contested' rivalry with the other families over the wool trade.

Hey, I'm spinning up some downtime goals for my character in Rathakos. I need an executive summary on the Five Families—how do the Peixota and Vitelli families handle the export taxes under High King Ezevir's new trade edicts? I'm trying to optimize my logistics.

It looks like there's a bit of a timeline conflict in your notes. The 'Five Families'—including the Peixota and Vitelli—are actually from the Qaringil prequel campaign, set about 200 years before the current events in Rathakos. 

In the present-day Qamis campaign where you're in Rathakos, the nation is actually a newly formed Princedom governed by Regent John Dunne. High King Ezevir Arth rules Artkos, which is a separate nation in the Artklan League. The mercantile politics in Rathakos are currently driven by internal unrest and mountain communities rather than the island-based merchant families of the past. Would you like the breakdown of the Five Families from the Qaringil era instead, or are you looking for the power players in modern Rathakos?

My bad, context switching is a killer. I am indeed prepping for the Qaringil prequel session this weekend. Let's pivot to the island city-state. Give me the TL;DR on the Five Families' 'market sectors' and who's currently sitting on the board, so to speak.

No problem. In Qaringil, the political landscape is dominated by the Great Guilds, and the Five Families hold the levers of power. Here is the executive summary for the prequel era:

1. Peixota (Sigil: Dolphin): The market leaders in the Shipping Guild. They also have significant influence in the Mercantile and Judicial sectors.
2. Vitelli (Sigil: Moth): They dominate the Silk Guild. Their secondary interests include shipping and general mercantile trade.
3. Volmer (Sigil: Crow): The heavy hitters in the Banking Guild, with additional logistics interests in shipping.
4. Manos (Sigil: Hand): They lead the Judicial Guild. They are currently attempting a hostile entry into the wool sector, which is causing significant friction.
5. Qanto (Sigil: Dove): Strong in the Banking and Judicial sectors. They recently lost their leadership of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family.

The current 'CEO' of the city is Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, though he is on death's doorstep. He's currently aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. If you're looking for an outlier, the Tiddwar family (gnomes) currently runs the Mercantile Guild and is aligned with the Exarch, much to the Qantos' frustration.
Hey, can I get an executive summary on Kard? My DM mentioned it is a financial hub but hinted the politics are a mess. What is the high-level situation there?

In the current Qamis era, Kard is a major financial and shipping center located in a southwestern enclave of Artkos. On paper, it is part of the Artklan League, governed under High King Ezevir Arth. However, the notes describe its governance as 'fragile' because of a deep cultural and historical divide. While it is technically an Artklan territory, much of the population speaks Edran and identifies more with the culture of Edrad than with the High King in Ath'r.
So it is a demographic mismatch. Why does Artkos keep a grip on it if the locals are hostile? Is it just the revenue from the shipping?

Revenue is a massive factor, but it is also about the post-war restoration. When the thirty-year Ryraxian War ended, the treaties attempted to put everything back to 'historical lines.' Twenty-eight years ago, Edrad invaded Kard, and the local Edran-speaking population actually welcomed them. After the dragon Ryraxos was exiled and the war concluded, Kard was returned to Artkos. The fragility comes from the fact that the High King is trying to enforce an old status quo on a population that spent nearly three decades being part of the Edran sphere of influence. It is a major economic engine that Artkos cannot afford to lose, but the subjects are nominal at best.

yooo! I was just reading the notes on Old Draconic and I'm losing it—the 'Q' is actually a 'Kh' sound? That is so cool. Does that mean Qaringil is 'Kharingil' and Qamis is 'Khamis'? Also, I noticed the navigation guide says longitude is way more precise near the prime meridian and the equator. Is that just because of the 'Kh' influence or is there a math reason for it?

You nailed it! Yes, in both the present-day Qamis era and the Qaringil prequel, that 'Q' is a 'Kh' sound derived from Old Draconic (or Old Qis). So it's 'Khamis' and 'Kharingil' all the way. It gives the setting a very distinct linguistic texture, especially since that 'q without u' shows up in so many place names like Qama and Qeth.

As for the navigation, the precision isn't actually linguistic—it's pure geometry! Because Primus is geostationary directly over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0, 0), it sits at the 'zenith' or directly overhead for anyone at that point. 

When Primus is high in the sky, a small change in your physical position on the ground results in a large, easily measurable change in its elevation angle. Near the equator and the prime meridian, the 'sensitivity' of the measurement is at its peak. As you move toward the edges of visibility (the horizon), the geometry flattens out, and a large change in longitude only results in a tiny, shallow change in elevation, making it much harder to get a precise fix!

That is wild! So the closer you are to the 'Kh'enter—sorry, center—of the world, the better your maps are? I love that. Does the language reflect that too? Like, is the Old Draconic for Primus something related to 'The Fixed Point' or 'The Eye'?

Haha, 'Khenter' works perfectly! To be honest, the campaign notes don't explicitly give us the Old Draconic name for Primus yet—it's one of those 'blank spaces' the DM hasn't filled in. However, we do know that Old Draconic is the language of scholarly treaties and diplomacy, and the sub-Primus point is the most natural prime meridian because any navigator can see it.

It makes total sense that the prestige dialects of Edran or Artklan would borrow heavily from Old Qis for their maritime terms. If you're building a character, you could totally pitch a name for Primus based on that 'Fixed Point' idea! Just remember that the further west or east you go, the lower Primus hangs. Once you hit the far-side hemisphere (roughly 81 degrees away), Primus disappears entirely, and you're stuck with 'Earth-style' difficult longitude math. Those navigators probably have a very different name for the moon they can't see!

Hello! I've been looking into the theology of the Church of Bahamut, particularly with the current schism in Qama. While researching, I saw that the first moon, Primus, creates a permanent static bulge in the ocean rather than a normal daily tide. Why doesn't it cycle like the others? And do the followers of Qachu II or the drow-appointed Qe'lth IV view this unmoving moon as a religious symbol of stability?

The reason Primus produces a static bulge instead of a daily tide is its geostationary orbit. It is magically anchored at an altitude where its orbital period exactly matches the rotation of Qaia. Because it is fixed directly above the equator at the prime meridian, it never rises or sets. Since the moon doesn't sweep across the sky, its gravitational pull on the oceans remains constant in a single direction, creating a permanent 2 cm elevation in sea level along that axis rather than a rhythmic wave. As for the religious implications, the campaign notes do not specify if the rival high priests use Primus as a specific symbol, though the city of Qama (pronounced Kha-ma) is the traditional holy seat for both. Qe'lth IV, the drow-appointed priest, currently holds the city and its relics, while Qachu II operates from Qis without them. Whether they interpret the unmoving moon as a sign of Bahamut's stability or a divine anchor is a 'blank space' you might explore with the DM.

Hey there. I am looking through your campaign notes for some inspiration for my own homebrew world. I noticed you mentioned there are 'blank spaces' where players can contribute. Does that mean I could drop in something like a localized version of Baldur's Gate or Waterdeep if I were playing? How much of the map is actually set in stone?

In the Qamis setting, 'blank spaces' are areas explicitly left open by the DM for players to flesh out through their backstories. While the core nations like Edrad and the Artklan League have established histories, large portions of the map are intentionally undefined. Specifically, the continent to the west called Westron is almost entirely a blank space, described only as very large and mostly non-Bahamut worshiping. There are also undefined regions within the interior of the continent and around the islands of the central sea. 

Regarding your comparison to the Forgotten Realms, it is important to note that Qamis is quite different. For instance, there are no racial languages like Elvish or Dwarvish; languages are national (Edran, Artklan, Qis, etc.). Also, the letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh' in this world, derived from Old Draconic. If you wanted to add a city-state, it would need to fit the established geopolitical vibe—perhaps as one of the 'free cities' near Lock or a pirate enclave on the big southern island near Freeport—rather than being a direct port of a Sword Coast city.

That is interesting. The 'no racial languages' thing reminds me a bit of how some people run Eberron. Speaking of Eberron, I saw that Lock has an industrial presence. Is that like magical steampunk?
