And you mentioned the Red Mountains have 'dragon cults'—is that a Tiamat thing, or more like the Cult of the Dragon from FR?

Lock is described as having a 'burgeoning industrial presence,' but the notes do not specify the exact level of technology or if it is fueled by magic like Eberron's dragonshards. It is one of the areas where the DM has left room for detail. As for the dragon cults in the Red Mountains (specifically in the nation of Rathakos in the present-day Qamis era), they are not linked to Tiamat. The primary deity mentioned in the setting is Bahamut, with his holy city at Qama. The cults in the mountains are likely remnants of the era when dragons or dragon-emperors ruled directly. 

If you are looking at the Qaringil prequel era (200 years earlier), the Red Mountains were not even part of established nations like Rathakos or Acrokos yet; they were occupied by non-state peoples and only claimed—not administered—by the Holy Qis Empire. The religious landscape is also shifting; in the present day, there is a major schism in the Church of Bahamut because the drow occupied Qama and appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV.

Wait, did you say the drow are on the surface? That is a huge departure from the Underdark-heavy lore in Greyhawk or FR. You said they are in Qama? Are they like the main villains of the post-war era?

In the present-day Qamis era, the drow are not necessarily villains. They were actually recruited as allies by Artkos in a 'desperation move' during the Ryraxian War. After the war ended, they remained on the surface in about a dozen scattered enclaves. The most prominent one is indeed the holy city of Qama. While they have appointed their own high priest, the notes explicitly state they have not despoiled the city. This creates a political and religious tension rather than a simple 'monsters from below' dynamic. If you were building a character or a location in a blank space, you could certainly explore how one of these drow enclaves interacts with the local human or half-elven populations.

Hey! I am jumping into the Qaringil prequel campaign. I am thinking of playing a navigator for the Peixota family - they are the ones with the dolphin sigil who lead the Shipping Guild, right? I am used to playing in the Forgotten Realms where we just kind of hand-wave navigation or use a compass. How do people here actually find their way on the sea? Is it like Toril where you just follow the stars?

Welcome to Qaringil! You are correct: the Peixota family holds leadership of the Shipping Guild and uses the dolphin as their sigil. Navigating the Central Sea in this era is actually much more mathematically precise than in the Forgotten Realms because of Qaia's unique celestial mechanics.

For finding your latitude, it is quite similar to traditional methods: you use the pole star. Qaia's rotational axis points toward a star near the celestial north pole. Because this star stays fixed while the planet rotates, its elevation above the horizon is exactly equal to your geographic latitude. If you are at 30 degrees North, the pole star sits 30 degrees above your northern horizon. A Peixota navigator would use a cross-staff or an astrolabe to get a reading within 0.1 degrees, which gets you within about 11 kilometers of your north-south position.

Okay, that makes sense. So the pole star is basically the North Star. But what about the moons? I saw the MOONS.md file and there are a ton of them. In the Realms, we just have Selune. Does having seven moons make the tides crazy for the Shipping Guild? And can I use them to find where I am going east-to-west?

It definitely makes the tides more complex. On Qaia, the total oscillating tidal force is about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's. This is driven by three moons - Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus - plus the sun. A navigator in Qaringil would need very detailed tide tables to handle those shifts.

As for going east-to-west (longitude), you have a huge advantage that Toril doesn't have: Primus. Primus is a geostationary moon magically anchored directly over the equator at the prime meridian. It never rises or sets; it just hangs motionless in the sky for the near-side hemisphere. By measuring the elevation of Primus and comparing it to your latitude from the pole star, you can calculate your longitude exactly without needing a clock. It is a revolutionary tool for the Shipping Guild, though it's useless if you ever sail to the far side of the world where Primus is permanently below the horizon.
A stationary moon? That sounds like a total game-changer. Is it easy to see? If it's dark like some of the other moons, maybe it's hard to use for navigation if the weather is bad.

It is visible, but it's not the brightest object in the sky. Primus is quite small (about one-third the size of Earth's moon) and has a dark, iron-rich surface with an albedo of 0.06. This makes it about 3 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's moon. 

However, because it never moves, you don't have to wait for it to be in a certain position. Any clear night will work. Your character would likely use a 'primsine' table - a precomputed set of values that helps you turn the angle of Primus into a longitude fix. In Qaringil, being able to use these tables is likely a requirement for any high-ranking member of the Shipping Guild.

I am looking into running a one-shot set in the Qaringil prequel era for my group. I want to ground it in the regional powers of the time. What is the capital of Edrad in this period, and where is it located geographically?

In the Qaringil era, which is set roughly 200 years before the present-day Qamis campaign, the capital of Edrad is the coastal city of Jia. It is located on a peninsula that juts out into the Central Sea. At this point in history, the Keyberos dynasty has been ruling Edrad for about two centuries, and Jia is already established as the most culturally influential city in the region. Because of its position on the Central Sea, it exerts significant political and economic pressure on its neighbors.

Is the political landscape of Edrad different than in the later Qamis setting? I am particularly interested in their borders and the requirements for their nobility if I have players making half-elven characters.

Yes, there are a few key differences. In the Qaringil era, Edrad does not yet control the city of Qeth or the territory to the northeast of it; conquering those lands is currently the primary goal of Edran foreign policy. Regarding nobility, it is a strict requirement that the ruling class be half-elven. To hold a noble title in Jia, an individual must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. This bloodline requirement is central to their identity as the Keyberos dynasty continues to expand royal control over the nation.
How does Jia's culture compare to the city of Qaringil? If the one-shot starts in Qaringil, would a noble from Jia feel out of place, and how would they likely travel between the two cities?

A noble from Jia would likely find Qaringil quite alien. While Jia is an 'old money' cultural center defined by its elven aristocracy and dynastic tradition, Qaringil is an incomprehensible republic of roughly 100,000 people ruled by merchant guilds and five prominent families. Qaringil is built on a wizard-made isthmus with two massive bridges and a series of canals, meaning its elites are commercial and nautical rather than landed gentry. Travel between them is almost exclusively by sea; Qaringil's armed merchant fleets dominate the Central Sea, and its fortifications control the Great Western Bridge. The noble would also notice a linguistic shift, as the Islas language spoken in Qaringil has a much heavier Qis (pronounced 'Khis') vocabulary than the prestige Edran dialect of the capital.
I was looking over the map for the new Qamis game and saw Freeport way down south. What is the deal with that place?

Freeport is a city of exiles and pirates located on the shore of a large, mostly unexplored island to the south of the main continent. In the setting notes, it is described as a rough-and-tumble settlement where the residents basically try to ignore the massive jungle that presses right up against the city limits. It is one of the major 'blank spaces' on the map where the DM has not filled in the details yet, leaving it open for player backstories or future discovery.
You mentioned they try not to look at the jungle. Is there something specific in there, or is it just the general vibe of the place being dangerous?

The notes specify that the residents 'try to not look too hard' at the jungle, which suggests a certain level of dread or a desire to remain ignorant of what lies within. While the interior is officially unexplored, there is a prominent piece of lore or rumor mentioned: some people believe this southern island is where the dragons went after they departed the main continent. Aside from that rumor, the actual contents of the jungle are currently a blank space.

Interesting. I played in the Qaringil prequel one-shot a while back, and I remember Freeport being mentioned then too. Has anything actually changed there in the 200 years between the prequel and the 'present day' Qamis era? Does the restored Keyberos dynasty or the Artklan League have any influence there now?

Actually, the description of Freeport remains identical in both the Qaringil prequel era and the present-day Qamis era. In both time periods, it is characterized as a lawless haven for exiles and pirates that remains detached from continental politics. Neither the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad nor the High Kings of Artkos exert influence that far south. It remains a mystery regardless of the era, though the 'present day' notes specifically mention that the dragon Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon, Secundus, rather than heading south to the island where the other dragons are rumored to have gone.

hello! i'm so bummed i missed the last session. we were heading toward Qama, right? i heard the party ran into some drow there. how did they even get to the surface? also, as a side note, is Qama another one of those 'Q sounds like Kh' words? my inner linguistics hobbyist needs to know if i'm looking at an Old Draconic root here.

Welcome back. To catch you up: the drow are a fairly recent addition to the surface of Qamis. They were originally recruited as allies by Artkos in a move of desperation during the later stages of the thirty-year Ryraxian War. Though the war has since ended and the dragon Ryraxos is exiled, the drow remained behind in about a dozen scattered enclaves. The most prominent of these is indeed Qama.

Your linguistic hunch is spot on. Qama is pronounced 'Khama.' That 'Q without a U' spelling is the signature of Old Draconic (or Old Qis), which is the language of scholarly treaties and ancient place names. The city of Qama is the traditional holy seat of the Church of Bahamut, but it's currently under drow occupation.

Wait, the drow are occupying the holy city of Bahamut? That sounds like a powder keg. Did they destroy the temples or something? And what's this about a schism in the church? I caught a mention of a name like Qe'lth in the group chat.

Surprisingly, the drow have not despoiled or trashed the city. However, they have asserted religious control by appointing their own high priest, Qe'lth IV (pronounced 'Khelth'). This has created a major schism within the Church of Bahamut.

There are now two rival candidates for the leadership: Qe'lth IV, who holds the city and the physical relics of office, and Qachu II (pronounced 'Khachu'), who is operating out of the city of Qis. Because Qachu lacks the holy relics, his claim is contested. It's a tense situation for the people of Qis'mos, as that region contains some of the most productive land on the continent and was heavily fought over during the war.

So 'Qis' follows the 'Kh' rule too? Kinda cool that the language of the academy is based on the old dragon tongue. Since I speak the prestige dialect of my capital, would I be able to read Old Draconic treaties, or is that a totally different beast?

Yes, Qis is 'Khis.' The modern Qis language is the one most similar to the Old Draconic used in diplomacy. While your character's national prestige dialect (like Edran or Artklan) is likely influenced by those roots, Old Draconic itself is specifically the language of 'scholarly publications and treaties.' It isn't a standard 'racial' language—everyone in Qamis uses national languages—but Old Draconic is the high-register version used for formal international business. If your character has a background in law or the academy, they'd definitely recognize the vocabulary.

Hey, I'm joining a Qamis game soon and I'm playing a cleric of Bahamut. Is Qama like the Vatican for this god? I've read about places like Waterdeep or Candlekeep in other settings and I'm wondering if this is the big holy hub where my character would have gone for training.

Welcome to the setting! To start, a quick tip on the names: in this world, 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh', so Qama sounds like 'Khama'. To answer your question, Qama is exactly that—it is the traditional holy city of Bahamut and the historic seat of his church. However, compared to the stable temples you might see in the Forgotten Realms, Qama is currently in a very complicated political state in the present day of the Qamis campaign. While it remains the 'holy hub,' it was actually occupied by the drow toward the end of the Ryraxian War. They didn't destroy the city, but they did install their own High Priest, which has caused a major split in the religion.

Wait, the drow are in the holy city? Are they like the evil drow from the Underdark who serve spider gods? That sounds like a total disaster for a Bahamut worshiper. Are they holding the city hostage like a villain in a movie?

It is different from the standard 'evil drow' tropes you might find in other D&D settings. In Qamis, the drow were actually recruited as allies by the nation of Artkos during the Ryraxian War because Artkos was desperate. Now that the war is over, these drow have remained on the surface in various enclaves, with Qama being the most prominent. They haven't despoiled the city, but they've appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This has led to a religious schism. There is a rival candidate for the leadership, Qachu II, but he is currently operating out of the city of Qis and doesn't have the official relics of office because they are still back in Qama with the drow-appointed priest. So, your character would find their holy city at the center of a very tense political and religious standoff.
That sounds way more political than just 'go to the temple and pray.' If I was playing a prequel game instead, like in that Qaringil era people mention, would Qama still be a drow-occupied mess?

No, if you were playing in the Qaringil prequel—set about 200 years earlier—the situation would be very different. In that era, the Church of Bahamut already has an enormous presence in the region of Qis'mos, and Qama is its undisputed traditional seat. The Ryraxian War hasn't happened yet, so the drow are not on the surface or occupying the city. It would be a much more stable environment for a cleric, though the Holy Qis Empire would be the dominant power in the central continent at that time, which brings its own set of imperial politics.

Hey, I missed the last session where we actually got into the city of Qaringil. Can you catch me up on who the big players are? Also, the DM mentioned something about the letter Q being pronounced like 'Kh' and it being tied to Old Draconic. Being a linguistics nerd, I'm dying to know how that works and if it relates to the different races living there.

In the Qaringil era, which is about 200 years before the main Qamis timeline, you're looking at a very cosmopolitan but human-centric trade hub. The population is around 100,000. Regarding the races, Qaringil doesn't have segregated neighborhoods or 'racial languages' like Elvish or Dwarvish. Instead, the social structure is built around the Great Guilds and the Five Families. These elite families—the Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—are primarily human, but all of them have some elven ancestry. You'll also find a prominent gnomish presence, specifically the Tiddwar family, who currently lead the Mercantile Guild. As for the linguistics, you're spot on. The 'Q' without a 'U' (like in Qaringil or Qis) is a direct carryover from Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. It's the prestige language for scholarly work and treaties. The local language in the city is Islas, but because the city was originally built by workers from Qismos, the local dialect has a heavy infusion of Old Qis vocabulary. That 'Kh' sound is the authentic ancient pronunciation that has survived through these place names.

Interesting. So if 'Q' is 'Kh', then Qaringil is 'Kharingil'? Does that mean the gnomes and the half-elf nobility are essentially speaking a language influenced by dragons? And does that 'Q' shift happen in other names too, like the city of Jia or the nation of Edrad?

Exactly, it is pronounced 'Kharingil'. And yes, since Old Draconic is the language of the academy and diplomacy, the educated classes—including the gnomish Tiddwars and the human-elf Five Families—would be very familiar with it. However, the 'Q' shift is specific to names derived from Old Draconic. Names like Jia or Edrad come from the Edran language and don't typically follow that 'Q' convention. You see the 'Q' most prominently in the Holy Qis Empire (which rules central Qamis from Ath'r in this era) and in the region of Qis'mos. In Qaringil, the merchant elites use the 'Kh' sound because it marks them as sophisticated and connected to the ancient draconic institutions that persisted in Qis'mos long after the dragons themselves departed. It's a sign of prestige. If you're looking to fit in with the Silk or Banking guilds, getting that 'Kh' sound right is a good start.

Hey nanochat. I missed the last session and I'm trying to finalize my character sheet. My DM mentioned that languages work differently in this Qamis setting—something about no Elvish or Dwarvish? Can you give me an executive summary of how bonus languages work and what the options are?

That is correct. In this setting (both the present-day Qamis and the Qaringil prequel), there are no racial languages. You won't find Elvish, Dwarvish, or Orcish. Instead, languages are tied to nations and regions. Here is the executive summary:

1. **Starting Package**: You automatically speak the 'prestige' dialect of your home nation's capital (where the ruling class resides) and one regional dialect.
2. **Bonus Language**: Every player gets one additional bonus language, but the DM requires a backstory explanation for how you acquired it.
3. **The 'Q' Rule**: In all names and languages, 'Q' is pronounced 'Kh'. This comes from Old Draconic.

**Primary Language Options:**
- **Edran**: Spoken in Edrad; the language of the half-elven nobility.
- **Artklan**: The tongue of the Artklan Empire/League (Artkos, Rathakos, etc.).
- **Qis**: Spoken in Qis'mos; it is the closest living relative to Old Draconic.
- **Forthrian**: The language of the northeast, ruled by the Dragoncallers.
- **Islas**: The language of the islands and mercenary navies.
- **Old Draconic (Old Qis)**: The 'q without u' language used for scholarly journals, ancient treaties, and magic.

Which nation is your character from? That will determine your primary dialects.

I'm looking at playing a researcher from the island city of Qaringil—this is for the prequel campaign. If I take Old Draconic as my bonus, how does that work with the regional stuff? And is there anything 'exotic' I should know about?

For a Qaringil campaign (the prequel set ~200 years before the Ryraxian War), your setup would look like this:

- **Primary Language**: Islas (the prestige dialect of the island merchants).
- **Regional Dialect**: Since Qaringil was built by adventurers from Qis'mos, the local Islas dialect has a heavy Qis vocabulary. You would likely speak this 'Qaringil-Islas' fluently.
- **Bonus (Old Draconic)**: As a researcher, this is a perfect fit. It's the language of the academy and diplomacy. Your backstory just needs to mention your studies or access to old texts.

Regarding 'exotic' options: 
- **Westron**: The notes mention a massive continent to the west. It's largely a 'blank space' in the DM's current records, but it is known to have different religious traditions. 
- **The Southern Island**: There is a large, unexplored jungle island with a pirate city called Freeport. Some say dragons fled there. 

If you want a truly exotic bonus language, you might suggest a dialect from one of those areas, though the DM notes those are currently 'blank spaces' you could help fill with your backstory.

hey, got a minute for a setting question. I'm building a PC with a sailor background for a Qamis campaign. How does finding latitude work on Qaia? Is it the same as Earth or is there some weird magic moon stuff involved?

It's actually quite grounded in physics, though the 'weird moon stuff' mostly applies to longitude. For latitude, it's the classic celestial method: you use the pole star. Because Qaia's rotational axis holds a fixed orientation relative to the stars, the pole star stays at a constant elevation above the horizon. The rule is simple: the elevation of the pole star equals your geographic latitude. If the star is 30 degrees above the north horizon, you are at 30 degrees North latitude.
Nice, simple enough. Do I need any specific gear for that, or is it just a 'look up and roll Survival' thing? And what about the southern hemisphere—is there a South Star or are we just lost?

You would definitely want an astrolabe or a cross-staff to get an accurate measurement. If you're in the southern hemisphere, you use the south celestial pole instead of the north one; the math remains the same. Mechanically, the campaign notes specify that 0.1 degrees of arc corresponds to about 11 km of north-south distance. A simple astrolabe gets you within about 55 km (0.5 degrees), while a high-quality cross-staff gets you down to that 11 km range.

11km precision for a cross-staff is actually pretty tight for TTRPG travel. Does the DM use these numbers for navigation checks? And wait, you mentioned the moons are for longitude? I thought longitude was the 'impossible' problem without a high-end clock.

On Earth it was, but Qaia has a 'cheat code' called Primus. Primus is a geostationary moon magically anchored directly over the equator at the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude). It never rises or sets; it stays at a fixed point in the sky for the entire near-side hemisphere. Navigators just measure the elevation of Primus, check a 'primsine' table in their almanac, and combine that with their latitude to get a longitude fix. No chronometer or high-end clock needed. The only downside is that if you're on the far side of the planet, Primus is permanently below the horizon, and you're back to the 'impossible' Earth-style longitude problem.

I'm working on my backstory for a half-elven noble from the Keyberos line in Jia. I'm a bit confused about the drow situation in Qama. Did the Holy Qis Empire invite them in to spite Edrad, or was that something the Revolutionary Council did before Ryraxos kicked them out?

It sounds like you are mixing up the two different eras of the setting. For a Keyberos noble in the present-day Qamis campaign, the Holy Qis Empire is ancient history—it ruled from Ath'r roughly 200 years ago during the Qaringil prequel era. There were no drow enclaves on the surface back then.

The drow occupation of Qama happened very recently, during the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended. It wasn't the Revolutionary Council that invited them, either. The drow were actually recruited as allies by Artkos (specifically High King Ezevir Arth) in a desperation move during the later phases of the war. Even though the war is over and Ryraxos has been exiled to the moon Secundus, the drow stayed behind in several scattered enclaves. Qama, the holy city of Bahumat, is the most prominent place they still hold.

Okay, so it was an Artklan move. But since Ryraxos was the one who supposedly delivered Edrad from defeat and restored my family to the throne, why didn't he just clear them out of Qama? It's a bit embarrassing for the Keyberos to have a holy city occupied by drow right on the border.

From the perspective of your noble house, it is definitely a point of tension. However, Ryraxos's priorities shifted over time. While he originally helped restore the Keyberos dynasty to the throne in Jia, he then spent two and a half decades waging his own aggressive wars across the continent as a 'dragon overlord.' 

The drow in Qama haven't actually despoiled or destroyed the city, which might be why a full-scale crusade to remove them hasn't happened yet. Instead, they've played a political game by appointing their own high priest, Qe'lth IV (remember, 'Q' is pronounced 'Kh'). This has created a religious schism, as the competing candidate, Qachu II, is forced to operate out of the city of Qis without the official relics of office. Since Qis'mos is currently governed by an elected Potentate and not directly by Edrad, your family in Jia has to balance their desire for religious purity in Qama against the fragile post-war peace treaties.

I was looking at the moon charts and noticed Sextus and Septimus. Given that the setting has this distinct 'Q' pronounced as 'Kh' thing—like in Qamis and Qaringil—these names feel almost out of place. Are Sextus and Septimus Old Draconic terms, or is there a 'Kh' based name for them I should know about?

That is a sharp linguistic observation. In the campaign notes, 'Q' is indeed pronounced 'Kh' because it stems from Old Draconic (or Old Qis), which remains the language of treaties and scholarship. However, for the moon system, the documents use a numerical sequence: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Quintus, Sextus, and Septimus. While names like 'Qis'mos' or 'Qaringil' reflect that Old Draconic influence, the notes do not provide specific 'Q' or 'Kh' equivalents for the outer moons. Sextus and Septimus are treated as standard names for the two outermost retrograde moons, which are physically identical—both around 0.215 times the radius of Earth's moon—though Septimus is much farther out and appears smaller in the sky.

A numerical sequence implies a specific academic tradition. If the Holy Qis Empire or the dragon-cults were naming things, wouldn't they use Old Draconic roots? I'm trying to see if I can find a 'Qextus' or 'Qeptimus' somewhere. Or perhaps names from Westron? Is there a different naming convention used on the other continent?

I understand the desire to find a deeper linguistic pattern there, but I have to be honest: the DM has not filled in those details yet. In the campaign notes, Westron is explicitly marked as a 'blank space' that hasn't been prepared, other than the fact that it is a large continent to the west with different religious traditions. There is no mention of a Westron naming convention for the moons, nor are there 'Q' prefixed versions of the moon names in the current texts. The notes provide the physical data and these specific names, but the cultural origin of the 'Sextus/Septimus' terminology isn't established. It remains one of those areas where the DM might be open to player input for a character backstory.
That makes sense. What about Quintus? If there is a Sextus and Septimus, I'd expect a fifth moon in that sequence. Is it another 'blank space' or is it hidden?

Quintus is actually accounted for, though it isn't a moon you would see rising in the sky. It is described as a 'trace particle' sitting at the Sun-Qaia L4 point, about 60 degrees ahead of the planet in its orbit. It's effectively massless and librates over a 2,000-year period. While it fits the numerical naming convention of the system, it lacks the visible presence of Sextus or Septimus, which appear as small discs—Sextus at roughly 4.1 arcminutes and Septimus at 2.7. Like the others, there is no Old Draconic or 'Q' name listed for it in the authoritative notes.
I am looking for some family lore to borrow for a merchant republic in my own game. Who are the Tiddwars in your setting?

The Tiddwars are a prominent gnomish family in the city-state of Qaringil, which is the setting for the prequel campaign set about 200 years before the current Qamis era. In Qaringil, they are major political players whose sigil is a set of gears. Unlike many of the other noble-coded families who are human or half-elven, the Tiddwars represent a significant gnomish influence in the city's commercial hierarchy.
Are they an old money family that has always been in charge, or did they have to fight their way up?

They are definitely on the rise rather than being part of the ancient guard. In the Qaringil era, they recently secured leadership of the Mercantile Guild, which is one of the city's seven Great Guilds. This was a major political shift because they took that leadership away from the Qanto family, one of the established 'Five Families' of the city. Their rise is closely linked to their political alignment; they are currently favored by the Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, which helped them displace the Qantos.
That is exactly the kind of friction I need. How does a gnomish family like that manage to push out a family like the Qantos in a place like Qaringil?

It comes down to the unique structure of Qaringil. Since the city relies on fishing and importation rather than land ownership, power is concentrated in the Great Guilds. The Tiddwars used their position in the Mercantile Guild—which regulates the trade of most commodities outside of specific niches like silk or wool—to build a power base. By aligning themselves with the Exarch and a gnomish faction that was more cooperative with the current administration, they became the 'new' face of the merchant class. While the 'Five Families' like the Peixotas or Vitellis still control things like shipping and silk, the Tiddwars represent a shifting tide where commercial success and political loyalty to the Exarch can override traditional family prestige.

Hey, I am making a character for the Qaringil campaign. He is from Forthyr and a big believer in the Dragon Emperor revival sect. Since I am an adventurer, what kind of smaller guilds are in Qaringil that I would actually talk to?

In the Qaringil prequel era (set about 200 years before the main Qamis timeline), the notes specifically list three smaller guilds most relevant to an adventuring party: the Adventurer guild, the Alchemist guild, and the Mage guild. Since you are starting at fifth level, the notes mention you can already be a full member of any guild relevant to your class. There are also three 'underground' organizations that style themselves as guilds: the Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. Beyond those, Qaringil is dominated by the seven 'Great Guilds' like Shipping, Silk, and Banking.

My guy is pretty devout regarding the Dragon Emperors. Is there a specific guild or group in Qaringil that handles dragon-related relics or maybe a religious guild for Bahumat followers? I am also wondering if there is a guild for specialized smiths, like for dragonscale armor.

The notes do not mention a specific guild for dragon relics or specialized dragonscale smithing in Qaringil. Regarding religion, while the Church of Bahumat is a major force in Qismos (the region), the notes for the city-state of Qaringil do not list a religious guild specifically. Most of the elites in Qaringil are described as commercial rather than landowners, focusing on trade and shipping. The specialized smithing you mentioned would be a 'blank space'—the DM has not detailed those types of minor craft guilds yet, so that might be something you can develop in your backstory.

That makes sense. What about the Mage guild? Do they have any ties to the Dragoncallers in Forthyr? My character follows Dragoncaller Shanlong I, and I am trying to see if the revival sect has any reach in the city through the mages.

The notes are silent on any formal connection between the Qaringil Mage guild and the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr. Forthyr is actually described as being 'somewhat backwards' compared to other regions, and while the Dragoncaller dynasty gives 'tacit encouragement' to the revival sect, the reach of that sect outside Forthyr is not defined. The Mage guild in Qaringil is listed as a minor guild, but its internal politics or international ties are currently a blank space. You might have to rely on the fact that Q (pronounced 'Kh') and the Old Draconic language are the standards for scholarly work and treaties across the continent to find common ground with local mages.

I am rolling up a half-elven noble from the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad for the Qamis campaign. Since my character is part of the ruling class in Jia, what languages would I naturally speak, and what is the deal with the letter Q in all these names like Qeth and Qis?

As an Edran noble, your primary language is Edran, specifically the prestige dialect spoken in the capital city of Jia. Per the campaign rules, you also start with one regional dialect—perhaps from a city like Etops or Qeth—and one bonus language of your choice, provided you have a backstory reason for it. For a noble, Old Draconic (also called Old Qis) is a highly appropriate choice, as it is the language of scholarly publications, ancient treaties, and diplomacy. Regarding the letter Q, it is a hallmark of Old Draconic and is always pronounced like a Kh sound. So, the nation of Qis is pronounced Khis, and the city of Qeth is Kheth. Note that in the present day of the Qamis campaign, your family has just been restored to the throne after the thirty-year Ryraxian War, so your character likely grew up during a time of significant political upheaval for the Keyberos line.
Hey! I'm finally catching up after missing the last session. I heard the big bad dragon Ryraxos is gone? Someone in the group chat said he's literally on the moon now. Is that for real?

That is correct. The thirty-year Ryraxian War has officially ended, and as part of the peace settlements, Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, which is the second moon of Qaia. He's no longer a factor on the continent, which has allowed the Keyberos dynasty to be restored in Edrad and the borders to return to their historical lines.

That's wild. Why the second moon though? I remember you saying there's a moon that just sits in one spot in the sky—Primus, right? Wouldn't that be a better place to stick him so everyone can keep an eye on him?

You're right about Primus; it's geostationary and stays fixed over one point on the equator. However, Primus is tiny—only about 64 km in radius. Secundus is much larger, about 471 km in radius, and it's a dark, iron-rich moon. It rises in the west because its orbit is retrograde, unlike most of the other moons. While the notes don't explicitly say why Secundus was chosen over Primus, Secundus is further away (0.30 LD compared to Primus at 0.11 LD) and its dark, iron surface (albedo 0.06) makes it a pretty bleak place for an exile. Also, Secundus has a significant tidal impact—it's about 1.47 times as strong as Earth's moon—so it's a much more 'substantial' world to be sent to.

Gotcha. I'm working on a character from Rathakos, specifically the Southern Reaches that were just taken back from Edrad. How would people there feel about him being on Secundus? Is it like a constant reminder every time the moon rises?

In Rathakos, especially the Southern Reaches, the feeling would be complicated. That area was recently annexed from Edrad and is currently seeing some internal unrest. Many people there might have actually welcomed the Edran invasion twenty-eight years ago because they speak Edran. To them, Ryraxos was the 'Overlord' who helped Edrad initially but then waged war for decades. Seeing Secundus rise in the west would be a nightly reminder of his defeat. Since Secundus is nearly as large as Earth's moon in the sky but much darker, it looks like a dim, brooding iron sphere passing overhead. For those in the mountain communities who still follow the 'old dragon cults,' his exile might be seen as a temporary setback or a religious event.
Wait, what's this about dragon cults and artillery? I think I heard someone say he was a 'cannon commander' or something? I want my PC to have some history with the military side of the war.

Exactly! Ryraxos actually started his rise to power as an artillery officer. The campaign notes mention that dragons make excellent artillery commanders because they can help carry the guns themselves. He wasn't some ancient, god-like entity; he's a bronze dragon, maybe sixty years old, who was just very tactically competent with heavy weaponry. If your PC is from the Southern Reaches of Rathakos, they would have seen the transition from the 'Red Mountain Republic' (which was pro-Edran) to the current Princedom of Rathakos after the pro-Edran factions were purged. Your character might have served in a unit that faced those dragon-led batteries or even been part of the guerrilla resistance in the Red Mountains.

Hi! I am absolutely obsessed with the religious politics you mentioned regarding the Church of Bahamut. The schism between Qe'lth IV in Qama and Qachu II in Qis is such a cool dynamic! But I also saw a note about Forthyr and a sect calling for the 'return of the Dragon Emperors.' Is that a branch of Bahamut worship, or something else entirely? Tell me about Forthyr!

It is great to see someone digging into the theology! Forthyr is a massive nation in the northeast of Qamis, and its religious landscape is definitely distinct from the orthodox Church of Bahamut you find in Qis'mos. The 'Dragon Emperors' sect is a prominent religious movement there that specifically calls for the return of the ancient dragon rulers. While the Church of Bahamut is the heavy hitter in the heartlands, Forthyr's dynasty—the Dragoncallers—actually gives this 'Dragon Emperor' sect tacit encouragement. It is a bit of a rival tradition to the standard clergy, and the notes describe Forthyr as 'somewhat backwards' compared to the rest of the continent.
A rival tradition! That is fascinating. And the rulers are called 'Dragoncallers'? That sounds like a heavy-duty sorcerer-king setup. Is it the same family in both eras?

Spot on. It is a dynasty of sorcerer-kings. In the Qaringil prequel era (about 200 years ago), Forthyr was ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong I, who was half-dragonborn. By the present day Qamis era, we are up to Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. The notes mention that the dragonborn bloodlines have thinned out over the centuries, but the 'Dragoncaller' title and the sorcerous authority remain. They have historically stayed out of southwestern affairs, but after thirty years of the Ryraxian War, they are finding it impossible to ignore the rest of the continent any longer.

I wonder if they are trying to use the Bahamut schism to their advantage! Since Qama is held by the drow now and the Church is split, does Shanlong VI have a preference? Or is the 'Dragon Emperor' sect so different that they don't even care about who the high priest in Qama is?

That is the big question! The campaign notes don't explicitly state if Shanlong VI has picked a side between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II. Since the Dragoncaller dynasty supports a sect that wants the old Dragon Emperors back, they might view the struggle between the two Bahamut claimants as a sign of the 'old' institutions failing. Remember that Q is pronounced 'Kh', so the Holy City is Khama and the claimant is Khachu. While the rest of Qamis is dealing with the fallout of the dragon Ryraxos and his exile to the moon Secundus, Forthyr is sitting up in the northeast with a huge population and a very different idea of what 'divine' rule should look like.

I'm looking at the celestial setup for my cleric and it's wild compared to something like the Forgotten Realms where you just have Selune. How do the moons actually move here? I heard some of them go the 'wrong' way. Is there a religious vibe to that, like prograde moons being 'blessed' and retrograde ones being 'cursed'?

Qaia's moon system is indeed more complex than Toril's. You have six moons in total, and they are split evenly between prograde and retrograde orbits. The prograde moons—meaning they move west-to-east—are Primus, Tertius, and Quartus. The retrograde moons—which rise in the west and move east-to-west—are Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus. Primus is a special case: it is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit, so it remains fixed over one spot on the equator and never rises or sets for anyone who can see it. As for the 'blessed' or 'cursed' aspect, the campaign notes don't explicitly label orbital direction as a moral alignment, but Secundus (retrograde) is where the dragon Ryraxos was exiled after the war, which certainly gives it a dark reputation in the current Qamis era.

That makes sense. If Ryraxos is on Secundus, and it's 'backwards,' it fits the vibe of an exiled overlord. I'm really interested in the Church of Bahamut (pronounced Ba-kh-amut, right?). Does the schism between Qe'lth IV and Qachu II have anything to do with these moon alignments, or is it just about the Drow occupation of Qama?

Actually, Q is pronounced 'Kh', so it is 'Ba-kha-mut' and the city is 'Kha-ma'. The schism is primarily a political and ecclesiastical crisis resulting from the Ryraxian War. The holy city of Qama was occupied by drow who appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. While the drow didn't destroy the city, their appointment caused a rift. Qachu II is the rival candidate operating out of the city of Qis, though the notes mention he lacks the 'relics of office' held in Qama. There isn't a direct mention of moon alignments causing the schism in the records, but for a religious scholar, the fact that their holy city is now lead by a drow appointee while the 'legitimate' candidate sits in Qis without his relics is the core of the conflict.
Got it. One more thing on the moons—Tertius is the basis for the week, right? Is that because it's the biggest or brightest? In other settings, the calendar is usually tied to the biggest moon.

Tertius is the basis for the eight-day week because of its orbital period of 8.15 days. It actually appears as the largest moon in the sky with an angular diameter of 33.7 arcminutes, which is slightly larger than Earth's moon. However, it isn't the brightest; Quartus is slightly brighter because it has a higher albedo (0.12 compared to Tertius's 0.09). Quartus has a 29.5-day period, making it the natural choice for the monthly calendar cycle. So the calendar is split: Tertius handles the week, and Quartus handles the month.

So I am looking at the drow situation in Qamis. Usually in the Realms they are Underdark outcasts or worshippers of Lolth. How did they get to the surface here? Did they just crawl out of a hole in the Red Mountains?

In Qamis, the drow presence on the surface is a recent political outcome rather than a typical Underdark invasion. During the thirty-year Ryraxian War, the nation of Artkos recruited the drow as allies in what the records describe as a desperation move. After the war ended, they did not return to wherever they came from; instead, they remained in about a dozen scattered enclaves across the continent. The most prominent of these is the holy city of Qama (remember that Q is pronounced as Kh), which is the traditional seat of the Church of Bahumat. They have even appointed their own high priest there, Qe'lth IV.

Wait, so Artkos just invited them up? Where were they living before that? And you mentioned a High Priest in a city of Bahumat—are they following the Platinum Dragon now or do they have their own pantheon? It feels weird for drow to be occupying a holy city of a Lawful Good dragon god.

The notes are actually a blank space regarding where the drow lived prior to being recruited by Artkos. There is no mention of an Underdark or specific subterranean kingdoms in the current campaign documentation. Regarding their religion, it is definitely a point of tension. While they are occupying Qama, they haven't despoiled it, but their appointment of Qe'lth IV as high priest has caused a major schism. There is a rival candidate, Qachu II, operating out of the city of Qis without the official relics of office. The notes do not specify if the drow actually worship Bahumat or if this is a purely political move to control the church's influence. Deities like Lolth are not mentioned in the setting notes at all.

That is a huge shift from the standard lore. If Artkos is trying to restore its old dominance, having drow enclaves all over the place seems like a massive liability. Does High King Ezevir Arth actually trust them, or was this a deal with the devil he is now regretting?

The High King's personal feelings on the drow are not currently detailed in the notes, but the term desperation move implies it was not an ideal arrangement. The campaign notes mention that the High Kings of Artkos see the current Artklan League as a pale shadow of their old empire and that everyone is finding it hard to enforce edicts that reverse thirty years of war-time change. The drow staying on the surface is specifically listed as one of the things that has changed permanently, despite efforts to put everything back the way it was. If you were playing a character from Artkos or Qama, the specific nature of that trust—or lack thereof—is something the DM has left open for exploration.

Hey there! I am working on a character for the Qaringil game. I have been really interested in the religious side of things, specifically the schism in the Church of Bahamut between Qe'lth and Qachu. Would a cleric in Qaringil have to pick a side in that mess?

That is a great question, but you are actually looking at two different time periods! The schism involving High Priest Qe'lth IV in Qama and Qachu II in Qis is a result of the Ryraxian War, which takes place in the 'present day' Qamis campaign. 

In the Qaringil prequel, which is set about 200 years earlier, the Church of Bahamut is still centered in the holy city of Qama and is quite unified and powerful. Qaringil itself was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, so the religious influence is there, but the city is much more focused on trade and its Republic. If you are playing in Qaringil, you would likely just be a faithful follower of the unified Church.

Oh, my bad, I mixed up the timelines. So 200 years ago the Church is still solid. Since I am sticking to Qaringil, I was looking at the local power players. I know there are the 'Five Families' that run things with the guilds. Do any of them have religious ties? What are their sigils? I want to see if any of them align well with a Bahamut theme.

The Five Families are primarily commercial entities, though they all have some elven ancestry. While the notes do not specify deep religious ties for any one family, their sigils and guild interests might help you find a 'vibe' for your cleric. Here are the Five Families and their sigils:

1. Peixota: Their sigil is the Dolphin. They lead the Shipping Guild and are involved in mercantile and judicial matters.
2. Manos: Their sigil is the Hand. They lead the Judicial Guild and are heavily involved in the silk and wool trades.
3. Qanto: Their sigil is the Dove. They were leading the Mercantile Guild until recently, when they lost that position to a gnomish family (the Tiddwars).
4. Volmer: Their sigil is the Crow. They lead the Banking Guild and have shipping interests.
5. Vitelli: Their sigil is the Moth. They lead the Silk Guild and are also involved in shipping and mercantile work.

From a religious perspective, the Qanto family's Dove might feel the most 'clerical' or peaceful to some, though they are currently on the decline politically. Also, remember that 'Q' is pronounced like 'Kh,' so Qanto is 'Khanto' and Qaringil is 'Kharingil.'
The Qanto Dove sounds perfect for a cleric of Bahamut, actually. You mentioned they lost their guild leadership to gnomes—the Tiddwars? What is their deal? Do they have a sigil too?

Yes, the Tiddwars are a prominent gnomish family. Their sigil is Gears, which fits their role leading the Mercantile Guild. They are considered to be aligned with the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, which is likely how they managed to displace the Qanto family from their leadership position. If your cleric is a Qanto loyalist, you might have some friction with the Tiddwars and their 'new money' influence in the city's trade.

Hey! I'm moving over from a long-running Greyhawk game to this Qamis setting. I saw something in the notes about a geostationary moon called Primus and using it for navigation? How does that actually work at the table compared to just a Survival check? In the Flanaess, we usually just looked for the North Star.

Welcome to Qamis! It's a bit different from Greyhawk or the Realms. In those settings, finding your longitude usually requires a very expensive clock or complicated math involving star transits. Here, navigation is much more 'scientific' because of the moons—specifically Primus (pronounced with a 'Kh' sound for the Q in Qamis, by the way).

Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. It sits exactly over the equator at 0 degrees longitude. This means it never rises or sets; it just hangs in the sky as a fixed point for half the world. If you can see it, you can figure out exactly where you are with just an astrolabe and a lookup table. For a DM, this means a navigator character isn't just rolling a Survival check—they're actually performing a geometric fix that works even if they've been lost at sea for months.

A geostationary moon? That's wild. So it just sits there? Walk me through the math. Let's say I'm a navigator on a ship at 30 degrees North latitude and I see Primus at a certain height. How do I get my longitude?

Exactly. Let's do a worked example. First, you'd find your latitude (30 degrees North) by measuring the elevation of the pole star, just like in Greyhawk. 

Next, you measure the elevation (e) of Primus above your horizon. Let's say you measure it at 42.2 degrees. 

You then use a precomputed function called the 'primsine.' For an elevation of 42.2 degrees, the primsine value is approximately 0.7504. The formula for longitude (lambda) is: 

cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(latitude)

Plugging in our numbers:
cos(lambda) = 0.7504 / cos(30 degrees)
cos(lambda) = 0.7504 / 0.8660
cos(lambda) = 0.8665

If you look up the arccos of 0.8665, you get 30. Your longitude is 30 degrees. You just need to know if you're East or West of the prime meridian.

Okay, I see the math. But you mentioned East or West. In Greyhawk, we just kind of handwave the 'prime meridian' usually. Where is the 'Zero' point for this system? And how do I know which side of it I'm on just by looking at the moon if it doesn't move?

The prime meridian (0 degrees longitude) is actually defined by Primus itself. The point on the equator directly beneath the moon is the natural starting point for all maps in Qamis. It’s convenient because, unlike Earth, you don't need a committee in Greenwich to agree on where 'Zero' is—it's just where the moon is.

To tell if you are East or West, you look at the moon's azimuth (its compass direction). If you are in the Northern Hemisphere and Primus appears to the West of due South, you are East of the prime meridian. If it appears to the East of due South, you are West. Essentially, you're looking 'back' at the center of the map. If you see the moon to your left, you've moved right.

That is a massive advantage for sailors. But what about the 'far-side' hemisphere mentioned in the navigation guide? If I sail too far into the 'blank spaces' on the map, do I just lose my GPS equivalent?

You hit the nail on the head. Primus is only visible from one side of the planet. Because of the geometry, it drops below the horizon once you are about 81 degrees away from the prime meridian. 

This creates a huge technological and economic divide. On the 'near side' (where the campaign settings of Qamis and Qaringil are located), navigation is trivial. If you sail into the far-side hemisphere—towards the deep 'blank spaces' or the unexplored back of the world—you're back to the 'Old Ways.' You have to use dead reckoning or 'lunar distance' methods using the other moons like Secundus or Quartus. It's much more dangerous and prone to error, which is why much of that side remains a mystery to the scholars in Jia or Ath'r.
Wait, Secundus? That's the one Ryraxos was exiled to at the end of the Ryraxian War, right? Is its orbit stable enough to use for navigation? I saw it's a retrograde moon.

Sharp memory! Yes, Secundus is the dark, iron-rich moon where the dragon Ryraxos now resides. It is indeed retrograde, meaning it rises in the West and sets in the East, which would look very strange to someone used to Oerth's moons. 

Despite being retrograde, its orbit is stable (though it's slowly spiraling inward over geological time). It has a period of 4.53 days and appears nearly the size of our Moon. Navigators on the far side use it as a 'clock' in the sky. By measuring its position relative to the background stars (the lunar distance method), they can find the time, which then allows them to calculate longitude. It's just significantly harder than looking at the stationary Primus.
Speaking of all those moons—Secundus, Tertius, Quartus—the tides must be insane compared to Faerun. Does the geostationary one, Primus, add to the daily tides at all?

Actually, Primus doesn't contribute to the daily tidal cycle at all! Because it stays fixed over the same spot, its gravity creates a permanent 'static bulge' in the ocean—a constant 2cm offset in sea level. It's basically a geographic feature, not a cycle.

However, the other moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—combine to create a total tidal force about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's. With three different moon periods (4.5 days, 8 days, and 29.5 days) plus the sun, the tides are incredibly complex and powerful. Coastal cities like Qaringil or Jia have to be built with very sophisticated canal systems and sea walls to handle that kind of daily water movement.

So I'm looking at the Church of Bahamut in the Qamis present-day notes. Isn't this schism in Qama just going to confuse players? Usually, in settings like the Forgotten Realms, Bahamut is just the 'Good Dragon God' and you fight Tiamat's cults. Is this a 'Good vs. Evil' thing or more of a historical-political split?

In Qamis, it is much more of a political and ecclesiastical split than a cosmic 'Good vs. Evil' battle. Unlike the Forgotten Realms where the conflict is often between two different deities, the schism here is within the same institution. The Church of Bahamut (pronounced 'Ba-ha-mut' with the 'Kh' sound for the Q-prefix often found in the holy city of Qama) is dealing with a crisis of legitimacy. During the Ryraxian War, the drow occupied Qama and appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. Meanwhile, a rival candidate, Qachu II, is operating out of the city of Qis. It's less about different gods and more about who actually holds the authority of the office.

Drow following Bahamut? That's wild. In FR, they're almost exclusively Lolth-worshippers unless they follow Eilistraee. Are these 'Good Drow' or is it more like the drow are using the church as a political puppet? Also, the notes mention Qachu II is missing 'relics of office.' Is that like the stuff the Chosen of Mystra would carry, or just fancy hats?

The notes don't actually categorize the drow as 'good' or 'evil' in a cosmic sense; they were recruited as allies by Artkos during the war out of desperation. While they haven't 'despoiled' the holy city of Qama, their appointment of Qe'lth IV is definitely a move of political control. Regarding the 'relics of office,' the campaign notes don't specify exactly what they are—whether they are magical artifacts or symbolic regalia like a crown or staff. That is currently one of the 'blank spaces' the DM hasn't filled in yet. However, because Qachu II is operating without them, his claim is considered weaker by some, even though he's the candidate not controlled by an occupying force.

It sounds like a Medieval 'Avignon Papacy' situation. Does this religious tension extend to Forthyr too? I saw they have a sect wanting the 'Dragon Emperors' back. Is that just another branch of the Bahamut church, or are we talking about something completely different, like the difference between Pelor and Amaunator?

It's a different movement. While the Church of Bahamut is centered in Qis'mos and Qama, Forthyr's religious landscape is dominated by a sect calling for the return of the 'Dragon Emperors.' The Dragoncaller dynasty, currently led by Shanlong VI, gives this sect 'tacit encouragement.' While both involve dragons, the Bahamut faith in Qis'mos is a highly organized institution with potentates and guild involvement, whereas the Forthyr movement is more of a nationalist-religious longing for a return to an ancient era of dragon-born rule. The notes don't explicitly link the two as the same denomination.

Hey, I'm playing a member of the Gondolier guild in Qaringil. Our party is planning a long-range trade mission out toward Rathakos, and I was telling them we don't need to worry about getting lost because I can just use Primus to keep our longitude. But my DM said I might run into problems with visibility? Is that because of the dragon magic keeping it up there?

I should clarify a couple of things first to make sure your navigation is on the right track. In the Qaringil era—which is set about 200 years before the present day—the nation of Rathakos doesn't actually exist yet. At this point in history, that mountainous region is still part of the Red Mountains, largely claimed by the Holy Qis Empire ruling from Ath'r. As a Gondolier, you'd likely be navigating the Central Sea or the canals around the city-state of Qaringil itself. Regarding Primus, its 'magic' is what keeps it geostationary, but your visibility issues are purely a matter of geography and physics. Because Primus is fixed directly over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude), it is only visible from one hemisphere of the planet Qaia. If your journey takes you too far across the globe, the moon will simply sink below the horizon and stay there.

Wait, Rathakos doesn't exist? Man, I keep mixing up the timelines. So if I'm in the Qaringil prequel era, and I'm sailing around the islands, can I at least see it from the Great Western Bridge? Or am I on the wrong side of the world for that?

You are on the correct side! Qaringil is located on the islands in the Central Sea, which is within the 'near-side' hemisphere where Primus is visible. From the bridges of Qaringil, you would see Primus as a small, motionless disc in the sky, roughly one-third the size of the moon Quartus. However, there are still limits to using it for navigation. First, the 'horizon limit': the farther you get from the prime meridian, the lower Primus sits. Once you pass roughly 81 degrees of longitude, it disappears entirely. Second, precision requires math. Just looking at it isn't enough; you need to measure its elevation angle and use a 'primsine' table to calculate your longitude. Finally, remember that while Primus is great for longitude, you still need the pole star for your latitude. If it's a cloudy night and you can't see the stars or Primus, you're back to dead reckoning.

Hey! I've been geeking out over the celestial mechanics of Qaia. Having three moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—all driving tides at once sounds like a nightmare for anyone trying to build a harbor. 4.4x Luna's force is huge! But I was also looking at the map of Qismos and noticed the drow are holding Qama. That's the holy city of Bahumat, right? How did that happen?

It certainly makes coastal life interesting! Navigators and engineers have to track three different orbital periods (4.53, 8.15, and 29.5 days) plus the solar tide to predict high water. As for Qama, it is indeed the holy city of Bahumat, but the political map changed drastically during the Ryraxian War. Toward the end of those thirty years of fighting, the nation of Artkos was in a state of desperation and recruited the drow as allies. The drow occupied Qama during the late phases of the war and simply haven't left.
So they just moved in and stayed? Did they wreck the place? Also, since I'm looking at the moons again—does Primus affect the tides at all, or is it too small and far away?

Primus is actually very close (0.11 LD), but it is geostationary and magically anchored. Because it stays fixed over one point on the equator and never moves relative to the surface, it doesn't cause a daily tidal cycle. Instead, it creates a permanent 2 cm static bulge in the ocean—a constant geographic feature rather than a tide. Regarding Qama, the drow actually haven't despoiled the city. They've occupied it and remained in several scattered enclaves across Qamis, but they've been surprisingly orderly. The real issue is the religious schism they caused. They appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV (remember, Q is pronounced 'Kh'). Now the church is split; there's a rival candidate named Qachu II operating out of the city of Qis, but he doesn't have any of the holy relics because the drow still hold the city and the cathedral.

That is wild. I bet the 'Kh' pronunciation takes some getting used to. So in the current Qamis era, the war is technically over but the drow are basically squatting in the holiest site of Bahumat? Do the people in Qismos just accept that?

Accept is a strong word, but they are forced to deal with the reality of it. The war ended with the dragon Ryraxos being exiled to Secundus and the Keyberos dynasty being restored to the throne in Edrad, but enforcing old borders and religious traditions is proving difficult. Qismos is governed by a Potentate who is elected and impeachable by the nobility and guilds, and since Qismos was one of the most heavily fought-over regions during the war, their resources are spread thin. They're stuck with a drow-appointed high priest in their holy city while their 'rightful' candidate sits in Qis without his relics. It's a very tense peace.

Look, I will be honest, this whole setting feels a bit over-engineered. Every name has a Q in it and it is getting hard to track. Why can't we just have a normal place like the Dalelands? Anyway, what is Forthyr? Is it just the Thay or Mulhorand of this world?

I understand the naming can be a bit dense. A quick tip: the letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh' in this setting, derived from Old Draconic. As for Forthyr, it is a massive nation in the northeast of the continent. It is not quite like Thay; there is no magocracy of liches here. Instead, it is ruled by a dynasty of sorcerer-kings known as Dragoncallers. In the present Qamis era, the ruler is Shanlong VI. If you are playing in the Qaringil prequel era, it is Shanlong I. The dynasty actually used to be dragonborn, but the bloodline has thinned out over the centuries.

So it is like Tymanther from Forgotten Realms, but everyone's blood is 'running thin'? That is exactly what I mean by complicated. In FR, a dragonborn is a dragonborn. Why make it a sorcerer-king thing where they used to be dragonborn but now they are just humans with a fancy title? And what makes them 'backward' exactly?

The 'backward' label comes from how the rest of the continent views them. While nations like Edrad or Lock are moving toward industry or complex half-elven bureaucracies, Forthyr remains under the absolute rule of the Dragoncaller. The 'thinning blood' reflects the setting's history of dragons departing the world long ago. There is also a very prominent religious sect there that openly calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. The Shanlong dynasty gives this group tacit encouragement, likely to bolster their own legitimacy. It is less about a racial kingdom like Tymanther and more about a dynasty clinging to the prestige of an ancient, draconic past that has mostly faded away.

Right, so a cult wants the dragons back. Sounds like the Cult of the Dragon but without the dracoliches. Is there anything actually interesting there for an adventurer, or is it just a 'blank space' on the map like those other regions you mentioned?

The notes do describe Forthyr as enormous and somewhat isolated, but it is not a total blank space like Westron or the far south. Because of the religious fervor for the Dragon Emperors and the sorcerous nature of the ruling line, it is a place where ancient draconic traditions are still preserved. If you are looking for that classic 'return of the dragons' vibe, Forthyr is the center of it. It provides a distinct contrast to the post-war political maneuvering happening in places like Rathakos or Edrad.

I've been looking at the orbital data for the Qaia system. The inner moons obviously dominate the tidal dynamics, but what is the deal with the outer retrograde pair, Sextus and Septimus? Are they physically identical, and do they have any measurable impact on the daily tides?

Sextus and Septimus are physically identical in mass (0.010 lunar masses), radius (374 km), and density (3,344 kg/m3), which is the same as Earth's Moon. They both have a dark rocky albedo of 0.09. However, they occupy very different orbits in the outer reaches of the Hill sphere. Sextus has a semi-major axis of 1.64 LD and a period of roughly 57.7 days, while Septimus is much farther out at 2.47 LD with a period of 106.2 days. Regarding your question on tidal dynamics: their impact is negligible. Sextus exerts a tidal force only 0.002 times that of Earth's Moon, and Septimus exerts just 0.001. Together, they are a rounding error compared to the inner trio (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus), which combine for roughly 4.4 times Luna's tidal force. Visually, they appear as very small discs rather than full moons. Sextus is about 4.1 arcminutes across, and Septimus is 2.7 arcminutes. Since they are retrograde, you will see them rising in the west, moving in the opposite direction of the primary calendar moons like Quartus.

Hey! I am getting ready for a new game in the Qamis setting. I have played a ton of Forgotten Realms and Greyhawk, so I am used to big dragon-themed conflicts like the Tyranny of Dragons, but this 'Ryraxian War' sounds way more political. Can you tell me what it actually was and who was involved? I saw something about a dragon being an artillery officer and that sounds awesome.

It is definitely a unique take compared to the classic dragon-as-god-king trope! In the Qamis present day, the Ryraxian War is the defining event of the last generation. It was a thirty-year conflict that just ended. It started when the Artklan Empire and Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos) signed a pact to march on Edrad. Their goal was to depose a group called the Revolutionary Council and put the old Keyberos dynasty back on the throne in the city of Jia. Most people thought it would be over in two months, but it dragged on for three decades and changed the face of the continent.
Thirty years! That is a massive scale. And tell me more about Ryraxos. I love the idea of a dragon leading an artillery battery instead of just breathing fire from a hoard. Who did he fight for?

Exactly! Ryraxos is a bronze dragon, and he actually fought for Edrad. When the war started, Edrad was on the verge of total defeat. Ryraxos was a formidable commander who rose to power because dragons make incredible artillery officers—they can literally carry the heavy guns themselves to positions no one else can reach. He saved Edrad from the Artklan/Qis'mos alliance, but then he ended up waging his own aggressive war across the continent for twenty-five years as a 'dragon overlord.' He was eventually defeated and is currently exiled to Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. Even though he is gone, the political landscape is still messy.
So it was Edrad versus everyone else? Who are the big players left now that the dust has settled?

The main factions were Edrad, the Artklan League (led by Artkos), and Qis'mos. Edrad is ruled by half-elven nobility and the Keyberos dynasty is back in charge now, though they are struggling to actually enforce their rules after thirty years of chaos. The Artklan League is basically a shell of its old empire, with High King Ezevir Arth trying to regain control over his subjects. Then you have Qis'mos, which is the heart of the Church of Bahamut. Interestingly, towards the end of the war, Artkos got desperate and recruited drow as allies. Now those drow are still on the surface in enclaves like the holy city of Qama. It is a very tense 'peace' where everyone is trying to go back to the old ways, but the scars of the Ryraxian War are everywhere.

Yo, I'm working on a character for the Qamis campaign, probably a Draconic Bloodline Sorcerer or maybe a Drakewarden. I need to nail down the fluff for where the dragons actually are in this setting. Is it a total extinction event, or is there a 'Dragon Continent' somewhere? Trying to see if my PC's ancestry trait is a flavor-fail for a low-level start.

It is definitely not an extinction event, though the 'golden age' of dragons is long past. In the present-day Qamis setting, the big news is Ryraxos. He's a bronze dragon who basically ran a thirty-year war across the continent. After his defeat, he accepted exile to Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. So, if you're looking for a literal, living dragon, one is currently sitting on a dark, iron-rich moon visible in the night sky.

For more ancient or 'wild' dragons, the biggest rumor points to the large, mostly unexplored island far to the south. There is a pirate city called Freeport on the coast, but the residents are too scared to look deep into the jungle. Legend says that's where the dragons retreated after their old empire collapsed. 

Also, keep in mind that Q is pronounced like 'Kh' here, so the Old Qis or Old Draconic names will have that hard sound. If you're starting in Rathakos, there are actually mountain communities there that still follow old dragon cults, which could be a great hook for a Draconic Sorcerer.

Wait, he's actually on the moon? That's metal. Is Secundus like a T4 high-level zone then? Also, you mentioned Rathakos cults. If I play a 'Dragon Cult' refugee, what's the local color there? Are they worshipping Ryraxos or the 'old ones' from the south? I want to make sure my backstory doesn't clash with the regional politics.

Secundus is definitely out of reach for a starting character—it's a retrograde moon about 0.30 lunar distances away with 1.47 times Earth's tidal force. It's less a 'zone' and more a celestial prison right now.

Regarding the Rathakos cults: Rathakos is a mountainous border state that's currently quite unstable. It's governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a Prince. The 'old dragon cults' in the mountain communities generally pre-date Ryraxos. They look back to the era of the Draconic Empire. However, Ryraxos's rise as an 'artillery commander' and eventual overlord definitely stirred those embers. 

If you're a refugee, you might be fleeing the internal unrest in the Southern Reaches (which Rathakos recently annexed from Edrad) or perhaps you're from a mountain tribe that refused to pivot from the 'old ways' to the new central government in the capital, Rath. Most people in the cities see the cults as backwards or dangerous, especially since the Ryraxian War just ended and the wounds are still fresh.

That works. One more mechanical thing: for my bonus language, I'm thinking 'Old Draconic'. Is that like the 'Latin' of Qamis? Like, if I start chanting in it, are people going to think I'm casting a high-level ritual, or is it just what scholars use for treaties? I want to know if speaking it in a tavern in Rathakos is a social faux pas.

You've got it exactly right. Old Draconic (or Old Qis) is the language of scholarly publications, diplomacy, and ancient treaties. It's the 'q without u' language you see in place names like Qaringil or Qis'mos. 

In a tavern in Rathakos, speaking it would definitely mark you as either an academic, a high-born diplomat, or a cultist. Since Rathakos is currently struggling with 'mountain communities' that follow those old cults, chanting in Old Draconic might make the locals very nervous. They'd likely associate it with the old dragon-worshiping factions that the new government is trying to suppress. It’s prestigious in a library in Jia (the capital of Edrad), but potentially provocative in the Red Mountains.

Hey! I missed last night's session and the group mentioned we might be heading toward Qis'mos. I've played a lot of Forgotten Realms and Greyhawk, so I'm trying to get my bearings. The DM keeps calling Qis'mos the richest land in Qamis—is that like the Dalelands or something? Why is it so wealthy?

Welcome back! Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos) is definitely the 'breadbasket' of the continent, though it's much more politically complex than something like the Dalelands. It is described as the most productive land in Qamis because of its soil and agricultural output, making it the economic heartland. Unlike the more decentralized Dalelands, Qis'mos has deep institutional roots; it's where the administrative structures of the old Draconic empire lasted the longest after the dragons left. Today, it's a merchant-heavy elective state governed by a Potentate who is chosen and can be impeached by a council of nobility, clergy, and guildmasters.

Got it. So it's a mix of high-end agriculture and old-world bureaucracy. If it's that rich, did it survive the Ryraxian War okay? In my old Greyhawk games, the wealthy spots usually got pillaged first. Did Ryraxos leave it alone?

Actually, it was the opposite of 'left alone.' Because the land is so productive, Qis'mos was one of the most heavily fought-over regions during the thirty-year war. It's currently in a bit of a mess. The holy city of Qama, the seat of the Church of Bahamut, was occupied by drow allies of Artkos. Even though the war is over, the drow are still there in an enclave. This caused a massive religious schism: the drow appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV, in Qama, while a rival candidate named Qachu II is trying to lead the faith from the city of Qis without any of the actual holy relics. So, while it's physically the 'richest' land, it's politically and religiously fractured right now.
Wait, the drow are just... hanging out in the holy city of the dragon god? That's a huge hook. Is the rest of the country still speaking that 'Old Draconic' language the DM mentioned?

Exactly—the drow haven't despoiled Qama, but they haven't left either. As for the language, they speak Qis. It's the modern national language that remains the most similar to Old Draconic (or Old Qis), which is why Qis'mos is still the center for academic and diplomatic publications. If you're looking for that 'prestige' feel like you'd get in a Silverymoon or a Greyhawk City, Qis'mos is where the old-world scholarly and religious traditions are trying to hold on despite the recent war and the drow presence.

Hey there. I am starting to pull together a monk for a Qamis campaign, probably someone from the Red Mountains area. I was reading through the moon notes provided and I am a bit confused about Luna. I have played in the Realms for years, so I know Selune, and I know Greyhawk has Celene and Luna. Is the moon Luna actually in the sky over Qaia, or is that just for the DM?

In the context of the Qaia moon system, Luna is used as a scientific reference point rather than a moon you would see in the sky. The campaign notes use Earth's Moon (Luna) as the baseline for mass, radius, and distance to help describe the scale of Qaia's seven satellites. For example, when the notes say a moon has 0.54 times lunar gravity, they are comparing it to Luna. However, if you are looking for the moon that most closely resembles the one we know, that would be Quartus. The notes describe Quartus as a clone of Earth's Moon in terms of mass, distance, and brightness. It serves as the natural calendar body for the world's monthly cycle.

Got it, so Quartus is the big one I should care about for my monk's meditations. Since I am in Rathakos, what does the rest of the sky look like? I saw something about a moon that doesn't move. That sounds like a cool bit of local color for a traveler.

That would be Primus. It is a geostationary moon, which is quite rare. Because its orbital period matches the planet's rotation exactly, it hangs motionless over a single point on the equator. If you are in Rathakos, which is in the north, you would see Primus (pronounced Khrimus) as a fixed point in the southern sky. It never rises or sets; it just sits there, one-third the size of our moon and about three magnitudes dimmer. For a monk or a traveler, it is a perfect landmark. Navigators use it to find longitude because it never drifts. Just remember that the Q is always a Kh sound, so the moon Quartus is Khartus.

A stationary moon is definitely going into my backstory. Maybe my monastery uses it as a focal point. What about the others? If I am sitting out on a ridge in the Red Mountains at night, is it just a crowded mess up there?

It is definitely more crowded than Toril or Oerth. Besides the stationary Primus, you have three major moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) that drive the tides and the calendar. Secundus is interesting because it is retrograde—it rises in the west and sets in the east, which is the opposite of the others. Then you have the outer moons, Sextus and Septimus, which look more like very bright, small discs or large stars. Because their orbits are all different, you occasionally get Triple-moon alignments. In the current Qamis era, we just came out of a period of frequent triple-full moons and are heading toward a triple-new moon era around the year 2262.
Wait, the notes mention Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon. Is that Secundus? Does that mean people in Rathakos can look up and see the dragon's prison?

Exactly. Secundus (Khosekundus) is the prison of the dragon Ryraxos. In the present-day Qamis setting, the thirty-year war just ended and Ryraxos was sent there as part of his defeat. It is a dark, iron-rich moon, about 90 percent the size of our moon but dimmer because the surface is so dark. Knowing your character is from Rathakos, a region that suffered under various occupations during the war, looking up and seeing that dark moon rising in the west would be a constant reminder that the 'Dragon Overlord' is gone but still watching from above.

Hey! I am rolling up a character for a Qaringil campaign—thinking about a navigator or an astronomer type. I was looking through the moon charts and saw references to 'Luna.' Does Earth's moon actually exist in this setting, or is that just for me as a player to understand the math?

In the world of Qaia, 'Luna' is strictly a meta-reference for players. It provides a baseline for mass, radius, and brightness so you can visualize how the local moons compare to what we see on Earth.
