Your character wouldn't know the name Luna, but they would be very familiar with Quartus, which is essentially a clone of Earth's moon in terms of size, distance, and brightness. In the Qaringil era, just like in the present-day Qamis setting, Quartus is the primary body used for the monthly calendar.

That makes sense. Quartus is the 'normal' one. I'm really fascinated by the tidal dynamics mentioned in the notes. It says the oscillating tidal force is about 4.4 times what Luna does to Earth. As a navigator in the city of Qaringil, how do I even handle tides that massive? Do the canals in the city just overflow constantly?

The tidal situation is definitely complex for a Qaringil sailor. That 4.4x multiplier comes from the combined pull of Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Because they have different orbital periods (4.53 days, 8.15 days, and 29.5 days respectively), the tides don't just hit a single massive peak every day. They create a shifting, interference-heavy pattern. You'd have to track three different 'lunar' rhythms plus the solar tide. In the Qaringil prequel era, the city's republic is built around these canals, so they've likely engineered the infrastructure to handle the variation, though the notes don't specify exactly how. One thing your character would definitely know is that the tides get especially extreme during 'Triple-moon alignment' eras, which happen roughly once a year.

I'll have to keep a calendar for those alignments then! One more thing for my backstory: I want to be an expert in the 'primsine' method for finding longitude. Since Primus is magically anchored and geostationary, does it look the same in the Qaringil era as it does 200 years later in the Qamis setting? And is the 'prime meridian' at the sub-Primus point already a thing in Qaringil?

Yes, the physics of Primus are identical in both eras because it is magically fixed in place. In Qaringil, as in the later Qamis period, Primus stays at a fixed point in the sky above the equator. Measuring its elevation is the gold standard for longitude because you don't need a precision clock—just an astrolabe and your 'primsine' tables. The sub-Primus point (0 degrees Latitude, 0 degrees Longitude) is the natural choice for a prime meridian. Just remember, as an Islas speaker in Qaringil, you'd pronounce that 'Q' in names like Qaringil or Qis as a 'Kh' sound, following the Old Draconic tradition.

Perfect. 'Kharingil' it is. Since Primus and Secundus are described as 'iron-rich' with double the density of our moon, would my character's magnetic compass be pulled toward them? Or is the magnetic field of the planet the only thing that matters?

That is actually a 'blank space' in the campaign notes. While the documents detail the high density and iron-rich composition of those moons, they don't explicitly state whether they possess a magnetic field strong enough to interfere with surface compasses on Qaia. You should check with the DM to see if they want to treat those moons as magnetic interference sources. For now, the notes focus on their gravitational effects and their use in celestial navigation.

YO!!! I am so sorry I missed the last game! The group chat is blowing up about us heading toward Forthyr. I'm playing a dragonborn paladin so I am HYPED to finally see the land of the Dragoncallers. How much has changed since that prequel one-shot we did set 200 years ago?

It is great to have you back! Forthyr is a fascinating place for a dragonborn character. In the Qaringil prequel era (about 200 years ago), the nation was ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong I. He was a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king, and the dynasty was quite powerful. Fast forward to the present-day Qamis era, and the throne is held by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. However, there is a bit of a tragic twist: the notes mention that while the dynasty used to be dragonborn, the bloodlines have run thin over the generations. They still hold the title, but that draconic heritage isn't as physically prominent as it was in Shanlong I's time.

That is such a cool detail! 'Bloodlines running thin' is a total mood for a fading sorcerer-king dynasty. Is it still a bit 'backwards' like it was in the prequel? And what about that cult that wanted the Dragon Emperors to come back? Did they ever give up?

They definitely haven't given up! In both eras, Forthyr is described as being somewhat 'backwards' compared to the political maneuvering in places like Edrad or Qis'mos. The religious sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors is still very much active in the present day. Interestingly, the Shanlong dynasty—both then and now—gives this sect tacit encouragement or support. It seems like they really lean into that ancient draconic nostalgia to maintain their legitimacy. One big change in the present is that after decades of ignoring the southwest of the continent, the recent Ryraxian War has made it impossible for Forthyr to stay isolated anymore. They're being forced back onto the international stage!

I love that. My paladin is going to have words with those 'Dragoncallers' if they're just using my heritage for political points! Quick question on the name—I keep seeing it spelled with a 'y', is it 'For-thir' or 'For-thigh-er'?

The notes don't give a specific phonetic breakdown for the 'y' in Forthyr, but remember the general rule for the setting: 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh' (like in Qis or Qama). For Forthrian names, most players tend to go with 'For-thir,' but since it's a 'blank space' in terms of specific linguistics, you have some room to interpret how your character would say it. Just watch out for those Old Draconic terms—they can be a mouthful!

Hey there. I'm currently trying to verify some longitude tables for a navigator character in Rathakos. If I'm at 40 degrees North and Primus is at an elevation of 30 degrees, the primsine(30) is 0.693, so my longitude cos lambda should be 0.693 divided by cos(40), which is 0.766... that gives me roughly 25 degrees East, right? Anyway, while I'm doing this, I'm getting confused by the history. Which High King Ezevir Arth am I dealing with? I see one mentioned as doubling the size of Artkos, but then another is ruling now in the post-war era?

Your math for the longitude fix is spot on. Using the primsine(30) value of 0.693 from the navigation notes and dividing by the cosine of your 40-degree latitude (0.766) gives a cosine for longitude of roughly 0.904, which maps to about 25.3 degrees. As long as Primus is to the west of your meridian, you are indeed at approximately 25 degrees East.

Regarding the kings, you are looking at two different men separated by about two centuries. 

In the Qaringil prequel era (roughly 200 years ago), the ruler was King Ezevir Arth II. He is the one who doubled the size of Artkos in the northwest. His daughter, Queen Ezvia Arth I, succeeded him and sought to expand even further.

In the Qamis present day (post-Ryraxian War), the ruler is High King Ezevir Arth. He governs Artkos directly from the capital, Ath'r, and is currently trying to restore Artkos's dominance over the Artklan League after the thirty-year war. The notes don't actually specify a regnal number for him, though he is clearly a successor to the previous line.

Got it. So is the current High King Ezevir the III, or maybe the IV? And do we know exactly when Ezevir II died? I'm trying to map out the exact years of the Artklan expansion to see if it lines up with any specific triple-moon alignments, like the full-moon era of 2253.

That is actually one of the blank spaces in the records. The campaign notes don't provide a regnal number for the current High King Ezevir Arth, nor do they list the specific years of Ezevir II's reign or his exact date of death. We only know that Ezevir II's expansion happened roughly 200 years before the 'present' Qamis setting.

Because the timeline between the Qaringil era and the Qamis era hasn't been fully keyed with specific years yet, we can't be sure if the expansion aligned with a specific triple-moon era like the one in 2253. The DM has left those specific historical dates open for now.

That's a bit of a gap for a navigator! What about his personality then? If the current High King is in Ath'r, is he a dragon-cultist like the people in the Red Mountains near Rathakos, or is he strictly Bahumat-worshipping? The notes mention he's the High King of Artkos, but does he have any draconic blood like the Dragoncallers in Forthyr?

The notes are silent on the current High King's religious leanings or his specific bloodline details. We know that the Dragoncaller dynasty in Forthyr has dragonborn ancestry (though it has thinned over time), but there's no mention of the Arth line in Artkos sharing that trait. 

We do know his political motivation: he yearns to restore the dominance of Artkos over the rest of the Artklan League, viewing the current League as a 'pale shadow' of the old Empire. However, whether he leans toward the Church of Bahumat or tolerates the old dragon cults found in the mountains is another one of those blank spaces. If you're building a backstory or need that detail for your navigation logs, that's something you'd have to coordinate with the DM, as it hasn't been established in the core setting documents yet.

hey there, i am rolling up a character for the qamis campaign. i want to be from rathakos, but i am a bit confused about the lore. do they have powerful guilds there like the ones in waterdeep? i read something about great guilds in qaringil, is that the same place or like a sister city?

It is easy to get those mixed up because they are in the same world, but they are from different time periods. Qaringil is the setting for the prequel campaign, which takes place about 200 years before the current Qamis setting. 

Rathakos is a mountainous border state in the present day, located along the Red Mountains. It is quite a rugged, frontier-style place. It does not have the same kind of established, high-society Great Guilds that Qaringil does. In the prequel era, Qaringil is a wealthy island city-state with seven Great Guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. These guilds basically run the city's republic.

By contrast, Rathakos is a new nation formed fairly recently. Before it was Rathakos, it was part of the Red Mountain Republic, which was organized during the Edran occupation after the Ryraxian War. It is much more focused on adventuring and dealing with mountain unrest than international trade and banking guilds.

got it, so no fancy silk guilds in rath. was the red mountain republic like the lords alliance in forgotten realms then? a group of cities working together? and if i want to play a rogue, is there a thieves guild? i know waterdeep has the xanathar guild, anything like that?

The Red Mountain Republic was less like a loose alliance and more of a short-lived attempt at a unified nation for the mountain provinces. It formed after the dragon Ryraxos (pronounced Rih-rak-khos) was defeated and the Edran military withdrew. However, internal purges and political splits caused it to break apart into two separate nations: Rathakos and Acrokos.

Regarding a thieves guild, the DM notes that in the Qaringil prequel era, there are underground organizations that style themselves as guilds—specifically the Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. In the present-day Rathakos where your character is from, the notes do not mention a formal, named thieves guild like the Xanathar Guild. Instead, Rathakos deals with significant internal unrest, particularly in the Southern Reaches (land annexed from Edrad) and mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults. 

If you want a criminal background, you might find your place among those insurgent groups or the more dangerous, unexplored regions of the Red Mountains. There are also 'blank spaces' in the lore for Rathakos that the DM has not filled in yet, so you could potentially work with them to define a specific local gang or criminal element for your backstory.

that makes sense. i think i will stick with the rathakos mountain vibe. one more thing—you mentioned the dragon ryraxos was defeated. is he dead? or is he coming back like tiamat usually does in those other settings?

In the Qamis setting, Ryraxos is not dead, but he is also not a god-like figure like Tiamat. He was actually a bronze dragon and an artillery commander who rose to power during the thirty-year war. 

Upon his defeat, he accepted exile to Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. Because he is safely exiled there, the campaign notes state he will play no role in the current campaign. The Keyberos dynasty has been restored to the throne in Edrad, and the continent is currently trying to return to how things were before his thirty-year reign, even though the political landscape has fundamentally shifted.

My character comes from Forthyr and follows the sect calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, so Qaringil's 'republic' sounds a bit disorganized to me. How large is the population there exactly, and what keeps their economy going? Is it mostly just a fishing village built around that bridge?

In this era (the Qaringil prequel), the city is far more than a village. Qaringil has a population of around 100,000 people. While the city relies on fishing and importation for food, its economy is primarily commercial rather than agricultural. It functions as a thassolocracy of merchant fleets and nautical robber barons who control the Great Western Bridge and the wizard-made isthmus. The city's wealth is managed by seven Great Guilds, including Shipping, Banking, Silk, and Wool. Unlike Forthyr, where the Dragoncaller dynasty holds central authority, Qaringil is ruled by an Exarch and powerful families like the Peixota and the Volmer. Your character might find it a drastic change from the sorcerer-kings of the northeast, especially given that the elites here are merchants and guildmasters rather than traditional landowners. Also, as a follower of the old ways, you likely know that the 'Q' in Qaringil is pronounced 'Kh' in the Old Draconic style.

Hey! I am working on a backstory for a character from Rathakos. I want them to be a veteran who remembers the 'Red Mountain Republic' days before the split. I'm curious about the big bad—how old is Ryraxos exactly? If I am playing a human, would my character remember him as an ancient wyrm, or was he younger when he took over?

That is a great perspective for a Rathakos native. Actually, Ryraxos is not an ancient dragon at all. He is a bronze dragon and relatively young, likely around sixty years old. Since the Ryraxian War lasted thirty years, he would have been only about thirty when the fighting started. For a human veteran, Ryraxos would have seemed like a rising power—formidable and dangerous, certainly, but more like a brilliant, shape-shifting military commander than a primordial force of nature. In fact, his rise began as an artillery officer; dragons are highly valued in that role because they can physically move the heavy guns.

An artillery officer? That's wild. I love the image of him hauling cannons. So if my character served in the Red Mountain Republic, were they fighting for him or against him? I am trying to figure out if being a 'Republic veteran' makes me a hero or a traitor in modern Rathakos.

In modern Rathakos, being a 'Republic veteran' is a bit complicated. The Red Mountain Republic was actually organized by Edrad during their occupation of the mountains. When Edrad eventually withdrew due to guerrilla resistance and losses on other fronts, a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly took over. They purged the pro-Edran elements and split the Republic into the two nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. So, if your character served in the Republic army, they were likely seen as part of the Edran-aligned administration. Today, you might be viewed with suspicion by the nationalist types or the mountain communities that still follow the old dragon cults.

Got it, so I am probably an Edran sympathizer in the eyes of the current government. What is the vibe in the capital, Rath, these days? Is Regent John Dunne actually in control, or is it mostly just 'blank spaces' and chaos?

Rath is definitely in a state of 'tenuous independence.' Regent John Dunne is governing while the Grand Council deliberates on finding a proper Prince, but there is significant internal unrest. You have tension between the Southern Reaches—which were annexed from Edrad and are mostly Edran-speaking—and the mountain communities that follow the old dragon cults. It is a frontier environment with a strong tradition of adventuring, especially since the Red Mountains remain largely underexplored and dangerous. It is not total chaos, but the authority of the Regent is fragile.
One last thing—since Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon, Secundus, do people in Rathakos just look up and see him? Like, is there a dragon-shaped speck on the moon now?

Secundus is quite large in the sky—about 0.9 times the size of Earth's Moon—but it has a very dark, iron-rich surface with low albedo. While Ryraxos is definitely up there, he is a shape-shifter and only a single dragon on a moon with a surface area of nearly 3 million square kilometers. You wouldn't be able to see him with the naked eye. To most people in Rathakos, he is 'safely' out of the way, though the followers of the mountain cults might still look to Secundus with a bit more reverence than your average citizen.

Hey, I am working on a character from Forthyr who follows that religious sect hoping for the return of the Dragon Emperors. I am a bit confused on the geography though. What continent is Qamis on exactly, and who are our neighbors to the north or south?

In this setting, Qamis is actually the name of the continent itself, located on the planet Qaia. Since your character is from Forthyr, you are in the far northeast of the continent. To your south and west lie the other major powers like Edrad, the Artklan League, and Qismos (pronounced Khis-mos). Beyond the mainland, there is a large, mostly unexplored island to the south where the city of Freeport is located. To the west, across the ocean, is another very large continent called Westron. To the north, the notes do not specify any neighboring continents, as Forthyr is the northeastern limit of the known map.

Got it, so Qamis is the landmass. You mentioned that big island to the south with Freeport—is that the place where people say the dragons actually went? My character would definitely be obsessed with finding out if that rumor is true.

That is exactly right. The notes mention that there is a legend or rumor that the southern island is where the dragons went after they departed the mainland. The area beyond the pirate city of Freeport is covered in dense jungle that the residents are afraid to look at too closely, and the DM has marked much of it as a blank space. Since your character follows the sect in Forthyr calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, that southern island would likely be a major point of interest or even a site of pilgrimage for your order. Whether you are playing in the Qaringil prequel era under Dragoncaller Shanlong I or the present-day Qamis era under Shanlong VI, Forthyr remains the primary hub for those draconic revivalist beliefs.

Hey! I'm just starting to look at the Qaringil materials for our new game. One thing that jumped out at me is the spelling on the map. Why does almost every 'Q' name like Qaringil or Qis skip the 'u'? Is that just a stylistic choice or is there a reason for it in the world?

It is definitely a lore reason! In this setting, 'Q' is pronounced like 'Kh', and that specific 'q without u' spelling comes from Old Draconic, which is also known as Old Qis. It was the language of the ancient dragon empire. Today, it remains the language of scholarly papers, high-level treaties, and diplomacy. Since you are playing in the Qaringil prequel era, you'll see it everywhere because the Holy Qis Empire is currently a major power ruling from Ath'r.

That makes sense. So if my character is from the city of Qaringil itself, would they speak that language? I'm trying to figure out my background—I was thinking of being a member of one of the big merchant guilds.

Most people in Qaringil speak Islas as their primary language, but because the city was originally built by laborers and adventurers from Qis'mos, the local dialect is full of Qis vocabulary. If you're a guild member, you'd definitely know the Islas prestige dialect. Regarding the guilds, there are seven 'Great Guilds' that run the city's economy. If you want to be part of the elite, the 'Five Families'—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—control four of those seven guilds. For example, the Vitelli family (sigil of a moth) leads the Silk Guild, while the Peixota family (sigil of a dolphin) leads the Shipping Guild.
The Qanto family sounds interesting, especially with that 'Q' name. What's their deal in this era?

The Qanto family uses a dove as their sigil. They are quite prominent and involved in the Judicial and Banking guilds, but they are actually in a bit of a rough patch right now. They recently lost leadership of the Mercantile Guild to the Tiddwars, a gnomish family that is more aligned with the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro. It's a great time to be a Qanto if you want a backstory involving a family trying to regain its political footing in the Republic's complicated web of alliances.

yooo, I missed the last session where the party finally rolled into Qaringil. My notes are a mess. Can you give me the quick version of what this city is all about? Like, how big is it and what's the vibe with the economy?

No problem! Just as a reminder for your notes, this campaign is the Qaringil prequel, so we are about 200 years before the 'modern' Qamis era. Qaringil is a massive island city-state with a population of about 100,000 people. It is built on a wizard-made isthmus in the central sea and is famous for its canals and the Great Western Bridge, which the city's fortifications dominate. The 'vibe' here is very different from the continental kingdoms; the elites are primarily commercial, not landowners. The economy is built entirely on maritime trade, banking, and specialized industries like silk and wool. Since the city sits on an island, they have to import most of their food, though they also have a large fishing industry. Also, remember that 'Q' is pronounced 'Kh,' so it is 'Kharingil' (from Old Draconic).
100k people, dang. That is a lot. So who is actually running the show? I heard something about 'Great Guilds' and some big families?

Exactly. Because power is commercial rather than land-based, the city is ruled as a republic centered around seven Great Guilds: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. The Gondolier guild is especially unique because they manage the maintenance and control of the bridges. Most of these guilds are dominated by the 'Five Families': the Peixota (Shipping), the Manos (Judicial), the Qanto (who recently lost the lead of the Mercantile guild to a gnomish family called Tiddwar), the Volmer (Banking), and the Vitelli (Silk). Currently, an Exarch named Giovanni Pesaro is in charge, but he has been in power for 19 years and is considered to be on death's doorstep, which is making the political situation pretty tense.

Hey! I'm looking at the celestial notes for our Qamis game. So if I'm playing a character with the Navigator's Tools proficiency who grew up on the 'far-side' of the planet, I basically have a massive mechanical disadvantage for finding longitude compared to someone from Edrad or Rathakos, right? Why can't I just see Primus from over there?

In game terms, you definitely face a higher DC for longitude checks on the far side. The reason is purely geometric: Primus is a geostationary moon. Because it is magically anchored in a fixed position over the equator at 0 degrees longitude, it never moves relative to the ground. If you are on the opposite side of the planet, the entire bulk of Qaia is between you and the moon. It never rises and never sets for you; it's permanently below your horizon.

That's a trip. So for half the world, the 'easy mode' navigation moon just doesn't exist. What are they using instead? Is it like the old-school Earth problem where they need a super precise clock, or do the other moons like Secundus or Quartus help out?

Exactly. Navigators on the far side have to use much more difficult methods, similar to the 'lunar distance' method used on Earth before portable chronometers. They track the faster-moving moons like Secundus (which is retrograde and rises in the west) or Quartus against the background stars. It requires complex star catalogs and precise timing. On the 'near' side, where nations like Edrad or Qis'mos are located, you just measure the angle of Primus above the horizon and check a table—no clock needed. It's a huge strategic advantage for the central continental powers.
Wait, you mentioned Qis'mos—is that pronounced with a 'K' sound? Also, does this mean if my party sails too far west toward 'Westron' we might eventually lose sight of Primus and just get lost?

You've got it—Q is pronounced 'Kh', so it's 'Khismos'. And yes, if you sail far enough west or east, Primus will sink toward the horizon. The limit of visibility is roughly 81 degrees away from the prime meridian. Once it dips below the horizon, you're back to high-DC Survival checks and dead reckoning. The campaign notes mention that Westron is a bit of a 'blank space' for the DM right now, but losing your primary navigational anchor would definitely be a major challenge for any expedition heading that way.

Hey, I've been reading through these notes for the Qamis setting. It's really dense for a D&D game, isn't it? I was trying to map out a journey from Rathakos using those Primus navigation tables from the Holy Qis Empire era, and honestly, it feels like a lot for a player to track. Does the Exarch Giovanni actually expect his navigators to do trig at the table?

I can see how it looks daunting at first glance! However, you're actually mixing up two different time periods in the setting. 

Rathakos didn't exist during the era of the Holy Qis Empire. That's from the Qaringil prequel, set about 200 years before the current 'Qamis' timeline. Back then, the Holy Qis Empire ruled from Ath'r, and the city-state of Qaringil was led by Exarch Giovanni Pesaro. Rathakos is a much newer nation, formed after the Ryraxian War ended just recently.

As for the complexity, the 'primsine' method is actually meant to make things easier for characters in the world. Because Primus is geostationary (magically anchored so it never moves in the sky), a navigator doesn't need a clock or a chronometer to find their longitude. They just look at the moon's height, check a pre-printed table in their almanac, and they know exactly where they are. For a player, the DM would likely just have them roll a Survival or Navigator's Tools check—the math is there to make the world feel grounded, not to force players to do calculus!

Right, okay. Qaringil is the prequel, Qamis is the present. My mistake. So in the present day, if I'm playing in Rathakos, who is actually running things? I recall reading about a dragon king, but then there's something about a Republic and a Prince?

It's a bit of a mess in Rathakos right now, which is perfect for an adventure. 

During the war, the area was part of the 'Red Mountain Republic' under Edran occupation. But after the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the old Artklan Empire, things shifted. Currently, Rathakos is a Princedom, but they haven't actually found a Prince yet. It's being governed by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council tries to figure out who the monarch should be. 

Ryraxos isn't the king there—he was the 'dragon overlord' who ruled the continent for a few decades, but he was defeated and exiled to Secundus (the second moon). The setting starts just after his departure, with everyone trying to put the pieces of the old nations back together, even though the borders are still pretty unstable.
Wait, so the dragon is on the moon now? Is that the same moon the navigation tables use, or is that a different one? I saw there are like seven of them.

Different moons! There are six main moons (and a tiny particle called Quintus). 

Primus is the first moon. It's the one used for navigation because it's geostationary and never moves from its spot above the prime meridian. It's also the smallest of the inner moons.

Secundus is the second moon. That is where Ryraxos is exiled. It's a dark, iron-rich moon that moves in a 'retrograde' orbit, meaning it rises in the west and sets in the east. It's much larger in the sky than Primus—almost as big as Earth's moon—but because it's so dark, it's about a magnitude dimmer than you'd expect.

Hey nanochat. I was just running the numbers on a longitude fix for 45 degrees N latitude with Primus at 30 degrees elevation (primsine 0.693, right?), but I got distracted by the history notes. It says the Ryraxian War lasted thirty years and ended with Ryraxos being 'exiled to our second moon.' How did that actually work? Was it a magical seal or a military defeat that forced him up there?

The Ryraxian War ended when Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon. It followed a period where the dragon had waged aggressive war across the continent for twenty-five years. While Ryraxos was formidable—particularly as an artillery commander due to his ability to carry guns—he was ultimately defeated. The resolution involved a pact between Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire to depose the Revolutionary Council and restore the Keyberos dynasty to the throne in Edrad. Once defeated, Ryraxos accepted the exile rather than being physically blasted there, and he no longer plays a role in continental affairs. And your math is correct: at 45 degrees N, a 30-degree elevation of Primus (primsine 0.693) puts you at a longitude of roughly 11.5 degrees East or West.

Interesting that he's on Secundus. That's the dark iron moon with the retrograde orbit, right? I was looking at the alignment calendar for the 2253-2263 decade. There was a massive triple-full alignment on Feb 13, 2253, where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus were all at 99.8% to 100% fullness. Did that celestial event have anything to do with the exile, or is that just a coincidence of the 12-year cycle? Also, does his presence there change the tidal force? Secundus already pulls 1.47x Luna's force.

The notes don't explicitly link the Feb 13, 2253 triple-full alignment to the ritual of exile, though that date falls right during the era when the Keyberos dynasty was being restored and the war was concluding. As for the physics, there is no mention of Ryraxos's presence changing the tidal load or orbital mechanics of Secundus. The moon remains a dark, iron-rich body (albedo 0.06) with a 4.53-day period. While Primus is described as 'magically anchored' to stay geostationary, Secundus is a natural retrograde body that slowly spirals inward over geological time. The tidal complexity on Qaia remains driven by the three oscillating inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) plus the solar tide, totaling about 4.4x Luna's force regardless of the dragon's current residence.

OMG hello!! I've been diving deep into the linguistic notes for the Qamis setting and I am absolutely obsessed with the 'Q' without 'u' thing. Hearing that it's pronounced like 'Kh' makes names like Qaringil and Qis'mos sound so much more guttural and ancient—it's such a cool nod to Old Draconic! Speaking of the ancient stuff, I'm playing a character who follows the celestial cycles. When is the next 'great' triple-new alignment? I'm talking the really tight one where everything goes dark!

It really does change the whole vibe of the world once you start saying 'Khis-mos' instead of 'Kwismos,' doesn't it? That Old Draconic influence is everywhere in the formal treaties and scholarly works! 

If your character is looking for the absolute closest triple-new alignment—where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all in their 'new' phase simultaneously—you're looking for May 24, 2262, at 03:00 PMT. It is the 'great' alignment of the current decade! All three moons will be within 0.1% of a perfect new moon syzygy. 

We are currently in a 'new-moon era' (running from roughly 2255 to 2258, and then picking up again around 2262) where these triple-new events are much more frequent than triple-full ones. That May 2262 date is the crown jewel of the cycle!

May 24, 2262! Writing that down right now. That sounds incredibly ominous for a ritual. Since it's a triple-new, does that mean the sky is just pitch black? Or does Primus—the stationary one—still provide some light? I'm trying to figure out if my 'Kh-Draconic' chanting is going to be in total darkness or if I'll have that weird geostationary iron moon staring at me.

It’s going to be very dark, but not total pitch black if you're on the side of Qaia that faces Primus! Since Primus is magically anchored and geostationary, it doesn't participate in the orbital 'dance' of the other three. It stays fixed at one point in your sky. 

However, Primus is a dark, iron-rich moon with a very low albedo (0.06). Even at 'full,' it’s about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's Moon—roughly 1/18th of the light. During a triple-new alignment of the others, you'd just have the dim, steady glow of Primus (assuming it's not also new) and the stars. 

One thing to keep in mind: Secundus is retrograde, so as the alignment approaches, you'll see Secundus rising in the West and moving toward the others while Tertius and Quartus move East. It would look like the moons are all converging on a single point in the sky before they all 'vanish' into the new phase. Very dramatic for a ritual!

Hey nanochat, I'm playing in the Qaringil prequel game this weekend. Can I get an executive summary on the city itself? Specifically interested in the headcount and what the economic engine looks like there.

Good morning! Here is the high-level brief for the city-state of Qaringil as it stands roughly 200 years before the main Qamis timeline:

**The Executive Summary: Qaringil**

*   **Population:** Approximately 100,000 people. It's a dense, canal-filled urban center built on a volcanic island between two massive bridges.
*   **Economic Model:** It is a commercial thassolocracy. Unlike the continental powers, Qaringil’s elites are merchant-princes and guild leaders rather than traditional landowners. They rely heavily on fishing and importing food because they don't have much territory for farming.
*   **Key Industries:**
    *   **Trade & Shipping:** Their armed merchant fleets dominate the Central Sea.
    *   **Logistics:** The Gondolier Guild controls and maintains the bridges and canals, effectively managing the flow of trade across the wizard-made isthmus.
    *   **Finance:** Banking is a major pillar (led by the Volmer family).
    *   **Manufacturing:** Silk and wool are the primary exports, covering both production and trade.
*   **Governance:** An "incomprehensible republic" currently overseen by Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, who has held the post for 19 years but is reportedly on death's doorstep.

Keep in mind that Qaringil is the primary hub for the Islas language, though the local dialect has a heavy Qis (pronounced "Kh-is") vocabulary due to the city's history with adventurers from that region.

An "incomprehensible republic" with 100k people and a dying CEO. Sounds like a fun environment for adventurers. You mentioned guilds—how much power do they actually have compared to the Five Families?

In Qaringil, the Guilds and the Families are heavily interleaved—think of it like a tech conglomerate where the board seats are hereditary. 

There are seven Great Guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool). The Five Families—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—essentially control the leadership of these guilds. For example:

*   **The Peixotas** (Dolphin sigil) run the Shipping Guild.
*   **The Volmers** (Crow sigil) run the Banking Guild.
*   **The Vitellis** (Moth sigil) run the Silk Guild.

However, the Guilds provide the formal structure for power. Even the Exarch, who comes from a minor family, has to navigate the interests of these groups. If you're looking for work, there are also smaller guilds specifically for Adventurers, Alchemists, and Mages. 

It’s also worth noting that because the economy is purely commercial, if you don't have a guild membership or a family tie, you're basically an outsider in the republic's political "codebase."

Hey nanochat, I had to miss the last session when the DM was explaining the celestial mechanics of the planet. I know there are seven moons in the Qaia system, but I missed the specific order and how the tides work. Is the coastline just a disaster zone with that many satellites?

In the world of Qaia (pronounced Khaia), the moon system is quite complex. There are six major moons and a trace particle known as Quintus. In order of their distance from the planet (measured in Lunar Distances or LD), they are:

1. Primus (0.11 LD): Magically anchored and geostationary over the equator. It never rises or sets.
2. Secundus (0.30 LD): A dark, iron-rich moon that moves in a retrograde orbit (rises in the west).
3. Tertius (0.45 LD): A prograde moon with an 8.15-day period, which is the basis for the Qaian week.
4. Quartus (1.05 LD): Almost a twin of Earth's moon; it is the primary calendar body.
5. Sextus (1.64 LD): A smaller, retrograde moon.
6. Septimus (2.47 LD): The farthest major moon, also retrograde, with a 106-day period.
7. Quintus: Not a proper moon, but a trace particle sitting at the Sun-Qaia L4 Lagrange point.

As for the tides, you are right to be curious. The total oscillating tidal force is about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's, driven by the combined tugging of Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. Because they have different orbital periods and directions, the tidal cycle is a complex interference pattern. Interestingly, Primus does not contribute to the daily tides because it is geostationary; instead, it creates a permanent 2-centimeter static bulge in the ocean directly beneath it.

Wait, so with Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus all being retrograde, they rise in the West while the others rise in the East? That sounds like a navigational nightmare. How do sailors even keep track of where they are if the sky is moving in two different directions at once?

It actually makes navigation easier in the near-side hemisphere! Because Primus is geostationary, it serves as a fixed point in the sky. It stays exactly above the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude). 

Navigators use a system where they find their latitude from the pole star and their longitude by measuring the elevation of Primus. Since Primus never moves, you don't even need a chronometer to find your longitude; you just use a lookup table for a function called 'primsine' based on how high Primus is above the horizon. The only downside is that if you sail to the far side of the planet, Primus disappears below the horizon, and you're stuck using much more difficult methods like lunar distances or dead reckoning.
That's wild. What about the light levels? If Tertius and Quartus are both full, is it basically daylight at night?

It gets very bright, but not quite like daylight. Quartus provides about 90 percent of the illumination of Earth's full moon, and Tertius provides about 88 percent. Even though they are large in the sky, they have lower albedo (reflectivity) than Earth's moon. 

The system also goes through 'alignment eras' every 12 years or so. We are currently in a period where triple-full-moon alignments—where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all full at once—happen fairly often. During those nights, the combined light is significant, but because Secundus and Primus are dark iron-rich bodies, they don't reflect as much light as you might expect given their size.
Hey there! I'm really getting into the celestial mechanics of Qaia. Could you start by telling me about the moon Secundus? I know it's one of the inner ones.

Secundus is a fascinating moon, especially for an astronomy enthusiast. It is the second closest moon to Qaia, orbiting at a semi-major axis of 0.30 LD. What makes it immediately distinct is its retrograde orbit—it rises in the west and sets in the east. Physically, it has a mean angular diameter of 28.0 arcminutes, making it about 0.90 times the size of Earth's moon in the sky. However, it appears much dimmer because its surface is dark iron-rich rock with a low albedo of 0.06. In the current Qamis era, it is also notable as the place of exile for the dragon Ryraxos.
A retrograde iron moon? That's incredible. If it's that dense—I see the notes say 6,689 kg per cubic meter—how does that affect the tidal dynamics on Qaia? It has to be a massive driver.

You are exactly right. Despite being smaller than the moon Quartus, Secundus's high density and proximity give it a significant tidal influence. It exerts a tidal force on Qaia that is 1.47 times that of Earth's Moon. But it is just one part of a complex system; Tertius actually exerts an even stronger force at 2.04 times Luna. When you combine Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus, the total oscillating tidal load is roughly 4.4 times what we experience on Earth. Because Secundus is retrograde with a period of 4.53 days, its tidal contribution creates a unique rhythm that interacts with the prograde moons and the solar tide.
4.4 times Luna? The coastal cities like Jia or Qaringil must have some intense sea level shifts. Does the stationary moon Primus add to that, or is it too small?

Primus is magically anchored and geostationary, so its tidal contribution is fundamentally different. Because it doesn't move across the sky relative to the surface, it produces no oscillating daily tide. Instead, it creates a permanent static bulge in the oceans of about 2 centimeters along the Primus-antiprimus axis. It is a permanent geographic feature of the planet's sea level rather than a cycle. Navigators and coastal residents mostly have to worry about the three 'inner' orbiting moons, which occasionally reach triple-moon alignments—eras where all three are full or new simultaneously—leading to extreme tidal events.

Hey! I'm starting a campaign in Qamis soon. I'm a bit of a data nerd and I heard the calendar system is tied to specific lunar orbits—specifically Tertius? Can you give me the executive summary of why that moon defines an 8-day week? I'm hyped to see some actual orbital mechanics in my D&D lore!

It is a fantastic detail for the setting! To give you the executive summary: Tertius is a prograde moon (meaning it orbits in the same direction as the planet's rotation) with an orbital period of 8.15 days. Because this cycle is so regular and prominent in the sky, it forms the natural basis for the standard Qaian week. Physically, Tertius is an iron-enriched rocky world. While it is smaller than Earth's Moon in terms of actual radius (about 0.48), it orbits much closer to the planet at 0.45 Lunar Distance. This makes it appear slightly larger in the sky than our Moon (about 1.09 times the angular diameter). It is the primary timekeeper for short-term life on Qaia.

That is awesome. 8.15 days is a solid sprint length! You mentioned it's iron-enriched—how does that density affect the rest of the system? I saw a note somewhere about tides being way more complex than what we have on Earth.

You spotted the right variable! That density (1.6 times lunar density) combined with its proximity makes Tertius a tidal powerhouse. It exerts 2.04 times the tidal force of Earth's Moon on Qaia's oceans. But the 'release schedule' for tides is even more complex because Tertius is just one of three major oscillating drivers. You also have Secundus (which is retrograde and exerts 1.47 times lunar tide) and Quartus (a lunar clone at 0.87 times tide). When you add them all up with the solar tide, the total tidal force is about 4.4 times what we experience on Earth. It creates a very dynamic maritime environment, especially since the three moons have incommensurate periods of 4.53, 8.15, and 29.5 days!
Wait, one is retrograde? That means Secundus rises in the west, right? Man, the sky-watching in this game is going to be wild.

Precisely! If you are standing on the surface, you would see Tertius and Quartus moving west-to-east, while Secundus—along with the smaller outer moons Sextus and Septimus—sweeps across the sky from east-to-west. And if you are in the right hemisphere, you also have Primus, which is geostationary. It's magically anchored and doesn't move at all, hanging like a fixed point in the sky. For a navigator, this is a dream because Primus allows you to calculate longitude without a chronometer just by measuring its elevation. It's a high-precision system that makes the world of Qamis (pronounced Kh-amis) feel incredibly distinct from a standard fantasy trope.

Hey nanochat! I missed the last session and I am trying to catch up on the lore. The DM was talking about how we are using Rathakos as a base for the Qaringil campaign. He mentioned something about using the dragon Ryraxos to find our longitude, but that it only works well near the equator. Can you explain why the precision is higher there?

Welcome back! It sounds like a few details from the two different eras have been conflated. Rathakos does not exist in the Qaringil prequel; it was established roughly 200 years later after the Ryraxian War. In the Qaringil era, the region was still part of the Holy Qis Empire. Additionally, Ryraxos is a bronze dragon involved in the modern war; he is not a navigational tool. You are likely thinking of Primus, the geostationary moon.

Longitude is found by measuring the elevation of Primus. Precision is highest near the equator and the prime meridian (the sub-Primus point) because that is where the moon is closest to the zenith. When a celestial body is directly overhead, its apparent elevation changes significantly with even a small change in your geographic position. Near the visibility boundary (about 81 degrees away from the prime meridian), Primus sits very low on the horizon. At those shallow angles, the geometry becomes less sensitive, meaning a small error in your measurement results in a much larger error in your calculated longitude.
That makes more sense. So if we are right at the prime meridian, Primus is at 90 degrees elevation? Is that why that 'primsine' math the DM mentioned is easier there?

Correct. At the sub-Primus point (0 degrees N, 0 degrees E), Primus is exactly 90 degrees overhead. The primsine(e) function is a specific formula used by Qaian navigators where primsine(90) equals 1.0. 

The basic longitude formula is: cos longitude = primsine(elevation) / cos latitude. 

If you are on the equator, your latitude is 0, and cos(0) is 1. If Primus is also overhead, the equation becomes cos longitude = 1 / 1, which confirms your longitude is 0. The 'ease' comes from the sensitivity of the measurement: near the zenith, the change in elevation is nearly 1-to-1 with your change in position. Navigators prefer this because their instruments, like a cross-staff or astrolabe, can provide a very tight fix (within 6 to 14 km) when the geometry is that steep.

Morning! I am working on a character for a new campaign. I am playing a half-elven noble from the Keyberos dynasty in Jia, but I am trying to figure out the geopolitical landscape for my backstory. The notes mention 'thassalocracies' of 'nautical robber barons' in the Central Sea. What exactly does that look like in this world, and which nations are actually considered thassalocracies?

A thassalocracy is a state that derives its primary power from naval supremacy rather than control of large land territories. In the world of Qaia (pronounced Khaya), this term specifically applies to the islands scattered across the Central Sea. 

In the present day (the Qamis era, pronounced Khamis), these island states are composed of exiles from both the main continents. They do not have the vast agricultural heartlands of nations like Qismos or Forthyr; instead, they operate as a collection of nautical robber barons. They maintain powerful mercenary navies that they sell to the highest bidder among the continental powers. 

If you are looking at the prequel era (the Qaringil era, roughly 200 years earlier), the prime example is the city-state of Qaringil itself. It is a republic built on an island between two massive bridges, dominated by merchant fleets and powerful guilds (Shipping, Banking, Silk, etc.). While Edrad, your character's home, is the dominant land power, Qaringil and its peers control the flow of trade through the Central Sea.

That is helpful. Since my character is an Edran noble, and we are very strictly half-elven (between one-quarter and three-quarters elf), do these island thassalocracies have similar bloodline requirements for their ruling classes? I am trying to decide if my noble would look down on them as just commoners with boats or if they have their own ancient lineages.

The island thassalocracies generally lack the rigid racial requirements seen in the Edran nobility. In Edrad, the Keyberos dynasty has ruled for centuries (four hundred years in the present day, two hundred in the prequel) and strictly maintains the half-elven bloodline as a badge of status. 

In contrast, the island elites are primarily commercial. For example, in the prequel era of Qaringil, the city is ruled by the 'Five Families' (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli). These families are described as being mostly human, though they all possess some elf ancestry. Their status comes from their leadership of the Great Guilds—Shipping, Judicial, Banking, etc.—and their commercial interests rather than land ownership or a specific elven blood ratio. 

In the present-day Qamis era, the islanders are even more disparate, often described as 'nautical robber barons' and 'exiles.' Your Edran noble would likely view them as culturally inferior 'new money' or opportunistic mercenaries, lacking the refinement and legitimate dynastic weight of the Keyberos line, even if their naval power is impossible to ignore.

hey, got a minute for a setting question? I am reading through the Qamis notes and I am interested in building a character from Kard for the present-day campaign. The notes say governance there is fragile—can you dig into why that is exactly?

In the present-day Qamis setting, Kard is a major financial and shipping center, but its political situation is incredibly tense. It is currently a southwestern enclave of Artkos, which means it is governed by High King Ezevir Arth. However, the fragility comes from a deep-seated identity crisis: much of the population is Edran-speaking, not Artklan-speaking. During the Ryraxian War, about twenty-eight years ago, Edrad invaded Kard, and a significant portion of the local population actually welcomed them as liberators rather than conquerors. Now that the war is over and the 'Settlements of Ath'r' have restored the old borders, the people of Kard find themselves back under Artkos rule, even though many of them feel culturally and linguistically aligned with the Edran nobility in Jia.
That is a great hook for a backstory. So if I am playing a merchant there, what is the 'local color' like? Is it like a military occupation, or is it more of a cold war between neighbors?

It is more of a socio-political cold war. Because Kard is a vital shipping and financial hub, Artkos cannot afford to just burn it down or rule with a purely iron fist without destroying the economy. You have a situation where the wealthy merchant class and the laborers likely speak Edran in their homes and shops, while the official edicts and the tax collectors come from the Artklan capital of Ath'r. There is a lot of resentment because the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad signed treaties to give the city back, essentially abandoning the people who welcomed them. You would see Artkos officials struggling to enforce loyalty in a city where the 'prestige dialect' is that of their greatest rival.

I like the idea of my character being an Edran-speaker who has to navigate this. Were there any specific events in Kard during the war that still affect the vibe today? Like, did the dragon Ryraxos do anything specific there?

The notes do not mention a specific 'Battle of Kard' involving Ryraxos directly, but they do specify that the Edran invasion twenty-eight years ago was the turning point for the city. Since Ryraxos was the one who eventually pushed the Artklan Empire back and then established his own overlordship, the people in Kard would have seen him as the force that ended their brief period of being reunited with Edrad. For a merchant, the 'local color' today involves the frustration of the new Artklan League. While High King Ezevir Arth wants to restore Artkos to its former glory, his subjects in Kard likely view the Artklan League as a 'pale shadow' of the old empire and might still be looking across the Central Sea toward Jia for leadership.
One more thing—if I were playing this same character but in the Qaringil prequel era, would Kard still be this messy?

In the Qaringil era, roughly 200 years earlier, the dynamic is different. Kard is still a major financial and shipping center, but instead of being a contested enclave of Artkos, it is described as hanging on to a 'tenuous independence.' Back then, the Holy Qis Empire ruled from Ath'r over the central continent, and Artkos was much smaller, limited to its historical heartland in the northwest. So in the prequel, Kard is a wealthy city-state trying to avoid being swallowed by the bigger empires, whereas in the Qamis present, it has already been swallowed and is struggling to spit itself back out.

I am a DM looking to borrow some celestial lore for my own world, but looking at your notes for Qamis, Quintus seems like a waste of space. It's listed as a moon but then called a trace particle that is effectively massless. Is there any actual reason for this to be in the document, or is it just fluff?

Quintus is indeed a worldbuilding detail rather than a functional celestial body like the other six moons. According to the MOONS.md notes, it is a trace particle located at the Sun-Qaia L4 Lagrange point, which is 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit. It is included to provide a sense of astronomical completeness and deep-time cycles rather than immediate gameplay impact.

If it has no mass and players can't even see it, it seems like a lot of technical jargon for no payoff. The notes mention a 2,000-year period and libration between 45 and 80 degrees. How is a DM supposed to use that if it's invisible?

You are correct that it has no physical presence in the sky and doesn't contribute to the 4.4x lunar tidal load that the inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) create. Its utility lies in the 2,000-year libration cycle. While the notes state it was 'included for worldbuilding purposes,' a DM could use that specific mathematical point as a location for things not yet detailed, such as a hidden magical sanctuary or a planar anchor that only aligns with the planet every two millennia. It is essentially a celestial 'blank space' similar to the ones mentioned on the map of Qamis.

So it's basically a placeholder for a hidden location. I guess that's a bit more interesting than just a speck of dust. If I wanted to use it, does it ever get closer than that 45-degree mark, or is it always stuck way out there?

The notes are quite specific: it librates between approximately 45 degrees and 80 degrees from Qaia. It stays within that range relative to the Sun-Qaia line and never gets closer. Because it is at a Lagrange point, it is gravitationally stable, meaning it effectively marches ahead of the planet forever. It is the only object in the system that doesn't orbit Qaia directly but rather shares its path around the sun.

I am working through some navigation logs and trying to get a longitude fix before we reach the coast. I have the pole star at 30 degrees North, and I have measured Primus at an elevation of exactly 10 degrees above the horizon. According to my charts of the Holy Qis Empire, this should put me within striking distance of Jia. I am looking forward to seeing the architecture there—I have heard Jia is the cultural heart of the Artklan League. Can you verify my primsine(10) calculation? I have it at 0.398.

Your primsine calculation is perfectly accurate at 0.398, but I should help you troubleshoot your charts! You are currently conflating two different eras and mixing up your national boundaries. First, the Holy Qis Empire only existed in the Qaringil period, roughly 200 years before the present day. In the modern Qamis era, that empire is long gone. Second, while Jia is indeed the cultural heart of the continent, it is the capital of the Kingdom of Edrad, not the Artklan League. The League is a separate political entity to the north and west. If you are aiming for Jia, you are entering Edran waters, where the Keyberos dynasty has ruled for four centuries.

That explains why my history books felt so dusty. If the Holy Qis Empire is 200 years dead, why does Jia still hold so much sway? You would think a new power like the Artklan League would have shifted the cultural center by now. Also, let me finish this fix: if my latitude is 30 degrees, I will use cos(30) which is 0.866. So my longitude should be arccos(0.398 / 0.866), which is about 0.459... so roughly 62.6 degrees longitude? How does that sit relative to the Prime Meridian?

Your math is spot on—a longitude of 62.6 degrees puts you quite a distance from the Prime Meridian, which is defined by the sub-Primus point at 0 degrees. As for Jia, its cultural role is rooted in its longevity and the prestige of the Keyberos dynasty. The Edran nobility are half-elven—specifically required to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf—and their court in Jia has set the standard for art, fashion, and diplomacy for four hundred years. Even though the Artklan League is a major power today, Jia remains the home of the prestige dialect of the Edran language and is considered the most culturally influential city in Qamis. It is where everyone looks to see how a high court should behave, even after the disruptions of the Ryraxian War. One minor tip for your logs: remember that the letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh' in this world, following Old Draconic tradition.
hey nano, thinking about running a one-shot in that Qaringil prequel setting. what's the deal with the Great Western Bridge? is it just a big bridge or is there more to it for the city?

The Great Western Bridge is way more than just a commute; it is essentially the reason Qaringil exists as a power. The city is built on a volcanic island right in the middle of a wizard-made isthmus that cuts across the northern end of the Central Sea. There are two massive bridges connecting this island to the mainland on either side, and Qaringil sits on the western shore, specifically dominating that Great Western Bridge.

For a one-shot, it matters because the city basically acts as the gatekeeper for trade between the two continents. If you want to move goods across the isthmus by land instead of risking the open sea, you have to go through Qaringil's fortifications and over those bridges. It makes the city incredibly wealthy but also a constant target for political maneuvering.
okay so who actually runs the bridge? is it like a military thing or do the merchants have their own guards there?

It is actually managed by the Gondolier guild. In Qaringil's republic, the major guilds hold a lot of the power. The Gondoliers are responsible for both the maintenance and the control of the bridges. Interestingly, they are one of the few major guilds that has never been led by one of the 'Five Families' (the elite noble-ish families like the Peixota or the Vitelli), which adds a layer of political tension.

The city has a population of about 100,000 and is criss-crossed with canals, so the Gondoliers are everywhere. For your game, you could have the players dealing with guild corruption at the bridge checkpoints or perhaps caught in the middle of the Five Families trying to wrest control of the bridge away from the guild.
that sounds like a good setup. what about the Exarch? is he involved in the bridge stuff or is he just a figurehead?

The current Exarch is Giovanni Pesaro. He has been in power for 19 years, but the notes say he is on death's doorstep. He is from a minor family but is closely aligned with the Peixota and Manos families. 

Because he is so weak right now, the republic is in a bit of a state of 'incomprehensible' chaos. The elites are primarily commercial—merchants and bankers—rather than traditional landowners. Since the bridges are the lifeblood of that commerce, any instability with the Exarch's health immediately makes the bridge a flashpoint. If Pesaro dies during your one-shot, every family and guild is going to want their people guarding those bridge towers to ensure their own shipping interests stay protected.

Hi! I was reading about the place names in Qamis and noticed the note about Old Draconic—it's fascinating that the Q is pronounced like Kh. It really changes how I say names like Qaringil or Qismos in my head. I'm also looking at the celestial notes for a navigator character. Why does Primus only cause a static tidal bulge instead of a regular daily tide? Is that because of the magical anchoring mentioned in the logs?

You are spot on about the pronunciation; that Kh sound is a direct inheritance from Old Draconic, which is why you see so many q without u constructions in the setting's geography. Regarding Primus, the reason it does not produce an oscillating daily tide is due to its geostationary orbit. While the moon is indeed described as magically anchored—meaning it suffers no tidal torque or migration—the static bulge is a result of its fixed position. Primus's orbital period is exactly 1.00 days, matching Qaia's sidereal day. Because it stays fixed over a single point on the equator at the prime meridian, it never rises or sets. Since the moon does not sweep across the sky, the gravitational pull on the oceans remains constant in one direction, creating a permanent 2 cm offset in sea level along the Primus-antiprimus axis rather than a cycle of high and low tides. The daily tidal rhythms on Qaia are actually driven by the other moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—which do move relative to the surface.

So my DM is pitching this Qamis setting for a weekend game. Honestly, I have seen enough ancient fallen empire settings to last a lifetime. Give me the executive summary on Qismos. Is it just another standard fantasy kingdom with a slightly different spelling? And what is with the Q?

In this setting, the Q is always pronounced as a Kh sound, so it is Khis-mos. It is a legacy of Old Draconic. As for the nation itself, Qismos is less a standard kingdom and more of a bureaucratic theocratic republic that is currently falling apart. In the present Qamis era, the war has just ended, but Qismos was the primary battlefield because it is the most productive land on the continent. Instead of a hereditary king, they have a Potentate who is elected and can be impeached by a council of nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. It is essentially a corporate-religious merger with a failing leadership structure.

Impeachable leaders? That is a bit more interesting than a Paladin King. But you said the war is over. If the fighting stopped, why is it falling apart? Is it just post-war inflation or is there an actual hook there?

The hook is a massive religious and political schism. The holy city of Qama is the traditional seat of the Church of Bahamut, but it was occupied by drow during the war. The drow stayed on the surface in scattered enclaves and actually appointed their own High Priest, Qelth IV, in Qama. There is now a rival candidate, Qachu II, claiming authority from the city of Qis, but he does not have the holy relics of the office. So you have a nation where the religious center is occupied by former enemies who are technically allies now, and two different people claiming to lead the state religion. It is a jurisdictional nightmare.
Drow as high priests of a dragon god? That is a pivot. What about the language? You mentioned Old Draconic and the Kh sound. Does everyone talk like that, or is it just for the fancy maps?

The Q-without-U spelling comes from Old Draconic, which is the language of scholarship and diplomacy. In Qismos, the local language is Qis, which is the closest surviving relative to that ancient tongue. While there is no single standardized language across the continent, Qis is what you would use if you were dealing with the academy or writing a treaty. It is considered a prestige dialect. If your character starts there, you would likely know the capital dialect and one regional variation.

yo, i'm trying to crunch the numbers on the primsine formula for my navigator's toolkit. if i'm at 30 degrees north and see primus at an elevation of 42.2 degrees, i get 30 degrees longitude using rho=0.1511. but why exactly does this mean we don't need a chronometer? normally for longitude you'd need the exact time to compare local noon to a reference clock.

The reason you don't need a chronometer on Qaia (at least on the near-side hemisphere) is that Primus is geostationary. Unlike the sun or stars, which appear to move across the sky as the planet rotates, Primus is magically anchored in a circular orbit that perfectly matches Qaia's sidereal day. It sits permanently above the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0 degrees N, 0 degrees E).

Because Primus never rises or sets, its position in your sky is a pure function of your geographic location, not the time of day. On Earth, a navigator needs a chronometer to know where a celestial body 'should' be at a specific moment relative to a fixed point like Greenwich. On Qaia, Primus effectively acts as a fixed mountain peak that happens to be 35,800 km in the sky. 

Your calculation works because measuring the elevation (e) of Primus tells you how far you are from that fixed sub-Primus point. Since you already know your latitude (phi) from the pole star, the intersection of your latitude line and your distance-circle from Primus gives you your longitude (lambda). The primsine function handles the geometry of that distance-circle while accounting for the significant parallax caused by how close Primus is to the planet. As long as you can see Primus, the geometry is static, so 'when' you take the measurement is irrelevant.

hi! I am building a character from Rathakos for a new game. I saw a mention of the Red Mountain Republic in the notes—what was that exactly? Also, what kind of smaller guilds would an adventuring party typically interact with in a place like Rath?

In the current Qamis era, the Red Mountain Republic is the former state that preceded Rathakos. It was organized from the mountain provinces while they were under Edran occupation after the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the Artklan Empire. It did not last long; a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly eventually purged the pro-Edran members and split the Republic into the two modern nations of Rathakos and Acrokos. As for guilds, the most specific list of smaller organizations in the campaign notes belongs to the city of Qaringil from the prequel era, which included the Adventurer, Alchemist, and Mage guilds, as well as criminal 'guilds' for Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. While the notes do not provide a specific list for the city of Rath, they emphasize that Rathakos has a very strong tradition of adventuring and frontiersmanship because the Red Mountains are so dangerous. You might also encounter groups in the mountains that still follow the old dragon cults. Just a reminder for your character's background: in this setting, the Q in names like Rathakos or Qis is always pronounced like 'Kh'.

Hey! I missed the last session and I'm trying to catch up on the lore for Forthyr. I think I heard someone say the Dragoncaller dynasty in Rathakos isn't dragonborn anymore because Ryraxos cursed them before he was sent to the moon? My character is a dragonborn so I wanted to know if that affects my backstory.

There are a few things to untangle there to get you back on track! First, the Dragoncaller dynasty doesn't rule Rathakos; they rule Forthyr, which is the enormous nation in the northeast of Qamis. Rathakos is actually the mountainous border state where the current campaign starts, and it is currently governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a Prince.

As for the dragonborn lineage, the notes say the Dragoncaller dynasty stopped being dragonborn because their bloodlines simply ran thin over the generations, not because of a curse. In the present day, the ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. While his family used to be dragonborn, he and his immediate predecessors are sorcerer-kings who look human or elven but retain their magic. 

Ryraxos is a separate figure entirely. He is a bronze dragon who acted as an overlord during the recent thirty-year war and was exiled to Secundus (the second moon) once the war ended. He doesn't have a direct connection to the Forthrian bloodline thinning out.
Oh, got it. I must have mixed up the names. So if we play in that Qaringil prequel the DM mentioned, is the King there also human-looking?

In the Qaringil prequel, which takes place about 200 years before the current Qamis campaign, the situation is a bit different. The ruler at that time is Dragoncaller Shanlong I. According to the notes, he is a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king. So in that era, the draconic physical traits were still present in the dynasty before they faded away over the next two centuries.
That makes sense. Since I'm playing a dragonborn in the present-day Rathakos, am I related to them at all? Or maybe I'm from that Holy Qis Empire?

Actually, the Holy Qis Empire only exists in the Qaringil prequel era; in the present day, that region has changed quite a bit. Regarding your ancestry, there are no 'racial' languages or specific nations just for dragonborn in Qamis. The Dragoncaller dynasty's bloodline is a specific royal lineage in Forthyr, so unless your backstory specifically places you in that family tree, being a dragonborn doesn't automatically make you related to them. Most dragonborn in the present day would just be normal citizens of whichever nation they were born in, like Forthyr or even Rathakos.

I am trying to get this primsine math down while planning my Qaringil prequel campaign. I have my character in the Red Mountains—Rathakos specifically—with a latitude of 40 degrees. If Primus is at 35 degrees elevation, I calculate primsine(35) as 0.74. Is that right? Also, how much do I need to worry about the dragon cults there? Are they still looking for the Dragon Emperors in the mountains?

A few things to adjust for your Qaringil prequel setting! First, a major geographic correction: Rathakos does not exist 200 years ago. In the prequel era, the Red Mountains are claimed by the Holy Qis Empire but are not effectively administered by any state. Rathakos and Acrokos are nations formed much later, following the Ryraxian War in the Qamis present day. 

Regarding the dragon cults: if you are playing in the Qaringil era, you won't find those 'old dragon cults' in the Red Mountains yet. Those specific groups are a present-day issue in Rathakos. However, in the prequel era, you should look toward Forthyr in the northeast. There is a prominent religious sect there calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, and they receive tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty. 

As for your celestial navigation math, let's look at the primsine formula: primsine(e) = rho cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - rho^2 cos^2(e)), where rho is 0.1511. For an elevation (e) of 35 degrees, the result should be approximately 0.671. It looks like you might have missed a step in the calculation or the rho multiplier. If your latitude is 40 degrees North, you would then use cos(lambda) = 0.671 / cos(40) to find your longitude.

Ah, 0.671? Let me re-check my notes. I see it now, I forgot to square the cosine in the first term. Thanks. So if I'm in the Holy Qis Empire during the prequel, is the Dragoncaller dynasty already in power in Forthyr? And do they actually support those cultists, or is it just a local superstition they ignore?

In the Qaringil era, the Dragoncaller dynasty is definitely in power. You are looking at the reign of Dragoncaller Shanlong I, a half-dragonborn sorcerer-king. 

To answer your question about the cultists: the dynasty doesn't officially declare it the state religion, but they do provide 'tacit encouragement.' It isn't just a local superstition; it is a significant religious sect that wants the Dragon Emperors to return and rule. While the rest of Qamis might view Forthyr as 'backward' because of this, the Dragoncallers find the movement useful for maintaining their own authority as scions of dragon blood. 

By the way, remember that the Q in names like Qis, Qaringil, and Qama is always pronounced like 'Kh.' It comes from Old Draconic, which is the scholarly language used for treaties in the Holy Qis Empire during this time.

Hey! I'm working on a character from Rathakos for a new Qamis campaign. I was reading about the history of the Red Mountains and saw that my home was once part of something called the Red Mountain Republic during the war. How did that turn into Rathakos, and how did a dragon like Ryraxos fit into that if the notes say he wasn't even that impressive for a dragon?

Welcome to Rathakos! To understand the shift, you have to look at the Ryraxian War. Originally, your region was part of the Artklan Empire. When the dragon Ryraxos rose to power and dissolved the Empire, Edran forces moved in and occupied the mountains, organizing them into the Red Mountain Republic. However, after Edrad withdrew, a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran leadership and split the territory into two new nations: Rathakos and Acrokos. As for Ryraxos, he is described as not being 'truly impressive' because, biologically, he is just a relatively young bronze dragon (about sixty years old). A high-level adventuring party or a coordinated artillery battery could actually take him down. He wasn't an ancient, world-ending titan; he was more of a formidable military leader.

Wait, if he was just a 'formidable' young dragon who could be beaten by a battery of cannons, how did he manage to wage war for twenty-five years and conquer so much? It seems weird that the notes call him an 'artillery officer' rather than a terrifying overlord.

It comes down to his background and tactics rather than raw magical power. Ryraxos's rise started because he was a brilliant artillery officer. Dragons are perfect for that role since they can carry the heavy guns themselves and fly them to advantageous positions. He utilized shape-shifting and strategic command to dominate the continent for decades, rather than just relying on his breath weapon. Even though he wasn't an ancient wyrm, he was dangerous enough that his defeat resulted in a formal exile. He eventually accepted a deal to be sent to Secundus, Qaia's second moon, which is where he remains today. So while he was 'just' a bronze dragon, his impact on Qamis was massive because of his military mind.

I'm looking into the Bahamut schism for my cleric's backstory. Since the Holy Qis Empire rules from the holy city of Ath'r, how did the drow manage to appoint Qe'lth IV there without the Emperor's guildmasters intervening? It seems like a massive security failure for the capital.

It looks like there are a couple of mix-ups between the two eras of the setting. First, the 'Holy Qis Empire' actually belongs to the Qaringil era, which is the prequel set 200 years ago. In the present day (the Qamis campaign), that empire no longer exists. Ath'r is now an exclave controlled directly by High King Ezevir Arth of Artkos. Second, while Ath'r has been the seat of various central Qamisi empires for centuries, it isn't the 'holy city' of Bahamut. That would be Qama. The drow occupation and the appointment of the high priest Qe'lth IV happened in Qama, which is located in the heartland of Qis'mos, not in Ath'r. The schism is between Qe'lth IV in Qama and the alternate candidate, Qachu II, who is currently operating out of the city of Qis.

Ah, thanks for straightening that out. So Ath'r is purely a political capital now under Artkos? I thought because Q is pronounced 'Kh' and comes from Old Draconic, the Church of Bahamut would be centered wherever the Old Qis institutions were strongest. If the High King in Ath'r controls the old imperial seat, does he stay out of the Qama/Qis religious fight entirely? And where does the Dragoncaller in Forthyr fit into the theology?

You're right about the linguistic link—the Q (pronounced 'Kh') does come from Old Draconic, and Qis'mos is indeed where those ancient institutions persisted longest. However, the religious weight stayed in Qama. Currently, High King Ezevir Arth is focused on restoring Artkos's dominance over the Artklan League; the notes don't mention him taking a direct side in the Bahamut schism, though the instability in Qis'mos certainly affects the region's geopolitics. As for Forthyr in the northeast, Dragoncaller Shanlong VI (a sorcerer-king) oversees a somewhat 'backwards' nation where a prominent religious sect calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. This sect receives tacit encouragement from the Dragoncaller dynasty, which is a different religious flavor than the orthodox Church of Bahamut in Qis'mos. While the Church of Bahamut is dealing with the drow-led schism in the south, Forthyr is looking back toward the age of true Dragon Emperors.

Alright, I have played in a dozen homebrew worlds and half of them fall apart the moment you ask about anything beyond the starting tavern. You have got seven moons here. Tell me about the outer ones, Sextus and Septimus. Are they actually relevant to the game, or just window dressing?

Sextus and Septimus are the outer retrograde moons of Qaia. They are significantly smaller and farther out than the main inner moons like Quartus or Tertius. Sextus has a period of about 57 days, and Septimus takes roughly 106 days to complete an orbit. They look like small, bright discs in the sky rather than full moons. They rise in the west and set in the east because they are retrograde. From a mechanical standpoint, they are used as long-cycle calendar bodies, but the notes do not assign them specific magical properties or surface features yet.

So they are basically just lights in the sky. What is actually on them? Are there ruins, lunar dragons, or some ancient evil trapped there? Or is this just a blank space the DM has not bothered to flesh out?

Currently, they are exactly that: a blank space. While the notes specify that the dragon Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus (the second moon), there is no mention of life, ruins, or specific inhabitants on Sextus or Septimus. Physically, they are described as dark, rocky bodies with an albedo of 0.09 and a density similar to Earth's moon.
