retrograde? like in astrology when people say mercury is in retrograde and everything goes wrong? in dragonlance the moons are literally gods like solinari. are these moons magical gods or just rocks?

In Qamis, retrograde refers to the physical orbit. A prograde moon moves in the same direction as the planet's rotation, so it rises in the east and sets in the west. A retrograde moon orbits the opposite way, meaning it rises in the west and sets in the east. Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus are retrograde. Primus, Tertius, and Quartus are prograde. As for being gods, the notes don't describe them as sentient deities like in Dragonlance, though the dragon Ryraxos was recently exiled to Secundus after the thirty-year war. Most people follow the Church of Bahumat, especially in Qis'mos.

wait so if i'm in a city like waterdeep... i mean jia, i think that's the capital? if i look up at night, some moons are going one way and others are going the opposite way? that sounds super confusing for sailors.

Actually, it makes navigation much easier! If you are in the city of Jia (the capital of Edrad) or anywhere in that hemisphere, you can see Primus. Primus is a geostationary moon, meaning it is magically anchored and stays fixed in one spot in the sky. Navigators use its elevation to find their longitude without needing a clock. But you're right about the others: while you watch Primus sit still, you'd see Tertius and Quartus drifting east, while Secundus, Sextus, and Septimus sweep past them toward the west.

Honestly, this whole thing with the nations is a headache. You've got the Artklan Empire and the Artklan League and Artkos—it's all too much. Are the League and the Empire even different things, or is this just more fluff to memorize?

It can certainly feel like a lot of nomenclature, but there is a specific historical distinction in the current Qamis era. The Artklan Empire is the old version that used to rule forty percent of the continent before the Ryraxian War. The Artklan League is the modern, post-war version, which the notes describe as a "pale shadow" of the former empire. While the High King in Artkos (the central heartland) wants to restore the old glory, the other members of the League, like Rathakos and Acrokos, are actually independent states that are only nominally his subjects. In short: the Empire was a unified superpower; the League is a fragile, modern alliance of states trying to keep things from falling apart further.

I've been diving into the lore of Qaia and it's a really cool setup. I'm a bit confused on the geography though—did the Holy Qis Empire found Rathakos before they exiled that dragon Ryraxos to the second moon? I'm trying to map out the major powers on the continent of Qamis.

You've actually combined two different points in history there! In the Qaringil era (about 200 years ago), the Holy Qis Empire ruled from Ath'r, but Rathakos and Acrokos did not exist as nations yet—those regions were simply non-administered parts of the Red Mountains. The dragon Ryraxos is a figure from the Qamis present-day era; he was only recently exiled to Secundus (the second moon) after the thirty-year Ryraxian War ended. Rathakos was established quite recently, following the collapse of the Red Mountain Republic and the withdrawal of Edran forces.

Ah, okay, I got the timelines crossed. So Rathakos is a 'modern' nation in the setting. That clears it up. What about the rest of the world? Is the continent of Qamis the only landmass, or is Westron the place where everyone says the dragons went after they left the mainland?

The world of Qaia has a few known landmasses besides Qamis. Westron is a very large continent to the west, though it is currently a blank space the DM hasn't filled in yet, other than noting that its people generally do not worship Bahumat. As for where the dragons went, the rumors actually point to a big, mostly unexplored island to the south. There is a pirate city called Freeport on the shore of that island, but the jungle beyond it is a mystery. Some say that is where the dragons vanished to, rather than Westron.

Interesting. And the moons are definitely a huge part of the worldbuilding. You mentioned Ryraxos is on the second moon—is that the one that stays in the same place in the sky? I read that navigators use a moon for longitude.

Actually, those are two different moons. Secundus is the second moon and the current prison of Ryraxos; it is a retrograde moon that rises in the west and moves across the sky. The moon used for longitude is Primus, the first moon. Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit, meaning it stays fixed over one spot on the equator (longitude 0). Because it never moves, navigators on the near-side hemisphere can measure its elevation to determine their exact longitude without needing a chronometer.

Look, I'm trying to get a handle on this 'Artklan League' but it feels like the lore is intentionally making it hard. Is the High King in Qaringil or Rathakos? And if it's an Empire, why call it a League? There are way too many names starting with Q and it's all running together.

I understand it can feel like a lot to track, especially with the 'Q' names which are always pronounced like a 'Kh' (so 'Qamis' sounds like 'Khamis'). To clear things up: you are actually mixing up two different time periods. The Artklan League exists only in the 'Qamis' present day. It is a looser, weaker alliance that replaced the old Artklan Empire after the thirty-year Ryraxian War. The High King, Ezevir Arth, rules Artkos, which is the heart of the League. He is not in Qaringil; that is an island city-state from the prequel campaign set 200 years in the past. Furthermore, Rathakos doesn't have a king at all right now; it is a new mountainous state currently ruled by Regent John Dunne while they search for a Prince.
See? This is the problem. If Rathakos is a brand new country, why does it have a regional dialect of Artklan? And where does that dragon Ryraxos fit in? Did he start the League or the Empire?

It simplifies once you see the timeline of the war. Ryraxos didn't start the Empire; he effectively destroyed it. He was a bronze dragon and artillery commander who took over Edrad and then spent twenty-five years conquering the continent. When he was finally defeated and exiled to the moon Secundus, the nations tried to restore the old borders. The 'Artklan League' was formed as a 'pale shadow' of the old Empire to keep some order. As for the language, even though the nation of 'Rathakos' is a new creation following the war, the people living in those mountains have been part of the broader Artklan cultural sphere for centuries. They speak a regional dialect of Artklan because of that history, even though their specific government is new.

Hey! My friend invited me to a game in this Qamis setting. It sounds cool but I'm mostly used to reading fantasy novels set in the Forgotten Realms. Is this basically like Faerun? I heard there's a big dragon cult in the northeast called Forthyr. Is that like the Cult of the Dragon who wants to bring back Tiamat?

Welcome to the setting! It is a homebrew world called Qaia, specifically the continent of Qamis, so it doesn't use Forgotten Realms lore like Tiamat or Faerun. One big thing to start: the letter Q is pronounced like a Kh sound, so Qamis sounds like Khamis. Regarding Forthyr, it is actually a massive nation in the northeast ruled by a sorcerer-king called the Dragoncaller. In the present day of the Qamis campaign, the ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. There is indeed a prominent religious sect there calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors, but it isn't exactly like the Cult of the Dragon. Instead of trying to turn dragons into dracoliches, they want to restore the ancient rule of the dragons. The Dragoncaller dynasty actually gives this sect tacit encouragement, likely because their own bloodline—which used to be dragonborn—has run thin over the generations.

Oh, so the Dragoncallers are like the Targaryens or something? If the bloodline is thin, does that mean they are losing their powers? In D&D books I usually see Dragonborn as a whole race, but you mentioned they are a dynasty. Is it like a class thing there?

It is more of a dynastic legacy in Forthyr. In this setting, there aren't traditional 'racial' languages or rigid racial divides in the same way you might see in a Forgotten Realms novel. For the Shanlong dynasty in Forthyr, being dragonborn was a mark of their power and right to rule as sorcerer-kings. The notes describe Forthyr as somewhat 'backwards' compared to the rest of the continent. The fact that their draconic blood is thinning is exactly why that religious sect is so interesting—they want to bring back the original Dragon Emperors to restore that lost glory. It is different from the present-day situation in the west, where a dragon named Ryraxos actually did take over for a while, but he was recently exiled to the second moon, Secundus, after a thirty-year war.

Exiled to the moon? That is wild. Is it like a Bahamut vs Tiamat setup then? My friend said something about a Holy City of Bahamut, which sounds very Paladin-heavy like some of the stuff I've read about the Platinum Dragon.

The Church of Bahamut is a major force, especially in the region of Qismos (pronounced Khis-mos). Their traditional seat is the holy city of Qama. However, it is a bit more politically messy than a standard 'good vs evil' god setup. In the current era, Qama was occupied by drow during the Ryraxian War. The drow actually appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This caused a schism, so there is another candidate for the leadership, Qachu II, who is operating out of the city of Qis without the official relics of the office. So while Bahamut is central, the 'holy' side is currently split and recovering from the war. It's less about a cosmic war between dragon gods and more about who controls the cathedrals and the ancient Draconic institutions that survived the fall of the old empires.

Hey! I am working on a character for the Qamis campaign. I want them to be from Forthyr, specifically part of that religious sect that's calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors. But I was looking at the notes for Edrad and saw their nobility has to be half-elven? Like, specifically between one quarter and three quarters elf? That is so precise. Why is the Keyberos dynasty so obsessed with the math of it?

That is a great starting point for a character. In Forthyr, your character likely looks at the 'thinning' bloodlines of the Dragoncaller dynasty with a bit of concern, which makes that sect's call for the return of the true Dragon Emperors feel very urgent. As for Edrad, the Keyberos dynasty has ruled for about four hundred years in the present day—aside from the recent mess with the Revolutionary Council and Ryraxos— and they've used that 'half-elven' requirement to maintain a very specific cultural identity. By mandating that nobility stay between 25% and 75% elven, they ensure the ruling class remains distinct from both the shorter-lived humans and the more 'alien' full elven societies. It keeps power concentrated in the coastal capital of Jia, which is the most culturally influential city in Qamis. It's as much about gatekeeping social status as it is about lineage.

So if an Edran noble marries a human and their kid is only 1/8th elf, they just lose their status? That sounds like a nightmare for their lawyers. Does that rule still hold up in places like Rathakos? My character probably thinks it is all silly compared to dragon blood, but I'm curious if the rest of the Artklan League follows those rules.

It definitely keeps the Edran genealogists busy! If a child falls outside that range, they technically lose their standing in the high nobility of Jia. However, outside of Edrad, things are much messier. In the Artklan League—specifically in Rathakos where the campaign starts—those Edran blood-math rules don't carry legal weight. Rathakos is a mountainous border state currently ruled by Regent John Dunne while they hunt for a Prince; they are more concerned with surviving the Red Mountains than checking elven percentages. Many people in the Southern Reaches of Rathakos speak Edran and might respect those old traditions, but the nation itself is trying to forge its own identity after being part of the Red Mountain Republic. To your Forthrian character, the Edran obsession with 'pointy-ear ratios' probably seems like a massive distraction from the true spiritual power of the dragons.

Exactly! We have a real dragon, Ryraxos, and everyone just sent him to the moon. I was reading about the moons—is Secundus the one where he is trapped? Since it's retrograde and rises in the west, is it easy for my character to pick it out in the sky and, I don't know, pray to it or something?

You nailed it. Secundus is indeed where Ryraxos was exiled after the thirty-year war. It's a dark, iron-rich moon, so even though it's nearly the size of Earth's moon in the sky, it's about a magnitude dimmer because of its low albedo. Because it's retrograde, it is the one moon that rises in the west and sweeps across the sky in the opposite direction of Tertius and Quartus. For a member of a Dragon Emperor revival sect, Secundus is a powerful symbol—a dark, iron cage in the sky. When the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) align for a 'triple-new' or 'triple-full' event, it’s probably a huge deal for your character's faith. Just remember that while Ryraxos is a 'dragon overlord,' he’s actually a relatively young bronze dragon who started as an artillery commander, not one of the ancient god-emperors your sect is likely dreaming of.
Hey! I'm starting my first D&D campaign in Qamis and I'm making a character from Edrad. My DM said it's the most powerful place. What's the capital called and where is it exactly on the map?

Welcome to your first campaign! The capital of Edrad is a city called Jia. It is located on a peninsula that juts out into the Central Sea. Because of its location and history, it is by far the most culturally influential city in Qamis. In both the present-day Qamis setting and the Qaringil prequel era, it is the seat of the Keyberos dynasty. One unique detail about Jia and Edran society is that the nobility is specifically half-elven; to hold a noble title, a person must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf.

That's a cool detail about the half-elves. Since it's on a peninsula in the Central Sea, does that mean there are a lot of ships and stuff? And are there other big cities in Edrad I should know about for my backstory?

Yes, being on a peninsula makes Jia a major hub for trade and maritime activity, and Edrad is described as the most economically dominant nation. Regarding other cities, the notes for the present-day Qamis setting mention Etops and Qeth as other Edran cities. If you are playing in the Qaringil prequel era, Edrad doesn't control Qeth yet—conquering it is actually a major goal of their foreign policy at that time. Also, keep in mind that the 'Q' in names like Qeth is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound, which comes from the Old Draconic language used in scholarly works.

Hey! I am prepping a session for my own group and looking to steal some of your dragon cult lore. I have a note here that says the Keyberos kings in Rathakos are secretly backed by the Dragoncaller's cult from Qaringil. Is that right?

I can help clarify that for you, but it looks like a few different names and eras have gotten mixed up. First, the Keyberos dynasty actually rules Edrad (the coastal nation), not Rathakos. Rathakos is a mountainous border state that is currently governed by a Regent named John Dunne while they look for a prince. Second, the title 'Dragoncaller' belongs to the sorcerer-kings of Forthyr, a nation in the northeast. Lastly, Qaringil is the setting of the prequel campaign set 200 years ago; it is an island city-state in the Central Sea. In the present day of Qamis, the dragon cults persist in two specific places: the mountain communities of Rathakos (remnants of the 'old' cults) and a religious sect in Forthyr that calls for the return of the ancient Dragon Emperors.

Right, my mistake. The borders are a bit a messy in my head. So if I'm running a game in the present day, right after the Ryraxian War, are these cultists worshipping Ryraxos? He seems like the obvious choice now that he is stuck on the second moon.

Interestingly, the notes do not actually link the cults to Ryraxos. In the Qamis setting, Ryraxos is a bronze dragon and is relatively young—only about sixty. He rose to power as an artillery commander first, not a god. While he is powerful, he is not considered ancient or divine like the entities the cults usually focus on. The 'old dragon cults' in the Red Mountains of Rathakos represent traditions that predate the current nations, likely looking back to the original Draconic Empire. In Forthyr, the cult specifically desires the return of the 'Dragon Emperors,' which is a title for the ancient rulers who preceded the current Dragoncaller dynasty. Ryraxos's exile to Secundus is a very recent event, so while someone might start a new cult around him, the established ones are looking much further back into history.
That makes sense. Is there a big difference between those cults and the main Church of Bahamut? I saw that the drow are in the holy city now.

The Church of Bahamut is the mainstream, institutional religion, particularly dominant in Qis'mos. Its seat is the holy city of Qama. The situation there is tense because the drow occupied Qama during the war and appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. This caused a schism with another candidate, Qachu II, who operates from the city of Qis but lacks the holy relics. The 'dragon cults' in Rathakos and Forthyr are generally seen as separate, more fringe, or even 'backwards' compared to the Church of Bahamut. The Forthyr sect is even described as a group calling for the return of the old emperors, which is a political and religious goal that the current half-dragonborn rulers of Forthyr treat with 'tacit encouragement' rather than official adoption.
Hey, I was reading through some notes and saw a mention of a place called Qama. Is that the city with the drow in it?

Yes, that is correct. Qama (pronounced Khama) is the holy city of the god Bahumat, located in the region of Qis'mos. In the present-day Qamis setting, it is currently occupied by drow. They didn't originally conquer it for themselves; they were actually recruited as allies by the nation of Artkos during the late stages of the thirty-year Ryraxian War. It was described as a desperation move by Artkos, but when the war ended, the drow didn't return to the Underdark. Instead, they remained on the surface in several enclaves, with Qama being the most significant one.
That is wild. If it is a holy city for Bahumat, why did the people of Qis'mos just let them stay? Did the drow burn the temples or something?

Actually, the notes specify that the drow have not despoiled the city. They haven't destroyed the temples or the relics. Instead, they did something much more politically complex: they co-opted the religious hierarchy. They appointed their own drow high priest, Qe'lth IV, to lead the church in Qama. This has caused a massive religious schism. There is a rival candidate for the high priesthood named Qachu II who operates out of the city of Qis, but he doesn't have any of the official relics of office because the drow still hold the holy city and all its treasures.
I love that. It is more of a cold war than a standard 'monsters in the city' trope. Who is actually in charge of the drow there? Is it still Artkos?

Artkos is technically the leader of the Artklan League, but their control is fragile. While they brought the drow in, the drow enclaves like Qama are largely independent now. In the Qis'mos heartland, the local government is run by a Potentate, but that region was one of the most heavily fought over during the war. Between the drow occupying their holiest site and the regular political instability of the Potentate system—where leaders are elected and impeached by guilds and clergy—the whole area is a powder keg. If you are looking for campaign hooks, the tension between the 'official' drow high priest in Qama and the exiled priest in Qis is a major one.
Hey! My DM mentioned that the moons in this world are super important for keeping track of time. There are like... six of them? What is the deal with them exactly?

There are actually seven moons orbiting Qaia, though six are the major ones you will see. They are named Primus through Septimus. The most unique one is Primus; it's magically anchored and geostationary, meaning it stays fixed in one spot in the sky and never rises or sets. The others move at different speeds. For example, Tertius has an 8.15-day period which is the basis for the Qaian week, while Quartus is very similar to Earth's moon with a 29.5-day cycle.
A moon that stays still is wild. But I heard some NPCs talking about a 'triple alignment' coming up. Does that happen a lot?

It happens more often than you might think, but the quality of the alignment varies. The three inner moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—are within 5% of being simultaneously full or new about five times a year. However, the system goes through 12-year cycles called 'eras.' You'll have a few years where 'triple-full' alignments are common, followed by an era where 'triple-new' alignments dominate.
A 'triple-new' era sounds way creepier for a first campaign. When does that start? Are we in one right now in the Qamis era?

In the current Qamis timeline, the decade between 2253 and 2263 is split. There was a full-moon era at the start, but we are moving into a new-moon era that spans roughly 2255 to 2258. There are several 'triple-new' events in that window, such as April 26 and September 21 in the year 2256, where all three moons hit their new phase within 0.1% or 0.2% of each other.
I want to know about the absolute biggest one. Like, the darkest night possible where they are all perfectly lined up. When is the next 'great' triple-new alignment?

The closest and 'greatest' triple-new alignment of this decade occurs on May 24, 2262, at 03h PMT. On that night, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus will all be within 0.1% of syzygy (the new moon phase) simultaneously. It is the most mathematically perfect alignment of the decade for a triple-new event.
Wait, if the moons are all 'new' at 0.1%, does the sky just go pitch black? And do the far-out moons like Septimus do anything then?

It would be exceptionally dark. Since the inner moons provide about 4.4 times the tidal force of Earth's moon, the tides would also be quite intense. As for the outer moons, Sextus and Septimus have much longer orbits (57 and 106 days respectively) and are much dimmer, so they don't usually sync up with the inner three. They'd just be tiny, faint discs elsewhere in the sky. Also, remember that Primus is always there, though as a geostationary moon, its phase changes based on the sun's position just like the others.
Is there any lore about dragons or the old empires doing stuff during that 2262 alignment? Like, is that a night where magic gets weird?

The campaign notes mention that the long-term stability of these moons might be tied to dragon magic, and there are 'old dragon cults' in the Red Mountains of Rathakos that still follow these celestial signs. However, the specific magical effects or historical events planned for the May 2262 alignment are currently a 'blank space' in the DM's records. It is a perfect opportunity for you to develop a backstory or for the party to investigate what the cults might be planning for that specific date.

yo, quick question about the moon data for the Qamis campaign. I was looking at the notes and saw that Ryraxos was exiled to Primus at the end of the war. But wait - I also noticed Primus and Secundus have the exact same density (6,689 kg/m3). Is that a copy-paste error in the docs, or is there a reason the geostationary one and the dragon's moon are identical?

Actually, a quick correction first: the dragon Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus, the second moon, not Primus. Primus is the tiny, geostationary moon that stays fixed over one point on the equator. As for the density, it is not a typo. Both Primus and Secundus have that specific density (6,689 kg/m3) because they are both iron-rich. The notes suggest they are likely fragments of the same differentiated progenitor body - essentially, they are both chunks of a metallic core from a shattered moon or planetoid.

Right, Secundus! Got my moons mixed up. I was thinking of the navigation guide where they use Primus for longitude since it never moves. Since they are fragments of the same thing, does that mean they look the same in the sky? And is it weird that Secundus is retrograde if they came from the same parent body?

They do share the same dark albedo of 0.06 because of that iron-rich surface, so they are both quite dim compared to a rocky moon like Quartus. However, they look very different from the ground. Secundus is much larger, appearing about 90 percent the size of Earth's moon, and it rises in the west because it is retrograde. Primus is much smaller (only about a third the size of Earth's moon) and it never rises or sets; it just hangs there like a faint, fixed star for anyone in the right hemisphere. As for the retrograde orbit of Secundus, it is unusual, but the notes say it is stable for now, though it will slowly spiral inward over geological time. Primus is a special case because it is magically anchored in its geostationary position, so its orbital physics are likely a result of that construction rather than natural capture.

Hey there! I'm rolling up a half-elven noble from Edrad for the Qaringil campaign. I'm trying to figure out if my family would have business ties in the city of Qaringil or if they'd look down on it. What's the deal with the ruling class there compared to the Keyberos dynasty back home in Jia?

In the Qaringil era (about 200 years before the Qamis present), the social divide between Edrad and Qaringil is quite sharp. 

Edrad is a traditional monarchy ruled by the Keyberos dynasty. Its nobility is strictly half-elven—by law, you must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf to hold that status. This creates a very specific, refined prestige culture centered in the capital, Jia.

Qaringil, by contrast, is a commercial republic of about 100,000 people. It's built on a wizard-made isthmus and ruled by an Exarch (currently Giovanni Pesaro) and a series of Great Guilds. The 'Five Families'—Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli—are the true power there. While they have some elf ancestry, they are described as 'mostly human.' 

Your Edran family might view Qaringil as a city of 'nouveau riche' merchants and nautical robber barons, but the city's control of the Great Western Bridge and its massive merchant fleets make it an essential, if perhaps 'crass,' economic partner for Edran trade.

That makes sense. If the Five Families are 'mostly human,' they probably wouldn't even qualify for nobility in Jia, right? I'm looking at the Vitelli family—they run the Silk Guild. Do the notes say if they have any specific marriage alliances with Edran houses like mine to try and 'elevate' their bloodline?

You're exactly right on the first point: based on the Edran requirement of being at least 25% elf, many of Qaringil's elite families likely wouldn't meet the legal threshold for nobility in Edrad. 

As for the Vitelli family specifically, the notes mention they lead the Silk Guild and have interests in shipping and mercantile trade, but their specific diplomatic or marital ties to Edran noble houses are currently a blank space. The DM hasn't detailed their family tree or specific international alliances yet. This could be a great opportunity for you to suggest a connection in your backstory—perhaps a Vitelli merchant-prince is trying to marry into your house to gain legitimacy in Jia, or vice-versa for access to the silk markets.

I like the idea of a contested marriage pact. Since Qaringil is a hub, what do we know about their trade with Westron or that big jungle island to the south? If my family is investing in Qaringil, they'd want to know where the money is actually coming from.

That is another area where the records are currently sparse. Regarding Westron (the continent to the west), the notes explicitly state it is a 'blank space' that hasn't been prepared yet, other than the fact that they generally do not worship Bahamut. 

Similarly, the big island to the south is described as mostly unexplored. There is a city of exiles and pirates there called Freeport, and some rumors suggest it's where the dragons went after they departed the continent, but the elites in Qaringil—and the rest of Qamis—generally 'try not to look too hard' at the jungle there. 

In short, while Qaringil’s fleets definitely fan out across the Central Sea, the specific details of their trade routes to Westron or the deep south are currently left to be filled in. If your character's family has a specific trade interest there, you might be the one helping the DM define what those 'exotic' imports actually are.

Hey! I'm putting together a character for a game set in the present-day Qamis era. I'm playing a half-elven noble from Jia, so a member of the Edran upper crust. I saw a note about a sect in Forthyr that wants to bring back the Dragon Emperors. What can you tell me about them? My character would probably find that whole 'dragon-worship' thing pretty primitive compared to Jia's culture.

That is a great character hook. From the perspective of an Edran noble in Jia, Forthyr is often seen as a bit 'backward' or stuck in the past. In the present day (the Qamis campaign), Forthyr is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. His dynasty has been in power since the time of Shanlong I, about 200 years ago during the Qaringil era. 

The sect you are asking about is a prominent religious group that specifically calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors—the ancient draconic rulers from before the current mortal dynasties took over. While they are a religious organization, they receive 'tacit encouragement' from the Dragoncaller dynasty. Even though the Shanlong bloodline has thinned over the centuries and they aren't as physically draconic as they used to be, supporting a sect that glorifies dragons helps legitimize their own 'Dragoncaller' title and right to rule.

So the Shanlongs aren't actually dragons themselves? Just 'dragon-themed' sorcerer kings? That definitely sounds like something my noble would look down on, especially since the Keyberos dynasty in Edrad has a solid 400-year track record of ruling without needing to pretend they are dragons.

Exactly. In the Qaringil prequel era, the first Shanlong was half-dragonborn, but by the time of your character in the Qamis era, those bloodlines have run thin. They are essentially sorcerer-kings. 

Your character's family, the Keyberos, definitely prizes stability and elven heritage—remember that as an Edran noble, you're required to be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. The cultural gap between the cosmopolitan, half-elven nobility of Jia and the 'backward' dragon-focused traditionalism of Forthyr is quite wide. 

One small detail your character would know: the 'Q' in names like Qamis or Qis is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound. It comes from Old Draconic, which is still the language of scholarship and treaties. Even if you find the Forthrian sect's beliefs primitive, you'd likely still use that 'Kh' pronunciation in formal diplomatic circles.

Interesting. Does the sect have a specific name, or do they have a leader other than the Dragoncaller? Also, how do they feel about Ryraxos? Since he was an actual dragon who took over Edrad for a while, did they see him as their guy?

The notes don't actually give the sect a specific name yet—that is one of the 'blank spaces' the DM has left for players to help fill in. If you want to invent a name or a specific leader for your backstory, that's encouraged! 

As for Ryraxos, the notes don't explicitly state the sect's official stance on him. However, we do know that Forthyr mostly stayed out of the southwest's affairs during the thirty-year war until it became impossible to ignore. Ryraxos was a bronze dragon and an artillery commander who rose to power, but he wasn't one of the 'Ancient Dragon Emperors' the sect usually pines for. Plus, Ryraxos is currently exiled to Secundus (the second moon), so the sect might view him as a failed example or a pale imitation of the old legends. 

Do you want to brainstorm a name for the sect, or are you more interested in how the Church of Bahamut in Qis'mos views them?

Hey nanochat, missed the last D&D session. We are in the Qaringil prequel campaign, right? I need an executive summary of the guilds our party actually interacts with. Just give me the high-level functional groups and who is running the show so I can catch up with the group.

Correct, you are in the Qaringil era, roughly 200 years before the Ryraxian War. In the city-state of Qaringil, the power structure is built around seven Great Guilds, but as an adventuring party, your daily operations usually fall under the smaller guilds. 

Here is the breakdown:

1. Functional Guilds (Most relevant to you):
- Adventurer Guild: The primary hub for contracts and party registration.
- Mage Guild: Handles arcane regulation and support.
- Alchemist Guild: Focuses on potion-making and chemical research.

At level 5, your characters are likely full members of these organizations. 

2. The Great Guilds (The real power):
These seven control the Republic's economy: Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool. 

3. The Underground (Illegal):
There are three criminal organizations that style themselves as guilds: Thieves, Bandits, and Cutpurses. 

The current head of state is Exarch Giovanni Pesaro, though he is reportedly on death's doorstep after 19 years in office.
Thanks. My notes mention 'Five Families.' Which of those families control the Adventurer or Mage guilds? And also, can you remind me how to pronounce the city name? Is it a soft Q?

To your first point: none of the Five Families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli) directly lead the 'smaller' guilds like the Adventurer or Mage guilds. Their focus is on the Seven Great Guilds. 

For example:
- Peixota (Dolphin sigil) leads Shipping.
- Manos (Hand sigil) leads Judicial.
- Volmer (Crow sigil) leads Banking.
- Vitelli (Moth sigil) leads Silk.
- Tiddwar (a gnomish family, not one of the five) currently leads the Mercantile Guild after the Qanto family (Dove sigil) lost control of it.

As for the name: the Q is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound, derived from Old Draconic. So it is 'Kharingil,' not a soft Q or a 'Qu' sound. 

One final note for your recap: while the elites are commercial and own the guilds, the Gondolier and Wool guilds are notable because they have never been led by one of the Five Families.

Hi there. I am rolling up a character with a background in religious studies, specifically focused on the Church of Bahamut. I noticed the notes mention that everyone starts with a 'capital dialect' and one 'regional dialect.' How does that actually work for a PC in the current Qamis setting?

In the Qamis setting, languages like Edran, Artklan, or Qis are not standardized. The 'capital dialect' refers to the prestige version spoken by the ruling class and in the seat of government—for instance, the dialect of Jia for Edran or Ath'r for Artklan. As a PC, you are assumed to speak this high-society version plus one regional variant, which represents the common tongue of a specific province. For a religious scholar, this usually means you can communicate with both the high clergy and the common folk of your home region. Also, remember that 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh'—so Qis is pronounced 'Khis'.

That makes sense. If my character is from Qis'mos, I assume I'd speak the Qis capital dialect. Since the Church of Bahamut is so central there, does the language tie into the current schism? I saw there are two rival leaders right now.

It definitely does. Qis'mos is where the old Draconic institutions lasted the longest after the dragons left, and the Qis language is the closest living relative to Old Draconic (or Old Qis), which is the language of holy texts and treaties. The schism is physically split between the holy city of Qama and the city of Qis. Qe'lth IV, the drow high priest in Qama, holds the traditional seat and the physical relics, but he was appointed during the drow occupation. Meanwhile, Qachu II operates out of Qis without the relics. For a scholar, the 'prestige' dialect of Qis is likely your tool for navigating these theological arguments, as it bridges the gap between common worship and the Old Draconic scripts.

So if Qachu II is in Qis, does he claim his dialect is more 'legitimate' because it's closer to the Old Draconic of the scholarship? And would a drow priest like Qe'lth IV be speaking the same prestige Qis, or something else entirely?

The notes don't specify a 'drow language'—remember, there are no racial languages in Qamis. Qe'lth IV and the drow enclaves in Qama would likely speak the Qis prestige dialect to maintain authority over the local population and the Church hierarchy. However, since Qis is the heartland of the faith and the most linguistically conservative region, Qachu II would certainly emphasize his connection to the local institutions. The 'capital dialect' of Qis is the language of the academy and diplomacy. As a PC, your bonus language could even be Old Draconic itself if you want to be able to read the original un-translated treaties and liturgical scrolls that both sides are currently fighting over.

hey. I'm rolling up a character from Rathakos, and the DM keep mentioning some 'Red Mountain Republic' that supposedly existed before it. Honestly, it sounds like some made-up nationalist myth to make the region sound more important than it was. Was that a real place or just a name for some bandit camp in the mountains?

In the context of the Qamis campaign setting, the Red Mountain Republic was a very real, albeit brief, political entity. It isn't a myth, but it was born out of chaos. During the thirty-year Ryraxian War, the mountain provinces were under Edran occupation. When the Edrans finally withdrew, the local leadership organized the territory into the Red Mountain Republic. However, it was plagued by internal conflict. The Red Mountain Assembly eventually purged its pro-Edran members, which led to the Republic dissolving and forming the two separate nations you see today: Rathakos and Acrokos. So, while it only lasted a short time, it was the direct predecessor to your character's homeland.

Alright, so it was a real mess of a transition. I'll buy that. While looking at old trade records for my backstory—my guy's family were merchants—I keep seeing the name 'Manos' pop up. People talk about them like they were the kings of the mountain wool trade, but also like they were total thugs. Are they a Rathakos thing?

The Manos family is actually from the Qaringil prequel era, set about 200 years before the current events in Rathakos. They were one of the 'Five Families' of the island city-state Qaringil. Their sigil is a Hand, and they were incredibly powerful because they led the Judicial Guild. Your 'thug' suspicion is grounded in the lore—while they were the legal authority in Qaringil, they used that influence to muscle into other industries. Specifically, their entry into the wool trade was 'hotly contested.' At that time, the Wool and Gondolier guilds were the only major guilds not controlled by the big families, so when the Manos family tried to take over the wool scene, it was seen as a massive, aggressive power grab.

So they used the 'Judicial' guild as a shield to start a turf war over wool? That's pretty corrupt. If they're from 200 years ago in Qaringil, does that mean they don't exist in the Rathakos I'm playing in now?

The campaign notes for the present-day Qamis setting don't mention the Manos family currently operating in Rathakos or the surrounding nations. The political map has changed drastically since the Holy Qis (pronounced 'Kh-is') Empire fell and the Ryraxian War ended. While the Manos family were titans of industry and law in the island city of Qaringil 200 years ago, their influence doesn't appear in the modern records for Rathakos. That being said, the DM has left many 'blank spaces' on the map and in the histories; it’s possible the family declined or relocated after the dragons departed, but as of right now, they are strictly a part of the Qaringil prequel history.

Yo! Coming over from a long Waterdeep campaign. Looking at this Qaringil setting for a prequel game. Who is this Giovanni Pesaro guy? Is he like the Open Lord or something? Give me some local color for a potential guild member PC.

Welcome to Qaia. To start with a quick pronunciation note: the Q is pronounced like a Kh, so the city is Kharingil. Also, Giovanni Pesaro belongs to the Qaringil prequel era, which is set about 200 years before the main Qamis campaign timeline.

Giovanni Pesaro is the Exarch of Qaringil. While that might sound like the Open Lord of Waterdeep, Qaringil is described as an incomprehensible republic rather than a city ruled by masked lords. Pesaro has held the post for 19 years, but the word on the street is that he is on death's doorstep. He comes from a minor family, which is notable because the city is really dominated by the Five Families who control the Great Guilds. Despite his minor background, he is closely aligned with the Peixota (Shipping) and Manos (Judicial) families.
Nice. I like the 'dying leader' angle. If I'm playing a Silk Guild member, how do I fit in? Are the Vitelli family cool with him, or are they looking for a change of pace?

If you are in the Silk Guild, you are likely connected to the Vitelli family, whose sigil is a Moth. They lead that guild and are also involved in shipping and mercantile interests. 

The notes suggest the Exarch's primary allies are the Peixota and Manos families. This leaves the Vitelli, the Qanto (the Dove sigil), and the Volmer (the Crow sigil) as the other major players. The Qanto family specifically just lost leadership of the Mercantile Guild to a gnomish family (the Tiddwars) who are more aligned with the current Exarch, so there is definitely some political friction you could use for your backstory. The Vitelli family hasn't been explicitly labeled as hostile to Pesaro, but in a city of 100,000 people built on canals and trade, everyone is eyeing the Exarch's seat while he's failing.
What about the minor family he comes from? Does it have a name or a sigil I should know about? I want to see if my PC has any old beef with them.

The notes actually leave the name and sigil of Giovanni Pesaro's minor family as a blank space. The DM has not filled that in yet, which means it is a great opportunity for you to work with your DM to create that family name and their sigil as part of your backstory. 

We know the five major sigils are the Dolphin (Peixota), Hand (Manos), Dove (Qanto), Crow (Volmer), and Moth (Vitelli). If you want to lean into local color, remember that Qaringil is a city of exiles and adventurers originally built by people from Qismos, so your family history might involve that migration. You could even be a member of the Mage or Alchemist guilds, which are smaller but relevant to the city's elite.

Hey, I'm rolling up a character for the Qaringil game. I'm going for an Edran noble, half-elf, obviously aiming for that sweet spot of being between one-quarter and three-quarters elf to stay in the Keyberos inner circle. The notes mention we're starting at level 5. In a place like Qaringil, does that level make me a 'somebody'? Also, how do the Edran elites usually interact with the Five Families there—like the Peixota or the Volmer?

Starting at 5th level in the Qaringil campaign definitely makes you a 'somebody.' The setting notes explicitly state that at 5th level, you can 'easily be full guild members of anything relevant' to your class. In Qaringil, which is a republic ruled by its Great Guilds, that membership is your ticket to real influence. While your Edran noble title carries weight back in Jia, in Qaringil, power is held by commercial elites rather than traditional landowners. As for specific interactions between the Keyberos dynasty and families like the Peixota (Shipping) or Volmer (Banking), the notes don't actually list any specific treaties or marriage alliances. Edrad is the most dominant nation in this era, so there's certainly trade, but the exact social standing of an Edran noble among the Qaringil families is one of those 'blank spaces' you can help define.

That's helpful. If the notes are thin on the social links, I might invent a debt the Qanto family owes my father. Speaking of blank spaces, what's the deal with the Red Mountains in this era? I know Rathakos doesn't exist yet, but if my family wanted to secure some mineral rights before the 'Holy Qis Empire' gets its act together, is that possible? Or is it a total no-man's-land?

The Red Mountains are a great place to look for blank spaces. In the Qaringil era, they are claimed by the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from Ath'r), but the notes say the Empire does not 'effectively administer' them. Instead, they are occupied by 'various non-state peoples.' Since Rathakos and Acrokos 'fully do not exist' yet, the region is essentially a frontier. Proposing a family mining interest there would fit the lore perfectly—it's a dangerous, underexplored interior. Just remember that in this period, the Holy Qis Empire is the major power in central Qamis, even if their grip on the mountains is weak.

Got it. One last question: my character's family has a legend about a 'dragon-touched' relic from the old days. I saw Forthyr has a religious sect wanting the Dragon Emperors back. Is there any mention of Forthrian agents or that Shanlong guy having presence in Qaringil? I'm trying to see if there's a canon reason my noble would be investigating the local guilds for dragon lore.

The notes don't mention any official Forthrian presence in Qaringil, nor any specific agents of Dragoncaller Shanlong I (the current ruler in this era). Forthyr is described as 'somewhat backwards' and located in the far northeast. While they have a sect calling for the return of Dragon Emperors, there isn't a recorded 'canon' event where they've sent people to the island city-state. This is another area where the notes are silent—if you want your character to be chasing a specific relic through the Qaringil guilds, you'll be the one helping to fill in that part of the world.

Hey, got a minute for a setting question? I am drafting a character for a new campaign and I am looking at Forthyr for some flavor. I have done the whole Cormyr and Purple Dragon Knight thing in the Forgotten Realms, so I am trying to get the pulse of the local leadership. Who exactly is Dragoncaller Shanlong? Is there more than one?

It depends on which campaign era you are playing in. In the present-day Qamis setting (set after the Ryraxian War), the ruler of Forthyr is Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. If you are playing in the Qaringil prequel campaign, which takes place roughly 200 years earlier, the ruler is Dragoncaller Shanlong I. 

The Shanlongs are a dynasty of sorcerer-kings. Historically, the family was dragonborn, but over the generations the bloodline has thinned out. By the time of Shanlong VI in the present day, they are mostly human in appearance, though they still maintain their magical authority as sorcerer-kings.

That is an interesting shift from the usual 'dragon-blooded' trope where the blood stays pure forever. I am thinking of a character who might be a bit of a traditionalist or a dissident. The notes mention Forthyr is 'backward' and has a weird religious sect. What is the deal with them? Do they actually like the Shanlongs?

The religious sect you are referring to calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors—the ancient draconic rulers from before the current era. Interestingly, the Shanlong dynasty gives this sect tacit encouragement, or at least they do not suppress it. 

Forthyr as a whole is described as somewhat backwards compared to nations like Edrad or the Artklan League. The combination of a thinning dragonborn bloodline and a population that yearns for the return of literal dragons creates a specific kind of tension. If you were playing a dissident, you might be someone who finds the Dragoncallers' reliance on ancient dragon-cultism to be a hollow mask for their fading power. 

One small piece of local color for your backstory: while the Shanlongs rule Forthyr, the prestigious language for diplomacy and academia in the region is still Old Draconic (or Old Qis). Remember that in this setting, 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh', so names like Qama or Qis follow that rule, though Forthyr itself is a straightforward pronunciation.

That helps a lot. So if I am playing in the Qamis era, Shanlong VI is essentially presiding over a nation that is obsessed with a past they can no longer physically represent. Are there any actual dragons left in Forthyr for him to 'call'?

The notes do not mention any active dragons residing in Forthyr during the Qamis era. In fact, most of the dragon-related news in the present day centers on Ryraxos, the bronze dragon who was recently defeated and exiled to Secundus (the second moon). There are rumors that dragons might be found on the big, unexplored island to the south near Freeport, but as far as Forthyr is concerned, the 'Dragoncaller' title is more of a dynastic legacy than a literal job description in the current year. The DM has left many of the specific details of the Forthrian interior as blank spaces, so there is room for you to coordinate on exactly what kind of 'backwards' customs your character might be running away from.

Hey! Sorry I missed the session. I heard the party ended up in Jia or was talking to Edran nobles? I am still geeking out over the phonology here—is it true the Edran elite use that 'Kh' sound from the Old Draconic roots, or is that more of a Qis'mos thing?

No worries! The party has been navigating the politics of Jia, which is the coastal capital of Edrad. To your linguistic point: the 'Kh' sound (spelled with a Q) is from Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. While Edrad is the most culturally influential nation in Qamis, the 'Kh' sounds are most concentrated in place names like Qeth or in the heartland of Qis'mos. The Edran nobility themselves are half-elven—strictly between one-quarter and three-quarters elf—and they speak the prestige dialect of the Edran language. They have just been restored to power following the thirty-year Ryraxian War, which ended with the exile of the dragon Ryraxos.

That is a very specific range for elven blood! Does that bloodline requirement influence how they view the 'q without u' spelling in their treaties? I noticed some place names like Rathakos don't have it, but Qeth does.

You have a sharp eye for the orthography. Rathakos is a newer nation; in the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years ago, it did not even exist. It was formed recently after the Red Mountain Republic collapsed following the Ryraxian War. Qeth, however, is an older city within Edrad, and its name reflects that Old Draconic 'q without u' heritage. In the current Qamis era, the Keyberos dynasty is finding that while it is easy to sign an edict in scholarly Old Draconic to reverse thirty years of change, it is much harder to actually enforce those laws across the different regional dialects and territories like the Southern Reaches.
So if my character knows Old Draconic, I am basically a legal or academic expert? How does that land in Qis'mos compared to Edrad?

Exactly. Old Draconic is the language of scholarly publications and treaties. In Edrad, it marks you as a member of the diplomatic or academic elite. In Qis'mos, the connection is even deeper; the Qis language is the most similar to Old Draconic because their institutions are modified remnants of the old Draconic empire. However, Qis'mos is currently in a religious schism. The holy city of Qama (pronounced Kha-ma) is occupied by drow who appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, while the rival candidate Qachu II operates from the city of Qis. Knowing the language would definitely help you navigate those high-stakes political and religious arguments.

I have been looking at the NAVIGATION.md data. On Earth, we had the whole 'longitude problem' that took centuries to solve with chronometers. In settings like the Forgotten Realms, players usually just hand-wave it with magic or Selune's predictable path. But on Qaia, Primus is geostationary? That's a total game changer for a navigator.

It really is. Since Primus is magically anchored in an equatorial orbit that matches Qaia's rotation perfectly, it stays fixed over the prime meridian (0 degrees longitude, 0 degrees latitude). For anyone in the near-side hemisphere, it is just as reliable as the pole star, but it gives you a reference for East-West position instead of North-South. You don't need a clock at all—just a way to measure the angle of Primus above your horizon.

Right, the primsine calculation. If I'm using rho = 0.1511 and I'm at 30 degrees North latitude (phi), and I see Primus at an elevation (e) of 42.2 degrees... the math says primsine(42.2) is about 0.7504. Dividing that by cos(30) which is 0.8660 gives me 0.8665. The arccos of that is roughly 30 degrees. So I'm at 30 degrees East longitude if Primus is to the West. This is way more precise than dead reckoning. But what happens if I sail to the far side of the planet, like past the visibility limit of 81.3 degrees?

Once you cross that limit, you are back to the difficult methods used on Earth before the 18th century. Primus is permanently below the horizon for the far-side hemisphere. Navigators there have to rely on dead reckoning, stellar transits, or lunar-distance methods using the other moons like Secundus or Quartus. It creates a massive navigational asymmetry—the nations on the near-side have a huge advantage in maritime trade because their positioning is purely geometric and requires no chronometer.

Speaking of the other moons, the tides must be a nightmare compared to Earth. We have one moon creating a predictable cycle. Here, you have Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus. The notes say the total oscillating tidal force is 4.4 times what we have on Earth. Does Primus add to that chaos?

Actually, Primus does not contribute to the daily tidal cycle. Because it is geostationary and co-rotates with Qaia, it doesn't sweep across the sky. Instead, it raises a permanent 'static bulge' of about 2 cm in the ocean—a fixed geographic feature where the sea level is slightly higher along the Primus-antiprimus axis. The real chaos comes from the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus) and the sun. They create three incommensurate oscillating drivers (9.85h, 13.69h, and 12.43h), making the tidal patterns far more complex than the simple cycles on Earth or in other settings.

That's wild. It's like the planet has a 'preferred' side for sailing. Does this affect the political power of places like Qaringil or the Edran coast? They must have such an edge over anyone trying to navigate the far side.

Exactly. In the Qaringil prequel era, the city-state of Qaringil is a dominant thassolocracy because its merchant fleets can navigate the Central Sea with extreme precision. Its elites are commercial masters rather than landowners because they control these nautical routes. Even in the Qamis present, the restored Keyberos dynasty in Edrad and the Artklan League benefit from this navigational ease, while the far-side remains a much more dangerous prospect for exploration.

I'm looking over the campaign notes for Qamis. I'm a tech lead in my day job, so I love the technical detail here, but could you give me the executive summary on navigation? I keep seeing this 'primsine' function mentioned and I want to know why it's a big deal.

The executive summary is that Qamis (pronounced 'Khamis') has solved the longitude problem much earlier than Earth did, and they did it through geometry rather than clocks. On Earth, finding longitude at sea required an incredibly precise chronometer to compare local time with a reference meridian. In this setting, the moon Primus acts as a permanent, motionless GPS satellite. Because Primus is geostationary and magically anchored over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0,0), its position in the sky is fixed. If you can see it, you can find exactly where you are.
Right, a geostationary moon. That's a clever way to handle it. So why the 'primsine' function? If I see it at a certain angle, isn't that just a simple mapping to my distance from the prime meridian?

It is a mapping, but the math isn't perfectly linear because of the curvature of the planet and the moon's specific altitude. The primsine function—primsine(e)—is a custom trigonometric formula designed to convert the elevation 'e' (the angle of Primus above your horizon) into the geocentric angular distance from the sub-Primus point. The formula is: primsine(e) = rho * cos^2(e) + sin(e) * sqrt(1 - rho^2 * cos^2(e)). The 'rho' here is a constant (0.1511), which is the ratio of the planet's radius to the moon's orbital radius. Navigators don't do this math on deck; they use a precomputed lookup table in an almanac.
Okay, that makes sense. It's an abstraction layer for the sailors. How does that translate into a final longitude fix? You mentioned it's a pure geometry problem.

Once a navigator has the primsine value from their table and their latitude (which they get the old-fashioned way by measuring the pole star), they use the longitude formula: cos(longitude) = primsine(e) / cos(latitude). It's a simple division. If Primus is to the west of their meridian, they are east of the prime meridian, and vice versa. This effectively reduces a complex 3D spherical positioning problem into a few table lookups and a single division. The catch is that Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. On the 'far side' of Qaia, navigators are stuck with dead reckoning or much harder stellar methods, creating a significant naval advantage for the nations on the 'near side'.

hey there, I've played in this world before back in the Qaringil days and I am trying to wrap my head around Qis'mos in the current Qamis campaign. It was just a region back then, right? What does the political structure look like now that it is a nation?

You are correct. In the Qaringil prequel era set 200 years ago, Qis'mos was a geographic and cultural region rather than a unified nation. In the present-day Qamis era, it has unified and is governed by a Potentate. The Potentate is elected by a group consisting of the nobility, clergy, and guildmasters, and importantly, they can also be impeached by those same groups. The region still maintains many institutions from the old Draconic empire. Also, as a reminder for your table talk, Q is pronounced as 'Kh' in this setting.

That is a big change from the Holy Qis Empire days. Who is the current Potentate? Also, the notes say the land is rich and productive—who actually owns the farms? Are there specific merchant houses like the ones we had in Qaringil?

The setting notes actually do not name the current Potentate; that is one of the 'blank spaces' the DM has left open. Similarly, while the notes mention that the land is productive and that guildmasters and nobility have a say in the government, they don't specify individual merchant houses or the exact mechanics of land ownership. Unlike the Qaringil era notes, which detail the Five Families like the Peixota and Vitelli, the Qamis-era Qis'mos notes haven't filled in those specific entities yet.

Fair enough. What about the drow? It says they have a dozen scattered enclaves but only mentions Qama. Where are the others? And what are they doing in the holy city besides appointing Qe'lth IV? I'm curious if they've kept the temples running.

The specific locations of the other enclaves are currently unmapped 'blank spaces.' The notes only confirm that there are about a dozen scattered across the continent, with Qama being the most prominent because it is the holy city of Bahumat. Regarding their activity in Qama, the notes state they have not 'despoiled' the city and have appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV. This has caused a schism with the alternate candidate Qachu II, who is in the city of Qis. Beyond that schism and the fact that they haven't destroyed the holy site, the notes are silent on their daily administration or whether they are actively maintaining the traditional Bahumat rituals.

I am looking at running a one-shot set during the Qaringil prequel era. The notes mention Qis'mos has some of the richest and most productive land in Qamis. Could you tell me why that is, and if that wealth is reflected differently in the Qaringil era compared to the present day?

In both the present day and the prequel era, Qis'mos is considered the heartland of the continent because its soil is the most productive. This agricultural wealth is tied to its history as the place where the institutions of the old Draconic empire persisted the longest after the dragons departed. In the Qaringil era (roughly 200 years before the present), Qis'mos is not yet a unified nation like the one ruled by a Potentate in the present day. Instead, it is a geographic and cultural region. The Holy Qis Empire, ruling from the city of Ath'r, controls the part of Qis'mos north of the river. Because the land is so valuable, it is frequently the site of conflict between continental powers.

That makes sense. You mentioned the Draconic empire's institutions—did they leave behind specific infrastructure like magic irrigation, or just a better legal system? Also, if Qis'mos is the heartland, why is the campaign centered on the island city of Qaringil instead of there?

The notes specifically mention that the 'institutions'—clerical, noble, and guild-based structures—are what survived, rather than describing specific physical infrastructure like irrigation. This institutional stability allowed Qis'mos to remain a productive power base even after the dragons left. As for the setting choice, the prequel focuses on the city-state of Qaringil because it represents a different kind of power. While Qis'mos is the agrarian heart of the continent, Qaringil is a mercantile thassolocracy of 100,000 people built on a wizard-made isthmus. Its wealth comes from trade, banking, and controlling the Great Western Bridge. Interestingly, the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, which is why the local Islas language there has a significant Qis vocabulary. For a one-shot, Qaringil offers a dense environment of competing guilds and the 'Five Families' that you won't find in the more feudal struggles of the Qis'mos mainland.

Hey, I missed the last session! I know we're in Rathakos right now, but I'm a bit fuzzy on why we're here. What exactly is the Grand Council deliberating about? Also, on a side note, I've been looking at the maps and noticed 'Rathakos' and 'Acrokos' both have that 'Q' in them. Is that the same 'Kh' sound from Old Draconic I keep hearing about?

Welcome back. To catch you up: you are in Rathakos, a mountainous state in the Red Mountains that formed after the Red Mountain Republic dissolved. Right now, the country is in a bit of a transition period. It was officially made a Princedom by the Settlements of Ath'r, but it doesn't actually have a Prince yet. The Grand Council is currently deliberating to find a suitable monarch to take the throne. In the meantime, the nation is being run by Regent John Dunne.

Regarding your linguistic observation: yes, you're spot on. The 'Q' in Rathakos and Acrokos comes from Old Draconic (also called Old Qis), which is the language of scholarly treaties and diplomacy. In this setting, 'Q' is always pronounced like 'Kh'. That 'q without a u' spelling is a hallmark of Old Draconic roots, and it shows up in many of the newer national names that emerged after the Ryraxian War.
That makes sense. So the Grand Council is basically job-hunting for a king. Is there any drama involving the neighboring countries or the people living there while they decide?

There is plenty of drama. While Regent Dunne and the Council deliberate, Rathakos is dealing with significant internal unrest. There are two main flashpoints: first, the Southern Reaches (which were annexed from Edrad) are full of people who aren't exactly thrilled with the new government. Second, many of the isolated mountain communities still follow the old dragon cults, and they don't necessarily respect the authority of a central council in the capital of Rath.

Because Rathakos borders so many nations—Edrad, Qis'mos, Artkos, and even the industrial 'free cities' of Lock—the political situation is very tenuous. Most of these nations are watching the Council's choice very closely, especially since Rathakos was part of the pro-Edran Red Mountain Republic before the local Assembly purged the pro-Edran factions to create the current state.

Wait, I just noticed the note about the letter Q being pronounced like 'Kh'! That is such a fascinating phonological choice. Is that a remnant of Old Draconic? I'm a huge linguistics nerd and I'm dying to know if 'Qamis' is actually 'Khamis.' Also, speaking of the Draconic influence, where did all the dragons actually go? I keep hearing different rumors!

You hit the nail on the head! That 'Q' without a 'u' is a signature of Old Draconic (which scholars also call Old Qis). So yes, Qamis is pronounced 'Khamis,' and the holy city of Qama is 'Khama.' It's the language of high diplomacy and ancient treaties, and it's left its mark all over the map. As for the dragons, that's one of the great mysteries of the setting! While they ruled the continent in the ancient past, most have vanished. The most persistent rumor is that they retreated to the 'big island' far to the south, beyond the pirate city of Freeport. The jungle there is so dense and terrifying that most people are too scared to go looking for them!

That is incredible! So the 'Kh' sound is basically the phonetic fingerprint of the old empire. I love that! And the southern island sounds like a total death trap. But wait, what about Ryraxos? If the 'dragon overlord' just lost the war in the Qamis era, did he flee to the southern jungle with the others, or is he hiding somewhere else?

Ryraxos is actually a very unique case! Unlike the ancient dragons of legend who supposedly fled to the southern island, Ryraxos was a major player in the recent thirty-year war. After the Keyberos dynasty was restored in Edrad, Ryraxos wasn't killed—he accepted exile. He isn't in the jungle; he is literally on the second moon, Secundus! It's a dark, iron-rich moon that orbits in the opposite direction (retrograde) compared to the others. So if you're looking for the 'overlord,' you just have to look up at the night sky. The notes say he won't be playing a role in the current campaign, but it's a wild thought that he's just up there on a chunk of iron watching the continent he almost conquered.
Exiled to the moon?! That is metal. Literally, since you said Secundus is iron-rich. Does the 'Kh' sound apply to his name too? Like 'Ryrakh-sos'?

Actually, since his name is spelled with an 'x' and not that specific Draconic 'Q,' the notes don't specify a 'Kh' shift for Ryraxos. Usually, it's those 'Q-without-a-U' names like Qaringil, Qis, or Qirkos where you really hear that Old Draconic 'Kh' kick in. It's a great way to tell which cities or regions have the deepest ties to that ancient draconic history!

Hey there. I am building a drow character for a Qamis game. My idea is that they were part of the drow units Artkos brought up from the Underdark during the war, but they decided to stay on the surface after everything ended. I keep hearing about something called the Red Mountain Republic in the history of the region where we are starting, Rathakos. What exactly was that, and why did it disappear?

That is a great hook for a character. As a drow who fought for Artkos, you would have seen the messy transition of the central continent firsthand. The Red Mountain Republic was a short-lived political entity that existed during the Ryraxian War. When the dragon Ryraxos dissolved the old Artklan Empire, the Edran military moved in to occupy the mountain provinces. They organized those territories into the Red Mountain Republic as a sort of pro-Edran buffer state. It did not last because of intense guerrilla resistance from the locals and military setbacks for Edrad elsewhere. When Edrad finally withdrew, a faction called the Red Mountain Assembly took over, purged the pro-Edran leaders, and split the territory into two new nations: Rathakos, where the campaign begins, and Acrokos.

That makes sense. Since I am playing a drow who stayed, I am trying to figure out where I would have been during that 'purge.' If I was technically an ally of Artkos, would I have been helping the Red Mountain Assembly kick the Edrans out? And give me some local color—what does the area look like now that the Republic is gone?

It is very likely you were involved in that resistance. Artkos recruited drow specifically as a desperation move to turn the tide against Edrad, so you probably fought alongside the mountain rebels. Today, Rathakos is a rugged, mountainous frontier. The capital, Rath, is tucked into the northern foothills of the Red Mountains. Local color is defined by a few things: first, the 'Southern Reaches' were recently annexed from Edrad and are full of Edran-speaking people who aren't happy about the new government. Second, there is a lot of internal unrest because Rathakos still hasn't found a Prince to rule it; it is currently run by a Regent named John Dunne. Also, keep in mind that many mountain communities still follow 'old dragon cults' from the imperial days. For a drow, the most prominent place for your kin is the holy city of Qama in Qis'mos, where a drow High Priest named Qe'lth IV (pronounced Khe'lth) was appointed during the war. Even though the war is over, many drow enclaves like the one in Qama remain on the surface.

The dragon cults and the 'Southern Reaches' sound like exactly the kind of trouble my character would get hired to deal with. One more thing—you mentioned the name Qama is pronounced with a 'Kh' sound? Is that a general rule for this setting?

Exactly. In Qamis, the letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh,' derived from Old Draconic (or Old Qis). So Qama is 'Khama,' Qis is 'Khis,' and the dragon Ryraxos's name follows the same phonetic logic. This 'q without u' spelling is a hallmark of the ancient draconic influence on the map. As a drow who lived through the war, you'd be well-acquainted with this, especially if you spent time near the Artklan heartlands or the scholarly circles where Old Draconic is still the language of treaties and diplomacy.

Hey nanochat. I've been looking over these campaign notes and noticed a lot of names like Qis, Qaringil, and Qama. As someone who loves linguistics, I have to ask: is that a 'q' like in 'quiet' or something else? And how does it relate to Old Draconic?

In the world of Qaia, the 'Q' is actually pronounced as a 'Kh' sound. This phoneme is a hallmark of Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. Most place names you see that use a 'q' without a 'u'—like Qis or Qeth—are derived from this language. It serves as the primary tongue for scholarly publications, ancient treaties, and diplomacy. It gives the setting a very distinct phonetic profile compared to standard fantasy naming conventions.

That's cool. It makes names like 'Qaringil' sound much more gutteral. I was also curious about navigation. The notes mention finding longitude by a moon called Primus. If I'm a navigator, how does that actually work? And what happens when I'm in one of those 'blank spaces' like Westron?

Finding longitude on Qaia is much easier than on Earth because of Primus. Since Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit directly over the equator at the prime meridian, it never moves in the sky. To find your longitude, you simply measure the elevation of Primus above the horizon. You then use a 'primsine' table—a precomputed mathematical reference—to calculate your distance from the prime meridian. No clock or chronometer is needed. However, you've identified a significant limitation: Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you travel more than roughly 81 degrees away from the prime meridian, Primus sinks below the horizon and stays there. In 'blank spaces' like the continent of Westron, which lies in the far-side hemisphere, navigators are forced to use much more difficult methods like lunar-distance calculations with the other moons (Secundus or Quartus) or simple dead reckoning.

So if I'm sailing to Westron, I'm essentially flying blind once Primus disappears? That's a huge shift in difficulty. Do we know if the people in Westron also use the 'Kh' sound for their names, or does the linguistic map change as much as the navigation?

That is exactly where we hit a 'blank space' in the records. The notes state that Westron is a very large continent to the west and is considered 'exotic,' with major religious differences (most are not Bahamut worshippers), but the linguistic details are not yet defined. Even the name 'Westron' is a placeholder—the DM mentions it might need a better name later. Similarly, while we know a city called Freeport exists on a big southern island, the culture and language of the jungle beyond it are also a blank space. If you were to create a character from these regions, you might actually have the opportunity to help fill in those linguistic and cultural gaps with the DM.

Yo. Just joined a game set in this world and trying to get my bearings. The DM mentioned Qamis and Qaringil—is that the same place? Also, I keep hearing about the 'stationary moon' and 'Kh' sounds. Give me the executive summary of the setting and the physics of the sky.

The setting is the continent of Qamis on the planet Qaia. Qaringil refers to a prequel era set 200 years before the 'present day' campaign. In the present Qamis era, a thirty-year war involving the dragon Ryraxos has just ended. Ryraxos was defeated and exiled to the second moon, and the Keyberos dynasty was restored to the throne in the nation of Edrad. The campaign typically starts in Rathakos, a mountainous state trying to maintain independence after the war. Regarding linguistics, the letter Q is always pronounced 'Kh' (like 'Khamis'), a remnant of Old Draconic. The 'stationary moon' is Primus, a magically anchored satellite that is geostationary. It stays fixed over a single point on the equator, never rising or setting, which allows navigators to determine longitude without a chronometer by measuring its elevation. There are five other moons: Secundus (retrograde), Tertius (the basis for the 8-day week), Quartus (lunar clone), and the smaller Sextus and Septimus. Combined, they create a complex tidal force about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's, making the oceans very active. Basically, the world is in a fragile peace after a dragon-led world war, and the sky is a precise clock for navigation.

Look, I've seen a dozen homebrew worlds. Most of them are just Greyhawk with different names. What exactly is this Qis'mos place? Is it just another generic high-fantasy kingdom where everyone is happy until the dark lord shows up?

Qis'mos is definitely not a generic happy kingdom. First, the name is pronounced 'Kh-is-mos'—the Q is a 'Kh' sound from Old Draconic. Geographically, it's the heartland of the continent, full of productive land that has made it the most fought-over region in the recent thirty-year Ryraxian War. Historically, it isn't where the dragons lived, but it is where their administrative institutions lasted longest. Today, it's a place of deep religious schism and political tension rather than a unified utopia.
You mentioned institutions. Is this just another feudal monarchy with a different coat of paint? Tell me how they actually run the place.

It’s actually a quasi-republican system left over from the old Draconic era. Qis'mos is governed by a Potentate. This leader isn't hereditary; they are elected by a council of nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. More importantly, the Potentate is impeachable by those same groups. In the present day of the Qamis campaign, the country is reeling because it was a primary battlefield of the war, and its political structure is under immense pressure from the neighboring Artklan League and the occupation of their holy sites.
If it's the 'heart of the old Draconic empire,' why aren't there dragons everywhere? And what's this about a schism?

The dragons departed long ago, leaving only their bureaucracy and language behind. As for the schism, it centers on the Church of Bahamut and the city of Qama. During the war, the drow—who were actually recruited as allies by Artkos—occupied Qama. They didn't destroy it, but they appointed their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. Now, there is a rival claimant named Qachu II operating out of the city of Qis. Qachu lacks the holy relics of the office, which are still in the drow-controlled city, creating a massive religious divide.
Drow occupying a holy city of Bahamut? That's a mess. You mentioned this was a region before it was a nation. When did that change happen? I saw something about a prequel era.

In the Qaringil prequel, set about 200 years before the current Qamis era, Qis'mos doesn't exist as a single nation. It was a cultural and geographic region. At that time, the northern half (north of the river) was ruled by the Holy Qis Empire based in Ath'r. That empire was famously described as 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' The unification into the modern Potentate-led state happened somewhere in the two centuries between the prequel and the present day.
Wait, so the 'Holy Qis Empire' wasn't even in Qis'mos? That's convoluted. Where does the 'Old Qis' language fit into this then?

The Holy Qis Empire claimed the name for legitimacy, but it was centered in Ath'r in the central continent. The language, Old Qis (or Old Draconic), is the prestige tongue used for treaties, scholarly works, and diplomacy. The modern national language of Qis'mos, called Qis, is the one most closely related to that ancient root. If you're a scholar or a diplomat in Qamis, you have to know 'the q without u' spellings and pronunciations to be taken seriously.

Fine, it's got some history. But what about the 'blank spaces' on the map? If I'm playing a character from the Southern Reaches or the borderlands of Qis'mos, is there actually any detail there or is it all just 'here be dragons'?

The DM has explicitly left 'blank spaces' in the notes for exactly that reason—to be filled by player backstories. For example, while we know Qis'mos is rich and productive, and we know about the situation in Qama and Qis, the specifics of the rural guild-holdings or the minor noble estates are open.
