If you wanted to play a character involved in the guildmaster council that elects the Potentate, you could help define how those local power structures survived the thirty years of Ryraxian warfare.

hey! I'm thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil era for my group. I was reading the Qamis notes and saw there are drow living on the surface now—did they come up 200 years ago, or is that a newer thing?

Hey there! That is a great detail to clarify before you start. The drow being on the surface is actually a very recent development in the timeline. In the Qamis 'present day' setting, they arrived during the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended. Artkos recruited them as allies in a move of desperation to hold back the dragon Ryraxos.
Oh, okay! So if I'm doing a Qaringil prequel game, the drow are still effectively an underground mystery? I saw they have a holy city called Qama in the main notes.

Exactly. In the Qaringil era (200 years earlier), the drow are still in the Underdark. Qama—which is pronounced 'Khama' because of that Old Draconic Q—is still the traditional, undisputed seat of the Church of Bahamut. The drow occupation of Qama and the appointment of their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, doesn't happen until much later during the Ryraxian War. For a Qaringil one-shot, the surface world is much more focused on things like the Holy Qis Empire or the merchant families in the city of Qaringil itself.

That makes sense. It sounds like the prequel era is less about dragon-war fallout and more about the 'republic of merchant lords' vibe in the islands. If I wanted some intrigue for my players, should I look at those Five Families?

Definitely. Since Qaringil is an 'incomprehensible republic' of around 100,000 people, the Five Families are your best bet for intrigue. You have the Peixota (Shipping), Manos (Judicial), Qanto (who just lost the Mercantile lead), Volmer (Banking), and Vitelli (Silk). They all have some elven ancestry but are mostly human. Since your players would be starting at 5th level, they could even be full members of the Adventurer, Alchemist, or Mage guilds, which puts them right in the middle of that commercial elite.

Hey! I was reading through the moon documents for the new Qamis campaign and I'm a bit confused about Primus. It says it's geostationary, which is wild compared to something like the Forgotten Realms where Selune and the Tears of Selune just sweep across the sky like normal. Why is it that only half the people on Qaia can ever see it? Is it just stuck in one spot?

That is exactly right. Unlike the moons in Toril or even our own Moon on Earth, Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. Its orbital period is exactly one Qaian sidereal day (23 hours and 56 minutes), and its orbital inclination of 23.5 degrees perfectly cancels out Qaia's axial tilt. This means it stays fixed over a single point on the equator—the Prime Meridian at 0 degrees longitude.

Because it never moves relative to the surface, it never 'rises' or 'sets.' If you live in the hemisphere centered on that point, Primus is a permanent, motionless fixture in your sky. If you live on the 'far-side' hemisphere, the bulk of the planet Qaia is always between you and the moon, so you would never even know it exists without traveling or reading reports from the other side. It is a massive geographical divide in how people experience the night sky.

That is such a cool mechanical tweak. So if I'm playing a character from Rathakos or maybe the old Holy Qis Empire region, am I on the 'lucky' side? In Greyhawk, everyone can see the moons eventually, but here it feels like a huge navigational advantage for whoever lives under it. How does a navigator in Qaringil use it compared to someone in the far east?

You hit on a major point of the setting. The 'near-side' hemisphere has a massive technological advantage in navigation. In most D&D settings, finding your longitude at sea is incredibly difficult because you need a precise clock. On Qaia's near-side, you just need to measure the elevation of Primus. Since it is fixed at (0,0), its height in the sky tells you exactly how far you are from the Prime Meridian. Navigators use a precomputed table called a 'primsine' to turn that angle into a longitude fix instantly.

As for the locations, Rathakos and the region of the former Holy Qis Empire (pronounced 'Kh-is') are both on the near-side, so they have access to this. Qaringil—the city from the prequel era—is also in a prime position to use it. If you were a sailor from Westron or the far-side of the continent, you would be stuck with 'dead reckoning' or much harder lunar-distance methods using moons like Secundus or Quartus. It creates a real asymmetry in who can safely cross the oceans. Also, just a quick note for your character: the 'Q' in Qis and Qaringil is always a 'Kh' sound, reflecting the Old Draconic roots of those names.

Got it, 'Kh-is.' That makes sense. It's funny because in most campaigns the moon is just for flavor or lycanthropy, but here it's like a GPS satellite. What happens during an eclipse? Do the other moons like Tertius ever hide it? I saw something about a triple-moon alignment in the 2260s.

Since Primus is so close (only about 0.11 lunar distances away), it is definitely part of the celestial dance. The other moons—Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus—do move across the sky, so they can certainly pass in front of or behind the stationary Primus from certain perspectives. 

Regarding that alignment, the 'Triple-moon alignment' usually refers to the three inner freely-orbiting moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) reaching full or new phases simultaneously. For example, on May 24, 2262, there is a predicted 'triple-new' where all three are within 0.1% of syzygy. Because Primus is stationary, it doesn't 'align' in the same way, but it acts as a constant witness to those cycles. It is also quite faint compared to the others—about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's moon—so during a bright full moon era like the one in 2253, the 'Big Three' would completely wash out the tiny, iron-dark surface of Primus.

hello, trying to wrap my head around this world. I'm thinking of running a Qaringil one-shot focused on a merchant vessel from the Shipping Guild. How does the navigation actually work? I see notes about a geostationary moon.

In the Qaringil era, navigation is a massive advantage for the island city-state's merchant fleets. Because Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit directly over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0, 0), it never rises or sets for anyone in the near-side hemisphere. It is a motionless, fixed point in the sky. To find your position, you simply need two angle measurements: the elevation of the Pole Star gives you your latitude, and the elevation of Primus gives you your longitude. Unlike Earth's history, Qais (pronounced Khais) navigators solved the longitude problem without needing precision clocks or 'lunar distance' math using moving moons.

That's a wild advantage for the Peixota or Vitelli families. But how precise is it for the tech level in Qaringil? If a ship is crossing the Central Sea, how close can they actually get to their destination just by looking at the moon?

The precision depends entirely on the instrument. In the Qaringil period, a standard merchant vessel using a simple astrolabe might have an error of about 0.5 degrees, which puts them within 55 to 70 kilometers of their target. However, the Great Guilds—especially the Shipping and Gondolier guilds—likely employ specialists with high-quality cross-staffs. A skilled navigator using a cross-staff can measure angles to within 0.1 degrees, narrowing the error to about 11 to 14 kilometers. This makes the city of Qaringil's control of the Great Western Bridge and the surrounding trade routes incredibly efficient compared to navigators on the far side of the planet, who have to rely on dead reckoning.
So if I'm running this at the table, how does a player actually 'do' the math? Is there a specific formula they'd use in-character, or is it all just DM fiat?

It's a mechanical process involving the 'primsine' function. In-character, a navigator uses a precomputed table in their nautical almanac. The formula they are solving is: cos(longitude) = primsine(elevation) / cos(latitude). The 'primsine' is a unique geometric constant for Qaia because the ratio of the planet's radius to Primus's orbital radius (0.1511) never changes. A player would first roll to find their latitude from the Pole Star, then measure the elevation (e) of Primus. They look up the primsine(e) value in their table, divide it by the cosine of their latitude, and the result is the cosine of their longitude. If Primus is to the west of their local meridian, they are east of the prime meridian; if it's east, they are west.
What about the visibility limits? Does the Shipping Guild have trouble once they get too far north or south?

The geometric limit for seeing Primus is about 81.3 degrees away from the sub-Primus point. At the equator, you can see it up to roughly 81 degrees east or west of the prime meridian. As you move toward the poles, that window narrows—at 60 degrees latitude, you only have about a 72-degree longitude range. In the Qaringil era, this isn't much of an issue for trade within the Central Sea or reaching Edrad, but it does mean that if a fleet tried to explore the far-side hemisphere or the deep regions of the big southern island near Freeport, they would lose their 'fixed' reference and be forced to use much more difficult methods, like tracking the retrograde movements of Secundus or the monthly cycle of Quartus.
Let's do a worked example. If my players are at 30 degrees North latitude and they measure Primus at an elevation of 42.2 degrees, what's their longitude?

First, the navigator looks up the value for primsine(42.2), which is 0.7504. Then they take the cosine of their latitude (30 degrees), which is 0.8660. Dividing 0.7504 by 0.8660 gives approximately 0.8666. Looking at their cosine table in reverse, an entry of 0.8666 corresponds to 30 degrees. So, their longitude is 30 degrees. If they see Primus hanging in the western sky, they know they are at 30 degrees East. It's a three-step lookup process that makes Qaringil's 'incomprehensible republic' the undisputed master of the sea 200 years before the Ryraxian War even begins.
Wait, I just realized—if Primus is geostationary, does it have phases? Or is it always the same brightness since it's 'fixed'?

It definitely has phases! Even though its position in the sky is fixed relative to the ground, the Sun still moves around Qaia. Primus goes through a full cycle of phases every solar day. Because it's an iron-rich moon with a very dark albedo (0.06), it's not actually that bright—at 'full moon' it is magnitude -9.62, which is about 3.1 magnitudes dimmer than Earth's moon. For navigators, the phase doesn't change the elevation measurement, but it does mean that during the 'New Primus' phase (when it's between Qaia and the Sun), the moon is effectively invisible for a short period, requiring them to wait for it to catch some sunlight or rely on the stars until it reappears.

Sorry I missed the last session! I am playing a member of the Gondolier guild in our Qaringil game. Can you remind me what level we are and what that actually means for my standing in the city? I want to make sure I am roleplaying my status correctly.

In the Qaringil campaign, the party is 5th level. This is a significant milestone because, according to the campaign notes, being 5th level qualifies you as a full guild member in any guild relevant to your character. For a Gondolier, this means you are not just a trainee or a simple laborer; you are a recognized professional within one of the seven Great Guilds of the city. Specifically, your guild is responsible for the maintenance and control of the enormous bridges and the canal system that defines Qaringil. Notably, the Gondolier guild is one of the few major guilds that has never been led by one of the 'Five Families' (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, or Vitelli), giving your character a sense of independence from the highest-level aristocratic infighting.
That is great. Since I have full membership, how much should I care about the political stuff? I heard the Exarch is dying.

You should definitely care, especially as a full member of a guild that controls the city's vital infrastructure. The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, has been in power for 19 years and is considered to be on death's doorstep. Because Qaringil is governed as an 'incomprehensible republic,' his passing will likely trigger a massive power struggle. The Five Families are already maneuvering: the Peixota family (Shipping) and Manos family (Judicial) are currently aligned with the dying Exarch, while the Qanto family is trying to regain the influence they lost when a gnomish family, the Tiddwars, took over the Mercantile Guild. As a Gondolier, your guild's control over the Great Western Bridge and the city's internal waterways makes you essential to anyone who wants to keep the city running during a succession crisis. You might not have the landholdings of the nobility, but your commercial and functional importance is quite high.

Hey, I am joining the Qaringil prequel game and reading through these moon notes. Is this for real? Seven moons? It feels like a lot of overhead for a fantasy game. How many are actually up there and do I really need to track all of them for my character?

It definitely looks like a lot at first! Qaia has six main moons, plus a trace particle called Quintus. In the Qaringil era—which is about 200 years before the 'present day' of the setting—these moons are essential to how people tell time and navigate. You have Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. Also, just a quick setting tip: the 'Q' in Qaia and Qaringil is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound. You don't necessarily need to track the physics of all seven, but they do provide the structure for the world your character lives in.

Seven objects in the sky sounds like a headache for a backstory. I am building a character from the city of Qaringil—the one with the canals and the bridges. Do these moons actually impact anything in the city, or is it just sky flavor for the DM?

They actually have a massive impact on Qaringil. For starters, Tertius is the basis for the 8-day week, and Quartus handles the monthly cycle. But for a city of canals and merchant fleets, the tides and navigation are the big ones. Because you have three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus), the oscillating tidal force is about 4.4 times stronger than Earth's. Managing those water levels in the canals would be a daily concern for the Gondolier guild. Most importantly, Primus is geostationary—it stays fixed in one spot in the sky. For a maritime power like Qaringil, that's how their sailors find their longitude without needing a complex clock. It's a huge reason why the city is such a dominant trade hub.
A moon that just hangs there and doesn't move? That sounds like a DM magic hand-wave. Is it actually useful for an adventurer, or is it just for high-seas captains?

The notes specify that Primus is 'magically anchored,' but it is incredibly practical for anyone in Qaringil. If you are part of the Shipping or Shipping-adjacent guilds, like the Peixota or Vitelli families, you'd know that Primus serves as a fixed reference point. By measuring its elevation above the horizon along with the pole star, you can get a nearly exact location fix anywhere on the 'near' hemisphere. It makes getting lost at sea much harder. Even within the city, having a 'fixed' moon that never rises or sets makes for a very unique environment—it’s a permanent fixture of the Qaringil skyline that never drifts.
If the tides are four times stronger than Earth's, wouldn't a canal city like Qaringil just flood every day? How do they handle that with the bridges and the five families and all that?

That is an excellent question, though the current campaign notes don't explicitly explain the engineering or the 'blank spaces' regarding the city's flood defenses yet. We know the Gondolier guild handles canal maintenance and bridge control, so they likely have a system for it. Interestingly, Primus itself creates a permanent 2cm bulge in the ocean, but because it co-rotates with the planet, it doesn't contribute to the daily shifting tides—the massive daily tide is driven by the other moons sweeping across the sky. If you're interested in that for your backstory, you might be able to suggest how your family or guild helps manage those high-water shifts!
I'm looking for some high-level antagonist lore to adapt for my own game. Can you tell me about this dragon Ryraxos? I heard he's on a moon now?

Ryraxos is a central figure in the recent history of the Qamis campaign setting. He's a bronze dragon who rose to power not as an ancient god-king, but as a competent artillery commander during the thirty-year Ryraxian War. He delivered the nation of Edrad from certain defeat but then spent decades waging aggressive war across the continent. Currently, he is in exile on Secundus, the second moon of the planet Qaia. It is worth noting for your table that the letter Q is always pronounced like a 'Kh' in this setting, so Qamis sounds like 'Khamis.'
Artillery commander for a dragon? That's unique. How did the war end, and how did he end up on the moon exactly?

The war ended when a pact between the nations of Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire restored the Keyberos dynasty to the throne in Edrad. Ryraxos was eventually defeated, though the notes clarify he was not an invincible ancient wyrm—his power was comparable to a well-commanded artillery battery or a competent adventuring party. Upon his defeat, he accepted exile to the second moon. This exile was part of the political restructuring that returned the continent's borders to their historical lines.
Is there anything actually keeping him there? A moon seems like a big place to just stay put if you're a shape-shifting dragon.

The setting documents describe him as 'safely exiled' and explicitly state he will play no role in the current Qamis campaign. While the exact mechanical 'leash' isn't detailed, the world of Qaia does feature significant dragon magic; for example, the first moon, Primus, is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit. Whether Ryraxos is held by a similar magical binding or simply the physical reality of the lunar environment is one of the 'blank spaces' the DM has left open for potential development.
Tell me about Secundus. If I'm going to borrow this 'dragon on the moon' idea, I need to know what his prison looks like.

Secundus is a dark, iron-rich moon with a density of 6,689 kg/m3, which is about twice that of Earth's Moon. Its surface is nearly black iron, with an albedo of only 0.06. It is a retrograde moon, meaning it rises in the west rather than the east. It orbits quite close to Qaia at 0.30 Lunar Distances, appearing nearly as large in the sky as our Moon does from Earth, though it is about one magnitude dimmer. It has a surface gravity of 0.882 m/s2 and an escape velocity of 912 meters per second.
Could his followers bring him back? You mentioned dragon cults in the notes for Rathakos.

That is a significant lore hook. In the present-day Qamis era, the mountain border state of Rathakos suffers from internal unrest, specifically from communities that still follow the 'old dragon cults.' Additionally, in the northeast nation of Forthyr, a prominent religious sect calls for the return of the 'Dragon Emperors' and receives tacit encouragement from the ruling Dragoncaller dynasty. While the DM notes Ryraxos is out of the picture for the current campaign, these factions are perfectly positioned to attempt a restoration.
Last thing—if he wanted to just fly back, could he? Or is the distance between the planet and Secundus too much for a dragon to cross?

Physically, Secundus is roughly 115,000 kilometers away from the planet. While Ryraxos is a powerful shape-shifter and flyer, the notes do not suggest dragons can survive the vacuum or the intense cold of the void between worlds. Even if he could survive the transit, he would first need to overcome an escape velocity of 912 m/s to leave the moon's gravity well. Without significant magical assistance or a specialized vessel, he is effectively stranded on that dark iron sphere.

Heyyoo! I am finally diving into the Qaringil prequel and I am stoked. I am playing a member of the Gondolier's Guild so I am basically a king of the canals and the Great Western Bridge. I keep hearing that Edrad is the big superpower in the present-day Qamis setting. Is that the case for us 200 years ago too, or are they still building up?

That is a fantastic character choice! Being in the Gondolier's Guild puts you right at the heart of Qaringil's infrastructure and trade, especially since your guild controls the bridges. To answer your question: yes, Edrad is still the heavyweight! In the Qaringil era, they are already the most politically and economically dominant nation on the continent. The Keyberos dynasty has been on the throne for about two hundred years at this point, and they are steadily expanding royal control. It is a very different vibe from the Qamis present day where they are recovering from the thirty-year Ryraxian War.

Whoa, only two hundred years? I guess that is young for a dynasty of half-elves! My guild buddies mentioned a place called Qeth—do they own that in our time? I want to know if my merchant friends are paying Edran taxes when they sail northeast!

That is a sharp question for a guild member! Actually, in the Qaringil era, Edrad does not control Qeth yet. Conquering Qeth and the lands to its northeast remains a prime goal of Edran foreign policy during your timeframe. In the Qamis present, Qeth is a known Edran city, but for your character in the prequel, it is still outside their borders. Your merchant friends in the Shipping or Mercantile guilds might be dealing with the Holy Qis Empire instead, which rules central Qamis from Ath'r in this era. Also, keep in mind that Edran nobility is strictly half-elven—specifically between one-quarter and three-quarters elf. That prestige culture from their capital, Jia, is already the most influential in the world, even if their maps are not quite as big as they will be in 200 years!

Hey! I am rolling up a cleric for the Qaringil campaign. I'm really interested in the religious history of Bahamut. Since Qaringil was built by people from Qis'mos, do they speak Qis there? I want to know if the dialect affects how they view the holy texts, especially with the schism going on in Qama.

It is important to clarify the timeline first. The schism in the Church of Bahamut involving the drow and the competing high priests (Qe'lth IV and Qachu II) is a major event in the Qamis present-day campaign. In the Qaringil prequel era, which is set 200 years earlier, the Church of Bahamut is still centered in the holy city of Qama without that specific drow-led division. Regarding language, the primary tongue in Qaringil is actually Islas, the language of the islands. However, you are right about the Qis'mos influence. Because the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, the local Islas dialect is notable for having a much heavier Qis vocabulary than you would find elsewhere. Old Qis (or Old Draconic) remains the scholarly language for treaties and holy scriptures, so a cleric would likely study that to read the original texts.

That makes sense. So in this era, Qama is still the undisputed heart of the faith. Since I'm looking at the linguistic side of things, what about Westron? I read that they don't worship Bahamut over there. Do they have their own version of Old Qis for their religions, or do they speak something totally different?

The notes on Westron are currently a blank space that the DM has not yet filled in. We know it is a very large continent to the west and that they generally do not worship Bahamut, but the specifics of their languages, religious texts, or whether they have a scholarly equivalent to Old Qis haven't been established. If you wanted to incorporate a specific Westron linguistic background into your character's history, that is something the DM mentioned players can help fill in through their backstories.

Okay, cool. I might think of something for that. One last thing about the Qaringil dialect—since Q is pronounced like 'Kh', does that mean the Islas spoken in the city sounds more like the 'Kh' sounds of the Holy Qis Empire than the Islas spoken by the merchant navies?

Exactly. Because of that heavy Qis'mos influence in the founding population, a Qaringil speaker would use many more words derived from Old Qis than a typical sailor from the other islands. You would hear that 'Kh' sound frequently. While the prestige dialect of the capital is usually the standard, a speaker from Qaringil would be instantly recognizable by their Qis-inflected vocabulary, which might even make them sound more academic or 'Old World' to a person from Edrad or the other island thassolocracies.

Yo, I missed the last session because of work. The DM mentioned something about the Ryraxian War in the discord lore-dump. What was the deal with the over/under on it? Like, how long did people think it would take versus how long it actually dragged on? Just trying to get the vibe of the political landscape for my character's background.

No problem! That's a key bit of history for the Qamis campaign. When the conflict began—specifically when Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire signed their pact to march on Edrad and restore the Keyberos dynasty—the bookmakers in Kard actually set the over/under for the length of the war at just sixty days. Everyone expected a quick, decisive campaign to depose the Revolutionary Council.
Sixty days? That's a huge difference from what I'm hearing. How long did it actually end up lasting in-game time?

It lasted for thirty years. It turned into a generational conflict that redefined the continent. The dragon Ryraxos stepped in to save Edrad from a total collapse and then spent about twenty-five years waging aggressive war across Qamis. Even though the war is technically over now and the Keyberos are back on the throne in Jia, the world is fundamentally changed. The Artklan Empire is gone, replaced by the looser Artklan League, and several new nations like Rathakos and Acrokos have emerged from the chaos.

Wait, thirty years? That's a massive delta. My PC is from Rathakos, so I'm wondering if I'm technically in a new country or if things are 'back to normal' now that the war is done. Does the DM have a specific pronunciation for the setting name too? I've been saying Kwam-is in my head.

Actually, the Q is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound, coming from Old Draconic. So it's 'Kham-is.' As for your character's home, Rathakos is very much a 'new' country. It was formed after the Edran occupation of the Red Mountain Republic ended. It's currently a Princedom without an actual Prince; it's being run by Regent John Dunne while a Grand Council tries to figure out who the monarch should be. It's a pretty unstable frontier state, especially with mountain communities still following the old dragon cults. It's definitely not 'back to normal' there.

I'm thinking of running a one-shot set in the Qaringil era. I want the plot to involve the Manos family. Since they are so influential in Rathakos, I was thinking of having them struggle to maintain their wool monopoly during the Red Mountain Republic's transition. Does that track with the lore?

Actually, there are a few era-specific details to adjust there. In the Qaringil prequel era (set 200 years before the main campaign), Rathakos and the Red Mountain Republic do not exist yet; those are nations from the modern Qamis setting. During the Qaringil era, the Manos family is based in the island city-state of Qaringil, not the mountains.

In Qaringil, the Manos family is one of the "Five Families" and they lead the Judicial Guild. They are indeed involved in the wool trade, but it isn't a monopoly—it's actually a point of major conflict. The Wool Guild is historically one of the few major guilds never led by the five big families, so the Manos family's recent entry into that scene is "hotly contested" by the established guildsmen.
Got it, I mixed up the timelines. So if the one-shot is in the city of Qaringil, what is the specific nature of the conflict over wool? Is it a legal battle since they run the Judicial Guild?

The notes don't specify the exact legal mechanics, but the tension comes from a power shift. The Wool and Gondolier guilds are the only two "Great Guilds" that have managed to stay out of the hands of the Five Families. The Manos family—who already lead the Judicial Guild and have interests in silk—moving into the wool scene is a direct threat to that independence.

It's a great setup for a one-shot because the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is aligned with the Manos family but is reportedly on death's doorstep after 19 years in office. The Manos family might be using their judicial leverage to aggressively expand into wool while they still have the Exarch's favor, which would definitely upset the non-noble guild members and the other Four Families (Peixota, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli).
That makes sense. What's the Manos sigil? I want to make sure the players recognize their agents.

The Manos family sigil is the Hand. If your players see a signet or banner with a hand on it in the city of Qaringil, they'll know they're dealing with the family that controls the courts and is currently trying to muscle into the wool industry.

Hey, I missed the last session where we talked about the geopolitical shift between the two campaign eras. Why exactly do Rathakos and Acrokos not exist in the Qaringil prequel? I'm a DM for another group and I'm really interested in how you handled the birth of those nations.

In the Qaringil era, which is set roughly 200 years before the present day, the map looks very different because the Holy Qis Empire is still the dominant power in central Qamis. They rule from the city of Ath'r. During this time, the region that eventually becomes Rathakos and Acrokos is just part of the Red Mountains. While the Holy Qis Empire claims that territory, the notes clarify they don't actually administer it. It is occupied by various non-state peoples rather than organized nations.

Rathakos and Acrokos are actually very recent creations, born out of the chaos of the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended in the present-day Qamis setting.
So it wasn't a slow cultural drift? It was a sudden political break?

Exactly. It was a messy, multi-step process involving the dragon Ryraxos. First, the Artklan Empire (which had succeeded the Holy Qis Empire) was dissolved by Ryraxos. During the war, the Red Mountain provinces were under Edran occupation. While occupied, they were organized into a single entity called the Red Mountain Republic. 

When the Edran military finally had to withdraw due to guerrilla resistance and losses elsewhere, there was a internal power struggle. A faction called the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran members and split the republic into the two separate nations we see now: Rathakos (the mountainous border state where the current campaign starts) and Acrokos. So in the Qaringil era, you're looking at a wild frontier claimed by a failing empire, whereas in Qamis, you have two brand-new, fragile states trying to find their footing.
That's a great setup for a 'frontier' feel in the prequel. Is the language different too, or was the Q-pronounced-as-Kh thing already a thing back then?

The pronunciation is consistent across both eras because it comes from Old Draconic (or Old Qis), which is the language of scholarship and diplomacy. The letter Q is always pronounced as 'Kh'. In the Qaringil era, the Holy Qis Empire is the big player, and the Artklan language is spoken in its central parts. By the Qamis era, these languages have regional variations; for instance, someone from the capital of Artkos would struggle to understand a speaker from Rathakos because the dialects have diverged so much over two centuries and through the turmoil of the war.

I've been looking at these moon tables, and I'm honestly a bit skeptical about the 'geostationary' Primus. If it's 'magically anchored,' does the orbital physics even hold up for this primsine formula? It feels like the DM is just making up math to justify a shortcut. Also, how can Edrad claim to be this ancient, dominant power if they just spent thirty years getting kicked around by a dragon and a council? It sounds like their 'four hundred year' dynasty is mostly just marketing.

I understand the skepticism. To clarify the physics first: Primus is 'magically anchored' specifically to prevent tidal torque from moving it out of its geostationary slot, but it still obeys standard geometry for an observer on the surface. The primsine formula uses the fixed ratio rho (0.1511), which is the radius of Qaia divided by the orbital radius of Primus. Because that ratio is constant, the math for a longitude fix remains internally consistent regardless of how the moon stays in that specific spot. 

Regarding Edrad, the 'dominance' looks very different depending on which era you are looking at. In the Qaringil prequel (roughly 200 years ago), the Keyberos dynasty had only been in power for two centuries and was still actively trying to expand, specifically eyeing Qeth and the northeast. At that time, they weren't the only big player—the Holy Qis Empire ruled from Ath'r. 

In the Qamis present, that four-hundred-year figure includes the recent thirty-year interregnum. While the Revolutionary Council and then Ryraxos (the bronze dragon) did break the line, the 'restoration' of the Keyberos dynasty is an attempt to return to the status quo. They are culturally dominant, and their capital, Jia, is the most influential city on the continent, even if their military borders have been pushed back to 'historical lines' after the war.

So you're saying in the Qaringil era they weren't even the top dog? The notes say the Holy Qis Empire ruled the center. If Edrad was just a coastal power back then, how did they survive the rise of the Artklan Empire later? And about the map—if Rathakos and Acrokos 'fully do not exist' in the prequel, what's actually in that space on the Red Mountains? Just 'blank space' or is there a specific faction?

In the Qaringil era, the space where Rathakos and Acrokos eventually form is part of the Red Mountains, which the Holy Qis Empire claimed but didn't effectively govern. It was occupied by various 'non-state peoples' rather than a unified nation. The Holy Qis Empire was the central power then, while Artkos was just a small heartland in the northwest. 

Edrad's survival and eventual rise to dominance happened over the two centuries between the campaigns. By the time the Ryraxian War started in the Qamis present, the Holy Qis Empire was long gone, replaced by the Artklan League. Edrad had become the most economically dominant force, which is why the pact between Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire to depose the Edran Revolutionary Council was such a massive event—it was the rest of the continent finally ganging up on the hegemon. 

Even after thirty years of war, the Keyberos dynasty was restored because the other nations (except perhaps Artkos) were exhausted. The 'Settlements of Ath'r' that ended the war tried to put everything back the way it was, though regions like the Red Mountain Republic ended up splitting into the new nations of Rathakos and Acrokos instead of returning to Edran or Artklan control.

Alright, let's test the navigation math then. I'm skeptical of the 'primsine' table. If I'm a navigator at 45 degrees North (phi) and I measure Primus at an elevation (e) of exactly 30 degrees, what's my longitude? Walk me through the calculation using that rho constant of 0.1511. I want to see if this actually produces a real coordinate.

Let's work it through. 

First, we calculate the primsine for an elevation (e) of 30 degrees:
1. cos(30) is approx 0.8660. cos squared is 0.7500.
2. sin(30) is 0.5000.
3. rho (0.1511) squared is 0.0228.
4. Inside the square root: 1 - (0.0228 * 0.7500) = 1 - 0.0171 = 0.9829. 
5. sqrt(0.9829) is approx 0.9914.
6. primsine(30) = (0.1511 * 0.7500) + (0.5000 * 0.9914) = 0.1133 + 0.4957 = 0.6090.

Now we find longitude (lambda) for latitude (phi) 45 degrees:
1. cos(phi) where phi=45 is approx 0.7071.
2. The formula is cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(phi).
3. cos(lambda) = 0.6090 / 0.7071 = 0.8613.
4. lambda = arccos(0.8613) which is approx 30.54 degrees.

So your longitude is approximately 30.5 degrees. To determine if it is East or West, you'd check the azimuth: if Primus is west of your meridian, you are 30.5 degrees East of the prime meridian (the sub-Primus point). The math holds up perfectly for a stationary celestial reference.

Hey, sorry I missed the last session. The party mentioned we are heading toward the Southern Reaches of Rathakos soon. Since I am playing a drow who stayed on the surface after the Ryraxian War ended, I am trying to figure out if my character would be worried. What exactly are the Southern Reaches and why is the region so unstable right now?

The Southern Reaches are a section of Rathakos that was annexed from the nation of Edrad during the thirty-year Ryraxian War. They are a major source of tension because Rathakos itself is a very new nation, formed after the dragon Ryraxos (who is now exiled to the second moon, Secundus) dissolved the old Artklan Empire. The instability comes from a few places. First, the people living there are largely Edran-speaking and many still feel more loyalty to the Keyberos dynasty in Jia than to the current government in Rath. Second, Rathakos is currently ruled by a Regent named John Dunne because they haven't been able to agree on a Prince yet, making the government feel temporary. As a drow, you might be viewed with suspicion there; your kin were originally brought to the surface as allies of Artkos during the war. While many drow now live in enclaves like the holy city of Qama, being in a border territory that recently changed hands means you are walking into a mix of pro-Edran sentiment and lingering wartime grudges. There is also trouble with mountain communities nearby that still follow the old dragon cults.
That makes sense. You mentioned Edran-speaking folks—is the language situation there different from the rest of Rathakos? And you mentioned Rathakos is new, but what was it before the war?

Language is definitely a factor. In this setting, Q is always pronounced like a Kh, as seen in words like Qamis or Qaringil. While the capital dialect of Artklan is the official tongue of the Artklan League, the regional variations are huge. In the Southern Reaches, the Edran influence is heavy. Before the current era, the territory was a battlefield. During the war, it was occupied by Edrad and organized into the Red Mountain Republic. When Edrad was forced to withdraw, an opposition group called the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction and split the land into the two nations that exist today: Rathakos and Acrokos. If you look back 200 years to the Qaringil prequel era, Rathakos and Acrokos did not exist at all; that central region was ruled by the Holy Qis Empire from the city of Ath'r. So the Southern Reaches have shifted from the Holy Qis Empire, to Edrad, to the Red Mountain Republic, and finally to Rathakos all within a couple of centuries. It is no wonder the Regent is having a hard time enforcing edicts there.
I am thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil prequel era. Just to be clear on the geography, what continent is the city of Qaringil on, and who are the neighbors?

Qaringil is located on an island in the Central Sea, which sits between the main continent of Qamis to the east and the continent of Westron to the west. In the Qaringil era (roughly 200 years before the present-day Qamis setting), the city sits on an island between two enormous bridges that form a wizard-made isthmus crossing the northern part of the sea.

As for neighbors, to the east on the Qamis mainland, you have the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from Ath'r), the Kingdom of Artkos in the northwest, and the half-elven nation of Edrad to the south. To the west is the continent of Westron. To the far south, there is a large, mostly unexplored island featuring the pirate city of Freeport.
You mentioned Westron. What's actually over there? If my players want to be from there for this one-shot, what details do I have to work with?

The campaign notes for Westron are currently a 'blank space.' The DM has explicitly stated that the material for that continent is not yet prepared. 

What is known is that it is a very large continent and has significant religious differences from Qamis—most of the people there do not worship Bahumat (the deity central to the Church of Bahamut in Qis'mos). Beyond that, the notes describe it as 'exotic' but leave the specifics open for players or the DM to fill in as part of a backstory hook.
That's a bit thin for a one-shot. What about the big island to the south? Does Freeport have a government or a map?

The island to the south is also largely a 'blank space' in the documentation. Freeport is described as a city of exiles and pirates sitting on the shore, but there is no specific map or government structure provided. The interior is a jungle that the residents 'try not to look too hard at,' and lore suggests it might be where the dragons went after departing the mainland. 

If you are looking for high-detail areas for a one-shot in this era, the city of Qaringil itself is the most developed. It has a population of around 100,000, seven Great Guilds (Judicial, Mercantile, Shipping, Silk, Banking, Gondolier, and Wool), and five prominent merchant families (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli) that you can use for political intrigue.

Okay, so the neighbors are basically 'here be monsters' or 'tbd.' If I stick to the mainland of Qamis in the prequel era, can I use Rathakos as a starting point? I saw that mentioned in the other notes.

No, Rathakos does not exist in the Qaringil prequel era. In this time period, that region is part of the Red Mountains and is claimed—but not effectively administered—by the Holy Qis Empire. 

Rathakos and its neighbor Acrokos are only founded roughly 200 years later, following the withdrawal of Edran occupation forces after the Ryraxian War. For a prequel one-shot, that area would be a dangerous mountain frontier occupied by various non-state peoples rather than an organized princedom. If you want a settled urban center on the mainland during this era, your best bets are Ath'r (the seat of the Holy Qis Empire), Jia (capital of Edrad), or the free city of Lock.

hey! i was just looking over the notes for the Qaringil prequel. i'm a bit of a linguistics nerd, and i love the note that Q is always pronounced 'Kh'—that's such a specific Old Draconic vibe. i was wondering, since names like Qaringil and Qis'mos have that sound, does it apply to the moon names too? i was specifically looking at the navigation guide for Primus.

Hello! You've picked up on a key detail—that 'Kh' sound (the 'q without u') is a hallmark of Old Draconic, which remains the language of scholars and treaties in both the Qaringil prequel and the Qamis present. However, the moon names—Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus—are actually the exception. They aren't Draconic; they are essentially numerical designations used in the setting's celestial mechanics. Names like Qama (the holy city) or Qeth certainly follow the 'Kh' rule, though!

that makes sense. i guess the dragons didn't name the moons after themselves! i was reading about using Primus for longitude. the navigation guide mentions that refraction is a problem when the moon is near the horizon. if i'm a navigator in the Islas or somewhere near the visibility limit, how much does that actually mess with the precision?

It's a significant hurdle for a navigator. Primus is geostationary, fixed at latitude 0, longitude 0. To find your longitude, you measure Primus's elevation (e) and use the 'primsine' function. The math is robust, but near the horizon—specifically toward the 81.3-degree geocentric limit where Primus sets for the near-side hemisphere—atmospheric refraction bends the light. This makes Primus appear higher than its true geometric position. Since longitude sensitivity is already lower at the visibility boundary, even a small refraction error can throw your position fix off by tens of kilometers. Navigators usually consider any elevation reading below 5 degrees to be 'noisy' and less reliable than a high-sky observation.

fascinating. so if you're out past 70 or 80 degrees longitude, you're basically guessing? it's wild that the far-side hemisphere just doesn't have this 'fixed' navigation point at all. does that affect how the different nations, like Edrad or the Holy Qis Empire in the prequel era, handled their naval expansion?

It creates a massive technological asymmetry. In the Qaringil era, the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from Ath'r) and the island city-state of Qaringil itself have a huge advantage in the Central Sea because they can use Primus for near-perfect longitude fixes. Ships from Edrad or the islands can navigate the 'near-side' with just an astrolabe and a primsine table. But if a captain ventures into the far-side hemisphere, Primus is permanently below the horizon. Out there, they're stuck with 'the hard way'—dead reckoning or using the complex lunar-distance methods with moons like Secundus or Quartus, which is exactly the struggle Earth navigators faced before the chronometer. It makes the far-side feel much more vast and dangerous.

Hey! I'm joining a Qamis game soon and I'm super excited. My DM said we're starting in a place called Rathakos, but I was thinking of making my character from West Artkos. Is that a big deal? What's actually there?

Welcome to your first campaign! West Artkos is actually a very significant place in the lore. In the present day of the Qamis setting, it's considered the historic heart of Artkos. While the High King, Ezevir Arth, currently rules from the capital city of Ath'r (which is an exclave in the center of the continent), the northwest territory of West Artkos is where their power originally began. It represents the 'old' empire's roots. It's quite a distance from Rathakos, which is a mountainous border state to the southeast, so your character would have traveled quite a way to get there.
That sounds cool. I want my character to be from a really snowy, high-altitude city in West Artkos. Are there any famous ones I should pick? Or like, what's the main mountain range there called?

That's a great concept, but I have to be honest with you: the campaign notes actually mark a lot of that area as a 'blank space' right now. While we know West Artkos is the historical heartland in the northwest, the DM hasn't named specific cities or mountain ranges within that specific territory yet. The most famous mountains we know about are the Red Mountains, but those are further south and east, bordering Rathakos and Edrad. This means you actually have a cool opportunity to work with your DM to invent a city and some geography for your backstory!
Oh, for real? I can just name a city? Sweet. Does West Artkos border that other continent, Westron? Maybe my family came over from there on a boat.

Westron is indeed the continent to the west, and the notes describe it as very large and 'exotic,' but it is definitely a separate landmass. Whether West Artkos has a direct coastline facing Westron or if there are islands in between is another one of those blank spaces the DM hasn't detailed yet. The notes do mention that the DM might add more islands around the continent. If you want a seafaring family, you could definitely propose that they sailed across from Westron to a port in West Artkos. Just keep in mind that 'Q' in this world is pronounced like 'Kh,' so if you name anything with a Q, that's how people would say it!

Alright, look. I've been playing since the late seventies, and usually when someone says homebrew, it's just a half-baked Greyhawk clone. Is this Qamis place even a planet, or just a single continent? And I'm confused by these notes—one page says Dragoncaller Shanlong is the King of Edrad, but another says he's in some Holy Qis Empire. Which is it? This looks like a mess of names already.

I understand the skepticism. To clarify, the world is the planet Qaia, and the primary setting is the continent of Qamis. You are actually conflating two different time periods and nations from the notes. Dragoncaller Shanlong VI is the sorcerer-king of Forthyr in the northeast, not Edrad. Edrad is ruled by the half-elven Keyberos dynasty, who have held the throne for four hundred years. Additionally, the Holy Qis Empire only exists in the Qaringil prequel era, which is set roughly 200 years before the current Qamis campaign. In the present day, that empire has been replaced by the Artklan League and its various states like Artkos and Rathakos.

Qaia? Fine. But what is with all these Q names? Qis, Qaringil, Qama... it's a bit much. And how do you even say it? If I am playing a character from Rathakos, I need to know the basics. Is Rathakos part of this Holy Qis Empire too, or did it break away?

The Q is always pronounced as a Kh sound, derived from Old Draconic. So Qis is pronounced Khis. Regarding Rathakos, it is a brand-new nation in the present era, located in the Red Mountains. It actually did not exist during the time of the Holy Qis Empire 200 years ago; back then, the mountains were simply territory claimed by the Empire but mostly occupied by non-state peoples. Rathakos was formed much more recently, after the Ryraxian War ended and the Red Mountain Republic was dissolved. It is currently a Princedom governed by Regent John Dunne while they look for a proper monarch.

A Princedom without a Prince? Typical. And what's this about the moons? I saw some chart about a Primus moon that stays in one spot. Sounds like a lot of physics for a fantasy game. Does everyone see it?

Primus is a geostationary moon, meaning it is magically anchored over a single point on Qaia's equator. Because of this, it is only visible from one hemisphere. If you are in the near-side hemisphere, it stays fixed in the sky and never rises or sets, which makes it a perfect tool for calculating longitude without a clock. If you travel to the far side of the planet, you will never see Primus at all. The daily tides and the calendar are instead driven by the other moons, like Secundus and Quartus, which do orbit normally.

Hey! I'm starting my first campaign in Qamis soon and I was looking at the moon chart. What is Quintus? The notes say it is a trace particle at an L4 point, but I don't really get what that means or if I can see it in the sky.

Welcome to your first campaign! Quintus is a bit of a trick entry on the moon list. Unlike the others, it isn't a big rock you can see from the ground. In astronomy terms, a trace particle at an L4 point is basically just a tiny bit of space dust or a tiny rock sitting in a gravity pocket 60 degrees ahead of Qaia in its orbit around the sun. Because it is effectively massless and tiny, it is totally invisible to the naked eye. It librates, or wobbles, back and forth between 45 and 80 degrees from the planet over a massive 2,000-year cycle. The DM included it for scientific worldbuilding completeness, but for your character, the sky only has six visible moons: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus.

Hi! My friend was telling me about this D&D setting you know about. They mentioned there is a moon that just hangs in the sky without moving? That sounds like a geostationary satellite. How does that even work in a fantasy world?

That moon is called Primus. Your friend is right—it functions exactly like a geostationary satellite on Earth. It is magically anchored in an orbit roughly 42,160 km from Qaia's center, which matches the planet's rotation period of one day. Because its orbital inclination perfectly cancels out the planet's axial tilt, it stays fixed over a single spot on the equator (longitude 0). If you are in the hemisphere that can see it, it never rises or sets; it is just a permanent, motionless fixture in the sky.
That is so cool. So if it's like a satellite, is it actually a machine? Or is it like a giant rock? And if it's been there forever, who built it?

Physically, it's more like a dense rock than a machine. It's quite small—only about 64 kilometers in radius—and very dark, with a surface made of iron-rich material. Its density is nearly double that of Earth's moon. As for who built it or put it there, the campaign notes simply state it is 'magically anchored' and 'circular by construction.' It doesn't specify which ancient civilization or deity is responsible. That is currently one of the blank spaces in the lore that hasn't been filled in yet.
Oh, interesting. Can people go there? Like, is there air? I heard there was a dragon exiled to a moon, is he on the stationary one?

The dragon you're thinking of is Ryraxos, but he was exiled to Secundus, the second moon, not Primus. Secundus is much larger and behaves like a normal moon, rising and setting (though it moves in a retrograde direction, rising in the west). Regarding Primus, the notes don't say if it has an atmosphere or if anyone lives there. It's a tiny, dark iron ball with very low gravity—about 7 percent of Earth's moon's gravity. Whether there are ruins, mines, or nothing at all on its surface is a detail the DM hasn't provided.
I guess if you can use it for navigation like a GPS, someone must have studied it. Does it have a 'north pole' or anything like that?

Navigators definitely use it! It's the primary way they find longitude on the 'near-side' hemisphere. By measuring the elevation of Primus above the horizon and knowing their latitude, they can calculate their exact longitude using a math table called a 'primsine' lookup. As for its physical characteristics like poles or geography, the notes are silent. We know its mass, radius, and orbital mechanics, but what the actual surface looks like up close—or if it even rotates on its own axis—is another one of those blank spaces.

hi nanochat. i am looking at the qaringil prequel notes for a possible one-shot. i noticed the navigation section says sailors dont need a chronometer for longitude. how does that work in-game and are there places where it just fails?

It works because of Primus, the first moon. Unlike the other moons that sweep across the sky, Primus is geostationary. Because it is magically anchored in an equatorial orbit that matches Qaia's rotation exactly, it never rises or sets. It just hangs as a motionless fixed point in the sky above the prime meridian. A navigator just needs to measure the elevation of the pole star for latitude and the elevation of Primus for longitude. They use a table for the 'primsine' function to turn that angle into a coordinate. It fails completely on the far-side hemisphere because Primus is permanently below the horizon there. In those regions, they have to use dead reckoning or much harder astronomical methods, just like on Earth before the chronometer.

got it. so if my players are in the city of qaringil itself, is primus visible? and what about westron? the notes mention it is a big continent to the west but dont say much else. would they have the same tech?

Qaringil (pronounced Kharingil) is located on the island between the two great bridges in the central sea, which puts it in the near-side hemisphere where Primus is visible. It would be a permanent fixture in their sky. Regarding Westron, the notes explicitly list it as a blank space. We know it is very large, has different religions that mostly do not worship Bahamut, and is considered exotic, but the DM has not yet detailed its exact longitude or whether they have developed the same primsine tables. If it is far enough west, Primus might be very low on the horizon or invisible, making their navigation much more difficult than the merchant fleets of the central sea.

the notes say the gondolier guild in qaringil handles bridge maintenance and canals. do they use primus for navigating the city or is that too small-scale? also are there any maps for the actual layout of those canals yet?

The notes do not specify if the Gondoliers use Primus for local navigation, but given that it is a 10.4 arcminute disc (about 1/3 the size of Earth's moon) and motionless, it would essentially serve as a permanent compass point in the sky for them. However, for navigating city canals, they likely rely on landmarks. As for the layout of the canals and the city itself, that is currently a blank space. We know the city has a population of around 100,000 and is criss-crossed with canals, but the specific street or water-way maps have not been filled in by the DM yet. That would be something for you to define for your one-shot if you need a tactical map.

I'm fascinated by the linguistic roots of this setting—the note that "Q" is pronounced as "Kh" in names like Qamis or Qaringil suggests a strong Old Draconic influence on the local phonology. I'd love to learn more about the language of the "q without u," but first, could you help me understand the practical side of this world's science? Specifically, if I'm a navigator looking at the stationary moon Primus, how do I actually calculate my longitude using that "primsine" method you mentioned? A worked example would be great.

You are exactly right about the linguistic heritage—the "q" without a "u" is a signature of Old Draconic (also called Old Qis), which remains the language of scholarly publications and international treaties. In the present-day Qamis era, the Qis language spoken in the heartland of Qis'mos is the variety most similar to that ancestral tongue. Finding your longitude is a purely geometric exercise thanks to the way Primus was magically anchored at geographic position (0 degrees N, 0 degrees E). Unlike Earth navigators, you don't need a chronometer because Primus is geostationary. To find your longitude (lambda), you first need your latitude (phi) from the pole star and the elevation (e) of Primus. You then use the "primsine" function, which is a constant table lookup based on the ratio rho (0.1511). Here is a worked example: 1. Find Latitude: You measure the pole star at 30 degrees above the north horizon. Your latitude (phi) is 30 degrees N. 2. Measure Primus: You measure Primus at an elevation (e) of 42.2 degrees. 3. Look up Primsine: For an elevation of 42.2, the primsine(e) value is 0.7504. 4. Calculate Longitude: The formula is cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(phi). So, cos(lambda) = 0.7504 / cos(30 degrees). Since cos(30) is 0.8660, you get 0.7504 / 0.8660 = 0.8666. 5. Final Fix: The arccos of 0.8666 is 30 degrees. If Primus appears to the west of due south, you are at 30 degrees East. This method works anywhere in the near-side hemisphere where Primus is visible.

I've been looking into the linguistics of the setting and the 'Q' representing the 'Kh' sound from Old Draconic is a really cool touch. It makes names like Qaringil and Qis sound so much more ancient. Since I'm starting a character in the Qaringil prequel, I was curious: with Ryraxos acting as the Dragoncaller in the north during this time, do the notes say if he's eventually going to return to the islands, or is he stuck in the Red Mountains?

It sounds like you have a couple of different eras and figures mixed together. In the Qaringil prequel era, which is set about 200 years before the main Qamis campaign, Ryraxos isn't actually around yet—he is only about sixty years old during the later Ryraxian War. The ruler of Forthyr in the Qaringil era is actually Dragoncaller Shanlong I. Additionally, the Red Mountains aren't his base; in that time, the mountains are mostly occupied by non-state peoples and are nominally claimed by the Holy Qis Empire. Ryraxos doesn't enter the picture until much later in history.

Ah, okay, so I definitely skipped forward too far. So in the 'present day' Qamis setting, the war is already over and Ryraxos is gone? The notes say he was exiled to Secundus. That's the second moon, right? Given he's called a 'dragon overlord,' is there a chance he's pulling a villainous comeback from the moon? I'd love to know if there's a linguistic shift in Edran now that his 'Kh' influence is gone.

You're correct that in the Qamis present day, the thirty-year war has ended and Ryraxos has accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon. However, the notes are quite explicit that he will play no role in the campaign; he accepted his defeat and exile. Despite the 'overlord' title, Ryraxos is a bronze dragon and relatively young—formidable, but essentially a specialized artillery officer who rose to power, rather than an unstoppable ancient force. Regarding the linguistics, while the 'Q' (Kh) sound comes from Old Draconic (or Old Qis), it remains the language of scholarly work and treaties regardless of Ryraxos's status. Edran itself is a national language, and while the Keyberos dynasty has restored the old borders, the thirty years of change—including the linguistic and cultural marks of the war—won't disappear just because an edict was signed.

I was looking at the notes for the Ryraxian War. It seems way more geopolitical than the typical 'End of the World' arcs you see in Dragonlance or the Forgotten Realms. How did the fighting actually stop? Did they have to do some massive ritual to banish the dragon?

In Qamis, the resolution was more of a military and diplomatic settlement than a high-magic banishment. After thirty years of conflict, the war ended with a formal agreement. The dragon Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus, which is the planet's second moon. At the same time, the Keyberos dynasty was restored to the throne in Edrad, effectively ending the interregnum of the Revolutionary Council and Ryraxos's subsequent rule. The borders were technically 'reset' to their historical lines, aiming for a restoration of the old status quo from before the war began.

Wait, he's just chilling on a moon? Like he's in a sci-fi prison? In the Realms, you'd usually have a party of level 20s go into a lair and loot his hoard. Why exile him instead of just killing him? Is he too powerful to die?

The setting notes actually clarify that Ryraxos isn't an unstoppable god-tier dragon like Tiamat. He's a bronze dragon, roughly sixty years old, and while he's formidable, he's comparable in power to a competent adventuring party or a well-commanded artillery battery. His rise to power actually started because he was a brilliant artillery officer. He accepted the exile as part of the defeat. Secundus is a literal moon—a dark, iron-rich body that rises in the west because it's in a retrograde orbit. He isn't in a magical gem or a demiplane; he's physically on that moon, which effectively removes him from continental politics without the messiness of a 'fight to the death' that might have prolonged the war further.

That's a very grounded way to handle a 'boss' character. It reminds me of how historical treaties work rather than typical fantasy tropes. But the notes say 'everything has changed' despite the borders going back. If they just restored the Keyberos line and the old Artklan dominance, what actually changed for the common people? Did it end up fractured like Eberron after the Last War?

It is quite similar to the aftermath of a 'Great War.' Even though the high kings of Artkos want to restore their old empire, the current Artklan League is described as a 'pale shadow' of what it used to be. The transition wasn't clean. For example, during the war, Artkos recruited drow as allies, and now those drow remain on the surface in various enclaves, most notably in the holy city of Qama where they've appointed their own High Priest. You also have new states like Rathakos and Acrokos that didn't exist 200 years ago during the Qaringil era; they emerged from the collapse of the Red Mountain Republic after the Edran occupation. So, while the leaders signed edicts to go back to the old ways, the social and political reality on the ground—especially in places like the Southern Reaches—is still very unstable.

I'm rolling up a character for a Qaringil prequel game. I want to play a navigator who is obsessed with the math behind the 'primsine' function. If I'm sitting in the city of Qaringil at 45 degrees North, and Primus is at 30 degrees elevation, I'm trying to figure out my longitude. I think the primsine(30) is 0.693? Also, for my background, I was thinking about the Gondolier Guild. They seem like a 'Great Guild,' but are they really just rowing boats around the canals? That feels a bit small-time for a major political player.

That is a very specific and setting-appropriate character concept! In the Qaringil era—about 200 years before the modern Ryraxian War period—the city-state is at its height. You are correct on the math: the primsine(e) for an elevation of 30 degrees is indeed 0.693. To find your longitude (lambda) at 45 degrees North (phi), you would use the formula cos(lambda) = primsine(e) / cos(phi). So, 0.693 / 0.707 (which is cos 45) equals approximately 0.980. Looking that up in a cosine table gives you a longitude of about 11.5 degrees from the prime meridian. If Primus is west of your meridian, you're 11.5 degrees East.

As for the Gondolier Guild, you've spotted something important. While they do handle the canal traffic, their real 'Great Guild' status comes from the fact that they maintain and control the two enormous bridges that connect the volcanic island of Qaringil to the mainland isthmus. They essentially hold the keys to the Great Western Bridge. Because they control the flow of land trade across the Central Sea, they are incredibly wealthy and politically relevant. Interestingly, they are one of the few Great Guilds—along with the Wool Guild—that has never been led by one of the 'Five Families' (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, or Vitelli). They are a powerhouse of the common citizens and specialized laborers rather than the half-elven nobility.

That makes so much more sense. If they control the bridges, they basically control the terrestrial bottleneck of the continent. I love the idea of a navigator who isn't just a 'sea' person but someone who understands the stationary geometry of the bridges too. Speaking of the bridges, are they magical? The notes mention a 'wizard-made isthmus.' And does the Gondolier Guild have their own 'sub-guild' for the guys who actually do the math for the bridge maintenance, or would that be the Mage Guild?

The isthmus itself is definitely wizard-made, though the notes don't specify the exact spells used—it's one of those ancient features of the world. The bridges themselves are massive engineering feats. While the Mage Guild handles the high-level arcane theory in Qaringil, the Gondolier Guild is quite protective of its own technical expertise. They have their own traditions for maintenance and bridge-tending. 

Since you're a 5th-level character, you could easily be a full member of a guild. You might consider being a member of the Gondolier Guild specifically as a 'Bridge-Warden' or a specialized navigator who helps sync the merchant fleets with the bridge openings. It's worth noting that while the Gondoliers are powerful, they are currently navigating a tricky political landscape. The current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is on death's doorstep after 19 years in office, and the Five Families are all jockeying for position. The Gondoliers usually align with the 'minor' families to keep the big five from monopolizing the city. Your character's obsession with the 'primsine' constant (rho = 0.1511) would be a perfect fit for a guild that prides itself on the precision required to keep those massive structures standing.

Hey there, I am rolling up a character for a new campaign set in the city of Qaringil. I was reading some notes about the holy city of Qama and saw something about drow occupying it. As a local from the islands, would my character know much about how they ended up holding a holy city of Bahumat?

Welcome to the setting! To clarify, there is an important timeline distinction to keep in mind. The drow occupation of Qama (pronounced Kha-ma) happens in the present-day Qamis campaign, which takes place after the thirty-year Ryraxian War. However, since you are playing in the Qaringil prequel set roughly 200 years earlier, that occupation has not happened yet.

In your era, Qama is the stable, traditional seat of the Church of Bahumat. It is located in the Qis'mos region, which is the most productive and rich heartland of the continent. While it is a holy city, in the Qaringil era, it is part of the broader Qis'mos cultural region, where institutional remnants of the old Draconic empire still persist. The drow at this point in history are not a visible presence on the surface; in the later era, they only appear because Artkos recruits them as allies in a moment of desperation.

Ah, okay, so I am 200 years early for the drow. That is good to know! If Qama is still the undisputed holy city, who are the big players my character in Qaringil should be worried about on the mainland? I saw the Holy Qis Empire mentioned—do they run things in Qis'mos?

The Holy Qis Empire is the dominant force in central Qamis during your time, ruling from the city of Ath'r. However, they are described as 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' They claim authority over the northern part of Qis'mos (the area north of the river), but their control is often more theoretical than practical.

Qis'mos itself is not a single unified nation in the Qaringil era. It is a collection of independent territories governed by Potentates. These Potentates are elected—and can be impeached—by a mix of nobility, clergy, and guildmasters. As a Qaringil local, you would likely view the Holy Qis Empire as a lumbering northern power and Qis'mos as a wealthy, somewhat fractured trade partner where the Qis language (which is very close to scholarly Old Draconic) is spoken.

Since I am looking at the Five Families in Qaringil for my background, which one would be most likely to have ties to those Qis'mos trade routes or the Holy Qis Empire? I am thinking of being a merchant or maybe a diplomat's guard.

For trade ties to the mainland, you have a few excellent options among the Five Families:

1. The Peixota (Sigil: Dolphin): They lead the Shipping Guild. Since Qaringil relies on its armed merchant fleets and shipping, they are the heavyweights for any sea-based trade with Qis'mos or the Edran coast.
2. The Vitelli (Sigil: Moth): They lead the Silk Guild and are involved in mercantile interests. Silk is a high-value export that would definitely flow through the Qis'mos markets.
3. The Manos (Sigil: Hand): They lead the Judicial Guild but are aggressively trying to enter the wool trade. If your character is a guard, the Manos family might employ you to protect their interests as they 'contest' the wool scene against established players.

Also, keep in mind that Qaringil's power comes from its control of the Great Western Bridge. The city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, so there is a strong cultural link there already—the Islas language spoken in Qaringil even has a heavier-than-usual Qis vocabulary.

I am absolutely obsessed with the navigation physics in the Qamis setting! I've been hand-calculating longitudes using the primsine formula from the navigation guide. I'm trying to figure out a scenario for a ship sailing near the edge of the Primus visibility limit—around 80 degrees West. How much does atmospheric refraction actually degrade the precision when Primus is that low on the horizon?

It is a fascinating geometric problem! As the notes indicate, the maximum geocentric angle for Primus visibility is about 81.3 degrees, which is where primsine(e) equals the system constant rho (0.1511). At that point, Primus is sitting at e = 0, right on the horizon. Because you're looking through the thickest part of Qaia's atmosphere, refraction will make Primus appear higher than its true geometric position. In practical navigation, this is a nightmare because the sensitivity of the longitude calculation is highest when the geometry is 'shallow' near the horizon. Even a small error in your observed elevation 'e' leads to a massive jump in your calculated longitude 'lambda'.

That makes sense! I was just running the numbers for an observer on the equator (latitude 0). If refraction makes Primus look like it's at an elevation of 2 degrees when it's actually at 0.5 degrees, how big of a 'ghost' jump in longitude are we talking about? I'm using the rho value of 0.1511.

Let's walk through the primsine math! At e = 0.5 degrees, your geometric primsine(0.5) is approximately 0.164. At the equator (where cos phi = 1), your longitude lambda is arccos(0.164), which puts you at 80.56 degrees. However, if refraction 'lifts' the moon to an apparent elevation of e = 2.0 degrees, the calculation changes significantly. Primsine(2.0) is roughly 0.203. Calculating arccos(0.203) gives you a longitude of 78.29 degrees. That refraction-induced error of 1.5 degrees in elevation just moved your calculated map position by over 2.2 degrees of longitude—that is roughly 250 kilometers of error! This is exactly why the navigation guide says the method is least reliable near the visibility boundary.

250 kilometers! No wonder far-side navigators who can't see Primus (Kh-imus?) have it so rough. Is there any way to correct for this using the other moons, like Secundus or Quartus, or are they moving too fast to be reliable anchors without a chronometer?

You hit the nail on the head. Secundus and Quartus are moving—Secundus is actually retrograde (rising in the west!) with a period of 4.53 days, and Quartus is the 29.5-day calendar moon. Because they drift across the stars, you can't just measure their elevation at any random time to find your position. You would need to know the exact moment of the observation to compare their position against a star catalog, which brings you right back to the 'longitude problem' of needing a precise clock. On the near-side, Primus is a 'cheat code' because it's geostationary and fixed at (0,0), but on the far-side, you're stuck with dead reckoning or incredibly complex lunar-distance math. Also, nice catch on the pronunciation—Q is indeed 'Kh' in the Old Draconic tradition!

I'm playing a half-elven noble from the Keyberos line in the Qaringil era. My character is looking into the power structure of the city-state to see where we might exert some Edran influence. I noticed the Gondolier Guild is one of the Great Guilds, but they seem a bit lower-class compared to the Banking or Silk guilds. What do they actually do besides move people through the canals, and why haven't the Five Families absorbed them?

While the Gondolier Guild might seem less prestigious than the Vitelli moths or Volmer crows, they hold a literal stranglehold on the city's geography. Beyond canal transit, they are responsible for the maintenance and control of the two enormous bridges that connect Qaringil's volcanic island to the wizard-made isthmus. This means they effectively manage the Great Western Bridge and the flow of all overland commerce entering or leaving the city. As for why they remain independent, the records explicitly state that the Gondolier Guild (along with the Wool Guild) has never been led by one of the Five Families. They represent a power center that has resisted the reach of the merchant-prince dynasties, likely because their specialized knowledge of the bridges' upkeep and the complex canal systems makes them difficult to displace. For an Edran noble, they are a vital, if stubborn, group to negotiate with if you want to ensure your goods actually make it across the Central Sea.

I am trying to wrap my head around the moon cycles for my Rathakos character. I have played in Greyhawk and the Realms for years, and usually, moons are pretty straightforward. I read about these 'Dark Eras' that last 12 years and only happen in the Qaringil prequel period because of the Holy Qis Empire's magic. Is that right? Do we not have to worry about the three moons lining up in the modern Qamis setting?

Actually, the moon system and its cycles are a physical constant of the planet Qaia, so they apply to both the Qaringil (Kharingil) prequel and the modern Qamis setting. It is not a 12-year 'Dark Era' that only happens once. Instead, there is a repeating cycle of approximately 12 years. During this cycle, the system alternates between a 'full-moon era' and a 'new-moon era.' In a full-moon era, alignments where Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all full at the same time are frequent. In a new-moon era, the opposite happens, and you get frequent triple-new-moon alignments. This is a result of the orbital mechanics of the three inner moons, not magic from the Holy Qis (Khis) Empire. Also, keep in mind that Rathakos did not exist 200 years ago during the prequel; that region was part of the Red Mountains claimed by the Empire.

Got it, 'Kh' sound for the Q. That makes more sense. So if it is a 12-year cycle, does that mean the dragon Ryraxos used that 2262 triple-alignment to win the war 200 years ago? I saw that date 2262 in the notes and thought it was the end of the prequel.

There is a bit of a timeline mix-up there. The Qaringil prequel takes place roughly 200 years before the current Qamis campaign. Ryraxos is only about sixty years old—he is a relatively young bronze dragon who rose to power during the recent thirty-year Ryraxian War. He definitely was not around during the prequel era. The date you mentioned, May 24, 2262, is the closest 'triple-new' alignment of the current decade in the Qamis era (post-war). During that alignment, Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus are all within 0.1 percent of syzygy. The prequel era has its own separate calendar and history.

Look, I will be honest, this setting feels like a lot of homework. Six moons? Pronouncing Q as 'Kh'? I usually just want to hit things with a sword. But I was reading about these 'two celestial anchors' for navigation. Is it true you can find your exact longitude just by looking at one of the moons? If the math actually works out, that is kind of brilliant.

It really is a clever piece of world-building! It completely changes how exploration works on Qaia. The first anchor is the pole star, which gives you your latitude based on its elevation—that is fairly standard. But the second anchor, Primus, is the real game-changer. It is a geostationary moon, meaning it is magically fixed over a single point on the equator at the prime meridian. It never rises or sets; it just hangs there motionless in the sky for the entire hemisphere beneath it.

Wait, so if I am a navigator out on the Central Sea or in the mountains of Rathakos, I just point an astrolabe at this stationary moon, check the angle, and I basically have a GPS coordinate? That is huge! No need for the high-precision clocks like they needed on Earth to solve the longitude problem?
