Spot on! You do not need a chronometer at all. Navigators use what they call the 'primsine' function. It is a simple table lookup where you take the elevation of Primus and your known latitude from the pole star to find your longitude. Because the moon's position is a fixed physical constant, the math never changes. It makes the near-side hemisphere of Qaia incredibly easy to navigate. However, if you sail to the far side of the planet where Primus is below the horizon, you are back to square one, which makes those regions much more mysterious and dangerous to traverse!

Hey! Sorry I missed the session last night. I was deep in the weeds trying to manually verify the primsine lookup for a position at 30 degrees North. It is fascinating how the geometry holds up—I got a primsine of 0.7504 for an elevation of 42.2 degrees, which puts me right at 30 degrees East longitude. Anyway, I heard the party finally reached Rathakos. What is the deal with the Grand Council there? Why have they not picked a Prince yet?

Your math is spot on—that rho constant of 0.1511 makes the Primus elevation method incredibly reliable once you have the tables down. 

Regarding Rathakos (pronounced 'Rath-akh-os'), you have arrived at a very tense moment. The country is a relatively new Princedom, established after the Red Mountain Assembly purged the pro-Edran faction of the old Red Mountain Republic. While the 'Settlements of Ath’r' officially designated it a Princedom, they haven't actually found a monarch yet. 

The Grand Council is currently deliberating on who should take the throne, but they are stuck. In the meantime, the country is being run by Regent John Dunne. The delay isn't just bureaucracy; the Council is trying to navigate a lot of internal unrest. You mentioned your character is from the Southern Reaches—that is actually a major talking point for them, as those lands were annexed from Edrad and the population there is quite restive.
That makes sense. My character definitely feels that Edran influence. Is the council only worried about the border with Edrad, or are there other factions making the deliberation difficult?

It is definitely more than just the Edran border. While the Southern Reaches are a headache, the Council is also dealing with the mountain communities in the Red Mountains. Many of those groups still follow the 'old dragon cults' and aren't exactly thrilled with the new central government in Rath. 

Because Rathakos is right in the middle of everything—bordering Edrad, Qis’mos, Artkos, Qirkos, Lock, and Acrokos—the Council is essentially trying to find a Prince who can satisfy the nationalist factions that fought the war, the mountain traditionalists, and the former Edran subjects, all while maintaining their 'tenuous independence.' It's a tall order, which is why John Dunne is still in the Regent's chair.

Hey! I am thinking about running a one-shot in the Qaringil era, but I am also trying to get some flavor for a character I am building for the main Qamis game. I saw a note about the moons having a Hill sphere of 3.89 LD. What does that actually mean for someone looking at the sky in Qaringil, and why does it matter for the moons?

The Hill sphere is the region around a planet where its gravity is strong enough to keep satellites in orbit instead of them being pulled away by the sun. For Qaia, that limit is 3.89 Lunar Distances (LD). It is the reason the moon system is so stable—even the farthest moon, Septimus, sits at 2.47 LD, well within that 3.89 boundary. For someone in Qaringil, the sky is much busier than Earth's. You have the three inner moons (Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus) that provide most of the light and drive the tides, plus the smaller, slower outer moons (Sextus and Septimus). Most importantly, if you are in the right hemisphere, you have Primus—a geostationary moon that never moves from its spot in the sky.

That sounds like a lot to track. I am building a PC who is a navigator from Qaringil. Since the city is famous for its merchant fleets and those massive bridges, would they use these moons for sailing? Give me some local color—how would a Qaringil sailor talk about the moons?

A Qaringil navigator would be an expert on Primus. Because Primus is geostationary and magically anchored, it is a perfect reference point for longitude. A sailor would measure the elevation of Primus and use a 'primsine' table to figure out exactly how far east or west they are from the prime meridian. In the Islas language spoken in Qaringil, they might call Primus the 'Fixed Star' or the 'Still One.' They would also be very aware of the 'Triple-moon alignment eras.' About five times a year, the three inner moons align closely, which can cause massive tidal shifts. A navigator would have to know if they were in a 'full-moon era' or a 'new-moon era' to predict the waters.

That is cool. My guy is probably tied to one of the big families in the city. Which family would be the best fit for a high-end navigator? And what is the actual state of the city? I read something about the Exarch being old.

For a navigator, the Peixota family is your best bet. Their sigil is the Dolphin and they lead the Shipping Guild, though they also have hands in the Mercantile and Judicial guilds. The Vitelli (sigil: Moth) are also heavily involved in shipping if you want a different vibe. As for the city, it is a bustling republic of about 100,000 people, but things are currently quite tense. Exarch Giovanni Pesaro has ruled for 19 years but is widely considered to be on death's doorstep. The Five Families are likely positioning themselves for the power vacuum that follows. It is a city of canals, merchant fleets, and the Great Western Bridge, but right now, everyone is looking toward the palace to see who moves first when the Exarch finally passes.
Hey! I missed the last recap and I'm a bit lost on the history. How did the Ryraxian War actually end? Like, who won?

It's a bit of a complicated 'reset.' The war lasted thirty years and ended with the dragon Ryraxos being defeated and exiled to Secundus, which is Qaia's second moon. Politically, the borders were mostly pushed back to their historical lines from before the war started. The Keyberos dynasty was restored to the throne in Edrad, and Artkos regained its status as the dominant power in the central continent.
Wait, the dragon is living on the moon now? And who are the Keyberos again? I thought they were the ones who started the mess.

Actually, the Keyberos dynasty has ruled Edrad for about four hundred years. The 'mess' started when a Revolutionary Council deposed them. Qis'mos and the Artklan Empire originally marched on Edrad to put the Keyberos back on the throne. Ryraxos was a bronze dragon who initially helped Edrad defend itself, but then he spent about twenty-five years waging his own aggressive war across Qamis (pronounced Khah-miss). As for the moon, Ryraxos accepted exile to Secundus as part of the peace terms. He's formidable, but as a relatively young bronze dragon, he wasn't invincible.
So if the borders are back to normal and the dragon is gone, is everything just like it was thirty years ago?

On paper, yes, but the reality is much more fragile. The old Artklan Empire is now just the 'Artklan League,' which is a weak shadow of what it used to be. Many of its subjects are pushing for independence. New nations like Rathakos and Acrokos—where our campaign begins—were formed out of the old Red Mountain Republic after Edran occupation ended. Plus, there's the 'drow' situation. Artkos recruited them as mercenaries during the war, and now they've stayed on the surface in several enclaves. Most notably, they still occupy Qama, the holy city of Bahumat, and have even appointed their own high priest, which has caused a major religious schism.

Hi! I am totally new to D&D and I am looking at the notes for the Qaringil game. I saw something called the Gondolier Guild. It sounds like they just row boats, right? Is that a boring choice for a character?

Welcome to the game! In the city of Qaringil (which is the setting for the prequel campaign set 200 years before the 'present day'), the Gondolier Guild is actually one of the seven Great Guilds. While they do operate the gondolas that traverse the city's many canals, their responsibilities go much deeper than just rowing. They are essentially the keepers of the city's infrastructure and its connection to the rest of the continent.
Wait, what do boats have to do with the rest of the continent? I thought Qaringil was an island city.

Qaringil (remember, the Q is pronounced like a 'Kh') is indeed an island city, but it sits right in the middle of a wizard-made isthmus that crosses the Central Sea. This isthmus is connected by two enormous bridges. The Gondolier Guild is officially responsible for the maintenance and control of these bridges, including the Great Western Bridge. If you want to move goods or armies across that part of the world, you have to deal with them.
That sounds like they have a ton of political power. Are they run by one of those 'Five Families' I keep reading about?

That is the most interesting part: the Gondolier Guild has never been led by one of the major Five Families (like the Peixotas or the Vitellis). While those families control shipping, banking, and silk, the Gondoliers have remained a stronghold for the city's commoner classes and minor families. It makes them a very unique political player because the nobles can't easily boss them around without risking the city's main transit routes.
How do they actually stop a noble family from just buying them out or taking the bridges by force?

It comes down to specialized knowledge and the 'Gondolier' side of their work. They don't just row; they navigate a complex system of canals and tide-gates in a volcanic region. The bridges themselves are massive and require constant upkeep that the Guild has mastered over generations. If the Five Families tried to seize the bridges, the Gondolier Guild could effectively lock down the city's internal and external movement. Plus, since the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, is from a minor family himself, he tends to stay aligned with the guilds that aren't controlled by the Big Five.
You mentioned 'volcanic' and 'wizard-made.' Is the maintenance they do actually magical?

The notes mention the isthmus was made by wizards, so there is definitely a legacy of magic there. While the campaign doesn't detail every spell used, the Guild would need to be very comfortable working around the unique geography of a volcanic island. This is why the Gondolier Guild is closely linked with the city's physical stability. It's a great hook for a character—you aren't just a boatman, you're a guardian of the 'Great Western Bridge' and the literal path between the east and west.
Since we start at Level 5, could my character be a high-ranking member who guards the bridge instead of someone who rows? Does that fit the lore?

Absolutely. In Qaringil, fifth-level characters are considered experienced enough to be full guild members. You wouldn't be stuck rowing tourists around; you could be a bridge warden, an inspector of the supports, or a guild enforcer who ensures that the 'robber barons' from the other islands pay their tolls. You’d have the backing of one of the most powerful organizations in the city, but you'd also have the Five Families watching you closely since you represent a power they can't quite control.

Hi there! I'm starting to look into the lore for the Edrad region. I'm a bit of a linguistics nerd, so I was immediately struck by the spelling of places like Qeth. How do you actually pronounce that? Is there a specific phonetic rule for the Q in this setting?

In the world of Qaia, the letter Q is always pronounced as a 'Kh' sound. This comes from Old Draconic, which is also sometimes called Old Qis. So, Qeth is pronounced 'Kheth' and the region of Qis is pronounced 'Khis.' That 'q without a u' spelling you noticed is a hallmark of Old Draconic, which remains the language of scholarly publications, treaties, and ancient place names throughout the continent. Even in Edrad, which is the most politically dominant nation in the Qamis era, these linguistic roots are everywhere.

That's cool—it gives it a very distinct flavor. Since there are no 'racial' languages like Elvish or Dwarvish, does the half-elven nobility in Edrad just speak a specific dialect of Edran? I'm curious if their elven heritage influences the language at all or if it's purely national.

It is purely national. In Edrad, the nobility is half-elven—by law they must be between one-quarter and three-quarters elf—but they speak the prestige dialect of Edran used in the capital city, Jia. There is no separate 'Elvish' tongue. Edran itself isn't standardized, so while the ruling class in Jia speaks one way, someone from a city like Etops or Qeth might speak a regional dialect that's quite different. If you were playing a character from the capital, you'd likely know that prestige version plus one regional variation. Old Draconic is treated more like a classical language for the academy and diplomacy rather than a daily spoken tongue for the common folk.

So if I'm looking at the history of Edrad, they've had the same dynasty for quite a while, right? The Keyberos? Did the linguistic landscape change much during that recent thirty-year war with Ryraxos?

The Keyberos dynasty has actually been on the throne for about four hundred years in the current Qamis era, though they did have a brief interregnum. First, the Revolutionary Council deposed them, and then the dragon Ryraxos took over as an 'overlord' before his eventually exile to the second moon, Secundus. While thirty years of war changed the borders and the political stability, the language remained fairly consistent. However, the notes mention that even though the war is over and the 'old ways' are officially restored by edict, enforcing those old cultural and linguistic norms is proving difficult for the restored Keyberos monarchy. The nation is still recovering from the influence of both the revolution and the dragon's reign.

I just read the bit about Ryraxos being an artillery officer because dragons can carry the heavy guns... that is absolutely the COOLEST take on dragons I have ever seen! I am planning a one-shot for the Qaringil prequel era and I really want to feature him. How does he get his start in that period?

It is such a fun, grounded image, right? A dragon literally hauling cannons into position! However, I have to be a bit of a chronomancer here—if you are running in the Qaringil prequel era, Ryraxos actually hasn't been born yet. The notes say he is only about sixty years old in the present-day Qamis setting. Since Qaringil takes place 200 years earlier, he is still more than a century away from existing. His rise to power and his time as an artillery commander are all tied to the thirty-year Ryraxian War that just ended in the present day.

Oh dang, I totally whiffed on the dates! So he is a relatively 'young' dragon then? Tell me more about how a bronze dragon ended up as an aggressive 'overlord' who gets banished to the moon. Usually bronzes are the good guys!

Exactly! In Qamis (pronounced 'Khamis'), he is formidable but not some ancient, untouchable god. He is a bronze dragon who can shape-shift, and his rise was actually quite practical. He initially saved Edrad from a certain defeat against the Artklan Empire and Qis'mos. He then spent the next twenty-five years waging aggressive war across the continent. While he is called a 'dragon overlord,' the notes mention he's not actually more impressive than a high-level adventuring party or a coordinated artillery battery. He eventually accepted exile to Secundus, the second moon, which is a dark, iron-rich retrograde moon. If you wanted to use a dragon in your Qaringil one-shot, you might look toward the Forthyr region in the northeast—they are ruled by the Dragoncaller dynasty, though their dragonborn bloodlines are thinning out in that era!

hi!! im so excited for this campaign!! im playing a drow who decided to stay on the surface after the war ended, and i saw that my people actually hold Qama now?? thats so cool! can you tell me why we are there and how we managed to take over the holy city of Bahumat of all places??

That is an incredible background for a character! It is such a unique part of the current Qamis setting. So, basically, during the late stages of the thirty-year Ryraxian War, the nation of Artkos was getting pretty desperate. In a desperation move, they actually recruited drow as allies to help them on the surface. When the war finally ended, the drow did not just disappear - they stayed in about a dozen scattered enclaves across the continent, and Qama (pronounced Khama) is the most famous one!
wait so we were invited in?? that makes it even better lol. i love the idea of my drow being a former mercenary or something. and what about the church? i read something about a drow high priest??

You nailed it! It was not a hostile invasion from below; it was a strategic alliance that turned into a permanent stay. And yes, the situation in Qama is a huge deal for the Church of Bahamut. Your people have not despoiled the city, but they did appoint their own High Priest, Qe'lth IV. Since Qama is the traditional holy seat of the faith, this caused a massive schism. There is another candidate, Qachu II, claiming to be the rightful leader from the city of Qis, but Qe'lth IV is the one sitting in the holy city with all the actual relics of the office! It is a total power-play that has left the religious landscape of Qis'mos in a really interesting state.
thats amazing! i definitely want my character to have some ties to the Qama faction then. it sounds like the drow are a major political force now rather than just being hidden away.

Absolutely! Instead of being a hidden threat, you are looking at a world where drow are established in surface enclaves and literally running one of the most important religious sites in the world. Being from Qama means your character comes from the heart of that new power. Just remember that while Qama is the big one, there are still those other enclaves scattered around, so drow are a visible part of life across Qamis now, even if the locals in places like Edrad or Rathakos are still adjusting to the fact that everything has changed since Ryraxos was exiled.
I'm looking into the theology of the Church of Bahamut for my character. How many moons are there exactly, and do they have a specific order in the religious calendar?

In the world of Qaia, there are seven moons in total, though they are usually referred to by their numerical names. In order of their distance from the planet (closest to farthest), they are: Primus, Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus. There is also a trace particle called Quintus located at the Sun-Qaia L4 point, though it is effectively massless and doesn't appear as a disc in the sky. 

From a religious and structural perspective, Tertius and Quartus are the most significant for timekeeping. Tertius has an 8.15-day period and is the basis for the Qaian week, while Quartus has a 29.5-day period and serves as the natural monthly calendar body. Primus is unique because it is geostationary—it stays fixed over one spot on the equator and never rises or sets for those who can see it. 

In the present day of Qamis, Secundus has taken on a dark political and perhaps spiritual significance, as it is the moon where the dragon Ryraxos was exiled after the thirty-year war.

That exile on Secundus is interesting. Since the Church of Bahamut is centered in Qama (or was, before the drow took it), how do the different factions feel about the moons? Is there a difference between how Qe'lth IV and Qachu II interpret these alignments?

The notes don't explicitly detail specific theological disagreements regarding the moons between the two rival high priests, but the physical reality of the moons likely influences their claims. 

Qe'lth IV, the drow-appointed high priest in the holy city of Qama, holds the traditional seat and the relics of office. Qama is a city of Bahamut, and the drow allies of Artkos who currently occupy it have kept the institutions running under their own leadership. Meanwhile, Qachu II operates from the city of Qis, lacking the relics but claiming legitimacy.

One thing they would both track is the 'Triple-moon alignment.' Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus align periodically. We are currently moving through distinct cycles. For example, from 2255 to 2258, Qaia was in a 'new-moon era' where triple-new moon alignments were frequent. Starting in 2259, the world entered a 'full-moon era.' A major event in the church's calendar might be May 24, 2262, which is predicted to be the closest triple-new alignment of the decade, where all three inner moons are within 0.1% of syzygy.

A triple-new moon sounds like an omen. If I'm playing a character following Qachu II in Qis, would I use Primus for navigation while traveling to reclaim Qama? How does that work if Qama is the holy center?

Primus (pronounced 'Pree-mus') is actually the ultimate tool for a navigator. Because it is geostationary, its sub-satellite point is fixed at 0 degrees latitude and 0 degrees longitude—essentially defining the prime meridian of Qaia. 

If you are traveling toward Qama, you can find your longitude simply by measuring the elevation of Primus in the sky and comparing it to your latitude (found by the pole star). You don't even need a chronometer. This gives the 'near-side' hemisphere, where Qamis is located, a massive navigational advantage. 

However, there is a catch: Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you travel too far to the 'far-side,' Primus permanently disappears below the horizon. As long as you are in the heartland of Qis'mos or near the holy city of Qama, Primus will be a motionless, fixed point in your sky—a permanent reminder of the center of the world, which might carry significant weight for a follower of Qachu II seeking to restore the 'proper' order of the Church.

I'm thinking about running a one-shot set in the Qaringil prequel era and I'm workshopping a PC who is a high-society navigator for the Peixota family. To get the local flavor right, I need to know about the night sky. What are the seven moons of Qaia in order?

In the Qaringil era, about 200 years before the Ryraxian War ends, the sky is dominated by these seven bodies. In order of distance from the planet Qaia (pronounced 'Khaia'), they are: 1. Primus, a magically anchored geostationary moon that never rises or sets. 2. Secundus, a dark, iron-rich moon that orbits retrograde, rising in the west. 3. Tertius, which provides the basis for the eight-day week. 4. Quartus, the largest and brightest, which tracks the monthly cycle. 5. Quintus, which is actually a massless trace particle at the Sun-Qaia L4 point and isn't a visible disc like the others. 6. Sextus, a smaller retrograde moon. 7. Septimus, the outermost moon, also retrograde, used for long-term calendar tracking. For a Peixota navigator, Primus is your most important tool, as it acts as a fixed longitudinal reference in the sky.

That geostationary one, Primus, sounds like a total cheat code for a sailor. If my character is working for the Shipping Guild in Qaringil, how would they actually use it? And which of the Five Families would be most annoyed by my family's navigational edge?

It is a massive advantage. While navigators on the far side of the planet have to rely on dead reckoning or complex lunar-distance methods using Secundus or Quartus, you just need a good cross-staff or astrolabe. You measure the elevation of Primus above the horizon and use the 'primsine' function—a permanent table lookup—to find your longitude. Combined with the pole star for latitude, you have a precise fix. This technical superiority is likely a point of pride for the Peixota family. The Vitelli and Volmer families, who both have heavy interests in shipping, would certainly be the most competitive with you. However, the Qanto family might be the most 'annoyed' in general right now; they recently lost leadership of the Mercantile Guild to the gnomish Tiddwar family, so they are likely sensitive about any other family, like yours, flaunting their guild dominance.

Hey! I missed a bit of the lore discussion last night. I'm looking at maybe running a one-shot in the Qaringil era, but I keep seeing stuff about Ryraxos. Who is he exactly, and how did he come to power? Is he a big player in the prequel timeline?

To clarify the timeline: Ryraxos is not in the Qaringil prequel. That setting is roughly 200 years before his time. Ryraxos is the central figure of the 'present-day' Qamis era, which follows the aftermath of his reign. In the Qaringil era, the Holy Qis Empire still rules from Ath'r and the political map is very different.

As for who he is: Ryraxos is a bronze dragon. Contrary to what you might expect from a 'dragon overlord,' he isn't an ancient, god-like entity. He's relatively young, perhaps sixty years old. His rise to power was actually quite modern and tactical. He started as an artillery commander—dragons are highly valued in that role because they can shape-shift and help transport heavy guns. 

He came to prominence during the thirty-year Ryraxian War. Originally, he delivered the nation of Edrad from a certain defeat. However, he then spent the next two and a half decades waging aggressive war across the continent. By the start of the Qamis campaign, he has been defeated and exiled to Secundus, Qaia's second moon.
Wait, so he's literally stuck on the moon now? That's wild. If I run the Qaringil one-shot, what's the equivalent 'big threat' or power structure I should know about instead of him?

Exactly, he accepted exile to Secundus as part of his defeat. Since he's on the moon, he has no role in the current Qamis campaign. 

For a Qaringil one-shot, the power structure is much more about the Holy Qis Empire and the city-state of Qaringil itself. Instead of a dragon overlord, you're looking at:

1. The Holy Qis Empire: They rule central Qamis from the city of Ath'r. It's described as 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire,' but they are the dominant mainland force.
2. Qaringil: The campaign is based in this island city-state. It's a republic of canals and merchant fleets ruled by an Exarch (currently Giovanni Pesaro, who is near death) and the 'Five Families' (Peixota, Manos, Qanto, Volmer, and Vitelli).
3. Dragoncaller Shanlong I: In the nation of Forthyr to the northeast, he is the first of a line of sorcerer-kings. 

The 'threats' in Qaringil are more about political maneuvering among the Great Guilds (Shipping, Banking, Silk, etc.) and the instability of the Empire, rather than the continental conquest seen later with Ryraxos.
YO!!! I'm getting ready for my first D&D game in the Qamis setting. I want to play someone who worships the Dragon Emperors. Is it true that Ryraxos is the current Emperor in that city Qaringil?

Welcome to D&D! That sounds like a cool character concept, but we should clear up some of those names first so you have the right era. In the present-day Qamis campaign, the Dragon Emperors are actually ancient figures who haven't ruled for a long time. There is a religious sect that wants them to return, but they are based in the northeast nation of Forthyr, not Qaringil. Qaringil is a city from a prequel campaign set about 200 years before your game starts. Also, Ryraxos isn't one of the ancient Dragon Emperors—he was a bronze dragon who acted as a dragon overlord during the recent thirty-year war, and he's actually been exiled to Secundus, our second moon.
Oh, so I'm 200 years too late for Qaringil? My bad. So if I want to be one of these guys who likes the old dragons, I should be from Forthyr? And is that where that Shanlong guy is?

Exactly. Forthyr is in the northeast and is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. He is a sorcerer-king from a dynasty that used to be dragonborn before the bloodlines thinned out. The sect that calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors gets some quiet encouragement from him, even if he doesn't officially lead them. If you want to play a character with those beliefs, Forthyr is the perfect homeland for you. Just remember that the rest of the continent might be a bit suspicious of those views, especially after the war with Ryraxos.
Got it. One last thing, is Qis'mos the same place as Forthyr? I saw something about it having dragon institutions too. And how do you even say that name?

They are different places. While Forthyr is in the northeast, Qis'mos is in the heartland of the continent. Qis'mos is where the institutions of the old Draconic empire actually lasted the longest after the dragons left. It's now governed by a Potentate elected by nobles and guilds. As for the name, the letter Q is always pronounced as a Kh sound in this world. So Qis'mos is pronounced Khis-mos, and Qamis is Kh-amis. It comes from the Old Draconic language!

Greetings. I am preparing a character for the Qaringil campaign. I am from Forthyr and belong to the sect that calls for the return of the Dragon Emperors. Since we are starting in the city of Qaringil, what is the linguistic situation there? Will my Forthrian tongue suffice?

In the city-state of Qaringil, you will primarily hear Islas. However, it is a very specific regional dialect. Because the city was originally constructed by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, the version of Islas spoken there has a much heavier Qis vocabulary than you would find in other island states. As for your Forthrian, it is the national language of your homeland, but it is quite distinct from the Islas of the central sea. One thing to remember across all regions is that Q is always pronounced as a Kh sound, a remnant of Old Draconic.

That makes sense. My character is quite devoted to the old ways of the Dragon Emperors, so I probably spend a lot of time with ancient scrolls. Is the Qis-heavy dialect in Qaringil related to the language of those scrolls, or is it more of a commoner's slang that has drifted away from the old draconic roots?

It is more of the latter. The language of your religious scrolls and scholarly treaties is Old Draconic, also known as Old Qis. While the Qis language spoken in Qaringil and Qis'mos is the closest modern relative to that ancient tongue, it has evolved significantly into a prestige dialect for the ruling class and a common tongue for the people. The dialect in Qaringil specifically is a hybrid of the nautical Islas tongue and those Qis influences. You might recognize the root words for certain draconic concepts, but the grammar and daily usage in the canals of Qaringil will feel very different from the formal language of the court of Dragoncaller Shanlong I in Forthyr or your sect's holy texts. Most inhabitants of Qaringil are commercial elites, not religious scholars of the draconic past.

Hello! I have been diving into the Qamis campaign notes and I am absolutely obsessed with the linguistics of this setting. Specifically, the note that Q is pronounced like Kh from Old Draconic! It adds such a distinct texture to place names like Qama or Qaringil. But I am curious—the notes mention that someone from the capital of Artkos would have real trouble understanding an Artklan speaker from Rathakos. If they share a national language, why are the regional variations so extreme? Is it a lack of standardization or something deeper?

It is great to see someone else excited about the phonology of Qaia! You hit the nail on the head regarding the Q—that Kh sound is a direct legacy of Old Draconic, or Old Qis. It acts as the scholarly high language for treaties and academia, which is why you see that q without u spelling preserved in official maps and place names. As for the regional variations, you are looking at a total lack of linguistic standardization. In the current Qamis era, these nations are often sprawling or have recently shifted borders. For example, Rathakos only just became its own nation after the Ryraxian War ended. Before that, it was part of the Red Mountain Republic and under Edran occupation. When you combine that kind of political upheaval with the rugged, isolated terrain of the Red Mountains, the local dialects drift away from the prestige Artklan spoken in the capital of Ath'r very quickly.

That makes total sense! If Rathakos was occupied by Edrad for a while, I bet their version of Artklan has all sorts of Edran loanwords or structural shifts that the High King's court in Ath'r wouldn't recognize. And since there is no standard version being taught in schools, the prestige dialect stays locked in the capital. Does this same fragmentation happen in the Qaringil prequel era, or was the Holy Qis Empire better at keeping the language unified back then?

Even 200 years ago in the Qaringil era, unity was more of a suggestion than a reality. The notes describe the Holy Qis Empire as neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire. While Artklan was the common tongue of the central regions, it was already splintering. In the city-state of Qaringil itself, they speak Islas, but because the city was built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos, their version of Islas is heavily flavored with Qis vocabulary. It is a linguistic melting pot. The DM has even left blank spaces on the map for players to define their own regional dialects in their backstories. So, if you were a linguist in Qamis, you would find that while the high Old Draconic remains stable for scholars and diplomatic treaties, the common national languages are a wild, evolving mess shaped by local history and geography.

HELLOOO! I am building my character for the Qaringil game. I am playing a member of the Gondolier guild who helps maintain the Great Western Bridge. I was reading about the navigation stuff and I am confused. If my brother moved to Rathakos to get away from Ryraxos's taxes, why can't he just look at Primus to find his way home? My DM said the 'far-side' people are basically lost without a clock, but it is a moon, right? It should eventually rise for them.

It sounds like you have a few details from the two different campaign eras mixed together! In the Qaringil prequel era (where you are playing a Gondolier), the nation of Rathakos and the dragon Ryraxos do not exist yet. Ryraxos does not appear until about 200 years later in the Qamis present day, and Rathakos is formed even later than that from the old Red Mountain Republic. In your time, those mountains are part of the Holy Qis Empire.

Regarding Primus: the reason your brother (who might be in the Holy Qis Empire instead of Rathakos) or anyone on the far side of the planet cannot use it is because Primus is geostationary. Unlike the other moons like Secundus or Quartus, Primus is 'magically anchored' and its orbital period exactly matches the planet Qaia's rotation. 

This means Primus hangs over one single spot on the equator (the prime meridian) forever. It never rises and it never sets. If you are in Qaringil, you can see it because you are in the near-side hemisphere. But if you travel to the far-side hemisphere, Primus is permanently below the horizon. It will never 'eventually rise' for them because it is stationary relative to the ground. That is why far-side navigation is so much harder—they have to rely on dead reckoning or complex lunar-distance math because they lack that fixed reference point in the sky.

Oh man, I totally forgot the dragon is a future problem. My bad! Okay, so I am in the Holy Qis Empire era. Since I am a Gondolier in Qaringil, I probably use Primus all the time to keep my bearings on the bridges, right? If it is a fixed point on the equator, does it just look like a star that doesn't move?

Exactly. For a Gondolier in Qaringil, Primus is a permanent fixture in your sky. However, it looks a bit more substantial than a star. According to the celestial notes, Primus has an apparent diameter of 10.4 arcminutes, which is about one-third the size of Earth's Moon. It is a dark, iron-rich moon, so it isn't very bright (magnitude -9.62), but it is definitely a visible disc.

Navigators like your character use it for the 'Elevation Method.' Since it stays exactly above the point where the equator meets the prime meridian (0 degrees Latitude, 0 degrees Longitude), you can measure how high it is above your horizon to help calculate your position. Because Qaringil is a city of canals and bridges, having a moon that stays perfectly still is incredibly useful for surveying and nautical fixes. If you ever sailed far enough away to the other side of Qaia, you would watch Primus slowly sink toward the horizon and eventually vanish forever, leaving you to navigate by the stars alone.

heyyoo! I am building a character for a Qaringil prequel campaign. I was reading about the 'Southern Reaches' and how they are a total mess of unrest and mountain cults. I want my character to be a mercenary in the city of Qaringil whose family fled that instability. Does that work for a backstory?

Welcome to the setting! To make your backstory fit perfectly, we need to clarify the timeline. The 'Southern Reaches' and the nation of Rathakos actually do not exist yet in the Qaringil era—that is a development roughly 200 years in the future. In your current timeframe, that region is the southern portion of the Red Mountains. It is still a 'mess,' but for different reasons: it is technically claimed by the Holy Qis Empire (ruling from Ath'r), but they have almost no effective administration there. It is inhabited by various non-state peoples who resist the Empire's reach. Does your family's history involve resisting the tax collectors of Ath'r, or perhaps the expansionist pushes from Edrad to the west?

Oh, my bad on the timeline. Let's go with the Red Mountains then. Why exactly is it so unstable if the Holy Qis Empire is supposed to be 'Holy' and an 'Empire'? You mentioned they claim it but can't rule it—is it just the terrain?

The terrain of the Red Mountains is certainly dangerous and underexplored, but the political instability is deeper. In this era, the Holy Qis Empire is famously described as being 'neither holy, nor Qis, nor an empire.' It is a decentralized elective monarchy. While the Emperor sits in Ath'r, they are constantly in a 'cold conflict' with free river cities like Lock over ancient prerogatives. Because the central government is so weak, the mountain regions have become a lawless buffer zone where Edrad (under the Keyberos dynasty) is trying to expand its royal control. Your family might have been caught between the Empire's neglect and Edrad's hunger for new territory.

That sounds perfect. If my character is now a mercenary in Qaringil, how do the 'Five Families' or the Great Guilds in the city-state look at the chaos in the mountains? Is there money to be made from the instability?

Absolutely. In Qaringil, the elites are commercial rather than landowners, so they view continental instability through the lens of trade and profit. The Vitelli family (sigil: Moth), who lead the Silk Guild, and the Manos family (sigil: Hand), who lead the Judicial Guild but are aggressively entering the wool trade, are very interested in those mountain passes. Instability in the Red Mountains disrupts the flow of raw materials from the interior to the coast. The 'thassolocracy' of Qaringil often sells its powerful mercenary navies or specialized land forces to continental powers like Edrad to 'escort' trade or protect their interests in these disputed borderlands.
So if the Manos family is trying to get into the wool trade, would they hire a mercenary like me to protect their interests against these 'non-state' mountain groups or maybe even Empire tax agents?

That is a very strong hook. The Manos family's entry into the wool scene is described as 'hotly contested.' They would certainly value a mercenary who has local knowledge of the Red Mountain trails. You could be working to protect their supply lines from mountain 'bandits'—who might actually just be locals refusing to recognize the Empire's claim—or guarding against the rival Qanto family (sigil: Dove), who recently lost leadership of the Mercantile Guild and might want to see the Manos family's new venture fail. Just remember that in Qaringil, the Gondolier Guild controls the bridges and the city's canals, so any wool you bring back from the mountains has to pass through their checkpoints too.

I like the idea of being a 'local expert' for the Manos family. You mentioned the Qis language is the language of the academy—would my character have grown up speaking that, or is there a specific mountain dialect from the Red Mountains?

In the Qaringil era, there isn't a single 'mountain language,' but the Qis language is a geographic and cultural staple in that region. However, since you are in the city of Qaringil now, the primary language is Islas. The Qaringil dialect of Islas has a heavier-than-usual Qis vocabulary because the city was originally built by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos. For your starting languages, you would likely speak the 'prestige' Islas of the Qaringil capital and perhaps a regional Red Mountain dialect of Qis. If you take a bonus language, you might choose Old Draconic (Old Qis) if your family has any scholarly or diplomatic ties, as that is the 'q without u' language used in treaties and ancient maps of the mountains.

One last thing—since I'm fifth level, am I already a member of the Adventurer Guild in Qaringil? And how does the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro, feel about mercenaries like me working for the Five Families instead of the city itself?

Yes, at fifth level you can easily be a full member of the Adventurer Guild. Regarding the politics: Exarch Giovanni Pesaro has held his post for 19 years and is currently 'on death's doorstep.' He is generally aligned with the Peixota and Manos families, so he likely views mercenaries working for the Manos as a stabilizing force for the city's economy. However, because Qaringil is an 'incomprehensible republic,' there are always competing factions. While you are protecting wool shipments in the Red Mountains, keep an eye out for the underground criminal organizations like the 'Cutpurses' or 'Bandits' who style themselves as guilds and might have their own eyes on the Manos family's coin.
Hey! I am brand new to D&D and joining the Qamis game. My DM said my character could be from a city called Kard. What is it like there? Is it a cool place for a first-timer?

Welcome to the game! Kard is a great choice for a new player because it is right in the middle of a lot of political drama. Geographically, it is a major financial and shipping center on the coast. It is actually part of Artkos (one of the big empires), but it is an enclave, meaning it is a little patch of Artkos territory separated from the main country.

The 'vibe' is pretty tense right now. Even though it is ruled by Artkos, most of the people who live there actually speak Edran. About 28 years ago, during the big thirty-year war that just ended, Edrad invaded Kard, and the local people actually welcomed them with open arms. Now that the war is over and the old borders have been restored, the Artkos government is back in charge, but the people living there are not exactly happy about it. It makes the whole city feel a bit like a powder keg.

Oh wow, so they liked the invaders more than their own kings? That is awkward. Why is the government 'fragile' exactly? Like, are there riots in the streets or is it more of a 'spies in the shadows' type of thing? I am thinking of playing a Rogue.

It is definitely more of the 'spies and secrets' variety, which fits a Rogue perfectly. The notes describe the governance as fragile because it is hard for the High King of Artkos, Ezevir Arth, to enforce his rules there. 

Since the city is a banking and shipping hub, there is a lot of money moving through it, but the loyalties are split. You have the official Artkos governors trying to re-establish control, while the wealthy merchant families and the commoners might still have ties to Edrad. There is a lot of 'malicious compliance' where people follow the letter of the law but secretly root for the other side. If you are a Rogue, you might be working for a merchant family that is trying to dodge Artkos taxes or even someone helping Edran loyalists stay in touch with the capital city of Jia.
That sounds awesome. Is there a specific language I should know? My DM said I get a regional dialect too. Also, how do you say the name of the place? Is it 'Kard' like a playing card?

You pronounce it exactly like 'card.' For your languages, since you are from Kard, you would definitely speak Artklan (the national language of your rulers) and Edran (the language most of your neighbors and family probably speak). 

Because Kard is a shipping center, you might even know a bit of 'Islas,' which is the language spoken by the sailors and 'robber barons' on the islands in the Central Sea. One quick tip for this world: whenever you see a 'Q' in a name, like Qamis or Qis, it is pronounced like a 'Kh' sound. Kard is straightforward, but if you start traveling toward the holy city of Qama (pronounced Khama), that rule will come in handy!
I am considering running a one-shot in the Qaringil prequel era and I am looking for a strong hook. What exactly is the Great Western Bridge, and why is it so vital to the city state of Qaringil?

The Great Western Bridge is one of two massive spans on a wizard-made isthmus that crosses the northern Central Sea. The city of Qaringil sits on the western shore of a volcanic island positioned right between these two bridges, and its heavy fortifications directly dominate the Great Western Bridge. This makes the city the literal gatekeeper for overland travel and trade across the sea. In terms of power, the bridge is managed by the Gondolier Guild, which is unique because it is one of the few 'Great Guilds' that has never been led by one of the five major merchant families. Controlling the bridge means controlling the flow of merchant fleets and the maintenance of the only stable land route in the region, making it the linchpin of Qaringil's military and economic security roughly 200 years before the events of the Ryraxian War.

I am making a character from Forthyr who follows that religious sect looking for the return of the Dragon Emperors. Since the campaign is starting in Rathakos, I was wondering if the Holy Qis Empire has any influence over in Westron? I want my character to have some secrets from the western continent.

It sounds like there might be a bit of a timeline mix-up in your character planning. Rathakos is a nation in the current Qamis era, appearing after the thirty-year Ryraxian War. The Holy Qis Empire, however, is part of the Qaringil prequel era, which is set roughly 200 years earlier. In the present day of Qamis, the Holy Qis Empire no longer exists; instead, the Artklan League and the restored Keyberos dynasty in Edrad are the primary players in that central region.

As for Westron, it is currently a blank space in the campaign notes. The DM describes it as a very large, exotic continent to the west that is important but lacks specific details like nation names or history. One key detail is that the people there are mostly not worshippers of Bahumat (the god whose holy city is Qama). This lack of defined lore means you have a great opportunity to work with the DM to invent the secrets or monastery ties your character might have there.

Right, okay, sticking to the Qamis present then. If Westron is mostly empty lore-wise, what about the rumors that the dragons went there? My Forthrian monk is really interested in where they disappeared to.

According to the campaign notes, the rumors about where the dragons went are actually centered on the big, mostly unexplored island to the south, rather than Westron. There is a city of exiles and pirates there called Freeport, and many believe the dragons are hiding in the dense jungle beyond the city limits.

In the Qamis present, the dragon Ryraxos (who is Bronze and was an artillery commander) has been exiled to the second moon, Secundus, after his defeat. While your sect in Forthyr—which is currently ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI—hopes for the return of the old Dragon Emperors, there is no official note connecting them to Westron yet. If you want your monk to believe they went west, you should discuss that with your DM as a personal character theory or a piece of local Forthrian folklore.

Okay, check this out! I am sitting here with a compass and a draft of the primsine table, trying to calculate my longitude from an elevation of 45 degrees. Primsine(45) is 0.837, right? If my latitude is 30 degrees North... let me see... 0.837 / 0.866 is about 0.966... that puts me at roughly 15 degrees East of the prime meridian! This system is incredible. Anyway, I am rolling up a character for the Qaringil prequel era and I saw that they speak Islas there, but with a twist. What is the specific dialect like in the city of canals and why does it sound different from the standard seafaring version?

Your math is spot on! Primsine(45) is indeed 0.837, and that calculation gives you a perfect fix. It is a brilliant system because Primus is geostationary, making that 0 longitude point a permanent physical reality. As for Qaringil, you are looking at a very specific regional variety of the Islas language. While Islas is generally the language of the nautical robber barons and the island thassolocracies, Qaringil is unique because it was originally built primarily by adventurers and laborers from Qis'mos. Because of that history, the Islas spoken in Qaringil has a much heavier Qis vocabulary than you would hear in the rest of the Central Sea. It is effectively a maritime trade tongue flavored by the scholarly and institutional weight of the Qis heartland.

That is so cool! So it is like a high-brow merchant speak? Since Qis is the language of the academy, having all those loanwords must make the Qaringil republic sound really sophisticated. Does the pronunciation rule for 'Q' still apply even in this mixed Islas dialect? If I am talking to a member of the Gondolier guild, am I saying 'Kharingil'?

Exactly! The rule that Q is pronounced 'Kh' comes from Old Draconic (or Old Qis), and that carries over into the city's name and its Qis-influenced dialect. So yes, you would definitely say 'Kharingil'. This linguistic flair reflects the city's complex social structure—even though the elites like the Peixota or Vitelli families are primarily commercial rather than land-owning, they live in a city criss-crossed by canals and dominated by the Great Western Bridge. That Qis influence in their speech is a constant reminder of the city's origins. Just remember that while you might speak the prestige dialect of the capital, someone in the Gondolier guild might have a bit more of a local 'canal-rat' edge to their Kharingil-Islas compared to a banker from the Volmer family!

Hey. My DM is starting a Qamis campaign and told me I should look into the navigation rules. Honestly, it feels like I am being asked to take an astrophysics exam just to play a rogue. Why is the moon system so unnecessarily complicated?

It can definitely look daunting at first glance, but the complexity is actually there to solve a problem that took Earth centuries to figure out: finding your longitude at sea. In most fantasy settings, you just hand-wave your location. In Qamis (pronounced Khamis), the presence of Primus—the geostationary moon—means you can find your exact position with just a basic tool like an astrolabe and a lookup table. You do not even need a clock. It is essentially a natural GPS satellite that the DM has baked into the physics of the world.

A 'natural GPS' that requires me to solve something called a primsine function? That still sounds like a chore. If I am sitting at the table and my character is at 30 degrees North latitude and looks up to see Primus at an elevation of 42.2 degrees, how do I actually turn that into a location without a calculator?

In-character, you would just use a precomputed table in a nautical almanac, but let us walk through the logic so you see it is just a few steps. First, you get your latitude (30 degrees N) from the pole star—that is the easy part. Then you measure Primus. Because Primus is always exactly over the intersection of the equator and the prime meridian (0,0), its elevation tells you how far you are from that point. For an elevation of 42.2 degrees, you find the primsine value. The formula uses a constant called rho (0.1511), which is just the ratio of the planet's radius to the moon's orbit. For 42.2 degrees, the primsine result is approximately 0.7504.
Okay, so I have 0.7504. Now what? Do I have to do long division at the bottom of a dungeon?

Not quite! You just divide that primsine value by the cosine of your latitude. So: 0.7504 divided by the cosine of 30 degrees. The cosine of 30 is 0.8660. When you divide 0.7504 by 0.8660, you get 0.8666. Now you just look at your standard cosine table backwards—find 0.8666 in the result column, and it tells you the angle is 30 degrees. Since Primus is stationary, you just check if it is to your east or west. If it is to the west, you are at 30 degrees East longitude. You are at (30N, 30E). It is a three-step lookup: table, simple division, table.

That is still more math than I usually want with my d20 rolls. And what happens if the party travels to the other side of the world? Does the whole 'natural GPS' just stop working because we cannot see the magic moon anymore?

Exactly. Primus is geostationary, meaning it stays fixed over one spot on the equator. If you travel more than about 81 degrees away from the prime meridian, Primus sinks below the horizon and stays there. This creates a massive technological divide on Qaia. Navigators on the near-side have perfect longitude, making trade and naval warfare much easier. On the far-side, they are stuck with the 'Earth problem'—using dead reckoning or complicated lunar-distance observations with the other moons like Secundus or Quartus. It makes the far-side of the planet feel much more isolated and dangerous because you can actually get lost there.

So it is a lore-based reason for why some cultures are better at sailing than others. Fine. But what about the other moons? If there are six or seven of them, why does only Primus get the special math? Is it because it was 'magically anchored' like the notes say?

Primus is the only one that is geostationary, which is why the math is fixed. The others—Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus—all move across the sky at different speeds and even different directions. Secundus is retrograde, so it rises in the west, while Tertius and Quartus rise in the east. Trying to navigate by them requires tracking the time and their specific phases, which is why the 'primsine' method is so prized. The notes suggest Primus and Secundus might be fragments of the same iron-rich body, but only Primus was anchored in place, likely by dragon magic in the distant past, specifically to serve as that permanent reference point for the world.

Hello. I have been looking over the celestial data for Qaia and I am extremely skeptical. You are telling me this planet has an oscillating tidal load 4.4 times that of Earth's Moon? Between Secundus, Tertius, and Quartus, the coastline should be a constant disaster zone, not to mention the 'magically anchored' Primus sitting there with a permanent 2cm bulge. It feels like a physics nightmare. Does the setting actually account for this, or is it just flavor text? Also, tell me about Forthyr—does a 'backwards' nation in the northeast even have a concept of the orbital mechanics they are living under?

The tidal math is indeed intense. The notes confirm a total oscillating tidal force of approximately 4.4 times that of Earth's Moon, driven by three moons with different periods: Secundus (9.85h cycle), Tertius (13.69h), and Quartus (12.43h). When you add the solar tide, the complexity of the daily rhythm is immense. The notes do not describe a global cataclysm, but they do treat these physics as absolute ground truth for the world. Primus is the outlier—it is geostationary and magically anchored, meaning it produces no oscillating tide, only a permanent geographic offset of the sea level. As for Forthyr, it is a massive nation in the northeast of the continent of Qamis. In the present day, it is ruled by Dragoncaller Shanlong VI. The notes describe Forthyr as 'somewhat backwards' and dominated by a dynasty of sorcerer-kings whose dragonborn bloodlines have thinned over generations. While they might not be publishing papers on orbital resonance, they are highly focused on draconic legacy; there is even a prominent religious sect there calling for the return of the Dragon Emperors.

Dragoncallers? That sounds more like superstition than science. If they are in the northeast, can they even see Primus? If the prime meridian is 0 longitude, and Primus is fixed above it, a nation that far east might be near the visibility limit. If they cannot see the one 'fixed' point in the sky, their navigation must be terrible compared to the central powers like Edrad or the Artklan League.

You have a sharp eye for the geography. Primus is only visible within roughly 80 degrees of the prime meridian. If Forthyr's territory extends beyond that to the east, they would indeed lose sight of it and be unable to use the 'Elevation Method' for easy longitude fixes. Navigators on the far side of the planet have to rely on dead reckoning or much harder stellar methods, similar to Earth's history before the chronometer. In the Qaringil prequel era—about 200 years before the current Qamis campaign—the ruler was Shanlong I, who was half-dragonborn. By the time of Shanlong VI in the present day, the nation is still powerful but viewed as regressive by its neighbors. They likely rely more on their sorcerous traditions and the 'Dragoncaller' legacy than the advanced celestial navigation used by the thassolocracies in the Central Sea or the scholars in Jia.

Wait, you mentioned the dragon Ryraxos was exiled to the second moon. That is Secundus, the retrograde one, right? The iron-rich fragment with the dark albedo? If that thing is spiraling inward over geological time because it is retrograde, sending a 'dragon overlord' there seems like a temporary solution at best. Is there any detail on how Forthyr reacted to Ryraxos's exile, given their obsession with dragons?

Correct, Ryraxos was exiled to Secundus, which is a retrograde moon with a density of 6,689 kg/m3—nearly pure iron. It rises in the west and, as you noted, will eventually spiral inward, though the notes say the Roche margin is currently comfortable. In the Qamis present, the thirty-year Ryraxian War has just ended. While the notes do not specify a direct official statement from Forthyr regarding the exile, they do mention that Forthyr spent decades neglecting the affairs of the southwest and now find them impossible to ignore. The presence of a living dragon on the second moon likely emboldens the religious sects in Forthyr that want a return to the era of Dragon Emperors. It is worth noting that 'Q' is pronounced as 'Kh' in this setting, so Qamis is 'Khamis' and Qaringil is 'Kharingil,' derived from Old Draconic. Forthyr remains one of the largest 'blank spaces' on the map for the DM to fill, but its shift from isolation to being an unavoidable continental power is a major part of the post-war landscape.

I've heard these stories about the so-called Great Families of Qaringil, but honestly, it sounds like a bunch of island merchants playing at being lords. Back in Forthyr, we follow the Dragoncaller and wait for the true Emperors to return to the continent. Why should I care about some family with a dove on their shield—the Qantos, was it? I heard they can't even hold onto their own guild.

In the current era of Qaringil, your skepticism is shared by many who value the ancient draconic traditions of the northeast. The Qanto family has indeed seen better days. Until recently, they held the leadership of the Mercantile Guild, one of the seven Great Guilds that effectively run the city-state. However, they lost that position to the Tiddwar family, a minor gnomish house that is much more closely aligned with the current Exarch, Giovanni Pesaro. While the Qantos still have their hands in the Judicial and Banking guilds, their loss of the Mercantile leadership is a significant blow to their prestige.

A gnomish family took it just because they're friends with the Exarch? That confirms my suspicion—it's all just favors and backroom deals. If they're losing influence to gnomes and minor houses, are the Qantos even relevant anymore, or are they just a name on a banking ledger?

They are certainly in a defensive position. The Exarch, who has been in power for 19 years, tends to favor the Peixota and Manos families, leaving the Qantos on the outside of the inner circle. To a follower of the Dragon Emperor revival, their 'Dove' sigil might look particularly weak compared to the power you expect from a ruler. However, they remain one of the Five Families. They still hold significant influence in the Judicial Guild and are deeply embedded in the Banking Guild alongside the Volmer family. In Qaringil, being 'just a name on a banking ledger' often carries more weight than a crown does in other lands, though they are currently losing the political tug-of-war against the Exarch's favorites.

I suppose money has its own kind of power, even if it's not the fire of a Dragon Emperor. But what's the word on the street in Qaringil? Is there any talk of them trying to claw that leadership back, or are they just going to sit in their counting houses while the Exarch dies?

The timing is critical because Exarch Giovanni Pesaro is widely considered to be on death's doorstep. With the leadership of the Mercantile Guild now in the hands of the Tiddwars (the gnomish family aligned with the Exarch), the Qantos are likely waiting for the transition of power. Because Qaringil is a republic—albeit an incomprehensible one to outsiders—the death of an Exarch usually triggers a massive shift in guild alliances. The Qantos are likely using their remaining influence in the Judicial and Banking sectors to ensure they aren't sidelined further when the next Exarch is chosen. For someone from Forthyr, it looks like squabbling, but for the Qantos, it is a fight for survival.

Wassup. I'm used to the Sea of Fallen Stars or the Azure Sea where we just roll Survival or use a compass, but this Primus geostationary moon in your setting sounds like a literal cheat code for longitude. Are there any actual downsides or hard limits to using it for navigation?

It definitely simplifies things compared to Earth-style history, but there are several hard limits grounded in the physics of Qaia. The most significant is the visibility horizon: Primus is only visible from one hemisphere. If you sail or travel to the far-side hemisphere, the moon is permanently below the horizon, and you are forced to rely on much harder methods like dead reckoning or lunar-distance observations using Secundus or Quartus. Even on the near side, your range is limited; at the equator, Primus disappears once you are roughly 81 degrees away from the prime meridian, and this window narrows the further north or south you go. At 60 degrees latitude, you lose sight of it at about 72 degrees longitude. Finally, there is a precision drop-off. Near the edges of the visibility zone, Primus sits very low on the horizon where atmospheric refraction distorts the angle, making your longitude fix significantly less accurate than it would be if you were closer to the sub-Primus point at the center of the map.

Hey! My friend was telling me about your D&D world and I am totally hooked already. I love fantasy novels with industrial vibes and weird politics, so I have to know more about Lock! They said it has these 'free cities' within a kingdom? That sounds so cool, how does that even work?

Welcome to the world of Qaia! It is awesome to meet a fellow enthusiast. You have a great eye for detail because Lock is one of the most unique spots on the continent of Qamis. In the present day, Lock is indeed a kingdom, but it is famous for its burgeoning industrial presence. The 'free cities'—which include the city of Lock itself and a few other major hubs—operate with a special status where they maintain their own local governance and ancient rights despite being part of the larger kingdom. It creates this fantastic tension between the crown and the industrial powerhouses of the cities!
That is so cool! It reminds me of those trade leagues in historical fiction. Was it always like that, or did the industry make them powerful enough to demand freedom from the King?

That independent streak actually goes back centuries! If we look back to the Qaringil era—about 200 years before the current campaign—Lock and those other river cities were already established as free cities. Back then, they were in a 'cold conflict' with the Holy Qis Empire, which ruled from the city of Ath'r. The Emperor claimed they were subjects, but the cities refused to give up their ancient prerogatives. So, while the industrial boom is more of a recent development in the present-day Qamis era, the 'free city' identity is a point of pride they have defended for a long, long time.
A cold conflict with an Empire? I love that. Does the industry there involve magic, or is it more like steam and gears? My friend mentioned something about a gnomish family with a gears sigil too!

The notes specifically mention a burgeoning industrial presence, though the exact mix of magic and machinery is one of those areas the DM often explores through gameplay. However, you are spot on about the gnomes! In the Qaringil prequel era, the Tiddwar family is a very prominent gnomish family in the island city-state of Qaringil. Their sigil is literally 'Gears' and they lead the Mercantile Guild. Given the connections between the islands and the continent, it is easy to see how those interests in trade and mechanical craft might overlap with the rise of industry in places like Lock!

Hi. I am the lead dev for a SaaS team and just joined a new D&D group. I need an executive summary of the moon system in this setting, specifically the one that apparently never moves? My DM mentioned only half the world can see it.

Welcome to Qaia. To get you up to speed: the moon you are asking about is Primus. It is a geostationary satellite, meaning it is magically anchored in a fixed orbit directly above the planet's equator at the prime meridian (0 degrees latitude, 0 degrees longitude). Because its orbital period perfectly matches Qaia's rotation, it never rises or sets; it is a motionless point in the sky. Consequently, because the planet is a sphere, Primus is physically blocked by the horizon for the entire opposite hemisphere. If you are on the far side of the planet, Primus is permanently invisible.

Okay, from a systems perspective, a geostationary moon is basically a GPS satellite. Why is it only visible to half the world though? Is the visibility exactly 180 degrees, or is there some fall-off at the edges?

The visibility limit is a bit less than a full 180-degree hemisphere. Technically, the maximum geocentric angle from which you can see Primus is about 81.3 degrees from the sub-Primus point. At the equator, this means you can see it up to 81 degrees East or West of the prime meridian. As you move toward the north or south poles, this window narrows. For example, at 60 degrees latitude, you have to be within roughly 72 degrees of longitude to see it. Past those limits, the curve of the planet puts the moon below the horizon. Near those boundaries, the moon sits very low in the sky, making it harder to use for precise measurements due to atmospheric interference.

That is a clean bit of geometry. But how does a moon even end up like that? A geostationary orbit requires perfect placement. Is this a 'natural' phenomenon in the lore or is there some 'ancient tech' or magic involved?

It is not a natural coincidence. The notes explicitly state Primus is circular by construction and magically anchored. It is quite small, only about 64 km in radius, with a very high density similar to iron. Its most important 'engineered' feature is its 23.5 degree inclination to the ecliptic. Because this exactly matches Qaia's axial tilt, the two angles cancel out, keeping Primus perfectly in the equatorial plane. Without that specific magical alignment, the moon would appear to wobble north and south throughout the day rather than staying stationary.

Got it. So it is a literal anchor for the world. My DM mentioned sailors use it for something called the 'longitude problem.' How does a stationary moon help a ship figure out where it is on the X-axis?

On Earth, finding longitude required high-precision clocks. On the near-side of Qaia, you just need a protractor. Since Primus is fixed at (0,0), its elevation in your sky is a direct function of your distance from that point. A navigator finds their latitude first using the pole star. Then they measure the elevation of Primus. By plugging that elevation into a lookup table for the primsine function, they get a value that, when combined with their latitude, reveals their exact longitude. It turns a complex time-keeping problem into a simple trigonometry problem.
I am looking at the notes on the 'primsine' function now. Can you break down the actual logic of that calculation? I want to make sure I can explain the math if my character has to navigate a storm.

The math relies on the constant rho, which is the ratio of Qaia's radius to Primus's orbital radius (0.1511). The primsine(e) formula is: rho * cos squared of elevation + sin of elevation * sqrt(1 - rho squared * cos squared of elevation). Once you have that primsine value, the longitude (lambda) is found by the formula: cos(lambda) = primsine(elevation) / cos(latitude). In practical terms, your character would have a pre-printed table in a nautical almanac. They would look up the primsine of their measured elevation, divide it by the cosine of their latitude (found from the pole star), and the result is the cosine of their longitude.

That is a massive technological advantage for the near-side civilizations. Does this create a 'digital divide' between the nations that can see Primus and those that cannot? Who is actually stuck on the far side?

It creates a significant navigational asymmetry. Nations on the near-side, like Edrad with its capital Jia or the island city of Qaringil, have much safer and more efficient trade routes because they never get lost. The 'far-side' includes the continent of Westron and parts of the big southern island near Freeport. Navigators there have it much harder; they are stuck with 'dead reckoning' or the lunar-distance method using the other five moons (Secundus, Tertius, Quartus, Sextus, and Septimus). Those moons all move at different speeds and directions (some are even retrograde, rising in the west), making the math for far-side sailors significantly more complex and dangerous.

Hey there, I'm a DM for another table and I'm looking for some flavor inspiration for my own game. Can you tell me about the Vitelli family from your setting? I heard they have a moth as their sigil, which sounds cool.

The Vitelli family is a major power player in the island city-state of Qaringil. This specific lore comes from the Qaringil prequel campaign, which takes place roughly 200 years before the main Qamis timeline. In that era, the Vitelli are one of the 'Five Families' that effectively run the city's politics and economy. Their sigil is indeed a moth, and they are defined by their leadership of the Silk Guild, which is one of the seven Great Guilds of the city.
A moth is a unique choice. Is there a specific reason they use it, or is it just for show? Does it tie into the Silk Guild stuff?

The connection is quite literal: the moth represents the silk moth, the source of the silk they produce and trade. As leaders of the Silk Guild, the Vitelli family oversees the entire lifecycle of the industry in Qaringil, from production to export. While the notes don't go into specific family myths, the choice of a moth emphasizes their commercial identity. They aren't just silk traders; they also have substantial interests in the shipping and mercantile guilds, allowing them to control the flow of their goods across the Central Sea.
How do they fit into the hierarchy of the city? Are they the most powerful family, or are they constantly looking over their shoulder?

They are definitely looking over their shoulder. Qaringil is described as an 'incomprehensible republic' where power is a messy balance between the Great Guilds and the Five Families. The Vitelli are on equal footing with families like the Peixota (who lead the Shipping Guild and use a dolphin sigil) and the Volmer (who lead the Banking Guild and use a crow sigil). They also have to watch out for the Manos family, who lead the Judicial Guild and are currently trying to move into the wool trade, which creates a lot of friction between the textile-focused houses. Like most of the Qaringil elite, the Vitellis are primarily human but carry some elf ancestry.

I've been reading the Qaia moon notes and the physics are fascinating. The concept of Primus as a geostationary longitude reference is such a cool bit of worldbuilding. How exactly does a navigator use a motionless moon to figure out where they are relative to the prime meridian?

It is a unique system. Because Primus is magically anchored in a geostationary orbit directly above the equator at 0 degrees longitude (the sub-Primus point), it serves as a fixed celestial landmark. To find your longitude, you need two things: your current latitude (found via the pole star) and the elevation of Primus above the horizon. You use a precomputed table called a 'primsine' table. The formula is cos(longitude) = primsine(elevation) / cos(latitude). Essentially, since the moon's position never changes, its height in the sky tells you exactly how far away you are from the prime meridian's center point. It removes the need for a precise chronometer, which was the biggest hurdle for longitude on Earth.

That is remarkably efficient. However, you mentioned it is only visible from one hemisphere. What are the actual visibility limits? If I am exploring the far northern reaches of Forthyr or sailing to the absolute edge of the known map, does the moon just dip below the horizon?

Exactly. Primus has a visibility limit determined by the geometry of the planet. The maximum geocentric angle at which it remains above the horizon is about 81.3 degrees from the sub-Primus point. At the equator, you can see it up to roughly 81 degrees east or west of the prime meridian. But as you move toward the poles, that window narrows. For example, at 60 degrees latitude, you can only see Primus if you are within about 72 degrees of the prime meridian. Once you cross that boundary, the moon vanishes below the horizon, and you lose your 'fixed' longitude reference entirely. Navigators near the boundary also have to deal with atmospheric refraction, which makes the elevation readings less precise when the moon is very low.

That creates a massive technological asymmetry. The 'near-side' has a huge advantage in trade and exploration. Do the notes mention how 'far-side' sailors cope? Without the 'Kh'-is anchor (using that 'Kh' pronunciation for Q), are they stuck with ancient methods, or have they developed something else?

You hit on a major point of the setting's geography. The 'far-side' navigators are basically playing on 'hard mode.' The notes specify that they must rely on dead reckoning, stellar transit observations, or the 'lunar-distance method.' This involves tracking the faster-moving moons—specifically Secundus or Quartus—against a star catalogue. It is a much more difficult mathematical problem, identical to what Earth navigators faced before the invention of the marine chronometer. This difficulty might be one reason why the far-side remains largely 'blank space' in the current documents; without the easy fix of Primus, mapping those distant oceans is a significantly more dangerous and error-prone endeavor.

Hi there! I am starting a Qamis game soon. My friend said something about a dragon god named Bahamut having a schism. Is this like the Church of Tyr in Forgotten Realms or something? I am totally new to this.

Welcome to the game! While Bahamut is a common dragon deity in D&D, the situation in Qamis is very specific. The Church of Bahamut is centered in a region called Qis'mos (pronounced Khis-mos, as Q makes a 'Kh' sound). The 'schism' refers to a current split in the church leadership that happened because of the recent thirty-year war. It is less about a change in the god himself and more about a political and physical takeover of his holy city.
Oh, so it is like a 'two popes' situation? I heard the names Kelth and Kachu. Are they the ones fighting? Are they like the high priests of a place like Waterdeep?

That is exactly it. Their names are Qe'lth IV and Qachu II (pronounced Khelth and Khachu). In Qamis, the traditional seat of the church is the holy city of Qama. During the late phases of the Ryraxian War, the city was occupied by drow. Instead of destroying the city, the drow appointed their own high priest, Qe'lth IV, to lead the church there. Because of this, the rest of the clergy supports a different candidate, Qachu II. Qachu II operates out of the city of Qis, but he is at a disadvantage because he does not have the actual 'relics of office'—those are still held by Qe'lth IV in the occupied holy city.

Drow? In the settings I've read about, drow are usually underground bad guys serving a spider queen. Why are they appointing priests for a dragon god in Qama? Is the DM still filling in those blank spaces?

The notes are actually quite clear on this part of the history! In this setting, the drow were recruited as surface allies by the nation of Artkos during a period of desperation in the war. Now that the war is over, they have remained on the surface in several enclaves. Qama is simply the most prominent of these. They haven't despoiled the city, but by appointing Qe'lth IV, they've created a religious crisis that the people of Qis'mos have to navigate. It adds a lot of tension to the region because while the war is technically over, the 'holy city' is still effectively under the control of these foreign drow and their hand-picked priest.

Hey nanochat. I have played in the DM's older Qaringil games, but I am just now digging into the hard science navigation notes for the new Qamis campaign. I see there is a lot of focus on using the moon Primus for longitude. What is the deal with this 'primsine' function? Is it just a fancy sine wave?

Welcome back to the setting. While the Qaringil prequel era focused more on the city-state of Qaringil itself, the current Qamis campaign notes delve into the global navigation advantages of having a geostationary moon. 'Primsine' is not a standard trigonometric function; it is a specific formula derived for Qaia.
